title: Lobbying firm pressed for details about payments to journalists for favourable stories
excerpt:
MPs and others condemn behaviour of CT Group, which offered cash for positive coverage of clients

A political lobbying firm secretly paying journalists for articles favourable to its clients is facin...
body_text:
MPs and others condemn behaviour of CT Group, which offered cash for positive coverage of clients

A political lobbying firm secretly paying journalists for articles favourable to its clients is facing calls to reveal full details of the payments, after the practice was denounced as a dangerous “attack on democracy”.

MPs, senior lobbyists and transparency campaigners all condemned the behaviour of CT Group, the firm co-founded by the campaign strategist Lynton Crosby, an election guru previously hired by both David Cameron and Boris Johnson.

There were calls for an overhaul of lobbying rules and demands for CT Group to reveal the full extent of the practice, exposed after a four-month Guardian investigation.

“Lobbyists paying journalists to produce apparently independent articles is an attack on the ethics of journalism, public relations and, indeed, on democracy,” said Alastair McCapra, the chief executive of the Chartered Institute of Public Relations. CT Group is not a member of the body.

“Anyone can practise public relations in this country without belonging to a professional body or answering to a code of conduct. The majority do that ethically but these reported practices undermine our profession and erode the credibility on which responsible lobbying and public relations depend.”

Lobbyists for CT Group told an undercover reporter they had paid journalists to place apparently independent stories in newspapers. They also offered hundreds of pounds in cash incentives to do the same for a property industry client and said that in return for the payment, CT Group would have increased editorial control.

Jemimah Steinfeld, the chief executive of Index on Censorship, said: “To spin what is lobbying as journalism is shortsighted and frankly dangerous.”

She called on CT Group to launch an internal investigation.

Steve Goodrich, the head of research and investigations at Transparency International UK, said: “When lobbyists secretly pay journalists to write favourable coverage for their clients, it undermines the integrity of the press. This investigation raises serious questions about the conduct of CT Group, and about how widespread these hidden arrangements are across the lobbying industry.”

Luke Taylor, a Liberal Democrat member of parliament’s public administration and constitutional affairs committee, said there should now be “full transparency on when money has changed hands when it comes to reporting”.

“Across the board we are seeing worrying signs of our democracy being in peril,” he said. “A truly free press is a fundamental pillar of a healthy democracy and so we must do everything in our power to maintain it.”

Rupa Huq, a Labour member of the Commons media select committee, said: “We need more transparency in lobbying which almost feels like the wild west, such is the lack of regulation.” She added: “I hope the new Andy Burnham government can grip this issue.”

The Guardian began investigating the arrangements after CT Group approached a freelance journalist and offered to pay him to pitch a piece to a British national newspaper about problems caused for the housebuilding industry by the Building Safety Regulator, set up in the wake of the Grenfell Tower fire that killed 72 people.

CT Group is lobbying company with a £40m-a-year turnover whose recent clients include the tobacco firm Philip Morris and the owner of the football club Manchester City.

In a statement, CT Group said: “We do not comment publicly on work we do for clients and we therefore cannot respond to the specific allegations you have made.”

It said the events had been “deliberately mischaracterised” and that it had not engaged the undercover journalist to do anything and did not pay him anything. It said the journalist was looking for work and “chased us relentlessly over a period of more than six months”.

The investigation has also raised concerns about the vulnerability of freelance journalism, which can often feature irregular and low-paying work.

“If reporters are being paid to place stories in papers by private companies this breaches the essential buffer zone between lobbying and journalism,” said Martin Bright, a senior lecturer in journalism at the University of Essex.

“The market is very tough for freelance journalists in the present media climate. They should never be exploited in this way.”

title: Former ESPN star Trey Wingo claps back at Emmanuel Acho over his claim about Ryan Clark building 'NFL Live'
excerpt:
OutKick reporter Dan Zaksheske joins Dan Dakich to highlight how Ryan Clark's end at ESPN went down.

Former ESPN star Trey Wingo wants to set the record straight.

Former NFL linebacker and ex-ESPN a...
body_text:
OutKick reporter Dan Zaksheske joins Dan Dakich to highlight how Ryan Clark's end at ESPN went down.

Former ESPN star Trey Wingo wants to set the record straight.

Former NFL linebacker and ex-ESPN analyst Emmanuel Acho claimed that Ryan Clark built ESPN’s "NFL Live" into the "juggernaut" it is today, and Wingo was not having any of it.

"Ryan Clark built NFL Live into the juggernaut it is today (along with Rutledge, Mina, and Spears), he didn’t ideate NFL Live, there’s a difference," Acho posted.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Trey Wingo looks on during the first round of the American Century Championship on July 10, 2026, at Edgewood Tahoe Golf Course in Stateline, Nev. (Matthew Huang/Icon Sportswire via Getty Images)

Wingo, who hosted "NFL Live" for 15 years, from 2003 to 2018, clapped back at Acho’s claim that Clark built the show by posting a graphic showing its ratings over the years, which he said disproved Acho’s claim.

The graphic, which was based on numbers that ESPN reported, showed that the "NFL Live" averaged 688,000 viewers during a comparable early-season period in 2013 and 624,000 in 2012. It averaged roughly 486,000 in 2015, 492,000 in 2016, 449,000 in 2021 and 440,000 in 2025.

Wingo captioned his post on X, "’Let your apology be as loud as your disrespect.’ I think someone said that once."

The former ESPN host’s line about letting your apology be as loud as your disrespect referenced a spat in which Acho insulted former San Francisco 49ers wide receiver Deebo Samuel and later apologized to him.

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

Emmanuel Acho speaks onstage during the ADL Never Is Now Summit at the Jacob K. Javits Convention Center in New York City on March 17, 2026. (Roy Rochlin/Getty Images for ADL)

Acho's initial tweet was in response to OutKick’s Dan Zaksheske. Zaksheske began the thread of interaction by responding to a clip from "The Breakfast Club," where the morning show hosts said Clark built "NFL Live."

"NFL Live premiered in 1998 when Clark was 18 years old, four years before he entered the NFL and 17 years before he joined ESPN," Zaksheske wrote on X.

Acho responded to Zaksheske and agreed with "The Breakfast Club," saying "And nobody was watching NFL Live then," referencing the show’s existence before Clark joined it.

ESPN is parting ways with longtime NFL analyst Ryan Clark following an 11-year run at the network. (Michael Allio/Icon Sportswire via Getty Images)

Wingo spent nearly 23 years at ESPN and was not retained in November 2020.

A large part of his tenure was spent hosting and building "NFL Live," and was the host during its peak year in 2013.

Clark joined ESPN in 2015 and spent more than 11 seasons with the network, overlapping for about five years with Wingo. As his time at ESPN progressed, he became an increasingly polarizing figure.

Fox News Digital reached out to Acho for comment but did not immediately get a response.

OutKick's Dan Zaksheske contributed to this report.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

Ryan Canfield is a digital production assistant for Fox News Digital.

title: ‘Customers prefer AI chatbots to humans’ says British Gas boss as 1,300 call centre staff axed
excerpt:
Chris O’Shea defends Centrica’s plans as it reports rise in retail profits despite falling customer numbers

The owner of British Gas has claimed that most households would rather speak with an AI cha...
body_text:
Chris O’Shea defends Centrica’s plans as it reports rise in retail profits despite falling customer numbers

The owner of British Gas has claimed that most households would rather speak with an AI chatbot than deal with the company’s staff as it prepares to cut 1,300 jobs from its call centres.

Centrica, the supplier’s FTSE 100 owner, plans to cut 800 jobs as the company carries out a “targeted deployment of AI tools”, on top of the 500 cutsit confirmed last month.

The plan to reduce the supplier’s customer service teams in Glasgow, Edinburgh, Cardiff, Leicester, Stockport and Leeds by 14% is expected to take place over two years, meaning some roles will be left unfilled after resignations, while the balance will come from redundancies.

Trade unions have previously said the company’s investment in artificial intelligence to help simplify its business would lead to “hundreds of human jobs” beiing given to chatbots.

But Chris O’Shea, the company’s chief executive, insisted that the changes were being made because customers preferred to take their queries to digital channels rather than people.

“AI isn’t driving these particular job reductions; that’s mainly due to changing customer behaviour,” he said. “What we’re seeing just now is over 90% of our customers actually use digital channels in the first instance, and we’ve seen a 20% reduction in customer calls, so this simply reflects the changing customer behaviour.

“We’ve got to reflect the changes in customer behaviour. We expect to grow more jobs around our digital interface, and maybe having fewer people on the phones.”

O’Shea defence of Centrica’s plans came as it reported a rise in retail profits over the first half of the year despite falling customer numbers.

British Gas has gradually lost customers to rival suppliers in recent years. It is expected to pay out up to £112m in compensation to thousands of British Gas customers who had prepayment meters force-fitted in their homes at the height of the Russian gas crisis in the biggest energy supplier settlement on record.

Profits from Centrica’s retail division, which includes revenues from British Gas as well as its boiler care and smart energy products, rose to £346m in the first six months of the year, from £338m in the same period last year.

Its profits climbed even as the number of British Gas domestic customers fell to 7.45 million, from 7.5 million at the end of last year, because the supplier has focused on making bigger profit margins from its fixed-price tariffs rather than “chase loss-making business”, O’Shea said.

title: New CIA thriller 'The Fix' blends heist, espionage genres with Liam Neeson, Zachary Levi
excerpt:
Marc Menchaca and Juan Pablo Raba preview the high-stakes shift to Texas for Beth and Rip as the Paramount+ series premieres May 15 with two episodes, followed by weekly releases.

The trailer has arr...
body_text:
Marc Menchaca and Juan Pablo Raba preview the high-stakes shift to Texas for Beth and Rip as the Paramount+ series premieres May 15 with two episodes, followed by weekly releases.

The trailer has arrived for Liam Neeson's upcoming CIA film, "The Fix."

HOLLYWOOD LEGEND MAKING MOVIE ABOUT EPIC IRAN RESCUE MISSION TO SAVE F-15 FIGHTER JET CREW

Liam Neeson on Wednesday, Feb. 15, 2023. (Nathan Congleton/NBC via Getty Images)

There's nothing that gets the blood pumping like a great CIA film. The world of black ops secrets and espionage is such a fun genre.

Zachary Levi has also generated controversy with his comments about the ongoing SAG-AFTRA Hollywood strike. (Jason Mendez/Getty Images)

A team of CIA commandos find themselves hunting money deep inside Iran. Pretty timely, given the massive war that is currently raging against the regime.

The trailer paints a picture that it's going to be the perfect guy film. Give it a watch below, and let me know your thoughts at David.Hookstead@outkick.com.

This movie looks like it's going to be legit, and it's not often you get a spy film involving Iran. Add in the fact the cast is being led by Liam Neeson with Zachary Levi playing a major role, and all the ingredients are there for a very fun film.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

It also blends the heist genre with the espionage genre. Again, it's almost like it was built in a lab for your average American male viewer.

That's not a complaint. That's a compliment.

Liam Neeson attends "The Naked Gun" UK Premiere at Cineworld Leicester Square on July 22, 2025, in London, England. (Jeff Spicer/Getty Images for Paramount Pictures)

You can catch "The Fix" starting Sept. 11, and make sure to let me know your thoughts at David.Hookstead@outkick.com.

David Hookstead is a reporter for OutKick.

title: Johnson delivers big for Trump, but frustration grows across razor-thin GOP majority
excerpt: The House speaker argued America's founding principles depend on belief in God, telling Christian leaders that removing the Creator from the nation's identity paves the way for Marxism, socialism and ...
body_text:
The House speaker argued America's founding principles depend on belief in God, telling Christian leaders that removing the Creator from the nation's identity paves the way for Marxism, socialism and the loss of individual freedom.

Speaker Mike Johnson, R-La., enjoyed a series of wins this week – but there’s little time to celebrate, as Republicans grow increasingly frustrated with how he is managing the razor-thin House majority.

"He’s capitulating to the wants of the Freedom Caucus," one House Republican, granted anonymity to speak freely, told Fox News Digital. "The Freedom Caucus are in control. The rest of us are just following."

The Louisiana Republican is fresh off a slew of legislative victories this week, notching success with bills curbing congressional stock trading, averting a government shutdown, and setting the stage for a $95 billion budget that includes a top election priority of President Donald Trump.

GOP'S $95B DEFENSE PACKAGE SURVIVES CRITICAL HURDLE AMID FISCAL HAWK REVOLT

Speaker Mike Johnson, R-La., speaks to reporters outside the House chambers in the U.S. Capitol Building in Washington, D.C., on June 30, 2026. (Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images)

Johnson has managed to snatch victory from the jaws of defeat dozens of times since winning the speaker’s gavel in October 2023. But the pressure of running a razor-thin House majority is still enough to send tensions boiling, particularly within an ideologically diverse Republican conference before what’s expected to be a politically bruising battle to keep control of Congress.

Several House Republicans have told Fox News Digital this week that Johnson is not doing enough to bolster his rank-and-file members and their own individual legislative priorities.

A second House GOP lawmaker granted anonymity to speak candidly, said, "there’s frustration within the conference around the speaker not pushing back, and not saying no to the White House."

'IT'S A MESS': GOP TURNS ON HOUSE CONSERVATIVES AS VOTER ID BLOCKADE STALLS TRUMP'S AGENDA

"Members have said ‘We don’t report to the White House. We don’t report to the executive branch. We should be able to make these decisions on our own,’" the second House GOP lawmaker said.

Johnson’s allies have consistently argued that his close relationship with Trump has been key to his staying power and success.

But not everyone believes Johnson is prioritizing the needs of his conservative right flank or the president over others.

TOP TRUMP OFFICIAL MAKES NEW SAVE ACT CASE TO REPUBLICANS BEHIND CLOSED DOORS

Rep. Keith Self, R-Texas, a member of the House Freedom Caucus, told Fox News Digital, "There does seem to be a focus on a few in the conference, and the reasoning is, the most vulnerable. But that by definition excludes some other members."

"All I will say is, we need to continue to focus on what our base wants, because we have a very important election coming up here pretty quick," Self said.

President Donald J. Trump delivers his first State of the Union address of his second term to a joint session of Congress in the House chamber of the United States Capitol on Feb. 24, 2026, in Washington, D.C. (Kenny Holston/The New York Times/Pool via Reuters)

Others, still, simply believe House GOP leadership could be doing more to help lawmakers across the conference.

"I know it’s hard. I mean, being the speaker is the hardest job in the country," Rep. Nicole Malliotakis, R-N.Y., told Fox News Digital on Tuesday.

And while she maintained that Johnson was still very much in control of the House floor and his fellow Republicans, she said, "I think there’s more that can be done to help members score wins that may be small in general, but are big and important for their districts."

"I think they haven’t been proactive in going to the membership and saying, ‘Give me your top three priorities, not controversial, don’t cost money,’ and help us navigate the committee process to get those to the floor," Malliotakis said. "I’ve got a bill for example, like, [high-occupancy vehicle] lanes for veterans — allowing veterans who are going to the VA, or going for treatment, to be able to use the HOV lanes…I’ve got one that would…go after heroin injection centers in New York."

Rep. Tim Burchett, R-Tenn., was even more blunt: "You’ve got to kiss a– to move legislation up here, and it’s just, it’s really getting to be a ridiculous situation."

The frustrations hitting Johnson from different sides of the conference are a consequence of Republicans’ razor-thin House majority — only being able to lose two seats on any party-line vote with full attendance — and the vastly different approaches to legislating.

Still, other House Republicans maintain that Johnson is doing a good job of sticking by his allies.

"The ones who are complaining today will be the same ones who are complaining even when times are good. It’s just who they are," a third House Republican, speaking anonymously, told Fox News Digital.

And Rep. Dusty Johnson, R-S.D., said House Republicans have managed to accomplish a significant number of their policy goals this term.

"It’s actually a pretty remarkable testament both to the patience of Mike Johnson and to the willingness of the White House to engage," the South Dakota Republican said.

Congressman Tim Burchett speaks to reporters following a House Republican Conference meeting at the U.S. Capitol on July 23, 2024 in Washington, D.C. (Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images)

Another Johnson ally who was granted anonymity pointed out that he spends his non-legislative time raising money for campaigns across the conference, whether people agree with him or not. "Outside the Capitol, Johnson has a second full-time job as lead fundraiser — he’s on track to raise $500 million to ensure everyone, no matter their caucus, returns next year in the majority."

Johnson’s allies would also argue that the broad range of complaints highlighted in this story is an example of his focus on trying to listen to every member of the House GOP, and that there has not been any push for his ouster despite the rumblings.

But with such a slim House majority, it’s a fact that every vote counts.

As the second House Republican put it, "Everyone has a bullet in the chamber, and they’re not afraid to use it."

Elizabeth Elkind is a politics reporter for Fox News Digital leading coverage of the House of Representatives. Previous digital bylines seen at Daily Mail and CBS News.

Follow on Twitter at @liz_elkind and send tips to elizabeth.elkind@fox.com

title: Michigan Democrat accuses Abdul El-Sayed of 'deeply sexist attacks' on female political rivals
excerpt:
Rep. Hillary Scholten accused Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed of making sexist remarks about female Democrats, including Rep. Haley Stevens and Gov. Gretchen Whitmer.

Rep. Hillary Scholten, ...
body_text:
Rep. Hillary Scholten accused Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed of making sexist remarks about female Democrats, including Rep. Haley Stevens and Gov. Gretchen Whitmer.

Rep. Hillary Scholten, D-Mich., accused progressive Democratic Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed of making "deeply sexist attacks" against female opponents during a Wednesday interview, citing his criticism of Rep. Haley Stevens, D-Mich., and Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer as voters prepare for the state's Aug. 4 primary.

"There is a uniquely sexist bent to the things that he has said about women," Scholten told anchor Jake Tapper on CNN's "The Lead." "He said that Congresswoman Stevens couldn’t string two sentences together, that, you know, she was sort of an empty vessel."

Scholten, who endorsed Stevens in the race to replace retiring Sen. Gary Peters, D-Mich., argued El-Sayed had disparaged Stevens’ intelligence and personality rather than focusing on policy disagreements.

DAVID MARCUS: IN BOOMING DETROIT, VOTERS SKEPTICAL OF SOCIALISM SNAKE OIL

Rep. Hillary Scholten accused Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed of making "deeply sexist attacks" against female Democrats. (Sarah Rice/Getty Images)

"It’s one thing to attack your opponent because of the substance of their issues," Scholten said. "It’s another thing to engage in what are deeply sexist attacks."

Fox News Digital reached out to Stevens and El-Sayed for comment but did not immediately receive a response.

El-Sayed previously told Semafor that Stevens was "a suit with a large AIPAC account" and suggested the pro-Israel group might teach her "how to string together two coherent sentences."

DEM CIVIL WAR HITS PRIMARY DEBATE STAGE IN FIERY BATTLEGROUND SHOWDOWN: 'WHAT ARE YOU HIDING?'

Scholten criticized El-Sayed’s remarks about Rep. Haley Stevens and Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer during a CNN interview. (AP Photo/Kristen Norman)

Scholten pointed to El-Sayed's rhetoric during his unsuccessful 2018 gubernatorial campaign against Whitmer, whose father previously served as president and CEO of Blue Cross Blue Shield of Michigan.

"Going back to his race with Governor Whitmer, he said she was bought and sold for by her dad’s company," Scholten said. "Essentially comparing her to property with no ideas of her own."

El-Sayed spokesperson Roxie Richner pushed back when Scholten first publicly raised concerns about his record with women.

"Unless the congresswoman has specific accusations she would like to make, perhaps it’s better to avoid repeating throwaway talking points to push an unfounded narrative," Richner said to Semafor.

ABDUL EL-SAYED SAYS RALLYING WITH HASAN PIKER DOESN'T MEAN HE ENDORSES 'AMERICA DESERVED 9/11' REMARK

Scholten warned that embracing candidates such as El-Sayed could take the Democratic Party in the wrong direction.

He addressed his remarks about Stevens during a Sunday interview on CNN's "State of the Union" after Tapper confronted him with the "two coherent sentences" comment.

"I don’t think it’s kind to make fun of somebody for how they talk or how they show up," El-Sayed said. "I also don’t think it’s kind to make insinuations about somebody’s character, as is being done to the tune of tens of millions of dollars."

Scholten said she was even more concerned by El-Sayed's decision to campaign with left-wing streamer Hasan Piker, who said in 2019 that "America deserved 9/11." El-Sayed has defended appearing with Piker while distancing himself from the comment.

"I give him credit for disavowing it after the fact, but the reality is he also didn’t have to choose to campaign with Hasan Piker," Scholten said. "To me, that’s a nonstarter."

When asked if she would support El-Sayed if he defeated Stevens, Scholten did not provide a direct answer to CNN.

"I cannot, I will not even come close to being associated with comments like America deserved September 11," Scholten said.

The congresswoman warned that embracing candidates associated with such rhetoric could further damage the Democratic Party.

"I think that condoning behavior like this from candidates like Abdul El-Sayed is taking us in a direction that we don’t want to go as a party," Scholten said.

El-Sayed's campaign urged supporters Friday to stop making personal attacks against Stevens.

"It’s become impossible to ignore the way that people online make fun of my opponent for things that have nothing to do with her policies or politics. It’s unkind and unhelpful," El-Sayed said. "If you support me, please stop."

CJ Womack is an associate editor at Fox News.

CJ joined Fox News Digital's team in 2026, which highlights the vital role journalism plays in shaping politics and culture. He has years of experience analyzing and reporting on the news media.

CJ graduated from Long Beach State University in 2025 with a Bachelor of Arts in Political Science and a minor in Journalism.

You can send news tips to cj.womack@fox.com and follow CJ on Twitter.

title: Republicans fall in line with Trump on Iran war despite rising costs and US death toll
excerpt: Chief foreign correspondent Trey Yingst reports President Donald Trump has declared Iran’s military capabilities are crippled, attributing it to the U.S. Armed Forces, while technical discussions for ...
body_text:
Chief foreign correspondent Trey Yingst reports President Donald Trump has declared Iran’s military capabilities are crippled, attributing it to the U.S. Armed Forces, while technical discussions for nuclear negotiations are set to resume. Sen. John Kennedy, R-La., criticizes Democratic efforts to undermine Trump’s Middle East diplomacy, following the Senate's rejection of a war powers measure.

Senate Republicans, despite a mounting death toll, surging energy prices and growing uncertainty, batted down another attempt by Democrats to handcuff President Donald Trump in his war with Iran.

Democrats pulled the trigger on yet another war powers resolution, the first since Trump resumed strikes in the Middle East, and were again unsuccessful in convincing enough Republicans to force the administration to get authorization from Congress.

Thursday’s war powers resolution was forced by Sen. Chris Van Hollen, D-Md., who argued that, "the main thing is that we need to continue to hold senators of both parties accountable for ending this senseless war."

REPUBLICANS BREAK WITH TRUMP TO REBUKE IRAN WAR — BUT IT WON'T CHANGE POLICY

President Donald Trump speaks during a meeting with Prime Minister of Iraq Ali al-Zaidi in the Oval Office of the White House in Washington, DC, on July 14, 2026. (SAUL LOEB / AFP via Getty Images)

"You know, President Trump said that we won the war back in March," Van Hollen said. "And the question we've got is: If we won the war in March, how is it that American service members are still being killed and gas prices are going through the roof again?"

Van Hollen, and nearly every single Senate Democrat — save for Sen. John Fetterman, D-Pa., who has routinely voted against war powers resolutions — only managed to carve off one Republican, Sen. Susan Collins, R-Maine, in their pursuit of reining in Trump. Sens. Lisa Murkowski, R-Alaska, and Rand Paul, R-Ky., who have routinely voted with Democrats on war powers, did not vote.

SENATE DEMOCRATS FINALLY CRACK GOP UNITY ON TRUMP'S IRAN WAR AS MURKOWSKI FLIPS

Whether the resolution would be successful remained up in the air until moments before the vote.

Sen. Bill Cassidy, R-La., who previously acted as the decisive vote to advance a war powers resolution last month, was again the key wild card on Thursday. But just before the alarm sounded to open the vote in the Senate, Cassidy revealed he would oppose Van Hollen’s effort.

"I am going to oppose the War Powers resolution," Cassidy said. "I think it's impractical to, like, withdraw tomorrow."

The Senate’s failed effort comes after Democrats in the House scored a win against Trump, where they carved off just enough Republican support to pass their own war powers resolution.

4 HOUSE REPUBLICANS REBEL AGAINST TRUMP, VOTE WITH DEMS TO BLOCK IRAN WAR POWERS

U.S. Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-LA) arrives for a meeting with Senate Republicans in the U.S. Capitol on June 28, 2025 in Washington, DC. Senate Republicans overnight released a new version of the "One, Big, Beautiful Bill," as they approach U.S. President Donald Trump's July 4 deadline. (Photo by Al Drago/Getty Images)

In arguing against the move, Sen. Tim Sheehy, R-Mont., urged Senate Democrats to recognize the dangers that Iran posed to the U.S., and to the service members overseas.

"They want all of us dead. They don't want us to disappear with the snap of a finger," Sheehy said on the Senate floor. "They want us to be raped, burned alive, and cut into pieces. Watch the video from October 7th."

"That's what they want to happen," he continued. "From Maine to San Diego, from Seattle to Miami, they want this country turned into a burning husk of its former self."

Alex Miller is a writer for Fox News Digital covering the U.S. Senate.

title: Ethiopian migrant allegedly killed 3 over $65 parking dispute, claimed he was victim of anti-immigrant bias
excerpt: Laura Ingraham and former Acting I.C.E. Director Jonathan Fahey strongly criticize Democrats for their approach to illegal immigration and criminal justice. They highlight Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz's co...
body_text:
Laura Ingraham and former Acting I.C.E. Director Jonathan Fahey strongly criticize Democrats for their approach to illegal immigration and criminal justice. They highlight Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz's controversial pardon of a child rapist, who was also an illegal alien, and a lenient sentence for an illegal alien truck driver in California. Ingraham also questions why victims' rights often seem secondary.

An Ethiopian migrant allegedly murdered three employees at his St. Paul apartment building after becoming enraged over a $65 parking deck contract, days after Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz was rebuked for defending the pardon of an illegal immigrant sex offender.

Police recovered nearly three dozen 9mm shell casings from the management office after Tsegaab Ademassu Binessu, 30, allegedly went on a rampage over apartment parking contract policies. Authorities said he later complained about the U.S. immigration system.

Binessu, who the local FOX affiliate said had been employed as a truck driver until recently, became angry after trying to cancel his parking contract shortly after renewing it because he was considering joining the U.S. Navy.

TIM WALZ PARDONS ILLEGAL ALIEN CONVICTED OF ARMED ROBBERY BEFORE ICE DEPORTATION TO LAOS

Federal agents are seen walking in uniform. A report from The New York Times said the Trump administration has lost or abandoned hundreds of cases in which it accused anti-ICE protesters and migrants of assaulting federal immigration agents. (John Moore/Getty Images)

Adam "AJ" Wilwerding, 43, Nansi Fuentes Zambrano, 25, and Dena Silcox, 56, were found dead Monday after Binessu allegedly told an employee who answered the phone that he was coming to the office, according to the New York Post.

Wilwerding and Silcox were shot six times each, while Zambrano was reportedly shot as she tried to escape through a back-office window.

MAN CHARGED IN CRASH THAT KILLED MOTHER AND TWO YOUNG SONS HAS ICE DETAINER, PRIOR DWI CONVICTIONS

Caution tape at a crime scene, left, inset by Ethiopian migrant Tsegaab Ademassu Binessu, inset right. (Al Drago/Getty Images; Ramsey County MN Sheriff's Office)

Binessu allegedly confessed during a phone call with his longtime roommate, who said the migrant had "argued with apartment management and ended up killing three people," according to the local FOX affiliate.

Binessu was eventually arrested in Virginia, Minnesota, several hundred miles north of St. Paul near International Falls and the Canadian border.

PATEL TURNS TABLES ON WALZ IN RESPONSE TO VIRAL TWEET ON MINNESOTA FRAUD RAIDS: 'COME AGAIN?'

He was detained while driving one of the victims' cars and reportedly complained to investigators about the American political system and his inability to bring his wife and children from Ethiopia to the United States, which the local FOX affiliate said contributed to his divorce.

Binessu also reportedly complained he was being treated differently because of his race and immigration status.

Fox News Radio host Guy Benson noted how Binessu had believed he was unfairly treated because "he is Black and an immigrant."

"So, he allegedly shot three women to death," Benson said. "Will Gov. Walz warn us not to judge this man over his ‘worst day,’ or whatever his recent excuse was for pardoning & defending that child sexual abuser?"

Fox News Digital reached out to Walz for a response.

Born and raised in Allentown, Pennsylvania, worked in both the New York and Washington bureaus for Fox News since 2013.

title: Mayor shot dead in Mexico months after surviving assassination attempt
excerpt:
Valentín Lavín Romero is the 14th mayor to be murdered since Claudia Sheinbaum became president in 2024

A mayor was shot dead on Wednesday in the Mexican state of Morelos after having survived a prev...
body_text:
Valentín Lavín Romero is the 14th mayor to be murdered since Claudia Sheinbaum became president in 2024

A mayor was shot dead on Wednesday in the Mexican state of Morelos after having survived a previous assassination attempt earlier this year. He is the 14th mayor to be murdered since Claudia Sheinbaum became president in October 2024.

Valentín Lavín Romero, who was from the Green party, an ally of the ruling leftwing Morena party, was wounded at the end of January when hitmen shot him at a petrol station on a motorway.

This time the hitmen entered the town hall of Temoac, near Mexico City, in the middle of the day before shooting Lavín Romero dead and escaping on a motorbike.

Authorities are yet to identify a motive or suspects, but the Morelos prosecutor’s office vowed to “exhaust all lines of investigation”.

Almost 100 mayors have been murdered in Mexico since 2006, when the then president, Felipe Calderón, deployed the military to take on Mexico’s organised crime groups, ultimately causing drug-related violence to rise.

The municipal level of government is the least protected layer of the state and where criminal groups seek deals with authorities to strengthen their hold over the local territory and its businesses.

Local media reported Lavín Romero himself allegedly had ties to local crime groups. His mother-in-law, a former treasurer for the municipal government, was arrested in September 2025 for alleged ties to a criminal group known as Los Aparicio and involvement in kidnappings.

In March, the People’s Front in Defence of Land and Water, an activist group, demanded authorities investigate Lavín Romero for his alleged involvement in the murders of two activists, Samir Flores and Sandra Rosa Camacho Flores.

In May – three months after the first attempt on his life – a banner was left in the centre of Temoac, seemingly by a criminal group, accusing Lavín Romero of protecting its rivals and demanding he fall in line with their interests.

His assassination comes as Mexico’s federal government takes steps to unpick the corruption networks between municipal authorities and criminal groups as part of Operation Swarm, under which 85 officials have been arrested across three states, including Morelos.

According to official data, the homicide rate in Mexico has fallen by roughly half over the past year, although security analysts say the true reduction is likely smaller, while more than 130,000 people are registered as missing.

title: Yankees player's pitch clock antics prompt wild hot-mic moment, backlash: 'You're picking on me'
excerpt:
New York Yankees infielder Jose Caballero got into a heated discussion with a home plate umpire about his pitch clock antics on Wednesday. (This video contains AI-assisted content.)

New York Yankees ...
body_text:
New York Yankees infielder Jose Caballero got into a heated discussion with a home plate umpire about his pitch clock antics on Wednesday. (This video contains AI-assisted content.)

New York Yankees infielder Jose Caballero's pitch-clock maneuvers irked multiple parties earlier this week.

On Monday night, the benches cleared against the Pittsburgh Pirates after pitcher Dennis Santana had some choice words for Caballero, and two days later, hot mics caught Caballero in a heated discussion with an umpire.

In short, Caballero has become notorious for waiting until the last possible second to make eye-contact with the pitcher, which, in the pitch-clock era, is required before a pitcher comes set or starts his windup. Batters and pitchers have until there are eight seconds left to become alert with the opponent.

New York Yankees shortstop Jose Caballero reacts after being called for a clock violation by home plate umpire Quinn Wolcott during the sixth inning against the Pittsburgh Pirates at Yankee Stadium in the Bronx on July 22, 2026. (John Jones/Imagn Images)

Caballero did his usual on Wednesday afternoon, though, and was promptly called for a violation before striking out, despite even looking up a second earlier. Mics picked up Caballero saying that the umpire was "picking on" him. Home plate umpire Quinn Wolcott said Caballero did not "get to do the three-four seconds thing in the box anymore."

Caballero replied that he had been "doing my f—ing routine," and manager Aaron Boone said the umps were "making up rules as we go."

Manager Aaron Boone said that neither he nor anyone else on the Yankees were made aware that Caballero could potentially be called for violations. However, Wolcott cited the MLB rules, which state that maneuvers by batters "to deceive a pitcher into beginning their windup or coming to the set position" will first result in warnings before being called for pitch-clock violations.

Crew chief Adrian Johnson said Caballero has "been warned all season," prompting a change in how he is dealt with.

New York Yankees shortstop Jose Caballero walks off the field after benches cleared during the eighth inning against the Pittsburgh Pirates at Yankee Stadium in the Bronx on July 20, 2026. (Vincent Carchietta/Imagn Images)

FATHER TURNING FAMILY TRAGEDY INTO EVERY BASEBALL FAN'S DREAM OF VISITING 30 MLB STADIUMS IN 30 DAYS

"He's been warned all season. We were instructed not to warn him. We were instructed to violate him. That's what it is. It's a violation," he said to a pool reporter.

So, as long as umps believe Caballero is purposely baiting pitchers, the Yankees might have a losing battle here.

Johnson also said that the violation was a result of the benches-clearing incident on Monday.

"He’s not allowed to do an inordinate amount of time looking down, trying to get the pitcher to cause a violation," Wolcott told a pool reporter. "There’s no other hitter that gets in the box, takes the amount of the time that Caballero does while looking down, not making eye contact with the pitcher. Obviously it’s caused a lot of problems in the past. It’s not going to be allowed anymore."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Boone called the umpires' decisions "embarrassing." Based on previous conversations with MLB, Boone instructed Caballero to "make sure you’re looking up at nine so that you are alert by eight."

Manager Aaron Boone of the New York Yankees argues with home plate umpire Quinn Wolcott after the sixth inning against the Pittsburgh Pirates in game one of a doubleheader at Yankee Stadium on July 22, 2026, in New York City. (Getty Images)

"He actually said that they were making new rules today and it’s on me because I was deceiving or something like that to the pitcher, which doesn’t make sense," Caballero said. "I don’t know, maybe my English is so bad that I don’t understand, but deceiving kind of means that you’re trying to trick someone and I’m not trying to trick someone because I do the same thing over and over. You can check my last 50 at-bats and see if I do it the same or not, or check maybe 100 at-bats or 150."

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

title: Florida woman on the run and wanted for murder relied on network of alleged accomplices to evade capture: Cops
excerpt:
Isabelle Johnson, 38, is facing a murder charge in the death of 43-year-old Jason Christopher Coulthart and has been evading law enforcement, authorities said. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

A woma...
body_text:
Isabelle Johnson, 38, is facing a murder charge in the death of 43-year-old Jason Christopher Coulthart and has been evading law enforcement, authorities said. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

A woman wanted in a murder case has spent weeks slipping through law enforcement's grasp, with investigators alleging a string of suspected accomplices helped keep her hidden while she remains on the run.

Isabelle Rose Johnson, 38, is wanted on charges in the killing of Jason Christopher Coulthart. A warrant for her arrest was issued on June 26, and she has been dodging detectives ever since.

The Walton County Sheriff's Office said Johnson, who is a "cunning master manipulator," has been relying on the help of others to stay incognito. Five people have been arrested in connection to the murder investigation.

FLORIDA WOMAN CHARGED WITH MURDER EVADING POLICE AFTER BODY FOUND IN SHALLOW GRAVE: SHERIFF

Five people have been arrested in connection to the murder of Jason Coulthart. Isabelle Johnson, 38, is wanted for murder and has allegedly been relying on these individuals to evade arrest. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

As the sheriff's office continues to hunt Johnson down, investigators shared different photos of Johnson seen on surveillance and a mugshot from Carroll County, Georgia.

Isabelle Rose Johnson, 38, wanted for murder has reportedly been using different disguises to avoid capture by law enforcement. Authorities shared different snapshots of her to ask for the public's help in tracking her down. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST TRUE CRIME NEWS

Johnson has worn different hairstyles, hats and even eyeglasses to change up her appearance.

She also has reportedly been giving out different names to identify herself, like "Harley, Jessica Elaine Bowman, and Denise" to different people she comes in contact with, the sheriff's office said.

As for the man she is accused of killing, Coulthart's remains were found on June 25, buried on a property in Freeport, located in Florida's Panhandle.

Jason Christopher Coulthart, 43, was found buried in a shallow grave at a property in Niceville, Florida. He leaves behind a son. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE

He was reported missing a month earlier on May 24. The last time Coulthart was seen alive was when he was leaving the College Condominiums on East College Boulevard.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

The 43-year-old victim leaves behind a son, according to his obituary.

Sheriff Michael Adkinson said, "Anyone found to have helped or is still assisting Isabelle Johnson avoid arrest will be charged accordingly."

He went on to say, "If you give her a place to stay, help her hide, or lie for her, you will face consequences. This is a homicide case. We will not allow anyone to get in the way of this investigation."

Multiple law enforcement organizations have come together to offer up a $16,000 reward for information that could lead to Johnson's arrest.

title: Men switched at birth uncover hospital mix-up after DNA test decades later
excerpt: Jeremy Morrison and Kyle Bylin, born in 1988, share their story after a DNA test revealed they were switched at birth nearly 40 years ago. The men are now suing Unity Medical Center in North Dakota fo...
body_text:
Jeremy Morrison and Kyle Bylin, born in 1988, share their story after a DNA test revealed they were switched at birth nearly 40 years ago. The men are now suing Unity Medical Center in North Dakota for answers, as the hospital claims no existing records from that time. This extraordinary case highlights the emotional impact of such a mix-up.

Two North Dakota men are suing a local hospital after discovering they were switched at birth nearly four decades ago, a revelation brought to light by a surprise result from a direct-to-consumer genetic test.

The men joined Fox & Friends on Thursday to discuss their journey.

The mix-up was uncovered two years ago when Kyle Bylin received an at-home DNA test as a gift. Bylin says he was shocked when the results showed no genetic match to anyone in the family that raised him.

TWO FAMILIES SUE NORTH DAKOTA HOSPITAL ON ALLEGATIONS BABIES WERE SWITCHED AT BIRTH

"It was absolutely mind-blowing," Bylin told Fox & Friends. "We knew the DNA trail didn’t make sense, but to find out you’ve been switched at birth is a huge revelation."

Instead, the data pointed to a biological aunt, leading Bylin to her nephew, Jeremy Morrison. Subsequent DNA testing confirmed that Morrison and Bylin had been raised by each other’s biological families.

"[My parents] will always be my parents. A DNA test isn’t going to change 38 years of memories overnight," Morrison said.

Kyle Bylin and Jeremy Morrison were the only two babies born at Unity Medical Center in Grafton, North Dakota, on Jan. 26, 1988, the lawsuit says. (Google Maps)

Morrison recalled having long-standing doubts while growing up, noting that he often made light of the physical differences between himself and the woman who raised him.

MELINDA FRENCH GATES SAYS IT TOOK 3 DOCTORS TO DIAGNOSE THE CONDITION SHE WAS SUFFERING FROM

"I kind of had a feeling inside that I was adopted or, you know, she stole me or something like that," Morrison said. But that doesn’t necessarily mean he’d change what happened, he added.

"It’s a little bit of nature, a little bit of nurture, and you end up with the life that you have," he said when asked if he’d go back and undo the switch.

SISTERS IN VIRAL VIDEO REVEAL TRUTH OF 'EERIE' FAMILY PHOTO THEY DISCOVERED AT RENTAL HOME

The emotional toll of the discovery has weighed heavily on both men as they grapple with the realization that their upbringings were altered.

The hospital has denied the men's allegations. (Getty Images)

"Do I feel like I lost the ability to truly connect with my biological parents? Absolutely," Bylin said.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR HEALTH NEWSLETTER

The two men filed a lawsuit against Unity Medical Center alleging emotional distress caused by negligence and medical malpractice, claiming the facility robbed them of the lives they were meant to live.

Unity Medical Center denied responsibility for the incident, asserting that an internal review yielded no evidence showing hospital personnel were at fault. However, the medical center did not dispute that the swap occurred.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

"We recognize the profound impact this discovery has had on them and their families," the hospital said in a statement.

The men said they knew that the DNA trail didn’t make sense, but that finding out they’ve been switched at birth was a huge revelation. (iStock)

"While we deeply sympathize with the men and their families, we have found no evidence to support claims that Unity Medical Center or its staff were responsible for what occurred."

Although both Bylin and Morrison have since met their respective biological parents, the two men have yet to meet each other in person.

"It’s a little bit of nature, a little bit of nurture, and you end up with the life that you have."

"There are no plans in the works right now," Morrison said. "I grew up with his entire family. And he grew up with my entire family," he added, as the reason the two men "don’t feel like" they need to meet.

Despite the legal battle and the lingering disruption to their lives, both men said they are focusing on moving forward and making the best of their circumstances.

"We’re just trying to raise awareness of this situation," Bylin added.

Khloe Quill is a lifestyle writer with Fox News Digital. She and the lifestyle team cover a range of story topics including food and drink, travel, and health.

title: Teen jailed for exam cheating in Guinea dies after sickle cell complications, father says
excerpt:
Mamadou Djouma Bah, 17, was accused of using his phone during Guinea's national school-leaving exam earlier in July

The father of a teenager from Guinea with sickle cell disease who died days after h...
body_text:
Mamadou Djouma Bah, 17, was accused of using his phone during Guinea's national school-leaving exam earlier in July

The father of a teenager from Guinea with sickle cell disease who died days after he was arrested for cheating in an exam has told the BBC he blames the authorities for his death.

Mamadou Djouma Bah, 17, was transferred to hospital after his health deteriorated while in prison and later died "under the care of his treating physician", according to the prosecutor.

His father, Mamadou Saliou Bah, has called for an investigation, telling the BBC that his son did not "receive good medical care" in detention.

People with sickle cell are often at an increased risk of contracting serious infections or they can become anaemic, which is when red blood cells cannot carry enough oxygen around the body.

Sickle cell disease is a lifelong illness, common in people of African descent, and caused by a faulty gene that produces unusually shaped red blood cells that can become stuck in blood vessels.

This can lead to severely painful episodes, which experts say can sometimes be prevented by wrapping up warm and drinking plenty of fluid, and daily antibiotics can reduce risk of infections. Other treatments can include blood transfusions.

The sudden pain in the chest, limbs, back and joints - from a lack of blood circulation - may cause organ damage.

Djouma Bah - who was a social sciences student - had allegedly been caught using his phone during Guinea's national school-leaving baccalaureate on 15 July, when he was arrested with other students, also suspected of exam fraud.

Two days later, he was convicted for cheating and sentenced to six months in prison, five of which were suspended.

He was serving his one month in a prison in the city of Kindia, about 137km (85 miles) north-east of the capital, Conakry, when his health deteriorated.

The teenager was subsequently transferred to the regional hospital for treatment.

Saliou Bah told the BBC that when he visited his son in hospital, the boy said he could not breathe properly and asked to lean against him.

"Then he looked at me and said: 'Dad, I'm dying,'" Saliou Bah said.

The teenager spent around two days in hospital before being discharged to go home, where he died, according to officials.

Education Minister Alpha Bacar Barry said he was "deeply saddened by the news" and extended his "sincere condolences" to the family.

Human rights organisations are also calling for an independent investigation into the circumstances surrounding his death and the prosecution of other students.

On Wednesday, students demonstrated over the detentions of their fellow classmates, calling for their release.

"A student should not be sent to prison. These young people are the future of our country," Saliou Bah said.

"I also ask the authorities to release the other students who are still detained before they too lose their lives.

Guinea junta chief wins presidential election by landslide

Guinea bans exports of raw gold to boost local refining

West Africa signs off $25bn mega gas-pipeline plan

Army commander convicted of Guinea stadium massacre dies in prison

Go to BBCAfrica.com, external for more news from the African continent.

title: Darline Graham reveals what Trump told her inside Oval Office after brother Lindsey's death
excerpt:
In an exclusive interview on "Hang Out with Sean Hannity," Senator Darline Graham breaks her silence following the tragic passing of her brother, Lindsey Graham.

Sen. Darline Graham, R-S.C., revealed...
body_text:
In an exclusive interview on "Hang Out with Sean Hannity," Senator Darline Graham breaks her silence following the tragic passing of her brother, Lindsey Graham.

Sen. Darline Graham, R-S.C., revealed what President Donald Trump told her inside the Oval Office just days after the sudden death of her brother, the late Sen. Lindsey Graham, saying the president offered both condolences and encouragement as she stepped into his Senate seat.

"The president was very sincere in his condolences about Lindsey," Graham told Fox News' Sean Hannity on the "Hangout with Sean Hannity" podcast.

"He talked about him and what a good friend he was, how he considered him family."

SEN DARLINE GRAHAM REVEALS WHETHER SHE WILL RUN FOR A FULL TERM DURING 'HANNITY' EXCLUSIVE

Sen. Darline Graham Nordone, R-S.C., participates in a ceremonial swearing-in in the Old Senate Chamber at the U.S. Capitol on July 14, 2026, in Washington, D.C. (Finn Gomez/Getty Images)

Graham, who was appointed by South Carolina Gov. Henry McMaster to temporarily fill her brother's seat following his death, said she used the Oval Office meeting to discuss one of the issues she believes was most important to Lindsey: advancing sanctions on Russia.

"I went on to mention that there were some things that I felt like were very important to Lindsey — Russia sanctions — and that I really wanted to push those across the finish line," she said.

Graham was also caught off guard when Trump later publicly urged her to run in the upcoming special election, promising his "complete and total endorsement" if she entered the race.

TRUMP PUSHES LINDSEY GRAHAM'S SISTER TO RUN FOR US SENATE AFTER SHE GETS APPOINTMENT TO FINISH OUT TERM

Late Sen. Lindsey Graham, R-S.C., then-chair of the Senate Budget Committee, during a confirmation hearing in Washington, D.C., on Tuesday, June 16. (Graeme Sloan/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Nevertheless, she remains determined to keep her brother's legacy alive and has announced plans to run for a full Senate term.

"I feel like I can do this, and the meeting with the president, it was such an honor," she said.

"I told him two things. I said, 'I can tell you this about me: I will work hard, and I'm loyal.'"

Fox News Digital's Nora Moriarty contributed to this report.

Taylor Penley is an associate editor for Fox News Digital and contributor to FOX Business and Fox Nation who covers media, culture and Flash

title: Saudis must recognise Israel for nuclear deal, says Trump
excerpt:
US President Donald Trump, seen with his Saudi counterpart Mohammed bin Salman, says Saudi Arabia will not be able to enrich uranium under the deal

The deal between the US and Saudi Arabia to help th...
body_text:
US President Donald Trump, seen with his Saudi counterpart Mohammed bin Salman, says Saudi Arabia will not be able to enrich uranium under the deal

The deal between the US and Saudi Arabia to help the Gulf kingdom develop nuclear energy is dependent on Riyadh recognising the State of Israel, President Donald Trump has said.

"The Civil Nuclear Deal (There will be no enrichment of material!)... which pertains only to non-military use... will be approved, but is totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords," Trump said in a post on Truth Social.

The Trump-brokered Abraham Accords have led to five countries - including two Arab nations - recognising Israel.

Saudi Arabia has previously said it will not do so without the establishment of a Palestinian state.

Trump's nuclear deal with Saudis jettisons longstanding US demands

Yemen's Houthis attack Saudi tanker as US launches more Iran strikes

During Trump's first term as US president, the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Bahrain signed the Abraham Accords, resulting in a historic normalisation of relations between Israel and two Arab nations - the first in decades.

Sudan, Morocco and Kazakhstan have since also signed them, too.

Saudi Arabia has not so far responded to Trump's remarks, which come after criticism from experts that the deal announced on Wednesday could run the risk of future nuclear proliferation in an already unstable region.

And it was not immediately clear if the condition of joining the Abraham Accords was stipulated in the text of the nuclear deal - which has not been released - or that Saudi Arabia had agreed to that condition.

The BBC has contacted the White House for comment.

Meanwhile, Israel welcomed Trump's statement.

Posting on X, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's office said Saudi Arabia joining the accords "would be an historic leap forward for peace in the Middle East".

The statement did not mention the nuclear deal. However, Israeli Economy Minister Nir Barkat said previously his country "can manage it" if Saudi Arabia had nuclear power for peaceful means.

Israel itself is widely believed to have nuclear weapons, but it neither confirms nor denies that this is the case.

The US-Saudi deal is described as a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" that will provide "great access for American companies in the Saudi nuclear energy programme" by the US energy department.

The nuclear issue has been at the centre of the months-long US-Israeli conflict with Iran which has for years denied Western accusations that it aims to acquire nuclear weapons.

Saudi Arabia's de facto ruler, Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, told CBS News in 2018 that his own nation was not planning to acquire a nuclear weapon, but that "without a doubt if Iran developed a nuclear bomb, we will follow suit as soon as possible".

Earlier US media reports suggested the deal could allow Saudi Arabia to enrich uranium in the future - which can be used as fuel for power plants, but also potentially to make nuclear bombs. However, Trump's remarks appear to deny this.

Rosemary Kelanic, the director of the Middle East programme at the US-based Defense Priorities think tank, said it would be "quite shocking" if the US were to allow Saudi Arabia uranium enrichment facilities.

"The US has never done that before. We have never helped another country enrich on their own soil," she said, adding that this was because "if you can produce your own nuclear fuel, you become a much bigger risk" for building nuclear weapons.

The agreement will now be sent to the US Congress for review.

Some lawmakers on both sides of the political aisle are expected to oppose the deal, though Trump's Republican Party controls both the upper and lower chambers and opponents of the deal do not appear to have the votes needed to block it.

title: Fox News ‘Antisemitism Exposed’ Newsletter: Why Fetterman called Mamdani a 'clown'
excerpt:
Sen. John Fetterman said he has no plans to leave the Democratic Party but warned a formal anti-Israel stance would push him out. (Credit: The Hill/NewsNation)

Fox News' "Antisemitism Exposed" newsle...
body_text:
Sen. John Fetterman said he has no plans to leave the Democratic Party but warned a formal anti-Israel stance would push him out. (Credit: The Hill/NewsNation)

Fox News' "Antisemitism Exposed" newsletter brings you stories on the rising anti-Jewish prejudice across the U.S. and the world.

IN TODAY’S NEWSLETTER:- Fetterman calls Zohran Mamdani a 'clown' over push to arrest Netanyahu in New York City- IDF says Palestinian soccer players it targeted served Hamas and Jihad terror groups- Mamdani’s ‘deranged’ Israel take resurfaces as he targets Netanyahu

TOP STORY: Pennsylvania Sen. John Fetterman ripped into NYC’s socialist Mayor Zohran Mamdani, branding him a total "clown" for threatening to arrest Israeli PM Benjamin Netanyahu in New York. Fetterman slammed Mamdani’s ICC-backed threat as empty posturing, telling the far-left mayor to "sit down" and stick to local issues instead of triggering a wild international circus.

Sen. John Fetterman, D-Pa., speaks to reporters in the Senate subway area in the U.S. Capitol on Tuesday, June 2, 2026.; New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani arrives prior speaking about the fiscal year 2027 budget in New York City on May 12, 2026. (Bill Clark/CQ-Roll Call, Inc via Getty Images; TIMOTHY A. CLARY / AFP via Getty Images)

VIDEO: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani branded Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu a "war criminal" and urged the federal government to arrest him under an International Criminal Court warrant. WATCH HERE:

FOUL PLAY: The Israeli Defense Forces slammed wild claims they deliberately targeted innocent Palestinian soccer players, exposing killed athletes, referees, and coaches as covert Hamas and Islamic Jihad terrorists. The IDF revealed the identities of 11 dead players—including three allegedly tied to the brutal October 7 massacre—accusing critics of using sports stars to hide deadly terror ties.

RADICAL RECKONING: A resurfaced clip of NYC’s socialist Mayor Zohran Mamdani calling Israel "an idea" rather than a real country has sparked furious online backlash. Critics blasted the anti-Israel Mayor as "deranged" and out-of-touch, as he continues taking heat for skipping the city's Israel parade and plotting a wild scheme to arrest Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in New York.

MARQUEE MESSAGE: A movie theater outside Portland, Oregon, is facing backlash after it displayed a marquee advertising Christopher Nolan's "The Odyssey" with a provocative message critics are calling antisemitic. The Lake Theater and Cafe's marquee read, "Before there were the Jews, there was... The Odyssey." The marquee sparked outrage in the Jewish community, and critics claimed the message was historically inaccurate.

GUEST EDITORIAL: Warren Cohn, CEO and founder of Rocketship PR and media adviser to the American Middle East Press Association, writes that Zohran Mamdani's obsession with Israel is coming at the expense of the city he was elected to govern. "New Yorkers elected a mayor to obsess over New York, over crime, over housing, over sanitation, over schools," not a country 6,000 miles away, Cohn writes.

QUOTE OF THE WEEK: "He's just a clown to even say that, so he won't try that. Just sit down and focus on other problems that they have in New York. That's really not your purview. You and I know that. Sit down." - Sen. John Fetterman on New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani.

title: Joe Rogan says ex-con guest allegedly killed man, hid dismembered remains month after podcast
excerpt:
Podcaster Joe Rogan blamed the "mainstream media" for radicalizing the people to the point they have attempted to assassinate President Donald Trump.

Joe Rogan revealed that a former guest on his pod...
body_text:
Podcaster Joe Rogan blamed the "mainstream media" for radicalizing the people to the point they have attempted to assassinate President Donald Trump.

Joe Rogan revealed that a former guest on his podcast allegedly murdered someone just a month after he appeared on the podcast.

During Wednesday's episode of "The Joe Rogan Experience," Rogan recalled his 2024 interview with Sheldon Johnson while discussing sociopaths with physician Zach Bush.

Johnson, an ex-con who portrayed himself as a reformed criminal justice advocate, was arrested in spring 2024 for allegedly shooting Collin Small dead in a New York City apartment before dismembering his body and stashing the remains in multiple locations. He was charged with second-degree murder.

Officers who responded to the scene first only discovered Small's headless torso, then located his dismembered feet that were in a plastic bin. His other body parts were allegedly found in Johnson's Harlem apartment.

Joe Rogan, left, recalled on Wednesday's episode of "The Joe Rogan Experience" that former guest Sheldon Johnson, right, allegedly murdered and dismembered a man in New York City about a month after appearing on the podcast in 2024. (Getty Images and obtained by Fox News Digital)

"We had a guy in the studio that cut a guy’s head off after the podcast. He killed a guy and cut his head off," Rogan said. "He does the podcast and then afterward goes and visits this guy that apparently he had a beef with, kills him, cuts his head off, puts him in a [suitcase], like puts a wig on, and tries to escape in the hallway through the cameras."

EX-CON NYC MURDER SUSPECT SEEN SMILING WITH DA BRAGG PLEADS NOT GUILTY, HELD WITHOUT BAIL

At the time, Johnson appeared on the podcast with civil rights attorney Josh Dubin, who leads an organization focused on advocating for people who have been wrongly convicted. He was released from prison in 2019 after serving time for a series of armed robberies in 1999.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

Sheldon Johnson appears in a courtroom at the Bronx Supreme Court in New York on Tuesday, April 2, 2024. Johnson, a criminal justice reform advocate, is accused of the murder and dismemberment of Collin Small last month. (Kevin C. Downs for Fox News Digital)

When Johnson appeared on the podcast, he was working as a client advocate for the public defender's office in Queens.

According to the New York Post, a man could be heard begging for mercy before two gunshots rang from the apartment building where Johnson was seen going in and out of in different disguises following the murder.

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST TRUE CRIME NEWS

Records from the New York Department of Corrections indicated that both Johnson and Small had served time in prison, though the nature of their relationship remained unclear.

Surveillance footage images provided to Fox News showing Sheldon Johnson with a blue bin in the hallway of an apartment building in The Bronx on March 5, 2024. Johnson is alleged to have shot and killed rival, Colin Small, in an apartment in the building. (Provided to Fox News)

During his appearance on Rogan's podcast, Johnson said turning his life around was his goal.

"I said to myself, ‘I’ve been doing bad for so long. I’m going to try to do something good,'" Johnson said.

Adam Sabes is a reporter for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to Adam.Sabes@fox.com and on Twitter @asabes10.

title: Pauline Hanson blames ‘fake news’ media for her bad press. But serious leaders have to own their actions
excerpt:
In a fortnight of rolling blunders, the One Nation leader digs deep into the Trump playbook of blame-shifting

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

There’s apparently only one ...
body_text:
In a fortnight of rolling blunders, the One Nation leader digs deep into the Trump playbook of blame-shifting

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

There’s apparently only one thing to blame for Pauline Hanson’s string of bad headlines in recent weeks, from her decision to pal around with the far-right convicted criminal Tommy Robinson to her  description of domestic violence on Wednesday as a “two-way street”.

According to the One Nation leader, it’s of course not Hanson herself who is to blame.

On Thursday she posted audio of the domestic violence exchange and claimed “there is a concerted effort by some in the media to constantly take my comments out of context”.

The One Nation leader criticised “journalistic integrity” and drew links to job cuts at the Nine newspapers.

“I’m growing less tolerant of many journalists within the MSM [mainstream media] and so too are the public,” she wrote.

Not content with aping Donald Trump’s policies and slogan, this week shows Hanson and One Nation are now leaning further than ever into his fallback method of blaming journalists when the consequences of their own remarks or actions spawn an entirely predictable controversy.

This is hardly new; Hanson herself has “banned” Guardian Australia, along with our fellow travellers at the ABC and others, from her public appearances. Media alerts, press releases and even returned phone calls used to come our way, but don’t any more. In a trend evident since Hanson first burst on to the national political scene in 1996, One Nation has blamed the media for reporting on its slip-ups, instead of turning that gaze inward.

It works for Hanson politically to pick fights with the “mainstream media”, positioning her as an anti-establishment outsider fighting against the system.

But Hanson, on current figures, could be the official opposition leader after the next election; that, undeniably, demands the same level of scrutiny that media give to the leaders of Labor or Liberal.

This predictable cycle, of controversy followed by blame-shifting, has come into sharp focus in the last fortnight amid the rolling blunders emanating from Hanson’s European holiday.

Hanson raising the end of the White Australia policy when Robinson asked “how have you ended up with Pakistanis, Somalis, all of these African problems?” That was simply “fake news”, Hanson said, the fault of the “leftwing media and their false, clickbait stories”.

Backlash over her decision to give Robinson a cosy interview, then accompany him on a UK road trip? Seven’s Spotlight was the one who pushed for it in the first place, according to Hanson, Barnaby Joyce and One Nation chief of staff James Ashby (Seven sources said the podcast was instigated by One Nation and that Hanson wasn’t forced to do anything).

For good measure, as scrutiny grew on the Spotlight special – including her gushing “I love it” when asked about the concept of mass deportation – Hanson published what she said was the “pitch” document from Seven proposing the documentary, insinuating she was the victim of a media stitch-up.

In between, Hanson’s loyal deputy Malcolm Roberts accused media of “selective cherry picking and dishonest distortions” and seeking to “drag up nothing stories” when his own past comments – about any manner of conspiracy theories, from Covid to chemtrails, and his support of Vladimir Putin – resurfaced.

But at each point, it is unclear exactly what One Nation’s complaints were – other than that they didn’t like the headlines. Few specific rebuttals were given, other than tone. The list of controversies are hardly confected outrages; they are obvious questions about things Hanson or her colleagues have said and done, in public.

At various stages, Hanson has accused journalists of taking her out of context, reposting her own comments “so you don’t get it twisted by the media or the Labor Party”. But in the media articles she complained about, those comments had already been reproduced at length and in context.

On Wednesday afternoon and Thursday morning, as criticism grew over her domestic violence comments, she posted first a written excerpt and then a full audio clip of the interview, claiming media had “decided to use only a few quotes”.

But in fact, the quotes she sought to highlight had already been published at length by the Guardian, the ABC, the Nine newspapers, News.com.au and more.

Hanson claimed it was “untrue” that she had downplayed domestic violence. Ashby on Sky accused media of taking a “five second clip” rather than a “30 second clip”.

But it is a fact that experts including the Women’s and Girls’ Emergency Centre have called out Hanson’s rhetoric, with its CEO Nicole Yade branding the comments “vile and shameful”.

Hanson has spoken of being the prime minister one day, and of dragging One Nation from the fringes to become a mainstream party. But the perks of popularity also come with responsibilities – including being responsible for your actions and the words that come out of your own mouth.

title: Murdoch and Musk in the crosshairs at Venice film festival
excerpt: Danny Boyle’s drama about the acquisition of the Sun and a four-hour Elon Musk documentary will premiere alongside new films from Martin McDonagh and Werner Herzog – but only one female director has a...
body_text:
Danny Boyle’s drama about the acquisition of the Sun and a four-hour Elon Musk documentary will premiere alongside new films from Martin McDonagh and Werner Herzog – but only one female director has a movie in competition

The Venice film festival promises to take a swing at media moguls of the print and digital era this year, with Trainspotting director Danny Boyle’s new film about Rupert Murdoch’s acquisition of the Sun and a new documentary about Elon Musk included in the lineup of the films for the festival’s 83rd edition.

In Boyle’s Ink, which will open the festival on the Lido on 2 September, Australian actor Guy Pearce plays compatriot newspaper tycoon Murdoch, whose purchase of the Sun in 1969 sensationalised the media landscape and transformed public life in Britain. The script is written by Olivier award-winning playwright and screenwriter James Graham (Dear England, Brexit: The Uncivil War).

Oscar-winning veteran film-maker Alex Gibney profiles the wealthiest person in the world, rightwing agitator and owner of social network X in his four-hour documentary Musk, which will be screened out of competition in Venice.

In recent years, Venice has sought to profile itself as the glitziest and starriest of the big three European film festivals when compared with artistically challenging Cannes and politically charged Berlin.

This year’s lineup, presented by long-running artistic director Alberto Barbera this week, will still draw major stars to Venice’s waterways.

British-Irish director Martin McDonagh will showcase his new film Wild Horse Nine, in which John Malkovich and Sam Rockwell are two unlikely CIA agents in action in Chile on the eve of the coup led by Augusto Pinochet. Steve Buscemi, Tom Waits and Parker Posey also star in the awaited return of the director of In Bruges, Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri and The Banshees of Inisherin.

Robert Pattinson will grace the Lido as the lead in A24’s Primetime, a psychological thriller inspired by the US reality TV show To Catch a Predator, directed by Lance Oppenheim.

Real-life spouses Penélope Cruz and Javier Bardem star in French playwright-director Florian Zeller’s Bunker, in which a publisher-architect couple move to London and are invited by a millionaire to design a building that could withstand a nuclear bomb.

Real-life sisters Kate and Rooney Mara star in Bucking Fastard, the first dramatic feature film in seven years by German cult director Werner Herzog. The film was originally announced as a biopic of Freda and Greta Chaplin, twins from York who spoke and moved in unison and gained notoriety after a brush with the law. It seems to have since veered off into a direction more familiar from the maker of Aguirre, the Wrath of God and Fitzcarraldo, centred around a mission to dig a tunnel through a mountain range “in search of an imaginary land”.

Barbera said that Liam and Noel Gallagher had confirmed their attendance at the festival, for the premiere of Oasis reunion documentary Don’t Look Back in Anger, while Russell Crowe is expected to attend a screening of What Love Builds, a feature-length documentary about his band Indoor Garden Party.

But there is some more serious fare on the Lido this year. Russian-born film-maker Ilya Khrzhanovsky will show the latest film to emerge out of his Dar project, likened to a Stalinist Truman Show and inspired by a Soviet research institute that contributed to the creation of the first atomic bomb.

Ukrainian director Sergei Loznitsa will bring his documentary Imperium, about a film that was commissioned by the Italian Communist party to glorify the triumph of the Soviet Union but ended up revealing its contradictions.

Laura Poitras’ latest documentary They’re Here, made with Rachel Mueller, dives into ICE’s intervention in Minneapolis and the killing of Renée Good, with some previously undisclosed footage.

Tragicomedies such as Caro Diario and The Son’s Room have made Nanni Moretti one of Italy’s most revered film-makers, but for the last two decades he has preferred to show his new works at Cannes rather than Venice. Showing in competition this year, Succederà Questa Notte (It Will Happen Tonight) will be Moretti’s first film in Venice since 1989.

After Tunisian director Kaouther Ben Hania made a mark on last year’s festival with The Voice of Hind Rajab, there is a dramatic drop in female directors in this year’s competition. After six female directors competed for the Golden Lion in 2025, Danish-Egyptian director May El-Toukhy’s drama Woman Unknown is the only female-directed film among the 20 titles in the main competition.

Il Fuoco Che Ti Porti Dentro Edoardo de Angelis

Mr. Nelson, Did You Kill People? Shinya Tsukamoto

Sumo: Spirit Weighs Nothing Erik Shirai (opening film)

La Ragazza con la Leica Alina Marazzi (opening film)

The Burning Giants Phuttiphong Aroonpheng

La Maison du Vent Auguste Bernard, Kouemo Yanghu

A Day in the Life of Jo: Chapter Phaedra Jacqueline Lentzou

Un Détour par Diane Ann Sirot, Raphaël Balboni

The Pervert’s Guide to Utopias Sophie Fiennes

Twist and Shoot Mister Suzuki Yves Montmayeur

Letters from the Silenced Country Andrei Kutsila

Biografía de Jorge Luis Borges Mariano Llinás

My Undesirable Friends Part II – Exile Julia Loktev

Shumei – The Living Legacy of Kabuki Takashi Miike

Oasis: Don’t Look Back in Anger Dylan Southern, Will Lovelace

Everest: The Other Side dirs. Elizabeth Chai Vasarhelyi, Jimmy Chin

From Inside Out – The Architecture of Peter Zumthor Wim Wenders

Dio Ride Giovanni Veronesi (closing film)

No Paradise If You Are Killed by a Woman Halkawt Mustafa

title: Mammoth Red Sox prospect hits home run to the moon, then promptly slaps the catcher and ignites a brawl
excerpt: Viral Baseball Analyst Rob Friedman, a.k.a. the PitchingNinja, joins Don't @ Me w/ Dan Dakich to discuss his favorite MLB pitchers, who has the nastiest stuff, robo umps, the pitch clock, and much mor...
body_text:
Viral Baseball Analyst Rob Friedman, a.k.a. the PitchingNinja, joins Don't @ Me w/ Dan Dakich to discuss his favorite MLB pitchers, who has the nastiest stuff, robo umps, the pitch clock, and much more!

The Boston Red Sox saw their 15-game winning streak come to an end Wednesday night, but the fire is still BURNING throughout the organization.

And while it may have died down at Fenway with a 5-1 loss to Baltimore, it was still raging in High-A Greenville, where No. 3 prospect Justin Gonzales belted a home run to Virginia, and then promptly ignited a brawl after touching them all.

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

Let's head down to South Carolina, where Gonzales — who stands at 6-foot-5 and 260 pounds — slapped Hub City Spartanburgers catcher Cal Stark in the face after he said something to Gonzales after a ... slow ... trot around the bases.

Amazing. There is SO much going on there, I don't even know where to begin.

For starters ... what a call by those minor league announcers! I was ready to run through a brick wall. Electric.

Secondly ... how great are minor league team names? The Hub City Spartanburgers? What is that? Nobody knows, but I want a shirt. I need a shirt.

Justin Gonzales #35 of the Boston Red Sox bats during a spring training game against the Tampa Bay Rays at JetBlue Park at Fenway South on March 7, 2026, in Fort Myers, Fla. (Getty Images)
