And how about the degrading slap from Gonzales? Not a punch. A slap. That's even worse. It's humiliating. He's such a large human, it's almost impossible not to get in his way. That wing-span is second to none.

Look at how much bigger Gonzales is than everyone else! His teammate, No. 12 (unlisted on the roster), looks like a child trying to hold him back. He stood no chance.

Justin Gonzales #35 of the Boston Red Sox looks on during a spring training game against the Tampa Bay Rays at JetBlue Park at Fenway South on March 7, 2026, in Fort Myers, Fla. (Getty Images)

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

And then the umpire! Look at the umpire. He attempts to stand in the middle of the two, but quickly retreats. He couldn't abandon ship fast enough. He was outta there.

That's what we call a "business decision," folks. I don't blame him. Fight or flight, and he wasn't gonna win that fight.

Anyway, it's good to see the Sox showing some fight this summer. The big league club just won 15 in a row and put themselves in a playoff race, and it's clearly permeating throughout the organization.

title: Oil prices hit $100 for the first time since May
excerpt:
Oil prices hit $100 a barrel for the first time since May as the escalating conflict in the Middle East reignited fears over global energy supplies.

Brent crude - the global benchmark for oil prices ...
body_text:
Oil prices hit $100 a barrel for the first time since May as the escalating conflict in the Middle East reignited fears over global energy supplies.

Brent crude - the global benchmark for oil prices - rose more than 6% on Thursday following several days of increases as the US stepped up military strikes against Iran.

Prices spiked after Houthi militia in Yemen attacked oil tankers in the Red Sea, threatening a key export route that Saudi Arabia has used to bypass the Strait of Hormuz.

Gas prices have also risen steadily over the past month, with the benchmark UK gas price currently at around 150 per therm, up from around 98p at the end of June.

Oil prices had been falling following a temporary ceasefire between the US and Iran.

They dropped back to levels last seen before the US and Israel began military action against Iran on 28 February.

However, the ceasefire has failed and this week US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the people in charge in Iran were "not ready to make a deal".

The ongoing conflict risks pushing up inflation for many countries, including UK and the US leading to higher prices for consumers.

Higher oil prices typically lead to petrol and diesel becoming more expensive.

While drivers are affected directly, households could also see prices of other goods, such as food, increase due to businesses passing on higher transportation costs to customers.

Inflation rates have fallen in the UK and US, but questions remain whether the the slow down will prove short-lived due to the renewed conflict in the Middle East.

UK petrol prices have risen by 5p a litre in the space of two-and-a-half weeks to hit almost £1.56.

Diesel is at £1.72 a litre, on average, according to the RAC.

Average gasoline prices in the US have surpassed $4 a gallon once more, up from $3.92 a month ago, according to motorist advocacy group AAA.

Kevin Warsh, the newly-appointed chair of the US Federal Reserve, last week told Congress that the central bank had "no tolerance to persistently elevated inflation".

US President Donald Trump had pushed Warsh's predecessor, Jerome Powell, to cut interest rates.

Trump has made it clear he expects Warsh to fulfil his demand for reductions in borrowing costs for Americans.

But the Fed held US interest rates between 3.5% and 3.75% at Warsh's first meeting last month. He also told Congress that he was committed to "restoring price stability" in the wake of the Middle East conflict impacting prices.

title: Father turning family tragedy into every baseball fan's dream of visiting 30 MLB stadiums in 30 days
excerpt:
A father is spreading awareness about water safety and drowning prevention by going to all 30 MLB stadiums in as many days this summer.

A lifelong baseball fan is living every fan's dream, but unfort...
body_text:
A father is spreading awareness about water safety and drowning prevention by going to all 30 MLB stadiums in as many days this summer.

A lifelong baseball fan is living every fan's dream, but unfortunately, it is under tragic circumstances.

Jonathan St. Clair is traveling to each of the 30 MLB stadiums in as many days to spread awareness about water safety, 12 years after losing his 21-month-old son, Jasper, to drowning.

While in the care of a trusted babysitter, Jasper, St. Clair's firstborn, was taken to a home with an ungated pool and left unattended for only minutes. Doctors made every effort to save him for roughly a week, but Jasper unfortunately lost the battle.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Jonathan St. Clair attends a Colorado Rockies game. (Wave Maker Tour)

"He had not learned how to swim and had not been to any swim lessons yet. And so, we were very, very taken aback. How come we didn’t know how dangerous water was, or how come we couldn’t put kids in swim lessons earlier?" St. Clair told Fox News Digital in a recent interview.

The interview took place before the sixth game of his trip, a night game in Toronto, after he flew from Denver to watch the Colorado Rockies.

"So, all of those things have transpired, and we’ve learned a whole lot from the community at large. Since then, we have come across not just a family like ours, but hundreds of us who are out there. And really, this campaign, what I’m doing right now, incorporates all of those people. I’m working with organizations just like ours in all 30 cities. The idea is to be able to bring awareness in the water safety and drowning prevention community that it isn’t something that can just happen to someone else. It is something that can happen to you."

Nothing will ever fix that unimaginable burden, but there has been a comfort level in meeting with families who share similar unfortunate experiences, well before this road trip was even a thought.

"I wouldn’t have been able to put one foot forward 12 years ago if it wasn’t for this group of families," St. Clair said.

St. Clair is wearing a Wave Maker Tour life jacket to each game, which initially draws some looks from fellow fans. But when they also spot his MLB cap with all 30 team logos, it becomes easier to strike up a conversation.

"It’s fun to walk around town with a life vest on because everybody, they don’t know what to ask, and then somebody will ask, and it starts the conversation. Actually, it’s been fantastic," St. Clair said.

"I feel very in touch with every team that I’m walking into because I’m not coming in as a certain fan. I’m coming in with a cause, and it’s been very well-received. It’s been so much fun."

Already a week into his trip, one father offered up his first-class seat to St. Clair, and another gave him an extra ticket to watch batting practice from behind home plate.

St. Clair, a Los Angeles Dodgers fan from Washington, said he had already been to about 10 or 11 stadiums before this trip, and he knows each one he is yet to attend, his baby should be there.

Jasper St. Clair was 21 months old when he drowned in a pool. (Wave Maker Tour)

"Well, I’m a huge baseball fan. I grew up loving baseball. I played baseball. I figured one thing about this sport specifically is it starts with really young kids, as young as three usually even start in Little League. My son didn’t get to play the game. I know that he definitely would have," St. Clair said.

"I’ve always wanted to do all 30 baseball stadiums, but I had no concept of doing all of them in 30 days. And, of course, this last year we had a lot of drownings throughout the United States. One thing that I thought, I came up with this harebrained idea: Maybe if I do 30 ballparks in 30 days, we’ll get some recognition and get people talking about drowning prevention."

After catching the Blue Jays lose to the Tampa Bay Rays at Rogers Centre, St. Clair took a flight to Detroit on Wednesday morning to drive to Cleveland for the Guardians' game against the Minnesota Twins. He is driving back to Detroit to catch the Tigers face the Kansas City Royals.

The map is built out - and yes, St. Clair is petrified of rainouts, canceled flights, and just about anything that puts him on a detour of his 17 flights, six drives, four trains, one boat, and one bike (he was ecstatic when he remembered that Rogers Centre has a retractable roof during his rainy day in Canada).

But he likes to think the baseball gods, and his loved ones, are on his side.

"I think even though this whole campaign I’m doing is only 30 days, the bigger goal is after the 30 days, right? What has transpired? What information did we get out there? How much of an actual reach did we make? I’m really hoping that reach was big," St. Clair said.

Jonathan St. Clair is going to all 30 MLB stadiums in as many days to honor his son. (Wave Maker Tour)

"I opened up my feed this morning, and five people in Ohio drowned in a river, and that is not what I want to see. I want to wake up in the morning — I know we’re never going to get it to zero — but the goal is to make people aware that if they’re in those rivers and things like that, wear a life jacket. So, we’re working on it one step at a time, and this is it. Thirty days; it’s a pretty tall feat.

"I was on the phone with my wife a couple of hours ago, and she’s like, ‘How are you holding up?’ And I was like, ‘I’m pretty tired today. I’ve got three hours on two flights.’ But it’s okay. I have got so many people behind me, and this isn’t just me. I’m not doing this by myself. I have hundreds of people behind me who have helped coordinate, who are at all of the stops in each place, and they’re making my experience at each stop a real experience, not just a layover flight, so to speak."

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

title: Burnham pauses prisoner early release scheme before September launch
excerpt:
Probation union and crime victims had raised doubts over plan to free 6,000 offenders across England and Wales

Andy Burnham has paused an early release scheme for 6,000 prisoners that was due to star...
body_text:
Probation union and crime victims had raised doubts over plan to free 6,000 offenders across England and Wales

Andy Burnham has paused an early release scheme for 6,000 prisoners that was due to start in September after an outcry from crime victims.

The prime minister said an “urgent review” will aim to “minimise risk to the public” amid concerns that killers, rapists and sex abusers would be freed on tags.

Probation officers have warned that a “sense of chaos” had descended across the Prison Service, with staff unable to make preparations for the mass release.

The mother of police officer Andrew Harper said on Thursday that the proposed early release of her son’s killers was “another letdown” for their family.

In a statement, Burnham said: “I am working closely with the new justice secretary and have instructed my team to put a pause on the changes that were due to start in September.

“No prisoners will be let out under this policy until we have conducted an urgent review and done everything we can to minimise risk to the public. Where changes are needed, we will not hesitate to make them.”

Under the early release proposals, killers, rapists and sex offenders would be released early into the community and monitored by tags.

Tania Bassett, Napo’s national officer, told the Guardian the union had asked prison managers whether all preparations for early release across England and Wales should continue but received no response.

She said: “The prime minister’s statement saying he will review early release plans for September has created a sense of chaos in His Majesty’s Prison and Probation Service.

“Napo has asked HMPPS what the plan is and, as yet, they do not know. This is really worrying for our members who are currently working hard on pre-release work for those due to come out in September. It was already an incredibly short timeframe to meet the implementation date.

“This review could potentially reduce the timeframes even further, putting more pressure on our already overworked members. We urge the prime minister for clarity as soon as possible.”

According to the union, preparations for release require probation staff to carry out extensive work that usually takes at least six to eight weeks.

This includes reviewing risk assessments, making referrals for accommodation, carrying out home visits, coordinating with victim liaison officers and domestic abuse support officers and developing multi-agency safeguarding plans.

It can also take several weeks to ensure that tagging measures are in place before an offender is placed in accommodation. Unless probation staff received “imminent, concrete direction” about who will be considered, established release plans could collapse, a union official said.

The review has caused chaos in prisons, where managers are responsible for calculating release dates over several weeks from 2 September.

The Sentencing Act 2026 introduces an “earned progression model” that replaces automatic halfway release for prisoners serving standard determinate sentences in England and Wales with a tiered, behaviour-based system.

It will allow most prisoners to be released after serving a third of their sentence behind bars rather than 50%, with the rest of their sentence served under strict supervision in the community with the help of electronic tagging.

Prisoners serving time for sexual or violent offences where the automatic release stage is set at two-thirds will become eligible for release at the halfway point, provided they have not committed serious breaches of prison rules.

This applies to Albert Bowers and Jessie Cole, two of Harper’s killers who were jailed for 13 years in 2020 for manslaughter. Burnham confirmed that Henry Long, the main perpetrator, “will not be subject to any early release” under the scheme.

Burnham has been urged to pause early release for all serious offenders.

Mark Fairhurst, the national chair of the POA union for prison officers, said: “He needs to seriously rethink this policy so that there are exemptions which will allow him to keep serious offenders behind bars, and that includes violent offenders, sexual offenders and those convicted for domestic abuse so that the victims can at the very least feel a sense of justice.

“We can free up capacity by releasing people convicted of less serious crimes who shouldn’t be locked up and can be supervised in the community. But this prime minister should try to get the public on his side.”

The chief secretary to the Treasury, Emma Reynolds, told BBC Radio 4’s Today programme that Burnham wanted to look at the implementation of the early release policy. Asked if that meant it might not happen, she replied: “That is something that he and the justice secretary will set out in the coming weeks.”

title: US House passes war powers resolution directing Trump to withdraw US military from Iran
excerpt:
Resolution, which comes after similar one in June, is largely symbolic, as Trump administration says it’s unconstitutional

The US House of Representatives on Thursday for the second time passed a war...
body_text:
Resolution, which comes after similar one in June, is largely symbolic, as Trump administration says it’s unconstitutional

The US House of Representatives on Thursday for the second time passed a war powers resolution directing Donald Trump to withdraw the US military from the conflict with Iran, amid renewed fighting in the Middle East that has driven oil prices to their highest levels in weeks.

The resolution proposed by Democrat Pramila Jayapal passed by a 214-208 vote, with four Republicans joining all Democrats in attendance to vote in favor.

Its passage comes after the House last month approved a similar resolution, which the Senate later approved in a significant bipartisan rebuke of the president’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran in February.

The resolutions are nonetheless largely symbolic, as the Trump administration has argued such measures are unconstitutional. In debate ahead of the vote, the top Democrat on the House foreign affairs committee, Greg Meeks, accused the president of violating the law.

“Let me be clear: this body has already spoken. The war powers resolution passed with bipartisan support in the House and the Senate. The Trump administration was required to end its hostilities against Iran. It has not,” Meeks said.

He added that he planned on introducing legislation to compel Mike Johnson, the House speaker, to sue Trump to force his compliance with the resolutions. Johnson is a close ally of Trump, and any such measure is unlikely to advance in this Congress.

The resolution’s passage came a day after House Republicans advanced both a $95bn budget blueprint that will chiefly be spent on military needs related to the Iran war, and a $1.15tn defense spending bill.

Fighting has meanwhile renewed in the Middle East, with Iran striking US military bases around the region in recent days amid retaliatory American airstrikes. The Tehran-aligned Houthis in Yemen have threatened to halt Saudi oil shipments through the Red Sea, and announced Thursday they had attacked two tankers, which drove crude prices upwards.

title: Saquon Barkley sees 'no reason why' NFL must keep turf fields after FIFA World Cup required grass surfaces
excerpt:
Saquon Barkley said he prefers grass over turf and thinks the NFL has no reason to keep artificial surfaces after the World Cup required grass.

Now that the World Cup has come and gone, NFL players m...
body_text:
Saquon Barkley said he prefers grass over turf and thinks the NFL has no reason to keep artificial surfaces after the World Cup required grass.

Now that the World Cup has come and gone, NFL players may be more vocal now than ever about their preference to play on grass.

That's largely in part because out of the 11 World Cup venues in the United States, seven of them were NFL stadiums that use artificial turf — but FIFA regulations require a grass playing surface.

The NFLPA has, not so subtly, joked about rolling out the "green carpet" for soccer players this summer, and more recently, players started a social media movement saying that their safety is "#WorthTheCost."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Saquon Barkley #26 of the Philadelphia Eagles runs the ball against the New York Giants at Lincoln Financial Field on Oct. 26, 2025, in Philadelphia. (Getty Images)

But players, including Saquon Barkley, feel that a precedent has been set.

"I think it’s going to be hard for them to argue against that because if you’re able to do it for the World Cup, it doesn’t really make sense," Barkley told Fox News Digital in a recent interview.

Barkley did admit that he is "not as educated on it as I should be" and wants to do some more digging on the injury research between grass and turf, but he knows his own personal preference.

"I do prefer grass. I think the majority of players prefer grass. And I will say this - if the numbers show that playing on grass versus turf is better for us, then I think that’s more than enough," Barkley said.

Workers install the pitch at New York New Jersey Stadium ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup in East Rutherford, New Jersey, on May 7, 2026. (Charly Triballeau/AFP)

FORMER NFL STAR SAYS HE 'WOULD DEFINITELY BE' FRUSTRATED WITH NFL LETTING FIFA BRING GRASS TO TURF FIELDS

"There’s no reason why, if you’re able to do it for the World Cup, and if that’s what we’re asking for as players, we can’t do it. I know that’s how players feel. I love playing on grass... Even when you’re practicing on turf, we hate it. We don’t like it. I’ve got to educate myself more on this topic. But if the numbers speak to what I believe they will, then there should be no reason why we shouldn’t."

Ironically, Barkley's serious injuries have come on grass, including a torn ACL in Chicago in 2020, which he admitted put him in a "sticky situation, but there’s a lot of stuff you just can’t control." His 2,000-yard campaign came in his first season with the Philadelphia Eagles, which plays its home games on grass.

Half the league’s stadiums use turf, despite NFLPA Executive Director Lloyd Howell saying that 92% of the league’s players prefer grass. The common speculation, though, is that turf is cheaper to use and easier to maintain than grass, considering the stadiums host plenty more events year-round.

It was announced late last year that each NFL team will be provided with "a library of approved and accredited NFL fields" before the 2026 season begins. Any new field will immediately have to meet those standards, and all teams will have two years to achieve them.

Both grass and synthetic turf fields will be subject to the new standards, but the NFL has no plans to require natural grass fields.

Saquon Barkley of the Philadelphia Eagles takes the field prior to the NFC Wild Card playoff game against the San Francisco 49ers at Lincoln Financial Field on Jan. 11, 2026, in Philadelphia. (Getty Images)

The league’s chief medical officer, Dr. Allen Sills, said there are no "statistically significant differences" in lower-extremity injuries or concussions that can be attributed to the type of playing surface or a specific surface. That is despite widespread preferences by players for grass fields and complaints about surfaces such as the one at MetLife Stadium, where the New York Giants and Jets play and where the World Cup final was held.

Fox News' Chantz Martin contributed to this report.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

title: Shane Bieber's strong Rays splits make Toronto Blue Jays the smart first 5 innings MLB bet
excerpt:
Bret Boone says he's jealous of current MLB players access to film and analytics.

A win can cure basically any frustration you have. The past few days have made me annoyed at how my baseball plays we...
body_text:
Bret Boone says he's jealous of current MLB players access to film and analytics.

A win can cure basically any frustration you have. The past few days have made me annoyed at how my baseball plays were ending up. It wasn't all bad; I split on Monday, and I won the only MLB play that I had yesterday. Tuesday's looks were not good, and I ran into some bad luck in a few other games that I put out recently. I had a nice, easy 8-0 win on the Washington Nationals yesterday, and I'm hopeful today's game will be another no-sweat contest.

The Tampa Bay Rays are among the best teams in baseball, and I think that most wouldn't have expected this to be the case. It is interesting seeing how this team has gotten it all done, considering they are not loaded with big names, and they never have one of the top payrolls. I always wonder why other teams - both big and smaller market - don't take some of the lessons from them and implement them into their own franchise.

I guess not everyone can replicate everything successfully, but whatever the Rays are doing, they replicate it seemingly year-to-year.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Tampa Bay Rays starting pitcher Ian Seymour pitches against the Boston Red Sox during the second inning at Fenway Park in Boston, Mass., on July 18, 2026. (Eric Canha/Imagn Images)

Today, the Rays are sending out Ian Seymour to the hill. A lot of teams have swingmen in their rotation who bounce between the bullpen and rotation. The Rays typically use "openers" for games, but Seymour seems like he is somewhere between an opener and one they actually want stretched out. He only has seven starts for the season, and you can expect him to throw around five innings and give up three runs.

He has faced the Toronto Blue Jays in a relief role this season, going four games and allowing two earned in four innings of work. Overall, Blue Jays hitters have done well against him, batting 14-for-38.

Toronto is not one of the best teams in baseball. If anything, they are among the most disappointing teams in baseball. After giving the Los Angeles Dodgers everything they could handle last year, expectations were very high. Unfortunately, they haven't come close to those expectations and are 10 games under .500.

I highly doubt they blow up the team, but they may trade away a few pieces. The most glaring issue with the team is Vladimir Guerrero Jr., who is hitting .256 (which isn't bad), but has just six homers for the year. He had nine in 2020, when they played 60 games, and just 15 in 2019, his rookie season, when he played just 123 games.

Toronto Blue Jays' Vladimir Guerrero Jr. points to a skybox after hitting a home run against the Baltimore Orioles in the second inning of a baseball game Sunday, Sept. 12, 2021, in Baltimore. (AP Photo/Gail Burton)

It isn't as if their pitching has been some shining star, either. They have had trouble on the mound, and that has magnified the offensive issues. Today, they bring in Shane Bieber, who is just 1-1 for the season with a 5.70 ERA and a 1.73 WHIP.

Bieber used to be one of the best arms in the game, and he is still only 31 years old, so I feel like there is more than enough time for him to regain form. He has shown flashes of it as well, including his most recent outing when he went six scoreless innings, allowing three hits, two walks, and striking out six. Rays hitters are batting just .172 against Bieber this season.

When I look at the total for this game, I want to say the over is the smarter option, but I can make a compelling case for the under.

I likely won't touch the total, but some brief notes on it: Bieber has actually thrown better at home this season, but it is just three starts, so it is a rather small sample size. He only has one game (of five) where he hasn't allowed a run. Seymour is likely to allow some runs if they let him go through the lineup twice. The Rays bullpen is better than Toronto's, so you do have to wonder a bit how long Seymour will go.

Toronto Blue Jays starting pitcher Shane Bieber throws a pitch against the Chicago White Sox during the first inning at Rogers Centre in Toronto on July 18, 2026. (Nick Turchiaro/Imagn Images)

For those reasons, I'm avoiding the total. Instead, I want to take the Blue Jays through five innings. I think that Toronto should get a good shot at knocking around Seymour. I also think Bieber has shown good success against the Rays hitters. Give me Toronto through five innings.

For more sports betting information and plays, follow David on X/Twitter: @futureprez2024

title: FIRST ON FOX: White House reveals who’s joining Trump at correspondents’ dinner — and who can’t attend
excerpt:
Fox News White House senior correspondent Jacqui Heinrich reports on the upcoming July 24 WHCA Dinner after a gunman opened fire at the April event on ‘America Reports.’

FIRST ON FOX: President Donal...
body_text:
Fox News White House senior correspondent Jacqui Heinrich reports on the upcoming July 24 WHCA Dinner after a gunman opened fire at the April event on ‘America Reports.’

FIRST ON FOX: President Donald Trump will return to the stage Friday to deliver remarks at the rescheduled White House Correspondents’ Association Dinner, nearly three months after an armed suspect stormed the event in an alleged assassination attempt against him.

The White House exclusively confirmed to Fox News Digital the lineup of administration officials expected to attend the dinner at the Waldorf Astoria in Washington, D.C.

While much of Trump's Cabinet will attend, first lady Melania Trump, Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio will not be there. A White House spokesperson told Fox News Digital their absences are due to prior commitments.

WHITE HOUSE CORRESPONDENTS' ASSOCIATION PRESIDENT ANNOUNCES EVENT BEING RESCHEDULED AFTER SHOOTING

Security rapidly escorts Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. out during the White House Correspondents' Association Dinner, April 25, 2026, in Washington. (Tom Brenner/AP)

JOURNALISTS DEMAND WHITE HOUSE CORRESPONDENTS' ASSOCIATION 'SPEAK TRUTH TO POWER' IN NEW DINNER WITH TRUMP

White House deputy chief of staff Dan Scavino jumps over a chair after an incident at the White House Correspondents' Association Dinner, April 25, 2026, in Washington, D.C. (Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images)

The banquet will be held at the Waldorf Astoria while the annual dinner has historically been held at the Washington Hilton. Chaos, however, broke out at this year’s banquet when an armed suspect stormed the event in what authorities say was an attempt to assassinate the president.

Weijia Jiang, president of the White House Correspondents’ Association (WHCA), said the event would be a "more intimate gathering" when the new date was announced in June.

President Donald Trump salutes during the annual White House Correspondents' Association dinner in Washington, D.C., on April 25, 2026. (Jonathan Ernst/Reuters)

Jiang announced the rescheduled date was decided by the WHCA board in an email to association members, sharing the rescheduled event will be a more "intimate gathering."

"This dinner will not only be an opportunity to carry out our program. It will be a statement that violence has no place in American life and a free press will not be intimidated into silence. As you have all demonstrated, courage and community can and should rise above," Jiang added.

Ashley J. DiMella reports on politics for Fox News Digital.

title: 4 House Republicans rebel against Trump, vote with Dems to block Iran war powers
excerpt:
Rep. Jason Crow, D-Colo., discusses a House War Powers Resolution vote concerning the conflict in Iran on ‘The Story.’

House Democrats successfully rebuked President Donald Trump’s war in Iran on Thu...
body_text:
Rep. Jason Crow, D-Colo., discusses a House War Powers Resolution vote concerning the conflict in Iran on ‘The Story.’

House Democrats successfully rebuked President Donald Trump’s war in Iran on Thursday morning, with the help of several Republican lawmakers.

Reps. Jason Crow, D-Colo., and Pramila Jayapal, D-Wash., forced a vote to halt Trump’s military campaign absent congressional approval in a vote of 214-208, with lawmakers largely voting along party lines.

Republicans who supported the Democrat-authored measure included Reps. Warren Davidson, R-Ohio, Brian Fitzpatrick, R-Pa., Tom Barrett, R-Mich., and Thomas Massie, R-Ky.

Massie and Davidson are sharp critics of foreign intervention, while Fitzpatrick and Barrett — two moderate Republicans — are facing competitive re-election bids.

The administration's memorandum of understanding with Tehran has exposed a divide among Republicans over what constitutes victory after the military campaign against Iran. (Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images)

RUBIO SAYS US REMAINS OPEN TO IRAN TALKS BUT WARNS THERE 'WILL BE CONSEQUENCES'

A similar resolution passed the House in June with help from the same four defecting Republicans.

Thursday's vote is largely symbolic, and the resolution will not go to Trump’s desk for signature if the Senate chamber were also to pass it.

But Democrats' war powers effort underscores that the war in Iran is continuing to divide Republicans, several of whom have complained the White House has not given enough visibility to Congress about the military’s actions.

Trump and his allies, however, have insisted that the main priority is to neutralize Iran’s ability to obtain a nuclear weapon.

Democrats forced a vote on a war powers resolution aimed at disapproving the Middle East conflict using a mechanism called a privileged resolution, which allows rank-and-file lawmakers to move legislation over the opposition of House GOP leadership.

Crow, an Army veteran, said in a statement in his resolution, "18 American servicemembers have been killed since this war began. Hundreds more have been injured. Billions of taxpayer dollars have been spent. The President’s war with Iran has been an unmitigated disaster. The costs are rising every day, yet the President is choosing to double down on his failures."

The vote is one of several instances this year of House Republicans rebuking Trump and his policies, a symbol of the razor-thin majorities the GOP holds in Congress.

Some holding pictures of the late Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. Mourners reach out to coffins during a funeral for people killed during the ongoing U.S.–Israeli military campaign in Isfahan, Iran, Thursday, March 5, 2026. (Payman Shahsanaei/ISNA via AP)

Trump ripped the four GOP defectors following the successful war powers vote in June, calling them "unpatriotic."

This is a breaking news story and will be updated.

Elizabeth Elkind is a politics reporter for Fox News Digital leading coverage of the House of Representatives. Previous digital bylines seen at Daily Mail and CBS News.

Follow on Twitter at @liz_elkind and send tips to elizabeth.elkind@fox.com

title: Trump's nuclear deal with Saudis jettisons longstanding US demands
excerpt:
The Trump administration's nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia reversed a years-long stance toward a key Middle East ally.

Past US presidents insisted that Saudi Arabia normalise relations with Israe...
body_text:
The Trump administration's nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia reversed a years-long stance toward a key Middle East ally.

Past US presidents insisted that Saudi Arabia normalise relations with Israel in exchange for receiving American nuclear technology for civilian energy programmes.

According to reports of the deal's contents, President Donald Trump gave Saudi Arabia what it wanted without extracting concessions for Israel in return - and over objections from critics who argued that it could spark a nuclear arms race in the region.

On Thursday he said in a social media post that the nuclear pact was conditioned on Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham Accords, a series of deals reached in his first term that normalised relations between Israel and several other Gulf states.

But it was not immediately clear if that was stipulated in the text of the deal - which has not been released - or that Saudi Arabia had agreed to that condition, or would be forced to join the accords.

The deal appears to be the latest example of Trump jettisoning the traditional American approach to the Middle East, which centred on Israel and finding a two-state solution to the Israeli-Palestinian conflict.

In his second term Trump has focused primarily on strengthening America's economic influence to compete with China and other rivals. Analysts said the nuclear pact with Saudi Arabia sent a signal that the US wants to keep regional allies in the fold even if it means angering Israel in the process.

"Countries in the Middle East want to develop their nuclear infrastructure and they're going to do it even if the US is not involved. Washington at this point is recognising that," said Yusuf Can, a Middle East expert at consulting firm Amena Strategies. With this nuclear deal, he added, "the US is basically saying you don't have to work with China. You can work with us".

US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia

Tankers make sharp U-turns after Houthi shipping threat

Transactional foreign policy has been a hallmark of Trump's second term. The US has ratcheted up economic competition with adversaries like China and Russia in the Arctic and other parts of the world since Trump returned to office vowing to revive his "America First" approach to global affairs.

But signing a nuclear pact in the Middle East is an especially bold move that will raise tensions in a volatile region where the US is at war with Iran.

It represents a major victory for Saudi Arabia's Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, who has sought a nuclear agreement with the US for years as part of a broader campaign to strengthen relations with the West.

The crown prince, known as MBS, said in 2018 that if Iran develops nuclear weapons then Saudi Arabia should be allowed to as well.

Details of the deal were not immediately released by the administration, but the pact reportedly does not include strict enforcement measures to block Saudi Arabia from developing a military nuclear programme in the future.

In that respect it differs from a 2009 nuclear cooperation agreement struck by the US and the United Arab Emirates which included strict inspections by the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA). Another key difference that critics of the Saudi deal are pointing to is that it reportedly allows them to enrich uranium themselves on their own soil.

Watch: What we know about the US-Saudi nuclear deal

Even if Saudi Arabia doesn't race to develop nuclear weapons right away, the potential ability to do so could still give the country a powerful deterrent force in the region, analysts said.

"It certainly helps lay the groundwork for nuclear weapons in the future," said Imran Bayoumi, a resident fellow at the Atlantic Council think tank and former policy adviser at the US Defence Department.

The agreement will allow Saudi Arabia to diversify its energy sector and use nuclear power to meet rising electricity demands, energy experts said. Having nuclear power could eventually lessen Saudi Arabia's reliance on oil, insulating it from volatilities in the global oil market, said Christopher Russo, an energy expert at the consulting firm Charles River Associates.

"Oil can be disrupted. You can bomb oil fields. Oil tankers can be stuck somewhere, as we're seeing right now" in the Strait of Hormuz, Russo said. "Nuclear power is pretty self-contained."

Saudi Arabia won't reap the economic rewards anytime soon. It will likely take a decade if not longer for the country to build and open new nuclear power plants.

But the short-term political payoff is clear, as Saudi Arabia - already one of the most powerful states in the Gulf - further cements its status as a major force in the region. Moreover, Saudi Arabia struck the deal without having to recognise Israel or drop its demand that an eventual normalisation deal with Israel be tied to the creation of a Palestinian state.

That concession appeared to reflect a calculus by the Trump administration that a diplomatic breakthrough between Saudi Arabia and Israel isn't likely to happen for some time. The countries were open to talks during the Biden administration, but negotiations stalled after the Israel-Hamas conflict in Gaza and the US-Israel war with Iran.

The timing for the administration's announcement isn't clearly explained. Officials did not say why the US has chosen to enter this agreement with Saudi Arabia now.

But the war with Iran likely played a part. Washington has sought to ease tensions with Saudi Arabia and other Gulf allies who have been targeted by Tehran since the start of the conflict. Saudi Arabia briefly denied the US access to Saudi airspace earlier in the war, and has pushed the US and Iran to de-escalate.

American companies will likewise benefit from the nuclear deal, though that also won't happen overnight. Saudi Arabia will work with US-based firms to develop its civilian nuclear technology, opening a lucrative market for companies like Westinghouse that manufacture large-scale conventional nuclear reactors.

Why and how is US blockading Iranian ports in Strait of Hormuz?

'What was their crime?': BBC visits Iran school where strike killed 120 children

Nevertheless the deal is already facing criticism in Israel and the US.

As news of the deal emerged on Wednesday, some Israeli politicians argued Saudi Arabia could become a new nuclear threat in the region. Israel is widely believed to have nuclear weapons, though it has not acknowledged so publicly, and it joined the US war with Iran over shared concerns about Tehran's nuclear programme.

"A civilian nuclear program in Saudi Arabia [will] lead to a mad arms race across the entire Middle East," Avigdor Lieberman, a member of the Israeli parliament and a former defense minister, said in a social media post.

Democratic lawmakers in Congress raised similar concerns.

"The deal really is a giveaway to the Saudis, and it's only going to fuel further ambitions from Iran. It's only going to further destabilise the region with Israel," Democratic congressman Ro Khanna of California told the BBC. "It's just not smart policy."

US Energy Secretary Chris Wright said the agreement would boost the American economy and strengthen national security. Wright also said in a statement posted to social media that the US-Saudi pact would "uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation."

But the administration fuelled scepticism over the agreement by not immediately releasing more information about it on Wednesday. And perhaps in a telling sign, Trump did not preside over the signing ceremony making the deal official.

Trump attended the dignified transfer ceremony for four US service members who were killed in Iran, before holding a rally in Georgia where he argued that his policies were making the Middle East more stable than ever.

title: Which Middle East nations have nuclear programmes, and how were they built?
excerpt:
The United States and Saudi Arabia have signed a 30-year nuclear deal that will see Washington help the Gulf kingdom develop a civilian nuclear programme.

The deal was sealed on Wednesday when US Ene...
body_text:
The United States and Saudi Arabia have signed a 30-year nuclear deal that will see Washington help the Gulf kingdom develop a civilian nuclear programme.

The deal was sealed on Wednesday when US Energy Secretary Chris Wright and Saudi Energy Minister Abdulaziz bin Salman signed a nuclear cooperation “123 agreement”, which permits US companies to transfer nuclear technology abroad. The US signs such deals under Section 123 of its Atomic Energy Act, which is why country-specific nuclear deals are known as 123 agreements.

Much of what the Saudi deal entails is unknown for now, even as some analysts have raised concerns about whether it has strong enough guardrails to prevent a nuclear arms race in an already volatile Middle East.

The US and Saudi Arabia are signatories to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT). However, the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), the international watchdog against nuclear proliferation, was not involved in the bilateral agreement. It’s unclear how guardrails will be enforced. Saudi Arabia is also not a member of the Nuclear Suppliers Group, a cartel of 48 nations that controls nuclear commerce, so it is unclear how the country will access key nuclear technology and uranium.

But the deal is only the latest of several nuclear programmes in the Middle East, many of them civilian. Here’s what else we know about these programmes:

The two countries signed the nuclear agreement allowing US transfers of technology and a separate “bilateral safeguards agreement”.

It’s unclear what they entail, but a bilateral safeguards deal suggests that pact might include a mechanism for American inspectors to monitor the Saudi nuclear programme to ensure fuel enrichment levels remain within limits commensurate with civilian reactor needs and don’t rise to weapons grade or close to it.

“The US is not going to reach any agreement with any country in the world that leads to the risk of proliferation,” US Secretary of State Marco Rubio told reporters on Wednesday while in the Philippines.

US companies such as nuclear reactor designer Westinghouse are likely to profit from the deal.

The agreement will now be submitted to Congress for review. It could face resistance from legislators on both sides of the aisle, but they are expected to be in a minority. Under that scenario, the deal would automatically go into effect after the 90-day review window.

The US joined Israel’s 12-Day War against Iran in 2025 by conducting one day of air strikes on Iran’s three main nuclear facilities. In February, the two allies launched another war on Iran. One of the reasons behind it was to stop the country from developing nuclear weapons. Tehran, however, has repeatedly insisted it maintains only a civilian nuclear programme.

Iran first launched its nuclear programme in 1957 with US assistance as part of a programme by then-President Dwight D Eisenhower to share US technology abroad for electricity generation and other civilian uses. The IAEA was created to monitor this process. Iran also signed the NPT in 1974.

Iran’s Bushehr Nuclear Power Plant was developed at this time by German companies. However, in the early 2000s, the IAEA found that Iran had secretly built the Natanz, Isfahan and Fordow uranium enrichment sites with Pakistan’s help, leading to accusations that Tehran was working on developing nuclear weapons. The United Nations Security Council sanctioned Iran for several years. US and Israeli covert operations also targeted Iran with cyberattacks and assassinations of Iranian nuclear scientists.

In 2003, then-Iranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei – who was killed in US-Israeli strikes on the first day of the current war on February 28 – issued a fatwa declaring that his country would not seek nuclear weapons.

During the administration of US President Barack Obama, Iran in 2015 signed the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA), a nuclear deal in which Tehran agreed to limit its uranium enrichment to just over 3 percent – enough for reactors generating electricity but well short of what’s needed to make weapons.

Iran also granted the IAEA unprecedented access to its nuclear sites. It’s believed to be the most intrusive monitoring the agency has secured in any country to date. And the IAEA as well as US intelligence agencies repeatedly concluded that Iran had adhered to the deal and to low enrichment levels.

But the US pulled out of that agreement and reinstated sanctions in 2018 during President Donald Trump’s first term after he claimed it was ambiguous and posed a threat to Israel. In response, Iran started increasing its uranium enrichment levels.

In June 2025, the US and Israel launched the 12-Day War after an IAEA report said Iran had accumulated more than 400kg (880lb) of uranium enriched to 60 percent, not far below the 90 percent enrichment needed for weapons.

Trump claimed the 2025 US bombings “obliterated” Iran’s nuclear programme. In August, the UN also reinstated sanctions on Iran.

Yet in February, the US and Israel again started a war with Iran that they said was intended to degrade Iran’s nuclear capabilities.

Israel has refused to say whether it has nuclear weapons, but it is believed to be the only Middle Eastern country with an advanced nuclear arsenal. It has an extremely opaque policy regarding this. The country is not an NPT signatory, and the IAEA thus cannot assess its capabilities. Israel has bluntly refused to submit details to the agency since the 1980s.

According to the Nuclear Threat Initiative (NTI) think tank, Israel has possessed nuclear weapons since the 1960s and has about 90 nuclear warheads. According to the NTI, Israel has enough stockpiles of uranium and plutonium to produce up to 300 more nuclear weapons.

There is speculation that Israel tested its weapons with apartheid-era South Africa in 1979. Its most important nuclear site is the Shimon Peres Negev Nuclear Research Center near the southern city of Dimona. The facility was built with help from France in 1957.

Israel maintains that the Dimona reactor is for civilian purposes. However, in 1980, an Israeli whistleblower released documents and photos from the site to the media, affirming long-held suspicions about Israel’s advanced nuclear capabilities. Satellite images in 2025 appeared to show expansion work at Dimona, even as Israel and the US attacked Iran.

The United Arab Emirates launched its strictly civilian nuclear programme in 2009 when it signed a 123 agreement with the US. In it, the country said it would not pursue domestic uranium enrichment or reprocess spent fuel – two key ingredients required for an atomic bomb. The country is also an NPT signatory.

The Barakah Nuclear Energy Plant is the centrepiece of the UAE’s nuclear infrastructure. The enormous site was built in 2012 and has four South Korean-designed reactors that use US technology. US experts were also involved in helping to build the plant and train local workers. Barakah generates about 5,600 megawatts of electricity, or about a quarter of the UAE’s demand. The IAEA has full access to the site and can monitor activities there with real-time access cameras.

Turkiye does not have nuclear weapons of its own for now although government officials appear to be changing their posture in light of the war on Iran. The US has nuclear weapons in Turkiye, its NATO ally, and Ankara has long relied on their presence to deter possible nuclear attacks. Turkiye is also a signatory to the NPT.

It maintains civilian nuclear infrastructure in the form of its Akkuyu Nuclear Power Plant. The plant is designed and maintained by Russia and produces up to 4,800 megawatts, or about 10 percent of the country’s total electricity supply. Turkiye, as part of the deal, does not have access to the spent fuel from the reactors, which it would need for nuclear weapons.

After the US-Israel war on Iran began in February, Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said Turkiye could acquire nuclear weapons, signalling a major shift. Analysts said Turkiye could begin expanding its civilian programme, but authorities have been strategically ambiguous on the topic. A Research Istanbul poll conducted during the 2025 war on Iran saw 71 percent of participants vote in favour of nuclear weapons.

Egypt, as an NPT signatory, also maintains a strictly civilian programme for electricity and seawater desalination. Its El-Dabaa Nuclear Power Plant is currently under construction by Russia and could generate up to 4,800 megawatts by 2028. Like Turkiye, Russia has oversight over Egypt’s spent nuclear fuel. Egypt also has research reactors for isotope production meant for medical research.

Egypt did secretly pursue a military nuclear programme in the 1960s under President Gamal Abdel Nasser in a bid to counter Israel, but it failed.

Under President Saddam Hussein in the 1970s, Iraq sought to secretly build nuclear weapons. The country bought the Osiraq reactor from France and housed it at the Tuwaitha Nuclear Research Center near Baghdad. Iran struck the reactor during the Iran-Iraq War in the 1980s while Israel bombed it a year later. The US also hit the site during the 1991 Gulf War. In 2003 after the US-led invasion of Iraq, the IAEA confirmed the nuclear programme had been dismantled.

In recent times, Iraq has sought partnerships with China and Russia to build nuclear power plants to help solve chronic power outages.

Just beyond the Middle East lies Pakistan with a large arsenal of advanced nuclear weapons as a deterrence to its rival India.

While India has a “no first use policy”, Pakistan’s stance has long been deliberately ambiguous. According to the Stockholm Institute for Peace Research, Pakistan is believed to have 170 nuclear warheads while India has 190. Analysts said they believe Pakistan expanded its nuclear programme after a brief armed conflict with India in May 2025. Neither Pakistan nor India is a signatory to the NPT.

Pakistan was also part of the Eisenhower nuclear energy programme in the 1950s, but it began pursuing nuclear weapons in the 1970s after three wars with India since their 1947 independence. India itself began nuclear testing at the same time. Pakistani Prime Minister Zulfikar Ali Bhutto declared at the time: “We will eat grass, even go hungry, but we will have our own nuclear bomb.”

The nuclear programme was largely masterminded by physicist Abdul Qader Khan, who secured black-market uranium procurement networks from Niger. Pakistan’s highly enriched uranium is produced at its Kahuta and Gadwal facilities. Pakistan has also shared nuclear technology and weapons with Iran, Libya and North Korea. Libyan leader Muammar Gaddafi surrendered Libya’s nuclear infrastructure to the IAEA in 2003 after sanctions were imposed on his country.

title: Obama Center subcontractor shuts down amid $4M fight, lays off 25
excerpt:
Adamson Plumbing owner Mike Owen says his company suspended operations, laid off 25 union workers amid a nearly $4 million payment dispute tied to the Obama Presidential Center.

Weeks after the Obama...
body_text:
Adamson Plumbing owner Mike Owen says his company suspended operations, laid off 25 union workers amid a nearly $4 million payment dispute tied to the Obama Presidential Center.

Weeks after the Obama Presidential Center’s star-studded opening in Chicago, a subcontractor who previously claimed his company was stiffed for nearly $4 million has shut down operations and laid off 25 union workers.

Mike Owen, the owner of Chicago-based Adamson Plumbing Contractors, told Fox News Digital that the financial fallout forced the company to abandon about half a dozen other construction jobs and left it on the verge of collapse. Owen described the shutdown as a painful decision he believed was necessary to save the company from bankruptcy.

"Laying off close to 30 people is something that no owner in our industry wants to do," Owen said. "It’s a hard thing to do, especially when you know you can finish them and the company can still make money. But we were put in a pretty bad corner."

SUBCONTRACTORS SAY THEY’RE OWED MILLIONS, FACE FINANCIAL RUIN, AFTER HELPING BUILD OBAMA PRESIDENTIAL CENTER

Adamson Plumbing owner Mike Owen, right, says a payment dispute over work at the Obama Presidential Center forced him to suspend operations and lay off nearly 30 workers. (Michael Dorgan/Fox News Digital)

Adamson, which performed its work on the center under the name Marsh-Adamson, has since filed a $1.72 million mechanic’s lien against the property, escalating the payment dispute into a legal fight.

The company is one of several subcontractors to report financial trouble after working on the center, which was promoted as a transformative economic opportunity for small and minority-owned businesses.

A previous Fox News Digital investigation identified several subcontractors — including Black-owned firms the project was specifically intended to support — that said they were owed money or had absorbed losses ranging from hundreds of thousands of dollars to millions. The largest dispute involves the Concrete Collective, which is seeking more than $40 million in additional payment.

Before the center opened, Owen said the project had left his company with $3.9 million in losses tied to delays, rework, labor overruns and changing project demands. He said he spent months negotiating with Lakeside Alliance, the project’s construction manager, before going public after failing to reach a resolution.

Against the backdrop of that larger dispute, Owen said the breaking point came around the time of the Obama Presidential Center’s opening celebration.

Owen said Adamson and Lakeside Alliance reached an agreement under which Adamson would provide two journeyman plumbers to perform last-minute "housekeeping" work on campus at premium nighttime rates.

In return, Owen said, Adamson was supposed to receive part of its outstanding payment days before the center opened on June 19. An email reviewed by Fox News Digital shows a Lakeside representative telling Owen that $100,000 would be released through Adamson’s May payment application.

The Obama Presidential Center in Jackson Park on Chicago's South Side will open this week. (Fox Flight Team)

Adamson completed the requested work, but the payment did not arrive on time, according to Owen, who said it was the company's breaking point and left him with no choice but to suspend operations on June 25.

"We negotiated it in good faith," he said. "Against my better judgment, I agreed [to do the work]."

"Not getting that large sum of money just kind of pulled the brakes on the train. It was just the final death blow to the company," he added. "Rather than try to stretch it out and go bankrupt, I just decided the responsible thing was to shut it down. We’ll regroup, and we’ll see where we’re at come September."

Owen said that the promised $100,000 retainage payment and around $35,000 for change orders payments have since arrived from Lakeside Alliance — more than two weeks after he suspended operations and laid off 25 workers.

Owen said the money helped reduce the company’s debt to one supplier but did not reverse the shutdown.

"It’s almost like too little, too late," Owen said. "It probably would have just prolonged the inevitable at this point," he said. "We’re still deep in the hole of what they owe us."

Owen said Adamson has now moved out of its building and that he is working from home while turning the dispute over to his attorneys and determining whether the company has a future.

OBAMA CENTER ISN'T A TRADITIONAL PRESIDENTIAL LIBRARY. CRITICS SAY IT'S AN ACTIVISM CENTER.

Former President Barack Obama speaks during the opening day of the Barack Obama Presidential Center in Chicago, Ill., on June 18, 2026. (Pedro Ugarte/AFP via Getty Images)

Owen has escalated his nearly $4 million payment fight.

Documents reviewed by Fox News Digital show that Marsh-Adamson filed a $1.72 million mechanic’s lien against the Obama Presidential Center property, alleging it remains owed money for plumbing work performed on campus. A mechanic’s lien is a legal claim filed against a property by a contractor or supplier alleging that it has not been fully paid for completed work.

The lien is considerably lower than the approximately $3.9 million Owen says Adamson lost over the course of the project. Owen said the lien covers the amounts he believes are most readily documented, including unpaid fees, logged change orders and labor overruns supported by company records.

"It doesn’t mean that I’m not going to pursue the $3.9 million in change that we lost overall," Owen said. "I’m still working with my legal team on that $3.9 million figure because I feel that it’s only right that we still hold ownership at the presidential center accountable."

Construction equipment is seen outside the Obama Presidential Center in Chicago ahead of the campus opening. (Michael Dorgan/Fox News Digital)

Owen said he had initially avoided filing a lawsuit or lien while attempting to negotiate a resolution.

"I’m not a litigious person, but at the same time, our legal system is there to protect the small guy," Owen said. "I’m just going to have to fight this out legally from here on out, and these aren’t cheap costs."

Last week’s payment will be deducted from the $1.72 million, he said.

Lakeside Alliance, a joint venture led by Turner Construction and four Black-owned Chicago construction firms, served as the project’s construction manager. The other firms are UJAMAA Construction, Powers & Sons Construction, Brown & Momen and Safeway Construction.

In a previous statement, Lakeside said approximately 475 contractors worked on the center. In its latest response, the alliance pointed to the project’s scale and said "contractual closeout continues long after the doors have opened" on major projects of such complexity.

"Lakeside Alliance remains committed to working through all outstanding matters to successfully close out the project," a spokesperson said. Lakeside Alliance did not directly address Owen’s allegations, the layoffs or the $1.72 million lien.

WATCH: Subcontractor says his company is owed nearly $4M for work on Obama Center project

The Obama Foundation, former President Barack Obama’s private nonprofit organization that oversees the construction, said Lakeside was responsible for managing and paying subcontractors and that the foundation had no outstanding disputed charges with the alliance.

While it did not directly address Adamson’s allegations, the foundation said its financial support for firms working on the project exceeded industry standards.

"Our commitment began with a 15-day payment cycle, which is largely unheard of in major construction projects," the foundation said in a statement, adding that it sometimes accelerated payment timelines or advanced payments months before work was completed.

The center has also faced scrutiny over its soaring price tag and promised taxpayer protections. Construction costs, initially projected at approximately $350 million, had climbed to $830 million by 2021 and have since reportedly surpassed $1 billion.

Meanwhile, just $1 million has been deposited into a promised $470 million reserve intended to protect Chicago taxpayers if the center encounters financial trouble.

Those questions have drawn added scrutiny because the Obama Foundation secured the 19.3-acre Jackson Park site through a 99-year lease requiring a one-time $10 payment.

Owen said the ordeal has left him financially and emotionally drained and uncertain whether he will ever restart Adamson Plumbing.

"This entire process really drained me out financially and emotionally," Owen said. "So that’s where I’m at — just kind of in a holding pattern."

Owen said the opening ceremony — which featured performances by Bruce Springsteen, Christina Aguilera, John Legend and U2’s Bono and The Edge, among others — only sharpened his anger. Against that celebratory backdrop, he said, the struggles of the workers who built the center went largely unrecognized.

Owen argued that Chicago’s proud protest culture had been conspicuously absent — and that the reaction would have been markedly different if the project were associated with figures such as Elon Musk or Ken Griffin, the Chicago billionaire whose name adorns the neighboring Griffin Museum of Science and Industry.

"There would be holy hell to pay," Owen said. "They would be picketing. They would be storming the castle."

Stevie Wonder, Common, Bruce Springsteen and Eddie Vedder, accompanied by The Roots, perform during the Obama Presidential Center dedication ceremony in Chicago on June 18, 2026. (Scott Olson/Getty Images)

Owen also singled out Illinois Gov. J.B. Pritzker, questioning why the pro-union governor had not addressed the layoffs or payment disputes.

"But he hasn’t said anything publicly about the union companies at the presidential center getting stiffed," Owen said. "And it bothers me."

Pritzker’s office did not respond to a request for comment.

Owen said he was not accusing former President Barack Obama of personal responsibility but believes the foundation should publicly acknowledge the subcontractors’ complaints and help seek a resolution.

"It’s become perfectly obvious to me that the ownership group there — they truthfully just don’t care," he said. "I don’t know any other way to say it."

Owen said contractors had held out hope that their disputes would be resolved once construction ended.

"But I think a lot of us out there, the main subcontractors, all still had this feeling that it was going to be worked out in the end — until it wasn’t," he said.

Now, with Adamson’s future uncertain, Owen said the project has left him facing a personal turn he never saw coming.

"I mean, the only leverage I have now is just to continue speaking out and see where it goes," he said. "But my career — it’s funny — I’m going to have to go find a job, which is fine. It’s just that I did not anticipate the completion of this project would take this turn."

WATCH: Black subcontractors at Obama Presidential Center still seeking payment as Juneteenth opening nears, advocate says

Michael Dorgan is a writer for Fox News Digital and Fox Business.

You can send tips to michael.dorgan@fox.com and follow him on Twitter @M_Dorgan.

title: Former Barclays CEO Jes Staley faces US congressional panel over Epstein ties
excerpt:
Staley is latest high-profile figure to testify before House amid investigation into Epstein, convicted sex offender

Former Barclays boss Jes Staley is due to be questioned by US lawmakers on Thursda...
body_text:
Staley is latest high-profile figure to testify before House amid investigation into Epstein, convicted sex offender

Former Barclays boss Jes Staley is due to be questioned by US lawmakers on Thursday in a closed-door interview about his past ties to convicted child sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

Staley, who spent more than three decades at JPMorgan Chase before joining Barclays in 2015, is the latest in a series of high-profile figures to appear in front of the House committee on oversight and reform as part of its investigation into Epstein, who died in prison in August 2019 while awaiting trial over child sex-trafficking charges.

A transcript of Staley’s interview is expected to be released at a later date, as the panel has done with previous witnesses, including Bill and Hillary Clinton, Bill Gates and Goldman Sachs’ former top lawyer Kathy Ruemmler.

Staley’s ties with the well-connected billionaire spelled the end of his career, with the American banker having resigned as the chief executive of Barclays in November 2021 after UK regulators launched an investigation into Staley’s ties to Epstein and Barclays said the pair “did not have a close relationship”.

However, the FCA investigation, triggered by a cache of 1,200 emails from Staley’s former employer JP Morgan, concluded that the pair were “indeed close” and had a relationship that “went beyond one that was professional in nature”. The former Barclays chief was eventually banned from taking any job in the UK’s financial sector for playing down those ties.

Barclays said in a recent statement that the FCA concluded the bank was also was “misled by Jes Staley”. The bank added that its own internal investigation was “based on the information that was available to us at the time”.

Staley unsuccessfully tried to challenge the regulator’s ruling, resulting in a court hearing that publicly detailed Epstein’s role in Staley’s life.

During that hearing, Staley admitted to having sex with a member of Epstein’s staff in New York but agreed with a lawyer during cross-examination that he would describe the intercourse as “consensual”.

Judges ultimately sided with the FCA, upholding its ban and scuppering efforts to restore his reputation. He also lost out on £18m ($24m) worth of pay and bonuses from Barclays as a result of the ruling.

After losing his legal challenge, Staley said he was “disappointed” by the outcome but was “never dishonest”. He previously said of Epstein in 2020, “obviously I thought I knew him well, and I didn’t” and “with hindsight of what we all know now, I deeply regret having had any relationship with Jeffrey Epstein.”

Staley originally met Epstein in 2000 after he became head of JP Morgan’s private bank, where Epstein was a client. He remained in touch with Epstein for several years after the financier pleaded guilty to state charges in Florida of solicitation of prostitution and of solicitation of prostitution with a minor, even visiting Epstein while he was serving his sentence and out on work release.

Staley has said that their relationship tapered off after he left JP Morgan, with the two men meeting for a final time in 2015, when Staley took his own yacht, the Bequia, to visit Epstein’s private island.

In March 2023, JPMorgan Chase sued Staley, arguing that he should be held liable for any financial penalties the bank faces from lawsuits accusing the bank of helping to facilitate Epstein’s sex trafficking. Later that year, the two sides reached a settlement, of which the terms are confidential.

But over the last year, documents released by the US Department of Justice (DoJ) have renewed scrutiny of Staley’s ties to Epstein. They included files, reported by the Guardian, showing that DoJ prosecutors had reviewed allegations of rape and serious bodily harm against a woman by Staley. There is no evidence that prosecutors decided to pursue the allegations. Staley has denied any wrongdoing and has never been charged with a crime related to the allegations.

Staley’s US lawyers were contacted for comment.

title: Vacation hot spot tells tourists in skimpy beachwear to cover up or face steep fines
excerpt: Viral video from Positano shows tourists packed onto a tight cliffside path with little room to move, drawing backlash online as experts say tour groups, day-trippers and social media are fueling over...
body_text:
Viral video from Positano shows tourists packed onto a tight cliffside path with little room to move, drawing backlash online as experts say tour groups, day-trippers and social media are fueling overwhelming crowds.

An Italian town is warning tourists against strolling through its streets shirtless or in swimwear — threatening hefty fines for visitors who ignore the rules.

Varenna, a popular lakeside village on Lake Como in northern Italy, recently updated its urban police regulations to prohibit visitors from walking around town shirtless or wearing only swimwear.

"Throughout the entire municipal territory of Varenna, it is prohibited to remain shirtless or wearing only swimwear, except on beaches, piers and boat docks," says the town's tourism website.

POPULAR CRUISE LINE’S BIKINI CRACKDOWN COULD SURPRISE PASSENGERS AT ISLAND PORTS

The fine can range from about $57 to about $228 — or €50 to €200.

Officials said the new rules are aimed at addressing the effects of overtourism, as hundreds of thousands of visitors flock to the village each year.

A popular village on Italy's Lake Como is reminding visitors that walking through town shirtless or in swimwear can result in fines. (iStock)

"Varenna is a wonderful village, and we are proud to welcome hundreds of thousands of visitors from all over the world every year," Mauro Manzoni, Varenna's mayor, told The Guardian.

"However, our residents’ quality of life cannot be sacrificed on the altar of mass tourism."

ISLAND CRACKS DOWN AS TOURISTS ARE SLAPPED WITH NEW FEES, FED-UP LOCALS REVOLT

Italy's national statistics agency says that Varenna spans about 4.85 square miles and has a population of 646 residents — highlighting the challenges that mass tourism can pose for the small village.

Fox News Digital reached out to the Municipality of Varenna for comment.

On a Reddit forum discussing the new rules, many users voiced support for the policy.

STUNNING VACATION HOT SPOT TURNS INTO OVERCROWDED NIGHTMARE AS TOURISTS FLOOD STREETS

"Their town, their law," one person wrote.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

"I wish more places would do this," another commenter said.

Officials said the rules are aimed at addressing the effects of overtourism. (iStock)

A third person chimed in, "They should implement this in Thailand too."

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

One Redditor said that the penalties were more surprising than the rule itself.

Tourists visiting the Lake Como destination could face hefty fines for violating the town's new dress code. (Manuel Romano/NurPhoto via Getty Images)

"I’ve seen a few small towns around Europe that have signs about no swimwear when you're off the beach and walking around town," the user wrote. "I haven’t seen fines for it, though."

The move follows similar action taken in France last summer.

"Varenna is a wonderful village, and we are proud to welcome hundreds of thousands of visitors from all over the world every year," the mayor of the town said. (Manuel Romano/NurPhoto via Getty Images)

In 2025, the seaside resort of Les Sables-d'Olonne reminded locals and tourists that walking around town topless or in swimwear was prohibited.

"It’s a question of respect for locals who don’t want people wandering around their town half-naked," the town mayor wrote at the time.

Andrea Margolis is a lifestyle reporter for Fox News Digital and Fox Business. Readers can follow her on X at @andreamargs or send story tips to andrea.margolis@fox.com.

title: President Trump urges Atlanta Braves fans to continue doing controversial 'chop' at home games
excerpt: CNN reporter Sara Sidner questions UFC President Dana White about President Donald Trump's presence at sporting events, suggesting his tactics resemble authoritarian leaders. Journalist Emily Austin c...
body_text:
CNN reporter Sara Sidner questions UFC President Dana White about President Donald Trump's presence at sporting events, suggesting his tactics resemble authoritarian leaders. Journalist Emily Austin criticizes CNN's comparison as 'ridiculous,' noting presidents historically attend sports to unify the country. She claims media networks are creating unnecessary friction.

Donald Trump has put his foot down on one of the biggest issues in all of sports ...

Nope. It's got nothing to do with Caitlin Clark, or the WNBA, or anything in the the transgender category. Nothing to do with the NFL, or CTE, or the MLB and the looming labor dispute.

Not college football or the NIL, either, or the crumbling LIV Golf situation.

I'm talking about the famous Tomahawk Chop at Atlanta Braves baseball games. Trump wants it back, and he wants it back NOW!

TRUMP DRAWS MUCH DIFFERENT REACTION AT NBA FINALS THAN HE DID AT CFP NATIONAL TITLE GAME

Amen, Mr. President! Say it again, this time even louder for the Libs in the back! Make sure they hear it.

Trump was in the Atlanta area Wednesday night, visiting a Cobb County high school to promote economic policies ahead of those pesky midterms.

Anyway, former Atlanta Braves manager Brian Snitker — who is now an executive with the team — was in attendance, so, naturally, Trump called him to the stage.

President Donald Trump shakes hands with Braves Development Company President and CEO Mike Plant as he invites Plant and former Atlanta Braves manager Brian Snitker on stage during an event at Wheeler High School in Marietta, Ga., on July 22, 2026. Trump traveled to Georgia to deliver remarks on education and promote his administration's recently launched Trump Accounts program. (Getty Images)

That's when fans started to do the famous chant, and the infamous chop. Yes, that chop. The one that used to be incredible, but now you rarely see it anymore because the wokes have kinda-sorta canceled it.

"Ohhh listen... they say you are not allowed to do that.. do it!" Trump said.

ATLANTA, Ga., Oct. 30, 2021 — Former first lady Melania Trump and former President Donald Trump do "the chop" before Game 4 of the World Series between the Houston Astros and the Atlanta Braves at Truist Park in Atlanta, Ga. (Getty Images)

Look, there's no real ban on the chop at Braves games that I'm aware of, but I went to last year's season-finale against the Pittsburgh Pirates, and I noticed a major difference.

Yes, they still do the chant during the game, but it's not nearly as often as they did back in the heyday. The music still plays, but it weirdly stops halfway through — right when it's about to get good.

It's a mood-killer. A vibe-killer. I noticed it right away.

Yes, you still see the chop, but it ain't what it once was. Folks still do it, but it's odd. It's not the same. It's lost some of the juice.

And that's because society has weirdly taken aim at the chop over the past decade, which is, frankly, a perfect reflection on how silly society has become. Sports media, which is 90% left-wing, HATES the chop.

Well, scratch that. They act like they hate it, which is good enough. Same with the Cleveland "Guardians." They didn't care that they were the Indians, but America went insane after 2020 and all of a sudden they needed the name changed.

That's what we have with the Tomahawk Chop in sports. Yes, they still do it at Braves games, but it ain't the same.

And relax, FSU fans. This isn't about you. I know you all still do it. Chop on!

A fan holds a sign stating "the chop is racist" during the ninth inning of Game 1 of the World Series at Minute Maid Park in Houston, Texas, on Oct. 26, 2021. (Getty Images)

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Bottom line? Trump likes it, which should surprise absolutely nobody. Kamala Harris, had she won, would've probably made it a felony by now. Trump wants everyone to keep doing it.

title: Former intel community chief James Woolsey dies at 84
excerpt:
Fox News contributor Dan Hoffman discusses how the killing of two U.S. troops in an Iranian strike in Jordan changes the course of the war on ‘Fox Report.’

Former Director of Central Intelligence R. ...
body_text:
Fox News contributor Dan Hoffman discusses how the killing of two U.S. troops in an Iranian strike in Jordan changes the course of the war on ‘Fox Report.’

Former Director of Central Intelligence R. James Woolsey Jr. died Tuesday at the age of 84.

The Washington Post and the New York Times both reported that his wife, Conchita Sarnoff Woolsey, noted that a stroke was the cause.

She indicated that her husband had been facing deteriorating health in recent years due to "Havana syndrome," the Post reported.

US COUNTERINTELLIGENCE 'WAY BEHIND,' POST-9/11 RECORD 'EXTRAORDINARILY NEGLIGENT', FMR TRUMP WH OFFICIAL WARNS

James Woolsey, former Director of Central Intelligence, listens during a Bloomberg Television interview in New York on Sept. 14, 2017. (Christopher Goodney/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

"We have traveled the world trying to find a cure and a treatment," she noted, according to the report, "which sadly we never found."

As director of central intelligence from 1993 to 1995, Woolsey oversaw the U.S. intelligence community and served as head of the CIA during the first two years of President Bill Clinton's first term.

ALDRICH AMES, CIA OFFICER WHO SPIED FOR RUSSIA, DEAD AT 84

R. James Woolsey Jr. is sworn in to testify before the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence on Capitol Hill, Washington D.C., Feb. 2, 1993. The hearing was on his nomination as U.S. director of central intelligence. (Ron Sachs/CNP/Getty Images)

"CIA extends our condolences to the family and loved ones of former Director James Woolsey. He was a great patriot who served his country as Director of Central Intelligence, Undersecretary of the Navy, Ambassador, and attorney," the CIA noted in social media posts.

Woolsey served as undersecretary of the Navy during a portion of President Jimmy Carter's White House tenure. He later served as ambassador to the Negotiation on Conventional Armed Forces in Europe during part of President George H.W. Bush's term.

EX-CIA CHIEF ACCUSED OF ORCHESTRATING 'RUSSIA HOAX' SUES TRUMP ADMIN FOR 'VINDICTIVE PROSECUTION'

The seal of the Central Intelligence Agency is displayed in the foyer of the original headquarters building in Langley, Virginia, on Sept. 18, 2009. (Andrew Harrer/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

In a Facebook post, the CIA added, "Director Woolsey advocated for the Agency during a time of budget uncertainty and tumult at the end of the Cold War, preserving officer morale and building a relationship between the Intelligence Community and law enforcement. That partnership continues to this day and has enabled some of our greatest successes."

Alex Nitzberg is a writer for Fox News Digital.

title: Cricket: No Ashes 2027 Tests to take place in the north of England
excerpt:
There will be no Ashes matches in the north of England next year, when Australia arrive for Test cricket’s oldest rivalry.

Headingley in Leeds and Manchester’s Old Trafford have both staged some of t...
body_text:
