There will be no Ashes matches in the north of England next year, when Australia arrive for Test cricket’s oldest rivalry.

Headingley in Leeds and Manchester’s Old Trafford have both staged some of the most dramatic clashes in the long history of Ashes cricket.

Don Bradman, arguably cricket’s greatest batsman, scored two triple centuries at Headingley in the 1930s.

Both grounds played host to remarkable individual feats by England hero Ian Botham during the 1981 Ashes series.

Headingley was also the venue for a remarkable unbeaten century by England’s Ben Stokes in a one-wicket win during the 2019 series.

But both venues have been left off the schedule for the 2027 Ashes – a decision criticised by new British Prime Minister Andy Burnham, then the mayor of Greater Manchester – when it was announced in 2023.

The England and Wales Cricket Board confirmed on Thursday the exact fixture list for next year’s Ashes, with all five matches limited to the Midlands and south of England.

Hampshire’s Rose Bowl, near Southampton, will stage its first Ashes Test from July 21 – the fourth of the series.

It will become the 10th ground in the United Kingdom to stage a men’s Ashes Test.

The series will start at Trent Bridge in Nottingham on June 18, with Lord’s – as is traditional – staging the second Test on June 30 and Edgbaston in Birmingham the third on July 8.

The Ashes will conclude at the Oval in south London with the July 29-August 2 Test.

Old Trafford and Headingley are both set to stage Ashes Tests in 2031.

Australia triumphed 4-1 on home soil during the 2025-26 Ashes. The last Ashes series in England ended in a 2-2 draw in 2023.

England’s last Ashes series success was a 3-2 home victory in 2015.

They are also due to play a one-off Test against Bangladesh at Lord’s from May 28.

The 2027 World Test Championship final will be held at the Oval starting on June 9, the second time the venue has hosted the title match.

title: Poorest children in England 40% more likely to be hospitalised for lung infections
excerpt:
Exclusive: Analysis of NHS England data reveals stark impact of air pollution in deprived areas

Children living in the poorest areas across England are 40% more likely to be admitted to hospital for ...
body_text:
Exclusive: Analysis of NHS England data reveals stark impact of air pollution in deprived areas

Children living in the poorest areas across England are 40% more likely to be admitted to hospital for respiratory infections, an analysis of NHS England data shows, with experts raising the alarm on the impact of air pollution on young people’s health.

The analysis, based on data obtained via freedom of information requests by the charity Impact on Urban Health, revealed 1,941 out of every 100,000 children living in the most deprived fifth of England were admitted to hospital for respiratory illnesses in 2024-25.

The high admission rate contrasted starkly with figures on England’s least deprived area, where 1,380 out of every 100,000 children were admitted to hospital for respiratory illnesses, including acute upper and lower respiratory infections, influenza and pneumonia, that same year.

The groupings were based on national deprivation quintiles, comparing the 20% most deprived areas in England with the 20% least deprived. In absolute terms, the poorest areas registered a total of 82% more hospital admissions for respiratory infections, equivalent to a 41% higher rate when adjusted for population size.

Although the data did not provide an exact cause for these hospital admissions, air pollution was identified as a significant risk factor, with children living in deprived areas exposed to the most extreme levels of toxins and pollutants.

“Children growing up in deprived neighbourhoods are often exposed to dirtier air,” said Dr Gergo Baranyi, a senior research fellow at University College London with a focus on air pollution. “But air pollution is only part of the story. Poor-quality housing, overcrowding, damp and mould, outdated heating systems, indoor air quality and exposure to smoking, are also more common in deprived areas. These conditions can all harm children’s respiratory health.

He added that the “stark” inequalities in admission rates should not come as a surprise. “They are shaped by individual circumstances and environmental exposures where children grow up. Respiratory infections in childhood can have long-lasting consequences on health and wellbeing. Improving air quality and housing conditions can therefore help to protect children’s health and reduce inequalities in the long run.”

Air pollution is estimated to cause up to 43,000 deaths each year in the UK. Studies have previously established how air pollution can slow the lung growth of children and also speed the progression of lung disease.

Ben Pearce, who is heading a programme looking at the health effects of air pollution at Impact on Urban Health, called the figures “shocking” and “appalling”.

“For decades, planning decisions and wider systemic inequalities have meant that many lower-income communities live closer to busy roads, industrial sites and other major sources of air pollution,” Pearce said.

He said air pollution was the biggest environmental threat to respiratory health. “It worsens conditions such as asthma and increases the likelihood that a child will end up needing hospital treatment. It is a serious harm, unequally shared. It is also one the government can act on quickly.”

Peace said the UK’s Clean Air Act needed an overhaul, “with legally binding targets” aligned with World Health Organization guidelines that prioritised “children and communities experiencing the greatest levels of deprivation”.

“No child’s future should be shaped by the postcode they are born into,” he said.

Dr Helena Clements, officer for climate change at the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health, said: “Air pollution is damaging children’s developing lungs, driving avoidable illness and hospital admissions, and widening health inequalities from the earliest years and throughout their life course.

“Tackling air pollution is essential to giving every child, regardless of their postcode, the healthy start they deserve.”

The Guardian approached the Department of Health and Social Care for comment.

title: EU hits Google with new $1bn fine, saying it broke digital antitrust rules
excerpt: The European Union has fined Google 890 million euros ($1bn), saying the technology giant broke digital antitrust rules by steering users of Google Play and its search engine towards its own services ...
body_text:
The European Union has fined Google 890 million euros ($1bn), saying the technology giant broke digital antitrust rules by steering users of Google Play and its search engine towards its own services and apps at the expense of rivals.

Thursday’s penalty is the latest in Brussels’ crackdown on Big Tech, which has seen the bloc lead the world in reining in the largest firms from Silicon Valley to Beijing.

Google had recently lost its appeal against a $4.5bn antitrust fine imposed for throttling competition and reducing consumer choice through the dominance of its mobile Android operating system.

The European Commission, the bloc’s executive branch, said it was acting in the interest of consumers.

“The best products should succeed because they’re better, not because they’re owned by the company running the search engine. And European consumers have a right to be told by app developers where to sign up to the best offers, even when the app store owner does not get a cut,” said Teresa Ribera, the commission’s executive vice president for clean, just and competitive transition.

“In the EU, businesses have the right to compete fairly. Gatekeepers have the obligation to ensure a level playing field and consumers the right to choose for cheaper alternative offers,” European Commission spokesperson Thomas Regnier said.

The fines are the biggest in total against one company under the competition law known as the Digital Markets Act (DMA), after the EU slapped penalties of 200 million and 500 million euros ($228m and $570m) against Meta and Apple respectively in 2025.

The DMA came into effect in 2024 and seeks to rein in what the EU views as Big Tech’s excesses in a bid to ensure fair competition in the digital realm.

The fines against Google have been expected for months as part of a probe that began in 2024, but Brussels has faced claims of delaying the move over fears of hurting ties with Washington.

The EU can slap fines of up to 10 percent of a company’s total global turnover for breaching the DMA.

A second EU official said the fines were worth 0.22 percent of Google’s turnover.

The fines could rise further if Google fails to comply within 60 days, the commission said, threatening it with “periodic penalty payments”.

Google’s head of global affairs, Kent Walker, said the company was being forced to “strip away real-time Search features Europeans love – like instant pricing and direct availability for hotels, flights, and restaurants – and dismantle safety protections on Google Play”.

Between 2017 and 2019, the EU hit the company with fines worth a total of 8.2 billion euros ($9.3bn).

Brussels imposed a 2.95-billion-euro ($3.4bn) fine in a separate case under different antitrust rules in September last year, after which Trump threatened to retaliate against the EU.

The EU, however, appeared sanguine on Thursday about any United States retaliation.

The EU’s duty is to “ensure that the regulation that is being adopted by our sovereign institutions is fully enforced and respected,” Ribera told reporters.

She said there were similar cases in the US and American authorities were “dealing with very similar approaches”.

The EU and the US agreed this year to address frictions linked to the bloc’s digital rules through talks, but they have yet to begin.

Some 25 US lawmakers belonging to Trump’s Republican Party in a letter on Tuesday urged the president to use tools against the EU’s “discriminatory” digital rules, such as trade investigations that could lead to higher levies.

title: Two Russian men jailed in Angola for terrorism and spying
excerpt:
The men were convicted over alleged plan to stage coup and organise deadly demonstrations over fuel prices last year

Two Russian men have been found guilty by an Angolan court of terrorism and spying...
body_text:
The men were convicted over alleged plan to stage coup and organise deadly demonstrations over fuel prices last year

Two Russian men have been found guilty by an Angolan court of terrorism and spying, after being accused of planning a coup and helping to organise deadly demonstrations in the southern African country last year.

Lev Lakshtanov and Igor Ratchin were sentenced to eight and 11 years in jail respectively. They had denied the charges, claiming they were in Angola for cultural and business activities.

From 28-30 July 2025, a minibus taxi strike over fuel price hikes descended into riots and looting, in which 22 people were killed and more than 1,600 arrested according to official figures.

Angola’s security forces were accused of firing live ammunition at protesters; the government said its “progressive” use of force was justified. Human Rights Watch had previously said Angola used “excessive force” when it used rubber bullets and teargas to break up a peaceful protest against rising fuel prices on 12 July 2025.

Angolan prosecutors said Lakshtanov and Ratchin wanted to influence next year’s presidential elections, including by funding Angola’s largest opposition party, the National Union for the Total Independence of Angola (UNITA), or by forcing a leadership change within the Popular Movement for the Liberation of Angola (MPLA), which has ruled the country since independence from Portugal in 1975.

Prosecutors also claimed the men were linked to Africa Politology, a Russian disinformation and propaganda network that emerged from the Wagner mercenary group.

Africa Politology was taken over by Russia’s Foreign Intelligence Service (SVR) after the death of Wagner chief Yevgeny Prigozhin in 2023, according to an investigation by media outlets including Forbidden Stories. Wagner was rebranded as Africa Corps and absorbed by Russia’s military.

Angola, whose economy is highly dependent on oil exports, has historically been close with Russia. During the cold war years of Angola’s 1975-2002 civil war, the Soviet Union and Cuba supported the MPLA, while the US and apartheid South Africa backed its opponents, including UNITA. However, Angola’s president, João Lourenço, has improved relations with the US and Europe since taking power in 2017.

In the last decade, Russia has increased its presence in Africa. The Wagner group and then Africa Corps have provided security for the Central African Republic’s president, Faustin-Archange Touadéra, since 2017.

In 2021, Wagner forces arrived in Mali to support the military junta. Africa Corps also backs the military regimes in neighbouring Niger and Burkina Faso. Since 2023, Russian troops have also been deployed in Equatorial Guinea, Libya and Sudan, according to a US Congress report.

Madagascar’s president, Michael Randrianirina, has also boosted ties with Russia since taking power in a coup in October 2025. There are Russians in his presidential guard and a weekly programme about Madagascar-Russia relations was launched earlier this month on national television.

Two Angolans were also tried alongside the Russians. Sports journalist Carlos Tomé received a two-year suspended prison sentence. Francisco de Oliveira, the secretary-general of UNITA’s youth wing, was acquitted.

Prosecutors have appealed against those sentences, according to the Portuguese news agency Lusa. Lakshtanov and Ratchin have also appealed against their sentences, but will remain in detention while the appeal is processed, Lusa reported.

Russia’s embassy in Angola did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Agence France-Press contributed to this report

title: Trump launches Gold Eagle to hunt cyber flaws with AI
excerpt: Matt Shumer, co-founder and CEO of OthersideAI, details Anthropic's new AI model, Mythos, on 'The Sunday Briefing'. The model's "emergent capabilities" to find software vulnerabilities autonomously ra...
body_text:
Matt Shumer, co-founder and CEO of OthersideAI, details Anthropic's new AI model, Mythos, on 'The Sunday Briefing'. The model's "emergent capabilities" to find software vulnerabilities autonomously raised alarms, prompting Anthropic to restrict public access. Shumer explains the proactive move of granting major companies and the US government early access to Mythos for cyber defense, anticipating future threats to critical infrastructure and national security.

Most of us tap Update Now and move on. However, a lot happens before that security patch reaches your phone or computer. Researchers must find the flaw. Someone needs to confirm it. Then the developer has to create a fix that will not cause a new problem.

Now, artificial intelligence can uncover software weaknesses much faster. That sounds promising, but it can also create a flood of vulnerability reports. Worse, attackers can use similar AI tools to hunt for the same openings.

The Trump administration wants to help defenders move first. The White House just announced the Gold Eagle AI cybersecurity clearinghouse. Officials say the program has already started receiving and prioritizing vulnerability reports.

Gold Eagle will connect federal agencies with private companies, critical infrastructure operators and open-source software teams. Its goal is to uncover serious flaws faster and coordinate the work needed to patch them.

AI CHATBOTS TAKE HEAT OVER LEFT-WING BIAS: ‘NO LONGER BE CONSIDERED NEUTRAL’

AI tools can help defenders find dangerous software weaknesses faster, but attackers may use similar technology to target the same flaws. (Kurt "CyberGuy" Knutsson)

Gold Eagle is a federal coordination center for software vulnerabilities. The Treasury Department leads the effort with support from CISA and other federal partners. President Trump ordered the creation of the clearinghouse through Executive Order 14409 on June 2, 2026. The order directed Treasury to work with the National Cyber Director and other agencies.

Gold Eagle must coordinate vulnerability scanning and reduce duplicated work. It will also help validate findings before teams spend time fixing them. Finally, the program will support the distribution of patches once they are ready. The White House calls Gold Eagle a "force multiplier." In other words, the government wants each participating security team to accomplish more by sharing reliable information.

Gold Eagle does not replace the developers who maintain the affected software. Instead, it creates a central place where government and industry can coordinate their response. That distinction is important. Finding a bug does not automatically fix it. Developers still need to understand the weakness and build a safe update.

AI models can review large amounts of computer code quickly. They can also examine how software reacts when someone sends it unusual commands or unexpected data. That speed may help researchers uncover weaknesses that survived years of conventional testing.

A senior White House official said closed-source AI models will participate in Gold Eagle's vulnerability work. That includes Anthropic's Claude Mythos, which the company designed for advanced cybersecurity research. Anthropic says Mythos-class models can find software vulnerabilities and develop ways to exploit them. The company also warns that those abilities could make attacks easier if the models fall into the wrong hands.

That creates an uncomfortable reality. The same AI that can help protect a system may also help someone break into it. Therefore, Gold Eagle's success will depend on more than the model's ability to find bugs. The program must control who receives the details and how quickly developers get a warning.

Imagine several repair crews checking the same water pipe while another leak goes unnoticed. Cybersecurity teams can face a similar problem. Several organizations may scan the same popular software without knowing that another team already found the flaw. Meanwhile, less visible software may receive little attention.

Gold Eagle aims to coordinate those searches. It can help participating teams avoid repeating work and direct their attention toward software that still needs review. The clearinghouse will also try to cut through low-quality reports. AI models can generate convincing findings that turn out to be harmless or inaccurate.

As a result, human validation remains essential. Security engineers need to reproduce the reported flaw and confirm that it creates a real risk. After that, developers must test the repair. They also need to release the update without breaking the product for existing users.

REDHOOK ANDROID MALWARE CAN QUIETLY HIJACK YOUR PHONE

The Trump administration’s Gold Eagle program uses artificial intelligence to help uncover software flaws and coordinate security patches. (iStock; Rob Newell - CameraSport via Getty Images)

Gold Eagle will use technology developed with Carnegie Mellon University's Software Engineering Institute. The system is called the Vulnerability Information and Coordination Environment, or VINCE. Carnegie Mellon's CERT Coordination Center already uses VINCE to accept vulnerability reports and communicate with affected software vendors.

Gold Eagle can use that platform as an intake point for AI-discovered vulnerabilities. Reports can then move through validation and coordination before details become public. This controlled process matters because revealing a serious vulnerability too early can give attackers a head start. Ideally, the software company receives enough time to prepare a patch before technical details spread.

However, several operational questions remain unanswered. The administration has not publicly identified every company participating in Gold Eagle. It has also released few details about daily oversight or the way sensitive reports will move between participants. The government has not said how many findings have resulted in completed patches either.

Open-source code sits inside a huge range of commercial products. It can power parts of a browser or business platform without the average user ever knowing its name. However, many open-source projects operate with limited resources. Some rely heavily on maintainers who contribute their time alongside other jobs.

AI could help those teams find dangerous flaws. At the same time, it could overwhelm them with reports that require careful review. Gold Eagle may offer a useful filter. The clearinghouse could validate a report before sending it to a project that lacks a large security department.

It could also connect maintainers with government or industry engineers who can help assess the problem. Anthropic has already worked with open-source groups through Project Glasswing. The company says its partners used Mythos Preview to find more than 10,000 high or critical-severity vulnerabilities. Those figures come from Anthropic and do not represent Gold Eagle's results. Still, those findings show why the government expects AI-generated vulnerability reports to arrive at a much greater scale.

The government's recent handling of Claude Mythos 5 shows how sensitive these capabilities have become. On June 12, 2026, the U.S. government applied export controls to Mythos 5 and Claude Fable 5. Anthropic suspended access because it could not immediately verify the nationality of every user.

The government lifted those restrictions on June 30. Anthropic restored Mythos 5 access on July 1 to a selected group of approved U.S. organizations. Anthropic currently limits Mythos 5 to vetted partners because the model could support defensive research or harmful activity.

CyberGuy previously took a closer look at how Anthropic's Mythos AI changed cybersecurity and why its ability to uncover thousands of hidden software flaws is raising concern.

Gold Eagle is betting that controlled access can give defenders an advantage. However, other advanced models will continue improving. That means Gold Eagle will need to move quickly. A flaw loses much of its defensive value once an attacker independently discovers it.

The idea behind Gold Eagle makes sense. Security teams should share validated findings and avoid wasting time on duplicate scans. However, coordination can become slow when too many organizations must approve each decision.

Gold Eagle will need clear rules for who validates a vulnerability. It also needs a reliable way to decide which reports deserve immediate attention. Transparency will matter too. The government should eventually publish useful performance information without revealing dangerous technical details.

For example, it could report how many findings were validated and how quickly affected developers received them. It could also show how many vulnerabilities led to released patches. The program faces a legal deadline as well. Its information-sharing process relies on protections in the Cybersecurity Information Sharing Act of 2015.

Congress temporarily extended that law through September 30, 2026. The administration says a lapse could undermine the cooperation Gold Eagle needs from private companies. Companies may hesitate to share sensitive information when they feel uncertain about legal protections. That could weaken the clearinghouse before it has time to prove itself.

FAKE PASSWORD-MANAGER ALERTS COULD PUT YOUR VAULT AT RISK

Gold Eagle aims to help security teams turn verified software flaws into patches before cybercriminals can exploit them. (Kurt "CyberGuy" Knutsson)

Gold Eagle works mostly behind the scenes. However, the patch still has to reach your device. You also need to install it. Here are several ways to reduce your exposure while companies work to close newly discovered flaws.

Enable automatic updates for your phone and computer. You should also update your browser and regularly used apps. Many software updates close security holes. Automatic installation reduces the time that a known flaw remains open on your device. However, review major operating system upgrades before installing them on a device you rely on for critical work. A short delay may make sense when an update has widely reported compatibility problems. For step-by-step help, see CyberGuy's guide on how to update all of your devices and keep them safe at cyberguy.com

Your router may not display update reminders as visibly as your phone. The same problem can affect security cameras and other connected devices. Open the manufacturer's app or support page and check for firmware updates. Some newer routers let you enable automatic updates. Also, change any default administrator password that came with the device. For additional router checks, see how Russian hackers hijacked old Wi-Fi routers and the steps you can take to protect yours.

A device can continue working after its manufacturer stops supporting it. However, newly discovered vulnerabilities may remain unpatched. Check the manufacturer's support policy when you own an older router or connected product. Consider replacing the device when it reaches the end of its security-update period. CISA warns that unsupported network equipment can increase an organization's attack surface.

A real vulnerability can inspire fake update messages. Criminals may send an email that claims you must download an urgent security patch. Do not install software from an unexpected email or text message. Instead, open your device settings or the manufacturer's official app. You can also visit its verified support page directly. CISA recommends downloading patches from validated sources.

Strong antivirus software can help detect malicious files that try to exploit a weakness on your computer. Keep the protection active and allow it to update automatically. New detection information helps the software recognize recently identified threats. Still, antivirus software cannot make an unsupported device safe. It works best as one layer alongside regular updates. Get my picks for the best 2026 antivirus protection winners for your Windows, Mac, Android & iOS devices at Cyberguy.com

A current backup can help you recover if malware damages your files or ransomware locks them. Use a trusted cloud backup service or an external drive. When using an external drive, disconnect it after the backup finishes so malware cannot easily reach it. Finally, confirm that your backup is working. A backup you have never checked may fail when you need it most. For help choosing a method, check out our article on how to back up your devices the right way.

What stands out to me is how quickly AI is changing the cyber fight. It can help researchers uncover serious software flaws faster, but finding the problem is only half the battle. Someone still has to confirm it, build the fix and get that update onto your device before criminals take advantage. Gold Eagle could make that process less scattered by giving government agencies, private companies and open-source developers one place to coordinate. That could save valuable time when a major vulnerability appears. The real test will be what happens next. We need to see whether Gold Eagle can protect sensitive findings and help turn them into patches people actually receive. There is also a legal deadline coming in September that could affect how freely companies share threat information. For now, do not wait for a federal program to protect every device you own. Keep your software updated and take a closer look at older routers or smart devices that may no longer receive security fixes.

Would you feel safer knowing AI is hunting for software flaws, or more concerned knowing attackers can use the same technology? Let us know by writing to us at Cyberguy.com

Copyright 2026 CyberGuy.com. All rights reserved.

Kurt "CyberGuy" Knutsson is an award-winning tech journalist who has a deep love of technology, gear and gadgets that make life better with his contributions for Fox News & FOX Business beginning mornings on "FOX & Friends." Got a tech question? Get Kurt’s free CyberGuy Newsletter, share your voice, a story idea or comment at CyberGuy.com.

title: First Thing: US agrees Saudi uranium deal as strikes on Iran continue and Yemen’s Houthis hit tankers
excerpt:
Conflict continues across the region as White House announces nuclear technology agreement. Plus, the artist who flattened a Tesla with a tank

Don’t already get First Thing in your inbox? Sign up her...
body_text:
Conflict continues across the region as White House announces nuclear technology agreement. Plus, the artist who flattened a Tesla with a tank

Don’t already get First Thing in your inbox? Sign up here

Good morning. The US has signed a landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could allow the kingdom to enrich uranium in the future. The deal will be submitted to Congress for review, where it could face opposition among lawmakers who fear a lack of monitoring safeguards.

In lengthy talks stretching over more than one administration, the US has feared that Riyadh would look elsewhere for nuclear technology if the US did not provide what it wanted. Saudi Arabia has long said that if it does not partner with the US, it could do so with China or Russia, which have different proliferation standards.

Why is a uranium deal with Saudi Arabia controversial? The full details of the deal have yet to be released, but it comes as the US’s war with Iran is being fought in part to prevent Tehran enriching uranium domestically and make it near impossible for Iran to build a nuclear weapon. Saudi Arabia has in the past said it would need a nuclear weapon if Iran acquired one. Kenneth Roth has analysed why Trump would agree it right now.

House Republicans have agreed to begin the process of writing a $95bn bill to fund the war with Iran as well as provide new aid to farmers, while making a long-shot attempt to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide.

The resolution’s passage comes amid an intensification of the conflict with Iran, which Pete Hegseth, the defense secretary, told the Senate on Tuesday had thus far cost $37.5bn. The Trump administration last month asked Congress to authorize approximately $88bn in supplemental funding, the majority of which – up to $67.1bn – will go to pay for the war.

What are the latest developments in the war? The US military launched its 12th successive night of strikes, which have now widened to western and central Iran. In attacks lasting five hours, the US claims to have struck military targets including maritime capabilities, missile and ⁠drone storage facilities, coastal surveillance sites and air defense assets. Yemen’s ⁠Houthi militia also attacked two ​Saudi Arabian oil tankers, the Iran-aligned group said on Thursday, with a Saudi news agency later confirming one of the vessels was ablaze in the Red Sea.

The leading US senator Elizabeth Warren has called for urgent answers from Barclays over what she claimed was its “apparent failure to meaningfully investigate” ties between the bank’s former boss, Jes Staley, and the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

In a letter sent privately to the Barclays chair, Nigel Higgins, and seen by the Guardian, Warren – the most senior Democrat on the US senate banking committee – said it was “deeply unclear how Barclays, supposedly investigating Staley’s connection to Epstein, failed to uncover this decades-long relationship”.

What other developments have there been in the Epstein case? A modelling scout who recruited women for the disgraced financier has been found dead at his home near Paris, French officials have said. Daniel Siad, 69, was under investigation by the prosecutors in France, accused of rape and other charges. He had not yet been questioned by investigators, but had denied the allegations, saying he wanted to give his version of events. His name appeared in more than 1,000 documents in the declassified US Department of Justice Epstein files. An investigation to determine the cause of death has been opened and an autopsy is to be carried out.

Just days before trial, a US teen has dropped his Meta lawsuit blaming the firms’s platforms ⁠for his depression ⁠and anxiety, in a victory for the social media giant after an earlier landmark loss relating to social media addiction.

Donald Trump has scoffed at the word “affordability” as he touted his economic policies in Georgia, describing it as a term concocted by Democratic political consultants.

A police officer has fatally shot a man in Madison, Wisconsin, sparking protests.

India’s prime minister has said he will set up “fast-track courts” to punish corrupt officials who leaked examination papers, as authorities seek to quell the youth-led Cockroach protest movement.

Pride organisers have vowed to march in a Romanian city that has blocked the event four years in a row.

Google’s parent company, Alphabet, reported double-digit revenue growth for its second-quarter earnings on Wednesday. This is the tech giant’s 12th consecutive quarter of massive revenue gains.

Portable fans, a “cooling necklace”, sun-protective hats and more products our writers reach for when the heat won’t quit. And once you have cooled down, here are seven screen-free activities that will help you survive summer indoors.

The British painter, sculptor and performance artist, who has devoted his life to making art available to the masses, discusses inequality, inspiration – and why despite being a centenarian he won’t be stopping any time soon.

You will almost certainly have come across social media posts describing Britain’s capital, London, my home town, as a lawless mess – a “fact” that seems patently untrue every time I step outside my front door. My mayor, Sadiq Khan – the target of widespread racist abuse on the web – has now written for the Guardian that “If you’ve heard that London is dystopian and dangerous – read this. It just isn’t.”

Our environment editor, Damian Carrington, reports that extreme heat supercharged by the climate crisis is sucking Europe dry in summer and intensifying the drought hitting the continent. Researchers said the link between the human-caused climate crisis and the drying of European summers was strong.

Orcas have been observed ramming into giant fish so hard that the prey explode, possibly as a form of gory entertainment. Dr Kathryn Ayres, a scientist at the non-profit organization Beneath the Waves, said: “We think this may help younger orcas feed more easily, or it could also just be for fun. Orcas are known for playing with their food.”

First Thing is delivered to thousands of inboxes every weekday. If you’re not already signed up, subscribe now.

If you have any questions or comments about any of our newsletters please email newsletters@theguardian.com

title: R James Woolsey, embattled ex-CIA director under Bill Clinton, dies aged 84
excerpt:
Woolsey died of a stroke at Washington home after facing ‘Havana syndrome’ reported by some government officials

R James Woolsey, a national security expert who led the CIA for two turbulent years af...
body_text:
Woolsey died of a stroke at Washington home after facing ‘Havana syndrome’ reported by some government officials

R James Woolsey, a national security expert who led the CIA for two turbulent years after his appointment by Bill Clinton, has died. He was 84.

Woolsey, who became CIA director in early 1993, died on Tuesday at his home in Washington, according to several newspapers. Conchita Sarnoff Woolsey, his wife, told the newspapers that he died from a stroke.

She told the Washington Post that his health had declined in recent years due to “Havana syndrome,” a mysterious condition that has been reported by some US diplomats and other government personnel. Symptoms, including headaches, balance problems and difficulties with thinking and sleep, were first reported in Cuba in 2016 – leading to the label.

Woolsey left the CIA in early 1995. Among criticisms he had faced were from those who felt there had been insufficient sanctions against supervisors of the confessed CIA turncoat Aldrich Ames, who betrayed western intelligence assets to the Soviet Union and Russia. Ames, a career CIA officer, admitted being paid $2.5m by Moscow for US secrets from 1985 until his arrest in 1994.

Woolsey said the agency couldn’t fully protect itself against spies including Ames, and he cautioned against overreacting to the vulnerabilities exposed by Ames. He said there was a danger in going too far too fast, adding that overly strict security measures could lead to “a mood or attitude of paranoia and false accusations” that would further damage morale.

With the end of the cold war, Woolsey also faced pressure from lawmakers who sought to make deep cuts to the agency’s budget.

Before leading the CIA, he served as undersecretary of the navy in the Jimmy Carter administration and as an adviser on arms control issues to both Republican and Democratic presidents.

When he became director of the CIA, he was known for getting things done. He was appointed by George HW Bush in 1989 as a special negotiator for the languishing talks between the Nato alliance and the Soviet-led Warsaw Pact on reducing weapons and troops in Europe. A year later, Bush and Mikhail Gorbachev, the Soviet president, signed the Conventional Armed Forces in Europe treaty.

Woolsey was born in Tulsa, Oklahoma, on 21 September 1941. The graduate of Stanford University was a Rhodes scholar who got his law degree from Yale University. He began government work in 1970 as a staff member on the national security council, then moved to Capitol Hill to become general counsel to the Senate armed services committee.

Between stints in government work, he practiced law at a Washington firm.

title: Sean Penn reveals chilling thought after finding himself alone with the 'Night Stalker' in jail
excerpt:
Actor Sean Penn joins Hannity to discuss his view of the Russian invasion and his new picture, which began filming prior to the conflict.

Sean Penn admits he briefly considered attacking one of Ameri...
body_text:
Actor Sean Penn joins Hannity to discuss his view of the Russian invasion and his new picture, which began filming prior to the conflict.

Sean Penn admits he briefly considered attacking one of America's most infamous serial killers after the two unexpectedly came face to face behind bars.

In a recent interview with VICE News, the actor recalled spending 34 days in Los Angeles County Jail during the height of his early Hollywood success. Penn said he was in his mid-20s, had already starred in "Taps" and filmed "Colors," and was married to Madonna when he crossed paths with the "Night Stalker."

"I got moved out of general population because of some kind of threat," the 65-year-old told the outlet. "... And a lot of guys on this department... they invest a lot in pretending not to give a s--- about Hollywood culture, but they sure do like to gossip [about] what they see."

LISA RINNA REVEALS HOW HER MOTHER SURVIVED BRUTAL HAMMER ATTACK BY NOTORIOUS ‘TRAILSIDE KILLER’

Sean Penn, left, told VICE News he was just 24 or 25 when he spent 34 days in Los Angeles County Jail and unexpectedly crossed paths with serial killer Richard Ramirez, right. (Stephane Cardinale/Corbis via Getty Images; Getty Images)

"So, I slept with one eye open, of course," he said. "... Then I got moved to [an] eight and a half by 11 by myself for most of the time. And through a little wire glass door, there's Richard Ramirez, not 20 feet away. And we're both insomniacs."

Shackled and belly chained for a phone call, the actor said he was escorted into an otherwise empty room — only to discover Ramirez sitting beside him.

WATCH: SEAN PENN DISCUSSES DOCUMENTARY ON UKRAINE WAR

"I came out of my cell for a phone call," Penn told the outlet. "So they take you shackled and belly chained, and they take one cuff off, put it on the eye hook so that you've got a hand for the phone. And Ramirez is seated right next to me. He's going to [make] a call also."

Richard Ramirez is seen in court on Oct. 9, 1985, in Los Angeles. (Getty Images)

"There's nobody else there," said Penn. "There's nobody in there because somebody was throwing feces at somebody. It was a crazy kind of thing happening. And I did have that question. I knew what he did to some of these people. This wasn't today's phones or your cellphone, right?"

"I'm right next to him," said Penn. "I could, I could ..."

Sean Penn opened up about spending 34 days near serial killer Richard Ramirez — and the split-second temptation he says he had during their face-to-face encounter. (Marc Piasecki/Getty Images)

The Oscar winner admitted he thought better of it despite having access to him "twice."

"But who was I?" said Penn. "It was just another person. And I'm so glad I didn't think I was important enough to do that, but you have the thought."

Ramirez was convicted of 13 murders during a killing spree that gripped Southern California in 1984 and 1985.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

Richard Ramirez received the death penalty in 1989 after being convicted of 13 murders and numerous other violent crimes. (Bettmann Archive)

His reign of terror peaked in the summer of 1985, when he slipped into homes through unlocked doors and windows before murdering victims, sexually assaulting women and burglarizing homes. He also left satanic symbols at several crime scenes.

The press dubbed him the "Night Stalker" as authorities warned Southern California residents to lock their doors and windows after dark.

Ramirez's slayings ended in 1985 after residents of an East Los Angeles neighborhood recognized him from news reports, chased him down and beat him before police took him into custody following a failed carjacking.

Richard Ramirez is seen here flashing the "devil's horn" sign before shouting "evil" to reporters. (Bettmann Archive)

During his trial, the Texas native attracted a following of young women who regularly attended court proceedings and sent him love letters.

Ramirez died in 2013 at age 53 from cancer while awaiting execution on California's death row at San Quentin State Prison.

Years before, Penn revealed another bizarre detail about their time behind bars: The serial killer wanted his autograph. Speaking to The Hollywood Reporter in 2015, Penn said Ramirez sent word through a deputy requesting a signed keepsake.

LIKE WHAT YOU’RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Sean Penn attends the "Into the Wild" By Sean Penn event during the 17th Film Festival Lumiere on Oct. 12, 2025, in Lyon, France. (WireImage)

"He wrote me [a letter]," said Penn. "I was down here on Bauchet Street, in LA County Jail, in the cell kind of across from him. And after a month of seeing each other around, he wanted my autograph. So, he sent one of the deputies over, [and the] deputy came to my cell and told me, ‘Hey, Richard Ramirez wants your autograph.’ And I didn’t trust the deputy because I’d gotten in some trouble inside there and just passing a piece of paper is contraband, so you can get extra days for that, and I already had extra days and I didn’t want more."

Sean Penn told VICE News he was already married to Madonna when his encounter with Richard Ramirez occurred. (Getty Images)

"So, I said, ‘Bring the sergeant down here, and I’ll talk to him, and if he approves it, then I want him to write something first, and I’ll write him something back,’" said Penn. "So, the sergeant came down and approved it, and they went over to Ramirez — this guard basically wanted to play cupid, in some way. So, I get this thing from him, and it says, ‘Hey Sean, stay tough and hit them again — Richard Ramirez, 666,’ with a pentagram and a rendition of the devil."

Sean Penn told The Hollywood Reporter what happened to the handwritten note he received from Richard Ramirez. (Samir Hussein/WireImage)

"I said, ‘You know, Richard, it’s impossible to be incarcerated and not feel a certain kinship with your fellow inmates. Well, Richard, I’ve done the impossible. I feel absolutely no kinship with you. And I hope gas descends upon you before sanity does, you know? It would be a kinder way out.’"

"And they gave it to him," said Penn. "And then my house burned down years later, and that damn thing of his burned with it."

Stephanie Nolasco is an Entertainment Reporter for Fox News Digital.

title: Cardinals tight end Travis Vokolek retires at age 28 after suffering severe neck injury during 2025 season
excerpt:
Former NFL star Drew Brees shares his journey to the Pro Football Hall of Fame, overcoming doubts about his physical traits to become an all-around quarterback.

The Arizona Cardinals’ roster took a h...
body_text:
Former NFL star Drew Brees shares his journey to the Pro Football Hall of Fame, overcoming doubts about his physical traits to become an all-around quarterback.

The Arizona Cardinals’ roster took a huge hit on Wednesday.

Cardinals tight end Travis Vokolek announced he was retiring from the sport at 28 after he suffered a neck injury during a game against the Indianapolis Colts. Vokolek was blocking during a kickoff and landed awkwardly after hitting a Colts player. He needed to be stretchered off the field.

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

Arizona Cardinals tight end Travis Vokolek (81) faces the Las Vegas Raiders during a preseason NFL game at State Farm Stadium in Glendale, Ariz., on Aug. 23, 2025. (IMAGN IMAGES via Reuters Connect)

The 28-year-old said in a post on social media he was retiring from the sport on doctor’s advice.

"After numerous visits with doctors and neurologists over the past couple of months, I have been advised to medically retire from the NFL due to a serious neck injury that I suffered this past season," he wrote in a post on social media. "I am extremely grateful and blessed to have been able to live out my childhood dream of playing football at the highest level for the past three years. Football has taught me many life lessons and given me unforgettable memories.

"I am forever grateful to all my teammates, coaches, trainers, and others who have supported my family and I along the way. A sincere thank you to the Arizona Cardinals training staff for being first class with the care and support they provided my family and I during this hard time."

Arizona Cardinals tight end Travis Vokolek leaves the field after an injury during a game against the Indianapolis Colts at Lucas Oil Stadium in Indianapolis, Ind., on Oct. 12, 2025. (IMAGN IMAGES via Reuters Connect)

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

He thanked his wife for being by his side the entire time as he tried to recover from the neck injury.

"I have attacked everyday of being a professional football player with passion, determination, and discipline," he added. "I can say that I truly left everything out on the field. Thank you for all of the love and support. Lastly, thank you football!"

Vokolek played college football with Rutgers and Nebraska before deciding to turn pro. He initially joined the Baltimore Ravens as an undrafted free agent in 2023 but didn’t see time on the field with the team.

Arizona Cardinals tight end Travis Vokolek warms up before the game against the San Francisco 49ers at Levi's Stadium in Santa Clara, Calif., on Sept. 21, 2025. (IMAGN IMAGES via Reuters Connect)

In December 2023, he signed with the Cardinals. He played eight games for the Cardinals but was never truly involved in the offense.

Ryan Gaydos is a senior editor for Fox News Digital.

title: Outcry after $1.15tn defense bill passes in the House – US politics live
excerpt:
Donald Trump’s decision to attack Iran without first seeking congressional approval has sparked further controversy over the National Defense Authorization Act

The House passed the National Defense A...
body_text:
Donald Trump’s decision to attack Iran without first seeking congressional approval has sparked further controversy over the National Defense Authorization Act

The House passed the National Defense Authorization Act in a 216-212 vote, with all but six Democrats opposing its passage and seven Republicans breaking ranks to oppose the measure.

These are the Democrats that voted for NDAA:

These are the Republicans that voted against it:

Josh Brecheen of Oklahoma. “While I support providing for a robust national defense, I could not in good conscience vote for this legislation as it authorizes a $263.4bn (29.6%) increase over the authorized level for the previous fiscal year,” Brecheen posted on X.

Hello and welcome to our live coverage of US politics.

The National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) typically passes in Congress each year with bipartisan margins. But the $1.15tn defense policy bill passed in a near-party-line vote in the House of Representatives on Wednesday has lawmakers on both sides of the aisle in an uproar.

That’s because the NDAA’s path to enactment this year has been complicated by the president’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without first seeking authorization from Congress – a war that has already cost $37.5bn, according to defense secretary Pete Hegseth. Last week, Senate Democrats blocked the advancement of that chamber’s version of the NDAA in protest of the war.

“While millions of Americans are struggling to afford basic necessities, Congress is choosing to authorize over $1tn for the Pentagon, once again funneling billions of taxpayer dollars to defense contractors instead of investing in the needs of working people,” Ilhan Omar, a Democratic representative from Minnesota, posted on X.

She continued: “The American people are sick and tired of funding atrocities abroad and want this war with Iran to end. Yet year after year, the Pentagon fails its audit with virtually no accountability, while Congress continues to increase military spending.”

Further complicating matters, Mike Johnson, the Republican House speaker, acceded to the demands of rightwing lawmakers and inserted into the legislation the Save America Act, a Trump-backed proposal to ban mail-in ballots and impose new identification restrictions on voters at the polls and when they register. “This budget resolution is our best shot at enacting as much of the Save America Act into law as possible,” Johnson -posted on X.

“This means the NDAA is now tied to Trump’s attempt to steal elections by making it more difficult for millions of Americans to vote,” Jerry Nadler, a Democratic representative from New York, posted on X. “The NDAA is a costly, bad bill that funds an illegal war and undermines our democracy.”

Section 219 of the NDAA – which establishes the US-Israel Defense Technology Cooperation Initiative – also had Republican representatives protesting the bill’s passage. “Section 219 is a betrayal of our sovereignty,” Kentucky conservative Thomas Massie said on X. Far-right firebrand Lauren Boebert also said she sat out on the NDAA vote because of Section 219.

title: GIANNO CALDWELL: Four years after my brother’s murder, I’m marching for justice
excerpt:
Gianno Caldwell discusses his advocacy for public safety on the anniversary of his brother Christian's murder in Chicago.

Four years ago, I got the call no family should ever receive. My innocent tee...
body_text:
Gianno Caldwell discusses his advocacy for public safety on the anniversary of his brother Christian's murder in Chicago.

Four years ago, I got the call no family should ever receive. My innocent teenage brother, Christian had been shot and killed on the streets of Chicago. He was just a kid, full of promise, full of life. Gone. This Saturday, I am going back. Not to mourn. To march.

Chicago is the shining example of how soft-on-crime policies devastate families across our nation. It is the epicenter of a political failure so profound and so deliberate that its consequences have spread to cities all across America. Those who have chosen to put virtue signaling ahead of victims have turned one of America's great cities into a graveyard of broken promises. The families paying the price are not the politicians who made those promises. They are the mothers, fathers, brothers, and sisters who have lost not one, but in some cases, multiple loved ones to violent crime.

Are sanctuary city policies destroying these communities? Yes. Is it the elimination of cash bail? Yes. Is it elected officials who have fought harder for criminals than for the public they were elected to serve? Yes. It is all of the above and more.

REPORTER'S NOTEBOOK: CHICAGO COP KILLED AFTER SUSPECT RELEASED UNDER SAFE-T ACT

Illinois became the first state in the nation to completely eliminate cash bail through the SAFE-T Act, a law that allows judges the discretion to release violent offenders without requiring them to post any money or collateral — simply by signing a form promising to return to court. No money. No bond. No consequence.

The results speak for themselves. Through the first six months of 2026, Chicago recorded 210 homicides, a nearly 6% increase compared to the same period in 2025, with 686 shooting incidents leaving 858 people wounded or killed across the city. These are not statistics. These are people. These are families. These are futures stolen.

Some will point to the fact that nationally, homicide rates fell dramatically in 2025, reaching what researchers called the lowest level since at least 1900. Good. But let's be honest about why. That progress did not come from the policies championed by blue city mayors. It came in spite of them.

GOP SENATORS RALLY AROUND NEW DHS PROPOSAL TARGETING SANCTUARY CITIES: 'SHOULD PAY A PRICE'

President Trump directed U.S. Marshals Service Director Gadyaces Serralta to lead a coordinated crime surge operation across America's most dangerous cities, bringing together 31 federal agencies to take violent criminals off the streets. In Washington D.C. alone, murders dropped 60%, robberies dropped 50%, and carjackings fell a staggering 70%. The Memphis task force surpassed 4,000 arrests. Nationally, U.S. Marshals arrested over 73,000 fugitives in 2025 alone. Now in 2026, with Chicago already trending back upward, you can see exactly what happens when a city refuses that help. Mayor Brandon Johnson signed executive orders directing Chicago police to withhold cooperation from federal agents, vowing publicly that his city "will not ever cooperate with ICE." Governor Pritzker has sued the federal government rather than work with it. Families are paying that price.

The political class has not only chosen to look away. In some cases, they have openly insulted the families left behind. Just weeks ago, Democratic Congresswoman Pramila Jayapal sat before Angel Families, meaning families of Americans killed by illegal immigrants, and told them: "Unfortunately, this hearing is the fourth time in this committee that we've had a hearing on sanctuary cities. There's many other things that we could be doing." A member of Congress sat in front of grieving mothers and fathers and told them their children's lives were not worth her time. Let that settle in.

Jessica Gorman, whose 18-year-old daughter Sheridan was shot and killed at the Chicago lakefront while walking with friends to see the Northern Lights, responded directly: "Thanks for telling me without telling me that you're here, but you don't want to be, for telling me you don't care." Sheridan was a freshman from Westchester County, New York, with her whole life mapped out in front of her. The man charged with her murder, Jose Medina-Medina, was a Venezuelan national apprehended by U.S. Border Patrol in May 2023 and released into the country under the Biden administration, even after officials flagged him as likely to abscond and noted he had no verifiable address or contact information. He was released due to, in their words, a lack of space. He was later arrested for shoplifting in Chicago and released again before he took Sheridan's life. Chicago's sanctuary policies made it possible. Governor Pritzker and Mayor Johnson's leadership made it inevitable.

BIDEN BORDER OFFICIALS RELEASED ALLEGED KILLER OF CHICAGO STUDENT 'DUE TO LACK OF SPACE,' DOCUMENTS SHOW

Maybe if they stopped enabling the policies that create Angel Families, Congresswoman Jayapal wouldn't have as many hearings to complain about. They refuse to stop. So we refuse to be silent.

The victims' families marching with me Saturday know what it means to be failed by a system built by elected officials who put criminals first. We are starting our march at Morgan Park High School, where my brother Christian walked the halls and where, four years later, our family still searches for the justice he deserved. We are extending a direct invitation to Governor JB Pritzker and Mayor Brandon Johnson: come march with us. Come look these families in the face. Come see the devastation your policies have caused. You have governed from behind podiums and press releases long enough. Come stand in front of the people your decisions have failed.

Chicago is where we start. But this fight is national. This march is for every family in America that has buried a loved one while politicians made excuses. It is for the Angel Families who traveled to Washington over and over, begging lawmakers to act, only to be told there are more important things to discuss. It is for every American family that deserves what too many have been denied: leaders who put their safety first.

When I founded the Caldwell Foundation for Public Safety in Christian's memory, I made a promise that his death would not be in vain. That I would stand with victims, amplify their voices, and hold the people in power accountable. This Saturday, that promise takes to the streets. We march for Christian. We march for Sheridan Gorman. We march for every family whose grief was treated as collateral damage by the people who were supposed to protect them.

The Community March Against Violence takes place Saturday, July 25, at 10:00 AM at Morgan Park High School in Chicago. Register here.

Gianno Caldwell currently serves as a political analyst for FOX News Channel (FNC), offering political, financial and cultural analysis across the network’s programming. He joined the network in 2017.

title: Why New Jersey’s erroneous noncitizen enrollment is far from proof of election fraud
excerpt:
Republicans have latched on to the announcement after Trump’s lie-laden speech, but experts say DHS’s data is ‘designed to create a false argument’

Donald Trump and his allies quickly seized on an an...
body_text:
Republicans have latched on to the announcement after Trump’s lie-laden speech, but experts say DHS’s data is ‘designed to create a false argument’

Donald Trump and his allies quickly seized on an announcement from Mikie Sherrill, the New Jersey governor, on Tuesday that 6,600 noncitizens had registered to vote because of a software error, and fewer than 400 of them voted, using thedisclosure to escalate false claims that there are scores of noncitizens on the voter rolls.

Trump seized on the news days after he said in a primetime speech that the Department of Homeland Security identified 278,000 noncitizens on the voter rolls in New Jersey and three other states. But a Guardian review of the letters DHS sent the states shows the agency expressed less certainty about the number and has offered no explanation of its methodology.

Since 2018, New Jersey has had automatic voter registration, which means that any time someone interacts with a motor vehicle office they are automatically registered to vote, unless they opt out. Between 2023 and 2024, some 6,600 people had indicated they were not citizens, yet nonetheless had been registered to vote. Around 400 of those people cast a ballot, Sherrill said.

The number represents a small fraction of the more than 6.6 million registered voters in the state. But within hours of the announcement, Trump and other election deniers inflated the number of people who registered to vote because of the error, and held it up as yet another data point that “illegals” are stealing American elections on behalf of Democrats.

Trump said in a post on Truth Social: “35,152 is the new number just released by Homeland Security of non-citizens registered to vote in New Jersey. These are just the ones that got caught. The real numbers will prove to be many times this amount. They are all Dumocrats!.” There were Democrats and Republicans erroneously registered, Sherrill said on Tuesday, with the majority unaffiliated with a party.

The incident is far from proof of the widespread fraud that Republicans allege, experts say.

“It’s not good, I don’t want to suggest otherwise. I don’t want to tell anyone that they’re stupid for thinking it’s less than perfectly optimal. But it’s not really an example of what they’re talking about,” said Stephen Richer, a Republican who oversaw elections in Maricopa county, Arizona from 2021 to 2025. “These people were trying to do the right thing and acknowledge that they’re not citizens, and then the vast majority of them, even though registered and presumably [were] sent election mail materials, ignored that and didn’t vote.”

The number of non-citizens the state says were registered represents a “minuscule fraction” of New Jersey’s active registered voters in 2024, said David Becker, an election administration expert and the executive director of the Center for Election Innovation and Research. “The number of potential ballots cast from those incorrect registrations would represent roughly 0.009% of all ballots cast in New Jersey’s 2024 general election, when Kamala Harris defeated President Trump by more than 250,000 votes.

“While administrative errors such as New Jersey’s do infrequently occur, they are very rare and affect a very small fraction of voters. The states entrusted to run our elections are best positioned to detect and correct these errors, as they have access to substantially better data than the federal government,” he said.

The firestorm over the New Jersey voter rolls came as Trump escalated his incendiary rhetoric about voting ahead of the midterm elections. Last week, in a primetime address from the White House, he offered what seemed to be an eye-popping statistic: the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) had found 278,000 non-citizens on the rolls in just four states – California, Nevada, Pennsylvania and New Jersey. That, too, is a misleading figure.

In its letters to election officials in those four states DHS expressed less certainty about the 278,000 number, saying it was a ceiling of how many noncitizens could be on the rolls. The agency also offered little to no explanation of its methodology for identifying noncitizens on the rolls. It gave the states two weeks to respond.

“It’s a laughable letter,” Becker said. “Imagine someone said to you: ‘Some of the items in your fridge are poisonous. Write us back in two weeks and tell us what you’re going to do about that.’” The first question one would ask, Becker said, is “which items?”

“They’re not serious. They don’t want the states to even maintain their records correctly. If they did, they would be completely transparent, and they would have acted immediately and said we absolutely need you to do this,” he added. “This is designed to create a false argument post-election that the election was stolen. This has nothing to do with actually cleaning up the lists.”

The noncitizen registration claim was part of a slew of misleading information Trump offered in a speech last week designed to weaken confidence in the US election system ahead of a November mid-term election in which his party is expected to lose seats. It also comes as Republicans are pushing legislation that would impose new proof-of-citizenship requirements on voter registration, even though several studies have shown noncitizen voting is extremely rare.

Last week, both Trump and Markwayne Mullin, the homeland security secretary, cited the 278,000 number as evidence that states should share sensitive voter information with the federal government, with Mullin threatening potential prison time for those who don’t comply. At least one Republican has cited the number as a bonafide example of voter fraud. “We know there are roughly 278,000 noncitizens registered to vote. The last three presidential elections were decided by fewer votes,” Marsha Blackburn, a senator of Tennessee, said on Tuesday.

“We can affirm that on its face, we refute these claims. These numbers are wildly speculative at best and the Department of Homeland Security hasn’t shared anything that backs it up,” said Cisco Aguilar, a Democrat serving as Nevada’s secretary of state. As of Wednesday afternoon, DHS had not provided Nevada with a list of suspected noncitizens in the state.

In a letter to New Jersey officials last week, DHS said there may be as many as 35,152 noncitizens on the state’s voter rolls. The agency said it had matched 19,497 voter records to a noncitizen in its system, but did not offer an explanation for the rest.

Sherrill said she had been informed of the voter registration issue in her state and begun investigating before Trump’s speech on Thursday evening. She said DHS had provided no information to back up its claims. “We have asked them for any evidence of this, where they got that number, who this is. We’ve received nothing back yet,” she said on Tuesday.

Understanding the methodology DHS used is critical to assessing the veracity of its claims, experts say. Matching records across different databases is difficult, and the system DHS uses to try and identify noncitizens – Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements (Save) – is known to be error-prone. DHS said it relied on publicly available voter information from the four states to try and identify noncitizens, information that likely lacks the level of detail needed to get a solid match between voter records and DHS data, Charles Stewart III, a professor at MIT and an expert in election administration, wrote in a post on Substack.

“DHS likely lacked the identifiers needed for high-quality matching. Save checks generally require name, date of birth, and a unique immigration or Social Security identifier, and public voter files ordinarily do not contain the most sensitive identifiers,” he wrote. “Anyone with experience in these data-matching exercises should expect those numbers to melt as quickly as ice cream in a Phoenix August.”

Even though the agency claimed there could be up to 278,000 noncitizens on their rolls, DHS said in its letters to the states that it was able to match the name, date of birth, address and Social Security number to a noncitizen in its system for around 118,000 people. The agency offered no explanation for why it believed there were an additional 160,000 noncitizens on the rolls

DHS did not respond to multiple requests for comment on its methodology.

“I am about as certain as I can be, without them coming up with some magical data technique that nobody has ever seen before, that these numbers cannot be even remotely credible,” Becker said. “What I can’t tell you is whether they just made them up. Because they have not been transparent about that. And it’s possible that they just made them up, because we just haven’t seen any methodology whatsoever.”

Claims of widespread noncitizen registration and voting often start with a headline-grabbing statistic, and then the numbers often whittle down once election officials investigate. In 2019, for example, Texas announced it suspected there were nearly 100,000 noncitizens on its rolls, 58,000 of whom may have voted. The state later admitted the claim was based on bad data and agreed not to cancel anyone’s voter registration based on it.

title: FDA to review 7 popular peptides touted for weight loss, healing and longevity
excerpt:
Dr. Alex Tatem, a board-certified urologist in Indiana, discusses why upcoming federal hearings on seven peptides were mandated and argues that years of patient use show a promising safety profile.

T...
body_text:
Dr. Alex Tatem, a board-certified urologist in Indiana, discusses why upcoming federal hearings on seven peptides were mandated and argues that years of patient use show a promising safety profile.

The Food and Drug Administration is taking a closer look this week at seven unapproved peptide substances that have surged in popularity for their purported health benefits.

At meetings scheduled for July 23 and 24 at the FDA’s White Oak Campus in Silver Spring, Maryland, the FDA’s Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee will consider whether to recommend adding seven peptide substances to the agency’s 503A Bulks List, which would allow them to be used in certain compounded prescription drugs.

Although these peptides are not FDA-approved, they are widely available through wellness clinics, compounding operations and gray-market online sellers.

THINKING ABOUT PEPTIDES? DOCTORS REVEAL KEY DOS AND DONT'S AS 'WILD WEST' MARKET GROWS

HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has voiced support for expanding access to certain peptide therapies, while FDA reviewers have raised concerns about the limited evidence supporting the safety and effectiveness of the seven substances under consideration.

According to Reuters, consumer demand is likely to "remain strong" despite the outcome, as peptides have surged in popularity for weight loss, muscle building, performance enhancement, chronic disease management and even wound repair.

Peptides have surged in popularity for weight loss, muscle building, performance enhancement, chronic disease management and even wound repair. (iStock)

FDA documents show that the following seven peptides are under review.

Proposed forms: Oral capsules, nasal spray, injections, transdermal cream, rectal suppository

Proposed forms: Subcutaneous or intramuscular injection

SECRET TO WEIGHT LOSS MAY BE HIDING IN YOUR GUT, NEW STUDY SUGGESTS

Proposed use: Wound healing and inflammatory condition

Proposed use: Cerebral ischemia (shortage of blood flow and oxygen to the brain), migraine and trigeminal neuralgia (chronic pain condition in the face)

Proposed form: Intranasal spray, subcutaneous injection

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR HEALTH NEWSLETTER

Proposed use: Opioid withdrawal, chronic insomnia, narcolepsy

"These were all medications that were designed to help people live well and live as healthy as possible," Tatem previously told Fox News Digital about peptides. (iStock)

Performance medicine and peptide expert Dr. Alex Tatem, a board-certified urologist and member of Rugiet's Medical Advisory Board, will testify at this week's FDA hearings.

In an interview with Fox News Digital, Indiana-based Tatem said that every product up for review is "exciting," as each targets a different problem faced by patients.

"BPC-157 or TB-500 are compounds that can help facilitate healing after injury and have been shown in multiple studies to be incredibly safe when administered — which is interesting when you compare that to the over-the-counter options that we use now for aches and pains, like ibuprofen," he said.

Although ibuprofen is available over the counter, the FDA warns that it can cause serious side effects — including stomach bleeding, kidney damage and cardiovascular complications — especially when used at high doses or for long periods.

Tatem claimed that millions of BPC-157 doses have been administered and that relatively few adverse events have been reported.

"There is no other drug on the market that had that sort of safety record," Tatem said. "And it's not because of any magic — it's because these are natural substances that our bodies make and are familiar with, so that's what gives them this incredible safety profile."

HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. testifies during the House Energy and Commerce Subcommittee on Health hearing titled "The FY2026 Department of Health and Human Services Budget," on Tuesday, June 24, 2025. (Tom Williams/CQ-Roll Call, Inc)

The FDA, however, has publicly stated that there is not enough evidence from human clinical trials on the safety or efficacy of these drugs. FDA reviewers have also cautioned that a peptide’s similarity to a naturally occurring substance does not eliminate potential risks.

Fox News senior medical analyst Dr. Marc Siegel cautioned that these substances "need careful study."

"I am not convinced the data is fully there yet."

According to the doctor, RFK Jr. told Siegel in their latest interview that he believes "not all peptides are created equal, whereas some are helpful, others may not be and need further study."

"I agree with that," Siegel told Fox News Digital. "I’m concerned about the impact some may have on the kidneys. I would like to see rigorous clinical trials before approval. I am not convinced the data is fully there yet."

Tatem said he expects the hearings to be a "hard-fought battle," but hopes committee members will consider what he described as real-world data from tens of millions of doses administered between 2014 and 2023. The data showed adverse event rates of "well under 0.1%," he added.

If the committee recommends inclusion, the FDA would still need to complete its review and rulemaking process before formally adding any of the substances to the 503A Bulks List.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

That list includes active ingredients that state-licensed pharmacies are legally permitted to use for compounding prescription drugs, Tatem said.

He also suggested that the FDA could place some substances under a more permissive enforcement category temporarily while the rulemaking process continues. (The agency has not announced that it intends to do so.)

"Ultimately, what we would hope is, before the end of the year, patients would be able to go to their doctor, work through maybe a telehealth provider, and then be able to get a prescription for this under monitored medical supervision," he said.

"The most important thing to take away is that your voice matters."

"The most important thing to take away is that your voice matters, because this committee is just issuing a recommendation to the FDA. The FDA can take it or leave it," Tatem noted. "Everything that happens afterward is still anyone's guess."

Tatem encourages people to reach out to their local representatives to request access to "safe, regulated peptides made here in the United States of America."

Fox News Digital reached out to the FDA and ibuprofen product manufacturer Haleon for comment.

Angelica Stabile is a lifestyle reporter for Fox News Digital.

title: Thousands evacuated in France fires
excerpt:
Around 12,000 people have been evacuated from the south-west of France as the country battles ongoing wildfires days after two firefighters died near Bordeaux airport, French media reported.

The Giro...
body_text:
Around 12,000 people have been evacuated from the south-west of France as the country battles ongoing wildfires days after two firefighters died near Bordeaux airport, French media reported.

The Gironde region government said more than 2,400 hectares (5,900 acres) of land have been destroyed.

The use of internal combustion and electric engines have been banned in predominantly forested areas, and a number of roads have been closed. No injuries have been reported on Thursday.

Meanwhile in Italy, authorities have paid tribute to a firefighter who died tackling a wildfire in San Cataldo, as Sicily President Renato Schifani wished a "speedy recovery" to another involved in the same operation.

Italian Interior Minister Matteo Piantedosi paid tribute to late firefighter Alessandro Marchi who he said died "in service to the community".

"He will not be forgotten," he said in a post on X.

In an update on Wednesday, head of the Sicilian Civil Protection Salvo Cocina said there had been more than 350 fires on the island including ten major ones which led to precautionary evacuations.

He said the fires had human causes - adding that firefighting has become a social and public order problem.

Emergency service workers have been tackling the fire in Gironde overnight and into Thursday morning

Meanwhile, in France, the AFP news agency reported that 10,000 tourists were among those evacuated.

The Gironde region remains under a red alert, with some leisure sites in exposed forest areas to be closed on Thursday afternoon and evening.

Yassmina de Freitas, 19, who lives in the coastal town of Le Porge, said she noticed smoke and the smell of burning at lunchtime on Wednesday.

By the afternoon "ash… [and] half-burned tree bark" were falling, the sky was "very dark" and the smoke was getting "thicker and thicker".

At around 20:00 local time (19:00 BST), her family was evacuated by law enforcement and taken to a gymnasium in a neighbouring town. They had to leave two of their cats and four chickens behind as they could not be found in the flurry to leave.

She said she could not sleep at all and was waking up every hour to check the fire's progress.

"I could see the number of hectares burned rising steadily," she said.

Further evacuations took place on Thursday morning on the Lège-Cap-Ferret peninsula, south of Le Porge.

Smoke could be seen clouding the sky at Les Pastourelles beach in Claouey, Lège-Cap-Ferret on Thursday

BBC South presenter Jo Kent is on holiday with her family in France, staying at the Lesley Pastourelles campsite in the village of Claouey, next to Lège.

She said multiple people at the campsite who were very anxious about the situation had already left, and those staying have been warned to be prepared to evacuate.

The main road down to the south of the peninsula, which is normally filled with "nose to tail traffic", is empty and the supermarket is "unnervingly quiet", Kent added.

In the Var region in south-east France, evacuated residents were told they could go home to assess potential damage to properties on Thursday morning. However, it warned people to limit their movements in the area due to the risk of the fire re-igniting as the wind picks up through the afternoon.

Several French departments have been ravaged by wildfires during a series of unusual and prolonged heatwaves that have swept across Europe this summer.

Interior Minister Laurent Nuñez paid tribute to the two firefighters who died on Tuesday near Bordeaux airport. He posted photos from an event on Wednesday, saying the moment was filled with "immense emotion" as he expressed "infinite gratitude" and "unwavering support" to their families.

Madrid has also been impacted by fires, but overnight most evacuated residents were told they can return to their homes - with the exception of one area in Villa del Prado.

A fire nearby is still burning, and almost 90 personnel and a helicopter have been deployed to get the flames under control. Local authorities continued to warn of an "extreme risk of fires" on Thursday morning.

Spain's weather service AEMET said temperatures of up to 36C are forecast for the afternoon.

Countries across Europe have seen record temperatures in recent months.

A report released in April by the Copernicus Climate Change Service found at least 95% of Europe saw "above average" annual temperatures in 2025 and identified Europe as the fastest-warming continent.

Wildfire-burnt area and fire emissions both reached record levels last year, the report said.
