The NHS has not met a target to start urgent cancer treatment within 31 days of a patient and doctor agreeing a treatment plan since December 2020. It has not met a target to start treatment within two months of a hospital receiving a suspected urgent cancer referral since December 2015.

Guy Forster, the president of the Association of Personal Injury Lawyers, said: “Some surviving victims need additional care, therapies and specialist equipment if the delay in diagnosis means they now have mobility or continence issues, for example.

“Some will be out of work for a long time. For those who have lost loved ones as a result of delays, lives will be turned upside down, partners and their children may be without the practical, emotional and financial support on which they are reliant.”

The Department of Health and Social Care did not respond directly to the rising costs of payouts for botched diagnosis of cancer, but admitted that mistakes happen.

“Early and accurate diagnosis is key to effective cancer treatment. This hasn’t happened in enough cases, and this government is determined to address this,” a spokesperson said.

“But we are making progress. In the last year, 82,000 more people received a timely diagnosis for suspected cancer or were given the all-clear compared to the year before.

“Through Jess’s rule we are supporting GPs in taking a ‘fresh eyes’ approach to cancer detection, and our national cancer plan will give family doctors digital tools to spot and flag concerning symptoms or test results earlier,” they added.

title: US senator wants urgent answers from Barclays over Jes Staley’s ties to Jeffrey Epstein
excerpt:
Exclusive: Elizabeth Warren says it is unclear how bank failed to uncover former CEO’s links with sex offender

The leading US senator Elizabeth Warren has called for urgent answers from Barclays over...
body_text:
Exclusive: Elizabeth Warren says it is unclear how bank failed to uncover former CEO’s links with sex offender

The leading US senator Elizabeth Warren has called for urgent answers from Barclays over what she claimed was its “apparent failure to meaningfully investigate” ties between the bank’s former boss, Jes Staley, and the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

In a letter sent privately to Barclays’s chair, Nigel Higgins, and seen by the Guardian, Warren – the most senior Democrat on the US senate banking committee – said it was “deeply unclear how Barclays, supposedly investigating Staley’s connection to Epstein, failed to uncover this decades-long relationship”.

The letter, sent also on behalf of the Congress members Ro Khanna and Raja Krishnamoorthi, gave Barclays two weeks to answer a series of questions about how they handled information on the links between its former chief executive and Epstein, who died while awaiting trial on child sex trafficking charges in 2019.

The questions included whether the bank conducted any reviews into “deficiencies” in its executive hiring process, which had “allowed the board to hire a CEO who held extensive professional and personal ties to a convicted sex offender”.

The US lawmakers raised a string of concerns about information arising from a UK court hearing last year, in which Staley unsuccessfully tried to overturn the regulator’s decision to ban him from the British banking industry, four years after he was forced to step down as boss of Barclays.

Those concerns included Higgins’s admission that he had not asked Staley about his last contact with Epstein before Higgins’s declaration to the Financial Conduct Authority that the last contact had been “well before” the CEO joined Barclays.

“It appears that neither you nor any other member of the board conducted any deeper due diligence to verify Staley’s claims and simply took him at his word,” the letter said, referring to exchanges between the executive and board members during Staley’s tenure. “It is unclear what specific actions, if any, Barclays took to investigate Staley and Epstein’s personal ties.”

The US lawmakers said the episode sparked questions about how the bank was managed. “Barclays’s apparent failure to meaningfully investigate or address Staley’s relationship with Epstein raises significant governance questions regarding the bank’s ability to hold senior executives accountable for wrongdoing,” the letter said.

The signatories also warned that it was a “privilege” to hold local banking licences and operate in the US, where the bank had “extensive” operations and held about $200bn (£150bn) in assets. That privilege “is contingent on the ongoing character and fitness of management and the ability of the firm to conduct its operations in a safe and sound manner”.

The letter was sent hours before Staley was due to be grilled in a closed-doors hearing by the House Oversight Committee, on which the letter’s co-signatories, Khanna and Krishnamoorthi, both sit.

Staley originally met Epstein in 2000 after he became head of JP Morgan’s private bank, where Epstein was a client. He later took over as chief executive of Barclays in 2015, but was forced to step down in 2021, after City regulators launched an investigation into the nature of the relationship between the two men.

Staley was eventually banned from taking any job in the UK’s financial sector for playing down those ties, which Barclays vouched for in its letter to the Financial Conduct Authority in 2019.

In the former Barclays boss’s failed attempt last year to overturn his banking ban, the court battle revealed intimate details of the men’s friendship. Staley also lost out on £18m worth of pay and bonuses from Barclays as a result of the ruling.

A spokesperson for Barclays said: “The UK regulator concluded that Barclays was misled by Jes Staley. We conducted an investigation based on the information that was available to us at the time.

“Since then, new information has emerged that was not available to Barclays that demonstrates Mr Staley misled Barclays and many others as to the nature of the relationship with Epstein. That information relates to activities that predated his employment at Barclays.”

title: Houthis claim attack on oil tankers as US launches more strikes on Iran
excerpt:
The Iranian-backed Houthis say they have closed the Bab al-Mandab Strait, a key shipping waterway and the gateway to the Red Sea

Yemen's Houthis say they have attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Re...
body_text:
The Iranian-backed Houthis say they have closed the Bab al-Mandab Strait, a key shipping waterway and the gateway to the Red Sea

Yemen's Houthis say they have attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea as the US launched its 12th consecutive night of strikes on Iran.

Iranian state media said two people had been killed in the US strikes on Wednesday, and a tanker caught fire in an explosion in a mined route in the Strait of Hormuz.

Ahead of the latest round of strikes, US President Donald Trump had threatened to destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant if Iran attacked a ship in the strait and to "take care of" the Houthis if they blocked the Red Sea.

If confirmed, the Houthi attack would be the first since the Iran-backed group announced a "maritime embargo" against Saudi Arabia.

The Houthis named the tankers as the Encelia and the Layla, though neither their responsibility, nor the attack on the Layla has been confirmed.

The group's military spokesperson Yahya Saree said they had targeted the ships with drones and missiles for allegedly violating its naval blockade.

Saree added that the Houthis promised to continue their naval operations and would continue "enforcing the 'siege for a siege' equation".

The state-run Saudi Press Agency confirmed early on Thursday that the Encelia was struck "while sailing in the Red Sea", resulting in a fire on its bow.

Citing an unnamed official source at the Transport General Authority, it described the attack as a violation of international law. All crew were safe, it reported.

UK Maritime Trade Operation (UKMTO) reported an "unknown projectile" struck a Saudi tanker about 130km from Al Shuqaiq in Saudi Arabia at 20:00 GMT. The attack caused a fire but no casualties, it said.

British maritime risk management group Vanguard also reported that the Saudi tanker Encelia had been hit.

US Central Command (Centcom) said at 21:30 GMT it had launched strikes at Trump's direction "to further degrade Iran's ability to threaten civilian mariners and commercial vessels transiting regional waters".

The strikes targeted "Iranian military targets including maritime capabilities, missile and drone storage facilities, coastal surveillance sites, and air defense assets," it said.

Kuwait, Jordan and Bahrain soon reported responding to airborne threats, before Iran's military said they had targeted US assets in all three countries, according to AFP.

Iranian state media reported two people had been killed in US strikes on the country's south near its border with Iraq.

Meanwhile, the Iranian Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) said the Strait of Hormuz was "completely closed" after an explosion in the channel, Iranian state media reported.

The IRGC said it had stopped three oil tankers from passing through the strait.

One tanker caught fire and two others turned back after attempting to pass through a mined route south of the strait, according to Iranian state media.

It comes after Trump promised earlier on Wednesday to attack an Iranian bridge or power plant if its forces hit ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi later warned of an "eye for an eye" response to attacks on Iran.

On Tuesday, Trump told reporters he would "take care of the Houthis" should they obstruct the Red Sea as they had said.

Few details of the "maritime embargo" were given by the Houthis when it was announced on Monday, but a media official said they were closing the Bab al-Mandab Strait - the Red Sea's southern gateway - to Saudi vessels.

The Red Sea has become crucial for oil shipments from Saudi Arabia during the US-Israeli war with Iran, which has led to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz in the Gulf.

Now, the threat from the Houthis risks further disruption to international shipping, with hostilities in the region having already led to global fuel prices fluctuating significantly.

So far, at least seven oil tankers have made U-turns close to Yemen since the embargo. Saree claimed the Houthis had "forced approximately ten ships to retreat and return".

The disruption in the Red Sea comes as peace negotiations have effectively stalled between the US and Iran.

Both countries signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) in June to halt military operations and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, as well as to reach an agreement to end the war over the following 60 days.

But three weeks after it was signed, Trump declared the ceasefire "over" after Iranian attacks on ships in Hormuz, and US strikes in response.

Trump threatens to target Iran's bridges and power plants if Hormuz attacks persist

title: Watch: Missouri suspect dives headfirst off bridge in wild bid to evade deputies
excerpt: A Missouri suspect's desperate attempt to escape authorities by diving headfirst off a bridge into the water was captured on dramatic police video before deputies took him into custody. (Stone County ...
body_text:
A Missouri suspect's desperate attempt to escape authorities by diving headfirst off a bridge into the water was captured on dramatic police video before deputies took him into custody. (Stone County Sheriff's Office)

A 26-year-old Missouri man's dramatic attempt to outrun police by diving headfirst off a bridge was caught on video.

Video released by the Stone County Sheriff's Office shows Gavin Thor Lundgren, of Kimberling City, sprinting across a two-lane bridge before launching himself headfirst into the waters of Table Rock Lake after leading deputies on a chase during the early morning hours of July 18.

"This was definitely a first and luckily he survived the fall," the sheriff's office said in a statement. "He resisted arrest and deputies suffered minor injuries taking him into custody."

VIDEO SHOWS ALLEGED CAR THIEF DANGLE UNDER BRIDGE AFTER WILD CHASE, SPARKING TENSE STANDOFF WITH POLICE

Gavin Thor Lundgren is seen diving headfirst off a bridge into Table Rock Lake after allegedly leading Missouri deputies on a pursuit, according to the Stone County Sheriff's Office. (Stone County Sheriff's Office)

Lundgren was arrested and charged with three counts of third-degree assault on a special victim, resisting arrest for a felony, resisting arrest by fleeing and careless driving.

According to a probable cause statement, deputies attempted to stop Lundgren around 1:50 a.m. after spotting his 2004 Chevrolet Malibu traveling nearly 75 mph in a 45-mph zone, according to KOLR.

Instead of pulling over, authorities said Lundgren led deputies on a pursuit before abandoning his still-moving car and diving off the bridge into Table Rock Lake.

FLEEING WISCONSIN DRIVER GETS AIRBORNE AND JUMPS CAR OVER ANOTHER CAR DURING HIGH-SPEED CHASE

Gavin Thor Lundgren was arrested after authorities said he led deputies on a pursuit before diving off a bridge into Table Rock Lake. (Stone County Sheriff's Office)

Three deputies were injured while taking Lundgren into custody, authorities said.

Investigators said Lundgren headbutted two deputies during the struggle.

Gavin Thor Lundgren is seen running across a bridge moments before diving into Table Rock Lake during an alleged attempt to evade Missouri deputies. (Stone County Sheriff's Office)

Officials said deputies suffered injuries including facial bruising, cuts to their hands, elbows and forearms, and abrasions.

Lundgren is being held in the Stone County Jail without bond.

Michael Sinkewicz is a writer for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to michael.sinkewicz@fox.com

title: Caitlin Clark proves there's no NBA 2K27 cover curse as Fever set franchise scoring record
excerpt: Sports analyst Dan Dakich slams Indiana Fever head coach Stephanie White for her evasive response regarding a GOP letter to the WNBA. The letter addresses concerns about Caitlin Clark's treatment, wit...
body_text:
Sports analyst Dan Dakich slams Indiana Fever head coach Stephanie White for her evasive response regarding a GOP letter to the WNBA. The letter addresses concerns about Caitlin Clark's treatment, with reports suggesting some attacks are racially motivated. Dakich questions the coach's failure to defend her star player and the league's lack of support, despite Congress's involvement in the matter.

The Indiana Fever kept their momentum rolling Wednesday night, cruising to a third straight victory with a 123-88 rout of the Connecticut Sun. Just hours after being unveiled as an NBA 2K27 cover athlete, Caitlin Clark led the way with 27 points and 11 assists for another double-double.

Clark's teammate, Kelsey Mitchell, added 23 points and the Fever set a franchise record for points in a game.

Aliyah Boston, the top pick in the 2023 WNBA Draft, scored 22 points for Indiana.

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

Indiana Fever guard Caitlin Clark (22) is introduced before the game against the Connecticut Sun on July 22, 2026, at Gainbridge Fieldhouse in Indianapolis, Indiana. (A.J. Mast/NBAE via Getty Images)

The Fever also had the biggest half in franchise history when they led 70-46 at the break. It was the third-highest scoring game in league history without overtime.

Monique Billings and Tyasha Harris added 12 points apiece for Indiana, while the Fever connected on 11 of 22 attempts from beyond the arc.

LIBERTY'S SABRINA IONESCU ADDRESSES ABSENCE FROM WNBA 3-POINT CONTEST: 'NEVER OFFICIALLY GOT INVITED'

Clark headed to the locker room with 21 points at the half.

Indiana Fever forward Monique Billings (25) drives to the basket against the Connecticut Sun on July 22, 2026, at Gainbridge Fieldhouse in Indianapolis, Indiana. (A.J. Mast/NBAE via Getty Images)

The Fever will now turn their attention to All-Star weekend, with Clark, Boston and Kelsey Mitchell set to represent Indiana in Chicago.

Indiana Fever guard Caitlin Clark (22) shoots the ball against the Connecticut Sun at Gainbridge Fieldhouse in Indianapolis, Indiana, on July 22, 2026. (Andy Lyons/Getty Images)

Wednesday's win improved Indiana's record to 17-10, and the Fever will enter the All-Star break atop the Eastern Conference standings, just a half-game ahead of Angel Reese and the Atlanta Dream.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

Chantz Martin is a sports writer for Fox News Digital.

title: Armed attackers kill more than 20 in northwest Nigeria
excerpt:
At least 20 people have been killed in northwest Nigeria after armed gang members attacked villages in Zamfara State, according to the AFP news agency.

“Today, between 3pm [14:00 GMT] and 4pm [15:00 ...
body_text:
At least 20 people have been killed in northwest Nigeria after armed gang members attacked villages in Zamfara State, according to the AFP news agency.

“Today, between 3pm [14:00 GMT] and 4pm [15:00 GMT], a large number of bandits have invaded our communities, targeting all the people they meet,” Nasiru Lauwali, the representative of the Sauna district in the local government zone of Talata Mafara, told AFP on Wednesday.

The attacks come amid increasing violence in northwest and central Nigeria, where armed gangs or bandits have raided villages in recent weeks. They are accused of stealing livestock and kidnapping residents for ransom.

Late on Tuesday, at least three suspected bandits were killed by security forces in the country’s Kwara State, according to Nigeria’s The Punch news website. The news service said that security forces managed to rescue a businessman, Tuhkur Sanni.

Kidnappings have become common in Nigeria, as armed groups seek large ransoms from the government and citizens.

The situation has been worsened by a security crisis, partly fuelled by the Boko Haram rebellion in the country’s northeast. In 2024, attackers earned more than $1.6m in ransom payments, according to SBM Intelligence.

Dozens of students and teachers were rescued earlier this month, 56 days after they were kidnapped from three schools in the southwestern state of Oyo.

Eight of the suspected kidnappers were arrested and an unspecified number were killed, according to a statement issued by President Bola Tinubu.

Defence Minister Christopher Musa said the suspects planned to use the hostages as leverage to pressure the government to release some of their commanders from prison.

title: In Gaza, a long path to recovery for one woman who lost eye, and daughter
excerpt: Nuseirat, Gaza Strip – When Najwa Abu Atiwi opened her eyes on October 11, 2024, after weeks in a coma, her first thought was not the extent of her injuries, but her desire to see herself. She knew th...
body_text:
Nuseirat, Gaza Strip – When Najwa Abu Atiwi opened her eyes on October 11, 2024, after weeks in a coma, her first thought was not the extent of her injuries, but her desire to see herself. She knew that something significant had changed, but she did not understand how much her appearance had been altered after the Israeli air strike that hit her home in central Gaza’s Nuseirat camp.

Najwa, now 42 years old, repeatedly asked those around her to let her see her face, but they delayed the moment, fearing the shock it would cause.

“I kept asking to see what I looked like, but they would give me painkillers and medication to calm me down,” Najwa says.

Later, doctors told her the truth: She had lost one of her eyes, and they were still trying to save the other, which had suffered internal bleeding.

When she finally looked in the mirror for the first time, it was almost as if someone else was staring back. Her face, marked by severe injuries and fractures, had changed dramatically.

“I looked at myself and did not recognise who I was. I felt as if I was looking at someone else, and I said: ‘Who is this?’” she recalls.

The trauma would only compound later, when Najwa learned that her daughter, Hajar, had also been killed in the air strike that put Najwa in hospital. Hajar had been only days away from celebrating her sixth birthday.

But for a month after she woke up from her coma, Najwa did not know about the tragic loss. During her time in intensive care, she dreamed of Hajar and repeatedly asked people around her to reassure her that her daughter was safe. She later learned the devastating truth, never having had the chance to see her daughter one last time or say goodbye, a loss that remains alongside the physical pain of her injuries.

Najwa has faced a long path of surgeries, treatment, and rehabilitation. Metal plates and meshes were placed in her face, and she continues to struggle to open her jaw, speak, and eat.

“I still cannot eat normally. My diet is limited to soups, water, juices, and milk,” Najwa says. She is still waiting for the opportunity to travel for specialised treatment after doctors told her that some of the procedures she needs are unavailable inside Gaza – a result of years of Israeli blockade and the destruction wrought by Israel’s genocidal war on Gaza, which has killed more than 73,000 people.

Dr Ayman Saidam, a consultant in oculoplastic surgery, says Najwa’s case is among the most complex injuries he has treated, involving severe trauma to the eye, face, and jaw.

When she first arrived for treatment, Najwa had suffered injury to her right eye that resulted in its loss, in addition to facial bone fractures and torn eyelids. Her other eye was also injured around the socket, which impaired her ability to fully close her eyelid, creating an additional risk to her vision.

Saidam explains that treating such injuries cannot begin with cosmetic procedures alone, because the primary issue is the damaged facial structure.

“Eye cosmetic surgery in cases like these must first be built on maxillofacial reconstruction [restoring the face, jaw, neck, and mouth], because cosmetic procedures alone cannot correct structural deformities,” he says.

During Israel’s war, Saidam treated many injuries caused by shrapnel and explosions, including orbital fractures (broken bones around the eye), eyelid tears, loss of facial tissue, and injuries that resulted in eye loss or required complex reconstructive surgeries.

He explains that some patients require multiple stages of treatment before receiving an artificial eye or restoring part of their previous appearance. Facial reconstruction is not a single procedure, but rather a series of interventions in which each stage depends on the success of the previous one.

“In Gaza, there are skilled doctors with the knowledge and experience needed, but the main problem is the lack of resources, equipment, and medical supplies,” Saidam says.

He explains that shortages of certain materials used in surgeries, including some screws and eye drops, can delay treatment stages and slow the healing process. Many of these shortages are the result of Israeli restrictions on imports into Gaza, which have continued despite an October “ceasefire” that Israel regularly violates.

For Najwa, the treatment journey is still ongoing. She has received a referral for treatment abroad but continues to wait for permission to leave Gaza, while living every day with pain, difficulty moving, and challenges reaching hospitals.

Hers is not an isolated case. Israel’s war has left thousands of people in Gaza with complex injuries that require long-term treatment and rehabilitation.

According to the World Health Organization, about a quarter of recorded injuries in Gaza are classified as life-changing. More than 50,000 injuries require long-term rehabilitation services. They include limb loss, severe burns, and other complex trauma caused by bombardment and explosions.

Najwa is not the only patient who required psychological support after her injury. Many people who suffered facial disfigurement caused by bombardment, including severe burns, facial fractures, loss of an eye, or shrapnel injuries, face psychological struggles that can be as painful as their physical wounds.

Dr Hassan Abu Shawish, a psychologist at the Deir el-Balah Mental Health Clinic who treated Najwa, says her case was among the most difficult because several traumatic experiences occurred at the same time: her physical injury, the loss of her daughter, and the dramatic change in her facial appearance.

He explains that such injuries can lead to post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), anxiety disorders, depression, and body image disturbance, in which patients struggle to accept their new appearance. This can lead some survivors to withdraw from social life or avoid interactions with others.

Psychological care, he says, is an essential part of recovery alongside reconstructive surgeries, helping patients adapt to physical changes, rebuild their confidence, and return to society.

Before her injury, Najwa lived a completely different life. She worked, went out, and was surrounded by family and social connections. She is now trying to rebuild her life while carrying the pain of losing her daughter and coping with the profound changes to her face and body.

The loss of Hajar remains one of the deepest wounds Najwa carries. She describes her daughter as a joyful, intelligent, and hardworking child, and says she looked like her mother, a resemblance that makes her absence even more painful.

Najwa says she sees Hajar in her dreams constantly. “In my mind, my daughter is travelling and will return,” she says. “Every day I say: When will she come back?”

Najwa is trying to put the pain of her loss to one side. Her journey since the air strike has been a long one – reclaiming what can still be recovered in her life: her face, her ability to speak and eat, her confidence, and her connection with the world around her.

As she waits for the chance to travel and complete her treatment, she holds on to the hope that she will one day be able to regain some more of what the war has taken from her.

“I want to return to my previous life and regain the beautiful personality I used to know in myself,” she says.

title: Boston Red Sox 15-game winning streak finally comes to an end, one short of setting franchise record
excerpt:
The Boston Red Sox are one win away from tying the longest winning streak in franchise history at 15 games, a record set in 1946.

Just a few weeks ago, the Boston Red Sox were one of Major League Bas...
body_text:
The Boston Red Sox are one win away from tying the longest winning streak in franchise history at 15 games, a record set in 1946.

Just a few weeks ago, the Boston Red Sox were one of Major League Baseball's biggest disappointments.

Despite their hefty payroll and a roster filled with stars and exciting young prospects, injuries had ravaged much of the team's depth through the first half of the season. Entering July, the Red Sox were dead last in the American League East at 37-48. They had the third-worst record in the AL as a whole, were 14 games out of first place and seemed posed to become sellers at the trade deadline. As recently as June 21, they had just an 8.4% chance of reaching the postseason, per FanGraphs.

RED SOX COME BACK FROM THE DEAD, MOVE INTO POSTSEASON CONTENTION WITH REMARKABLE 14-GAME WIN STREAK

Boston Red Sox's Wilyer Abreu celebrates after his RBI double in the third inning of a game against the Baltimore Orioles on Monday, July 20, 2026, in Boston. (Charles Krupa/AP)

Even without key players like Garrett Crochet, Trevor Story and Roman Anthony, the Red Sox entered a day-night doubleheader against the Baltimore Orioles on Wednesday having won 14 games in a row. Sure enough, they jumped out to a four-run lead they never relinquished, with the game fittingly ending on a great defensive play by shortstop Tsung-Che Chung.

That 15th consecutive win tied a franchise record set 80 years ago, and became just the eighth winning streak of 15 or more since 1961. And the first since the St. Louis Cardinals in 2021.

"You keep stacking one win over another, and then you get close, and once you get within three or four, they start to feel like playoff games because you want to get there," infielder Caleb Durbin this week, per MLB.com. "But yeah, then you look back at it, and think about where you're doing it and how long this franchise has existed. It's pretty incredible."

RED SOX HAD A NIGHTMARE TRAVEL DAY FOR THE AGES, YET STILL FOUND A WAY TO BEAT THE METS IN NEW YORK

Boston Red Sox starting pitcher Eduardo Rivera sits in the dugout after being pulled from the game in the first inning during the second baseball game of a doubleheader against the Baltimore Orioles, Wednesday, July 22, 2026, in Boston. (AP Photo/Charles Krupa)

Wednesday night's game provided a golden opportunity to set a new franchise record with 16 in a row. Still at Fenway Park and facing a struggling Orioles team. But the Orioles turned the tables, scoring four runs of their own in the top of the first. Jonathan Rodriguez's double off the Green Monster helped force Eduardo Rivera from the game in the first inning.

The Red Sox couldn't get anything going offensively though, falling 5-1 and coming up just short of even more history.

Still, what an incredible achievement. It not only turned their season around but also upended the American League East and postseason races. They went from being buried and seemingly out of the playoff picture before July to not losing a game from July 1 through July 22.

Before the loss in the night game, Boston's postseason probability had soared to 67% thanks to taking over sole possession of the third wild card spot in the AL. Remarkably, after the win in the day game, they moved to within six games of first in the AL East. Though the Rays win and Boston's loss made it seven entering Thursday.

Crochet is hoping to return by the playoffs, but even with Ranger Suárez returning soon, Boston would love to acquire more starting pitching. Roman Anthony has yet to start swinging after tearing a ligament in his finger, and Trevor Story is still a ways away, meaning Boston could use offense too.

OUTKICK IS NOW ON THE FOX APP: CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD

All that raises the question, how have they done so much winning with all the injuries? A combination of timely hitting, quality pitching and, of course, luck. Boston has the best team ERA in baseball in July, and an .877 OPS with runners in scoring position over the same timeframe. That'll work.

Boston Red Sox fans cheer after an RBI double by Ceddanne Rafaela during the seventh inning of a game against the Baltimore Orioles on Monday, July 20, 2026, in Boston. (Charles Krupa/AP)

15 games in a row is remarkable, especially with those injuries. And Red Sox fans can now dream of getting Anthony, Story and Suarez back for a playoff run. With Crochet looming if they get there. Not too shabby, considering where they were entering July.

title: Piker tells Mamdani to ‘shut the f--- up’ over cautious messaging, claims mayor needs to be more 'bold'
excerpt: Far-left Twitch streamer Hasan Piker at the College Democrats of America Summer Convention passionately advocates for a left-wing takeover of the Democratic Party, denouncing 'compromising' members. M...
body_text:
Far-left Twitch streamer Hasan Piker at the College Democrats of America Summer Convention passionately advocates for a left-wing takeover of the Democratic Party, denouncing 'compromising' members. Minnesota Senate candidate Peggy Flanagan further fuels the socialist revolt, openly calling for the dismantling of ICE. The segment highlights growing divisions within the Democratic establishment.

Far-left streamer Hasan Piker told New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani to "shut the f--- up" and urged progressives to present their policies more boldly during an interview released Wednesday on Zeteo's "We're Not Kidding" podcast in Washington, D.C.

"I just listened to his new New York Times conversation where The New York Times asked him, like, ‘If you had unlimited money, what would you do?’" Piker said. "He’s like, ‘Exactly the same policies I’m implementing.’ I’m like, ‘Shut the f--- up, dude.’"

Piker's criticism came during a broader discussion with Zeteo founder Mehdi Hasan about whether left-wing politicians are too concerned with optics and too willing to retreat when their positions draw criticism.

Hasan Piker urged New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani to be more "bold, brave and unapologetic" when presenting his progressive agenda. (YouTube/@uhhsid)

MAMDANI CALLS OUT DEMOCRATIC PARTY AS 'WELL-PAID CONSULTANTS AND BIG MONEY INTERESTS'

Piker said politicians, like Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., risk appearing uncommitted to their positions when they sound hesitant or defensive.

"If you come across as, like, even a little worried or even a little defensive, then people respond to that with, ‘Oh, maybe she doesn’t believe this either,’" Piker said.

Piker told Mamdani to "shut the f--- up" while criticizing what he viewed as the mayor’s overly cautious political messaging. (Sam Barnes/Web Summit via Sportsfile via Getty Images)

AOC SLAMS ‘OPPRESSED’ TRUMP STATES WHILE TOUTING NYC SERVICES DESPITE $5B DEFICIT AND TRANSIT CRIME SPIKE

The Twitch streamer continued that line of criticism of Ocasio-Cortez, saying she's embraced ambitious policies but sometimes focuses too heavily on public perception.

"The way she carries herself, I think, is a little bit too, like, optics-driven, too liberal and too optics-driven," Piker said.

Fox News Digital reached out to Mamdani and Ocasio-Cortez for comment, but did not immediately hear back.

Piker said he still supports Mamdani but argued the mayor should communicate his policies with greater confidence and less concern for optics. (Florencia Tan Jun/Web Summit via Sportsfile via Getty Images)

FLASHBACK: MAMDANI'S HISTORY OF DODGING QUESTIONS ABOUT WHERE HE STANDS ON KEY ISSUES

Piker contrasted Ocasio-Cortez with Mamdani, whom he praised for combining progressive policies with language capable of reaching liberal and less traditional audiences.

"I think Zohran has done a pretty good job of merging those two worlds where he speaks the language of liberalism," Piker said.

Hasan asked whether Piker wanted the interview to end with him telling one of his political allies to "shut the f--- up." Piker stressed that his frustration did not represent a break with Mamdani.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

"I mean, come on," Piker said. "I love the guy. I have my disagreements with him from time to time."

Piker later accepted Hasan’s revised description of the approach he wanted from progressive politicians.

"Bold, brave and unapologetic," Piker said.

CJ Womack is an associate editor at Fox News.

CJ joined Fox News Digital's team in 2026, which highlights the vital role journalism plays in shaping politics and culture. He has years of experience analyzing and reporting on the news media.

CJ graduated from Long Beach State University in 2025 with a Bachelor of Arts in Political Science and a minor in Journalism.

You can send news tips to cj.womack@fox.com and follow CJ on Twitter.

title: Liberty's Sabrina Ionescu addresses absence from WNBA 3-point contest: 'Never officially got invited'
excerpt: Sage Steele discusses Caitlin Clark's frustration with inconsistent WNBA officiating after a non-call incident involving Clark. Steele criticizes the league for mishandling Clark, referencing a series...
body_text:
Sage Steele discusses Caitlin Clark's frustration with inconsistent WNBA officiating after a non-call incident involving Clark. Steele criticizes the league for mishandling Clark, referencing a series of past controversial moments, arguing the WNBA risks losing its growing fanbase by not protecting its star.

The lineup for this year’s WNBA All-Star 3-point contest has been a talking point in the sports world since it was announced.

New York Liberty guard Sabrina Ionescu was among the most notable players absent from the annual competition.

Ionescu addressed the speculation ahead of Wednesday’s Liberty-Sky matchup, pushing back against reports that she chose not to participate in this weekend’s WNBA 3-point contest in Chicago.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

"I can't decline an offer I never got officially invited to. So, was pretty surprised to see that. Never got invited," the defending 3-point contest champion told reporters pregame.

Caitlin Clark and Kayla McBride were also among the high-profile stars who reportedly declined invitations to the competition.

New York Liberty guard Sabrina Ionescu competing in the Kia WNBA Skills Challenge and WNBA STARRY 3-Point Contest on July 18, 2025, at Gainbridge Fieldhouse in Indianapolis. (Mike Lawrence/NBAE via Getty Images)

"Whether it's against Steph Curry or in the W, I always go out and try and beat my record, every single time I've gone out," said Ionescu, who has won the 3-point contest twice, highlighted by her record-setting 37-point performance in 2023.

"I just kind of wanted to set the record straight," she continued. "Like that was just false, but I'm really excited for the participants in it, and obviously there's going to be a new winner. So, I'm excited to see who wins."

Ionescu later referenced her previous 3-point showdown with Stephen Curry when asked a follow-up about the issue.

"Maybe I'll do it with Steph again at NBA All-Star and get a really nice official invite," she said.

New York Liberty guard Sabrina Ionescu celebrates after winning the WNBA Starry 3-Point Contest July 18, 2025, at Gainbridge Fieldhouse in Indianapolis. (A.J. Mast/NBAE via Getty Images)

The confirmed list of 3-point contest participants includes Dallas' Azzi Fudd, Atlanta's Rhyne Howard, Golden State's Janelle Salaün, Portland's Bridget Carleton, Toronto's Marina Mabrey and Seattle's Natisha Hiedeman. Howard and Mabrey are the only players in that group with prior 3-point contest experience at All-Star weekend.

Earlier this week, Ionescu refuted a report that she had opted out of this year’s festivities, commenting "lies" under a since-deleted Bleacher Report Instagram post. McBride also weighed in via social media, saying her agent had texted her about the event in the 48 hours before her Instagram story post, but no formal invitation ever came.

"Posting things like I'm afraid of shooting smoke is weird and if you didn't hear it from me prob shouldn't believe it," McBride wrote. "We should focus on the great shooters in it already!!! Because there's some killas in there."

New York Liberty guard Sabrina Ionescu competes in the WNBA STARRY 3-Point Contest during the 2025 WNBA All-Star weekend at Gainbridge Fieldhouse in Indianapolis July 18, 2025. (Steph Chambers/Getty Images)

Despite dealing with an ankle injury early this season, Ionescu entered Wednesday night’s game averaging 13.8 points in 14 games.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

Chantz Martin is a sports writer for Fox News Digital.

title: Why Abderrahim Fakir’s death reignited Italy’s police accountability debate
excerpt: Bologna, Italy – Six minutes and 56 seconds. That is the length of the video capturing the final moments of Abderrahim Fakir, a 42-year-old Moroccan-born man who had lived in Bologna, northern Italy, ...
body_text:
Bologna, Italy – Six minutes and 56 seconds. That is the length of the video capturing the final moments of Abderrahim Fakir, a 42-year-old Moroccan-born man who had lived in Bologna, northern Italy, for more than three decades.

Filmed shortly after 12:30pm local time (10:30 GMT) on Sunday from the balcony of a nearby apartment block, the footage shows two Italian police officers and a civilian restraining Fakir on the ground. He repeatedly screams for help. Four Italian Red Cross volunteers, who arrived with an ambulance, stand nearby.

For more than four minutes, Fakir cries out. Then he falls silent. His ankles are secured with a black plastic zip tie. Only after the restraint ends do paramedics realise Fakir has lost consciousness.

“I ask you to listen very carefully to that video,” says Fabio Anselmo, the lawyer representing Fakir’s family, during a July 21 news conference in the Italian Senate hosted by Senator Ilaria Cucchi.

Cucchi’s brother, Stefano, died in police custody in 2009. Anselmo represented the family during the legal battle that led to the conviction of several officers, making the case a symbol of Italy’s wider struggle over police accountability.

“When the officers stand up, Fakir may still be alive,” Anselmo says. “Yet no one attempts to help him. No one removes the restraints.”

“I have spent almost 30 years dealing with cases like this,” he adds. “The European Court of Human Rights has already condemned Italy.”

Anselmo was referring to a January 2026 ruling by the European Court of Human Rights in the case of Riccardo Magherini, who died in Florence in 2014 after police restrained him face down.

The court found that Italy had violated Article 2 of the European Convention on Human Rights because the authorities had failed to show that maintaining him in that position was “absolutely necessary”.

Hours after Fakir’s death, Bologna police said officers had responded to reports of “a person behaving violently”. They said emergency services had been called immediately and that Fakir died after “restraint procedures” were carried out in the presence of medical personnel.

Prosecutors ordered a post-mortem examination, while Bologna’s local health authority opened an internal investigation.

The footage spread rapidly across social media. Within hours, dozens gathered at the site where Fakir died. Later that afternoon, during a spontaneous vigil, his sister held up a photograph of her brother as she spoke to reporters.

“My brother wasn’t an animal. He was a father, a worker, a good man. Why did they do this to him?” she said through tears. “I want everyone to see his face. I want people to know who he was. We don’t want revenge. We want justice.”

Friends and lawyers who knew Fakir said his final months were marked by fear and financial pressure.

Barbara Spinelli, who had represented Fakir on immigration matters for years, told Al Jazeera that he had struggled with severe financial difficulties.

“He started a small removals and logistics business, but after several clients failed to pay for completed work, he was forced to lay off employees and fell into debt,” she said.

According to Spinelli, Fakir had grown increasingly anxious about Italy’s tougher migration debate, including discussions around deportations and the implementation of the European Union’s Pact on Migration and Asylum.

The pact, adopted in 2024 and due to be fully implemented from 2026, introduced new rules on migration management, asylum procedures and returns of people without legal status.

She said Fakir feared he could be expelled from Italy, although there were no legal grounds at the time for such action against him. It remains unclear whether those fears had any connection to the events that led to his encounter with police on July 19.

“I kept reassuring him there were no legal grounds for that to happen,” she told Al Jazeera. “But that fear never left him.”

On the evening of July 20, Bologna Mayor Matteo Lepore called for a solidarity rally in Piazza Maggiore in support of Fakir’s family. Thousands gathered, including trade unionists, students and civil rights organisations.

What began as a peaceful demonstration descended into confrontation when part of the crowd marched towards the nearby Prefecture, the local office representing Italy’s central government, and the city’s police headquarters.

Clashes broke out after protesters reached a line of riot police.

Demonstrators threw bottles and fireworks, smashed shop windows and cash machines, and set two cars alight. Police used baton charges, water cannon and tear gas.

Several demonstrators later accused the police of using excessive force.

Jacopo, who asked to be identified only by his first name, told Al Jazeera that officers tackled him from behind and threw him to the ground.

“I covered my face with my hands, and they started hitting me with batons and punching me,” he said.

With his hands raised, he said he told officers: “I’m doing what you’re telling me. You’re hurting me.”

“I don’t care. Shut up,” one officer allegedly replied, as the beating continued.

Jacopo was later taken to hospital, where doctors diagnosed multiple bruises and an eye injury.

A freelance journalist working for L’Espresso, an Italian weekly news magazine, told Al Jazeera that officers beat him while he retreated from the clashes.

Video published by the magazine shows him shouting, “I’m a journalist! I’m a journalist!” as an officer repeatedly struck him with a baton. He said the assault ended only after he managed to escape.

Police said 64 officers were injured during the unrest and that Bologna’s historic centre suffered extensive damage.

Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni condemned the violence, calling the clashes “unacceptable”. She said Fakir’s death “must be fully established, without prejudice and without discounts for anyone”, while adding that “nothing can justify violence against law enforcement officers”.

Opposition parties called on Interior Minister Matteo Piantedosi to report to parliament on the circumstances surrounding Fakir’s death and release any body-camera footage recorded by the officers involved.

The investigation will be the first conducted under Italy’s new Article 45-bis procedure, introduced by the Meloni government’s Security Decree, which came into force in February 2026.

Under the procedure, police officers are not immediately entered into the register of criminal suspects when alleged offences are committed in the course of their duties. Opposition parties, legal scholars and human rights organisations say the change could make it harder to hold officers to account.

Anselmo called the reform “a legal monstrosity”.

“It wasn’t designed to strengthen the rule of law,” he said. “It was designed to satisfy part of public opinion.”

title: Bear caught on camera perched atop New Mexico utility pole before reportedly suffering fatal electrocution
excerpt:
The unusual encounter caught on video Monday, July 20, 2026, drew attention online before the bear died after being electrocuted, state wildlife officials said.

A bear that climbed to the top of a ut...
body_text:
The unusual encounter caught on video Monday, July 20, 2026, drew attention online before the bear died after being electrocuted, state wildlife officials said.

A bear that climbed to the top of a utility pole in rural New Mexico became the subject of a viral video this week before reportedly later dying after the unusual encounter.

The video, recorded Monday near Gladstone, New Mexico, roughly 170 miles east of Santa Fe, shows the bear precariously perched on the crossarm of the utility pole while panting and trying to maintain its balance high above the ground.

The U.S. Department of the Interior shared the video Wednesday, joking, "We appreciate the enthusiasm for American Energy Dominance, but this isn't what we meant."

WILDLIFE PHOTOGRAPHER STAYS ICE-COLD AS MASSIVE BROWN BEAR WALKS RIGHT UP TO HIS CAMERA

A bear perches atop a utility pole near Gladstone, N.M., after climbing the pole in a bizarre encounter captured on video. (Shannon Mullens via Storyful)

The Department of the Interior and the New Mexico Department of Wildlife did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital’s request for comment.

The footage, verified by Storyful, also includes audio from a 911 caller reporting the unusual sight.

"I'm calling to report a bear on top of a light pole," the caller tells a dispatcher.

WOMAN STALKED BY CHARGING BEAR ON MORNING DOG WALK CAPTURES TERRIFYING ENCOUNTER ON CAMERA

A bear balances atop a utility pole near Gladstone, N.M., in video captured by a passing motorist before the unusual encounter drew widespread attention online. (Shannon Mullens via Storyful)

The dispatcher responded that state officials had already been notified but said tranquilizing the animal while it remained atop the pole could have caused it to fall.

Shannon Mullens, who recorded the video, told Storyful she was traveling from Red River, New Mexico, to Oklahoma on Highway 56 when she and her family spotted what they initially believed was a dead bear.

"We really didn't believe what we saw at first, so we turned around to get another look," Mullens said.

HIDDEN DANGER AT POPULAR US NATIONAL PARK FORCES TRAIL CLOSURES AS BEAR SIGHTINGS CLIMB

The bear hangs from the crossarm of a utility pole in rural New Mexico during the unusual incident that later drew widespread attention online. (Shannon Mullens via Storyful)

"At first, we thought that the bear was dead, until we got closer to it, and then we realized that he was still alive."

The bear was electrocuted and died before wildlife officials could safely rescue it, the New York Post reported.

Wildlife experts say bears can climb utility poles after becoming startled by people, traffic or dogs, treating them much like trees while trying to escape danger.

A similar incident ended differently in Arizona in 2021, when wildlife officials safely rescued a bear that had climbed a utility pole.

Brittany Miller is a Fox News Digital breaking news writer and former Independent reporter who graduated from Syracuse University in 2023.

title: Teachers who once dated and share child arrested after classroom argument spiraled into violence: police
excerpt:
Fox News Flash top headlines are here. Check out what's clicking on FoxNews.com.

A personal dispute between two teachers who were formerly a couple erupted inside a Miami charter boarding school, end...
body_text:
Fox News Flash top headlines are here. Check out what's clicking on FoxNews.com.

A personal dispute between two teachers who were formerly a couple erupted inside a Miami charter boarding school, ending with both under arrest after investigators say a classroom meeting turned violent.

According to a Miami-Dade Schools Police arrest affidavit obtained by Fox News Digital, Terrod Tiyan Torrence, 36, and Odiria Biggins were previously involved in a romantic relationship and share a child together.

A few days before the confrontation, Biggins told investigators Torrence left a handwritten note on her desk asking if they could talk.

She agreed to meet him July 17 inside Classroom 119 at The SEED School of Miami, the affidavit said.

SJSU VOLLEYBALL COACH'S ALLEGED ASSAULT VICTIM SPEAKS OUT FOR THE FIRST TIME SINCE ALLEGATIONS SURFACED

Terrod Torrence was charged with domestic violence after investigators say a confrontation with a fellow teacher at The SEED School of Miami turned physical. (Miami-Dade County Corrections and Rehabilitation)

What began as a conversation quickly turned physical, according to investigators.

Biggins told police Torrence repeatedly punched her in the left side of the head before slamming her head into a wall, causing her to lose consciousness and collapse to the floor.

MARCELLUS WILEY'S WIFE CLAIMS EX-NFL STAR THREATENED TO KILL HER BEFORE FOURTH OF JULY DOMESTIC BATTERY ARREST

But the confrontation didn't end there, investigators said.

After regaining consciousness, Biggins allegedly walked to her vehicle, grabbed a canister of pepper spray and later tracked Torrence down elsewhere on campus before spraying him, according to Miami ABC affiliate WSVN.

Surveillance video captured Biggins entering a classroom where Torrence was, the outlet reported.

BRING THE BEAR SPRAY TO THE WEDDING — YOU NEVER KNOW WHEN YOU NEED TO USE IT ON A FAMILY MEMBER

A courtyard at The SEED School of Miami, where investigators say an argument between two teachers escalated into violence. (The SEED School of Miami Facebook)

Moments later, both emerged with Torrence appearing to struggle walking and suffering visible eye irritation before school staff rushed to help him. He was later taken to a hospital for treatment.

Biggins was arrested Monday and charged with discharging a weapon on school property for allegedly using pepper spray on campus, along with misdemeanor domestic violence battery, according to WSVN. A judge found probable cause and set her bond at $2,000.

Torrence was arrested Tuesday by Miami-Dade Schools Police and charged with misdemeanor domestic violence battery.

The Miami-Dade Turner Guilford Knight Correctional Center (Google Maps)

Court records show he was booked into the Turner Guilford Knight Correctional Center before appearing before Judge Alicia Garcia Priovolos, who imposed a $2,000 standard bond and a pretrial no-contact order barring him from contacting Biggins. His arraignment is scheduled for Sept. 23.

It remains unclear what sparked the argument or whether any students witnessed the confrontation.

Fox News Digital has reached out to the Miami-Dade Schools Police Department and The SEED School of Miami for additional information, including whether either teacher remains employed.

Stepheny Price is a Writer at Fox News with a focus on West Coast and Midwest news, missing persons, national and international crime stories, homicide cases, and border security.

title: Police officer fatally shoots man in Madison, Wisconsin, sparking protests
excerpt:
Shooting happened on street in popular neighborhood lined with restaurants, bars, shops and homes

A police officer in Madison, Wisconsin, shot and killed a man Wednesday in an incident that was captu...
body_text:
Shooting happened on street in popular neighborhood lined with restaurants, bars, shops and homes

A police officer in Madison, Wisconsin, shot and killed a man Wednesday in an incident that was captured on cellphone footage, sparking local protests.

John Patterson, the Madison police chief, acknowledged the shooting at a news conference, but stressed footage circulating was just one perspective of what happened.

“I want to take a moment to honestly and sincerely offer my heartfelt sympathies to his family for this loss,” Patterson said. The dead man was in his 30s, Patterson said, but police did not release his name.

The shooting happened on a city street in a popular neighborhood lined with restaurants, bars, shops and residential homes less than 2 miles from the state capitol. Footage of the incident shows numerous cars stopped in the intersection with people watching as it unfolded.

“Another Black man is dead on a Madison street, and our community watched him die before we even knew his name,” said Brandi Grayson, the head of Urban Triage, a Madison-based Black community advocacy group. “That is the cruelty of state violence.”

A crowd formed near where the shooting occurred on Wednesday with people chanting: “Arrest the cops!”

Police had been responding to calls that someone was attempting to enter parked vehicles in the neighborhood, Patterson said. The suspect fled on a bike through nearby backyards in an attempt to get away before officers contacted him in the street, Patterson said.

The suspect either fell off his bike or was taken off his bike by officers and during the struggle pulled out a knife and injured an officer, Patterson said.

In one of the videos circulating online, three officers are seen holding the man down and attempting to arrest him. The officers lose control of the suspect, who frees his arms and attempts to get up off his knees. One officer appears to reach for his pistol, when a female officer runs toward the others shouting: “Taser! Taser!” That officer did not draw his firearm.

After some jostling, the suspect falls to the ground. As he goes down, another officer rises to his feet, pulls out his pistol, leans down toward the man on the ground and fires three shots at close range.

The officer who fired the shots then re-holsters his gun and places the suspect’s arm behind his back, before standing up and walking away. Two other officers push the suspect over onto his belly, before others arrive to attempt to offer medical attention.

The officer who fired the lethal shots had been injured by the suspect with a knife, Patterson said. He described the officer as a veteran of the police force, but didn’t provide his name.

Another officer was injured, but it’s not clear how, Patterson said. The other two officers were uninjured.

The race of the officer who fired his gun on Wednesday and that of the man killed were not released by police. But advocates for the Black community in Madison said the man killed was Black.

Sabrina Madison, the city council president, called the videos “both disturbing and painful”, in a statement. “I’ve received several calls from alders with questions and what I can say without a doubt is that I expect a full, comprehensive, and transparent independent investigation into today’s shooting of a community member,” the statement reads.

The Wisconsin department of justice criminal investigation will investigate the shooting because it involves police officers.

A white police officer shot and killed Tony Robinson, who was biracial, on the same street in 2015, a shooting that spurred large protests. The district attorney did not file charges against police in that incident, determining the officer had been justified.

title: Spain and France battle raging wildfires, face high risk of more
excerpt: Spain and France are at high risk of more wildfires in coming days, according to weather forecasters, while still fighting to contain deadly blazes that have already ignited amid soaring summer temper...
body_text:
Spain and France are at high risk of more wildfires in coming days, according to weather forecasters, while still fighting to contain deadly blazes that have already ignited amid soaring summer temperatures.

Spanish weather agency AEMET warned on Wednesday that the danger of wildfires was “very high or extreme” across most of mainland Spain and the Balearic Islands.

Wildfires have already burned more land this year in Europe than the annual average over the past two decades, data shows. The world’s fastest-warming continent has endured three stifling, virtually back-to-back heatwaves so far this year.

Scientists say human-driven climate change is increasing the intensity of heatwaves, droughts and wildfires, while also creating conditions for sudden, destructive storms.

Firefighters in central Spain are fighting a wildfire that has burned over 32,000 hectares (79,000 acres) in one of the country’s largest blazes on record.

The fire in rural Guadalajara, about 100km (62 miles) north of the capital Madrid, comes less than two weeks after a deadly blaze devastated a small expat community in southern Spain, killing 13 people including several foreigners.

Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez visited the fire-ravaged area in Guadalajara on Wednesday and called for a pact between political parties to fight climate change.

“Year after year we clearly see how climate change kills and destroys wealth in our communities,” Sanchez said in the village of Tamajon. “This isn’t politics. It is science, it’s about facts.”

Spain’s weather agency warned the already high risk of wildfires would likely increase further in coming days. It urged people to “exercise extreme caution” as dry ⁠thunderstorms and Saharan dust worsened the danger from the ongoing heatwave.

Although temperatures will ease slightly in some areas on Thursday, a dry westerly wind is forecast to drive a fresh surge of ⁠heat in Mediterranean regions, where highs of 42 to 44 degrees Celsius (108 to 111 degrees Fahrenheit) could be recorded in some areas.

In France, less severe temperatures and lighter winds aided efforts to fight a wildfire that has burned 2,500 hectares in the southeastern Var region, damaging dozens of houses and forcing 400 people to ⁠flee their homes, local authorities said.

But the risk of more wildfires remains very high in western and southwestern France for the rest of the week,  official weather forecaster Meteo-France said.

In Var, helicopters were dropping water on the wildfire to try to extinguish it.

“The situation has been very difficult,” Commander William Vogl, a spokesman for the Var ⁠firefighters, said on RMC radio. He said the fire was not yet contained but winds should become “more favourable”.

The region’s prefect, Simon Babre, said barbecues were banned, and asked municipalities to cancel any plans for traditional summer fireworks.

Officials said another fire was raging in the Hautes-Alpes, also in southeastern France.

France, which endured a stifling heatwave in late June, recorded 5,764 more deaths than usual between June 17 and July 2, according to authorities.

While the extra deaths were due to “all causes” – not all clearly linked to the heat – authorities described the excess mortality among people aged 15 and older as “unprecedented”.

Extreme weather continued to spark warnings and restrictions in other European countries, including Italy, Greece and Cyprus.

In Italy, several fires burned across Sicily on Wednesday, prompting scattered evacuations.

For days, Italy’s civil protection agency has declared all of Sicily at the highest risk for wildfires. Winds and temperatures near or above 40C have fuelled blazes from near Palermo on the northern coast to Catania in the east and now Agrigento in the west.

In Greece, with temperatures forecast to top 41C, authorities have banned outdoor work during peak midday sun.

In Cyprus, which is also used to high summer temperatures, working outdoors was ‌banned during afternoon hours on Wednesday.

title: US tariffs ‘strengthen’ Lula against Bolsonaro’s son in upcoming elections
excerpt: A slew of United States actions against Brazil, including a range of tariffs and sanctions on local criminal gangs, is playing into Brazilian voter sentiment ahead of the presidential elections in Oct...
body_text:
A slew of United States actions against Brazil, including a range of tariffs and sanctions on local criminal gangs, is playing into Brazilian voter sentiment ahead of the presidential elections in October.

As incumbent leftist President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva seeks a fourth, non-consecutive term, he will compete against the Liberal Party’s Flavio Bolsonaro, son of former far-right President Jair Bolsonaro, who is serving a 27-year prison term.

Brazil will hold the first round of the general election on October 4, with a run-off, should Lula and Flavio Bolsonaro both advance, on October 25.

Lula has cast himself as a defender of Brazilian sovereignty in response to US tariffs and sanctions.

Last week, the administration of US President Donald Trump announced 25 percent tariffs on many Brazilian goods, the result of a yearlong investigation that alleged Brazil’s trade practices were “unreasonable”.

The US argued that Brazil unfairly favoured its state-run Pix payment system over private competitors, imposed an 18 percent tariff on US ethanol imports, and failed to enforce deforestation laws strictly enough, thereby benefiting Brazilian timber producers.

The levies are part of the administration’s strategy of rebuilding its tariff wall after the Supreme Court struck down in February the emergency powers Trump had used to unleash tariffs on most trading partners.

The previous tariffs of up to 50 percent on Brazil were tied to the criminal case against Jair Bolsonaro for plotting to overturn the results of the 2022 elections, which he lost to Lula. Trump had called the investigation a “witch-hunt”.

A recent Quaest poll for Folha de S Paulo suggested Lula is leading Flavio Bolsonaro 45 percent to 37 percent in a second-round run-off election.

“His political movement, Bolsonarismo, is afraid that tariffs actually strengthen Lula’s support in the upcoming elections, because Brazilians are very disgusted with the US imposing tariffs,” Gimena Sanchez, director for the Andes at the Washington Office on Latin America (WOLA), said.

She said that a Bolsonaro win in October would serve Washington’s broader interests.

Trump was an early and vocal backer of the elder Bolsonaro, whose 2018 rise to Brazil’s presidency drew comparisons with Trump’s political style. Before Bolsonaro’s 2022 re-election bid, Trump recorded a video endorsement, calling him “one of the great people in all of politics”.

After Bolsonaro lost the race, he did not concede and questioned the reliability of Brazil’s electronic voting machines. Thousands of his supporters later stormed government buildings in Brasilia, a federal district.

Prosecutors said Bolsonaro and his allies had plotted a coup to keep him in power. In July 2025, while the trial was still under way, Trump imposed the first round of tariffs on Brazil and sanctioned the Brazilian Supreme Court justice overseeing the case.

“Trump has been a leading figure not only to the Bolsonaros but also to the wide range of extreme-right politicians in Latin America since his first term,” said Gustavo Blum, a Brazilian geopolitics analyst.

He said the alignment was simpler in 2018, when Trump’s focus was chiefly on countering China, a significant trading partner for Brazil and other countries in the region, and the continent’s left-leaning political leaders.

“Now, with the imposition and re-imposition of trade tariffs, it’s been harder for the Bolsonaros to give a perspective in which Trump is looking out for Brazilians’ interests and not only Americans’ interests,” Blum said.

In June, a Brazilian Supreme Court panel convicted Eduardo Bolsonaro, Jair’s third son, of lobbying the US to interfere in his father’s trial. Eduardo had moved to the US full-time to “focus 100 percent” of his energy on “a single cause”: freeing his father.

The panel said that Eduardo’s actions amounted to coercion against Brazil’s justice system and sentenced him to four years and two months in prison.

Flavio, on the other hand, has faced separate scrutiny at home since May, when reports emerged that he sought roughly $24m from Daniel Vorcaro, the jailed former head of collapsed lender Banco Master, to help finance a film about his father.

Flavio acknowledged requesting the money but denied any impropriety or wrongdoing.

Blum said the scandal has added to the family’s difficulties with both Washington and Brasilia. “The family hasn’t been able to use the connection with Trump with success,” he said.

Flavio has asked Washington to delay the new tariffs until after the election, arguing they would hand Lula “precisely the political victory it has been engineering”.

Lula has accused Bolsonaro of inviting the pressure himself, calling the request “yet another act of treason against the Fatherland”.

WOLA’s Sanchez said the Bolsonaro camp’s lobbying campaign with Washington has largely failed to produce results.

But one request did prove effective. Days after Flavio met Trump at the White House in May, the State Department designated Brazil’s two largest criminal factions, the PCC and Comando Vermelho, as foreign “terrorist” organisations.

Flavio had personally requested the designation and said it was a result of his lobbying. Lula called the measure an “arbitrary” step that threatened Brazilian sovereignty.

The tariff order Washington issued weeks later separately named Pix, Brazil’s free, government-run payment network used by more than 90 percent of Brazilians, as an unfair trade practice.

Flavio, defending the system before US officials, said Pix was “good for Brazil”.

Brazilians across the political spectrum have reacted negatively to Trump’s involvement in the election.

“Even right-wing Brazilians, when they see President Trump trying to meddle with Brazilian elections, are not very pleased, because they think that Brazilian problems should be solved in Brazil, not in Washington, DC,” said Patricio Navia, a political scientist at New York University.

Navia said the reaction also reflected Brazil’s desire to avoid being drawn into the broader rivalry between the US and China, its largest trading partner.

“China is the most important trade partner; the US is the most important strategic partner,” he said. “They don’t want their best friend and their most important trade partner to fight with each other.”

Brazil’s manufacturing and agribusiness sectors, which broadly backed Bolsonaro in 2018 and 2022, have grown more cautious amid the economic pressure from Trump’s tariff policies.

Navia said the unpredictability of how the Trump administration wields tariffs makes it difficult for any ally to plan around it. “Not even those who declare themselves to be Trump’s friends can tell you what will happen,” he said.

title: Sophie Cunningham doubles down ahead of Sun game after ESPN profile stirs controversy: 'I said what I said'
excerpt:
As Jemele Hill doesn't understand why Sophie Cunningham has a shoe deal, Dan breaks down that Sophie is the face of the league, her looks sell, and that businesses are in business to make money.

Befo...
body_text:
As Jemele Hill doesn't understand why Sophie Cunningham has a shoe deal, Dan breaks down that Sophie is the face of the league, her looks sell, and that businesses are in business to make money.

Before Wednesday's game against the Connecticut Sun, Indiana Fever guard Sophie Cunningham doubled down on her call to protect women's sports from biological male competitors, brushing aside recent backlash over her comments.

"I think I said what I said. I think it's kind of common sense," Cunningham told reporters when asked to expand on her views.

"I'll always believe in that. It's really important to protect children, and that's little girls who are also involved in that category."

SOPHIE CUNNINGHAM MANAGES TO ANGER BOTH THE LEFT AND RIGHT WITH A SIMPLE SOCIAL MEDIA POST

For Cunningham, defending women's sports isn't about politics. It's about protecting female athletes.

"I'm not a very political person, but I do have beliefs. I have morals, and I'll always stand on those," she explained.

Indiana Fever superstars Sophie Cunningham and Caitlin Clark are arguably the two most popular players in the WNBA. (NBAE via Getty Images)

She pointed to Title IX as the foundation of women's sports, arguing the law exists to protect opportunities for female athletes. Without those protections, she said, the category loses its purpose.

TRANS ATHLETES DROP LAWSUIT TO GAIN ACCESS TO GIRLS' SPORTS IN NEW HAMPSHIRE AFTER SCOTUS RULING

Cunningham also rejected the idea that her position stems from bigotry, saying defending women's sports doesn't mean harboring hatred toward anyone else.

Indiana Fever guard Sophie Cunningham jokes with Max Holloway following UFC 329 at T-Mobile Arena in Las Vegas, Nev., on July 11, 2026. (Mark J. Rebilas/Imagn Images)

"I extend love, and I don't dislike anyone. I think there is room for absolutely everyone here," she said, adding that she has never expressed hatred toward the trans community.

Her focus, she said, remains on protecting biological women and girls.

TRACK STAR SAYS SHE'S TIRED OF CHRISTIANS BEING CALLED 'BIGOTS' FOR OPPOSING TRANS ATHLETES IN GIRLS' SPORTS

OutKick founder Clay Travis shared the clip on X.

"Love all of this. Well done @sophaller," Travis wrote in part.

He later tagged author J.K. Rowling, writing, "Hey @jk_rowling, want to make sure you see this. Sanity is winning in America."

As for the backlash, Cunningham said she tunes it out.

"I really don't care," she said regarding the political labels attached to her views. "If I'm gonna live by the love, I'll die by the hate, and that's just not who I am. I beat to my own drum."

Jun 16, 2026; Indianapolis, Indiana, USA; Indiana Fever guard Sophie Cunningham (8) celebrates a made shot in the first half against the Toronto Tempo at Gainbridge Fieldhouse. Mandatory Credit: Trevor Ruszkowski-Imagn Images (IMAGN IMAGES via Reuters Connect)

Send us your thoughts: alejandro.avila@outkick.com / Follow along on X: @alejandroaveela

Alejandro Avila is a longtime writer at OutKick, living in Southern California.

title: Jon Stewart presses Mamdani on where he breaks with fellow Democratic socialists on prisons and borders
excerpt:
Talk show host Jon Stewart noted his skepticism of certain far-left policies and asked New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani where he disagrees with his fellow democratic socialists.

Talk show host Jon ...
body_text:
Talk show host Jon Stewart noted his skepticism of certain far-left policies and asked New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani where he disagrees with his fellow democratic socialists.

Talk show host Jon Stewart asked New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani where he disagrees with fellow Democratic socialists who are rising in the Democratic Party during a Wednesday episode of his podcast, "The Weekly Show."

Stewart, who has long been a fixture of centrist Democratic Party politics, noted on his show that times have changed, in that "now Democratic socialism is considered the hottest trend in the Democratic Party and there's grassroots energy and all — and these Democratic socialist candidates are popping up, and you're having to answer for every one of their policy positions."

"Look, maybe I'm an old man or a s--- lib or whatever they want to call me," Stewart said. "If someone says we're going to abolish all prisons and borders, like I do go, ‘I don't think that's a good idea.’ Like, it feels like order and security is also a progressive value. Why shouldn't people in neighborhoods with less resources have the same safety and security that neighborhoods with lots of resources?"

SOCIALISM’S RISE INSIDE THE DEMOCRATIC PARTY NOW THREATENS THE AMERICAN DREAM

Months after being inaugurated himself, NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani has been credited for ushering in a new wave of democratic socialists behind him. (Michael M. Santiago/Getty Images)

He then proceeded to ask Mamdani, "So are there tenets of those that are kind of following in the wake of Zohran Mamdani's success that you're not comfortable with or that give you pause?"

Mamdani replied, "If you have more than one socialist in a room and there isn't disagreement, then you don't have more than one socialist in a room."

Mamdani went on to say he is perfectly comfortable with such discussions, so long as they are productive.

"Well, I'm comfortable with disagreement, and I'm comfortable with disagreement that doesn't end the conversation entirely, because I think what motivates a lot of people is, ‘How do you deliver public safety?" Mamdani said. "And the thing that I've said time and again is, ‘We cannot have New Yorkers choose between safety and justice.’"

DEM REP HENRY CUELLAR REJECTS DSA AGENDA, SAYS SOCIALISTS ARE TRYING TO 'USE' DEMOCRATIC PARTY

Comedy Central host Jon Stewart asked NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani to tell him where he disagrees with his fellow democratic socialists. (Phillip Faraone / Getty)

"And yes, I am, you know, doing the work with our police department, and we have been able to deliver the lowest number of murders and shootings in recorded history," Mamdani touted. "And at the same time, I'm doing the work to close down Riker's Island and ensure that we are actually building the four smaller borough-based jails that brings an end to a horrible chapter in New York City history ."

Mamdani went on to agree that one’s safety should not depend on how upscale their neighborhood is.

"I think, to your point, you know, when we're thinking about safety, there cannot be a hierarchy of safety where you experience it more or less depending on where you live in our city, or how much money is in your pocket," Mamdani said. "And there also can't be a definition of safety that is purely after the fact, meaning that how you respond to violence. We also have to do the work of investing so that we prevent violence."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

Mayor Zohran Mamdani noted that, "The thing that I've said time and again is ‘We cannot have New Yorkers choose between safety and justice.’" (Lev Radin/Pacific Press/LightRocket via Getty Images)

Alexander Hall is an associate editor for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to Alexander.hall@fox.com.

title: Yemen’s Houthis claim attacks on Red Sea tankers as US launches 12th night of strikes on Iran – Middle East crisis live
excerpt:
US military says it is degrading Tehran’s ability to threaten ships in strait of Hormuz; Houthis say tankers violated their blockade; US signs nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia

Before the US launched it...
body_text:
US military says it is degrading Tehran’s ability to threaten ships in strait of Hormuz; Houthis say tankers violated their blockade; US signs nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia

Before the US launched its 12th night of strikes against Iran, the Islamic republic’s foreign minister warned: “Our defense doctrine is clear: eye for an eye.”

Abbas Araghchi also said in the post on X several hours ago:

double quotation markAny aggression against Iran, including our infrastructure, will compel a powerful and decisive response.

Those who contribute to such aggression, whatever the kind of support, will also be considered as legitimate targets.

More on Yemen’s Houthis claiming strikes on two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea:

The master of one tanker reported being struck by an unknown projectile about 70 nautical miles south-west of Al Shuqaiq, causing a fire that the crew was fighting, the British naval security monitor UKMTO said.

The Iran-aligned Houthis warned shipping companies on Wednesday that their tankers risk being attacked if they used Saudi Arabian ports, as the militia enforced a blockade it announced the previous day.

The Houthi warning for the Red Sea – coming on top of Iran’s threat to shipping on the other side of the Arabian peninsular in the Gulf – could affect the substantial Saudi oil exports that have so far been unscathed by the months-long conflict and deepen the rapidly escalating crisis in the region, as Peter Beaumont has written.

Donald Trump said he would “take care of” the Houthis if they followed through on their blockade threat against Saudi ports.

The Houthis have been broadcasting radio warnings to vessels, the International Chamber of Shipping told Agence France-Presse.

The message warned that “in case of non-compliance of Yemeni armed forces decision, those ships will be in our target”.

Kuwait’s air defences are responding to hostile drone threats amid “Iranian aggression”, the Kuwaiti army is saying.

Any explosion sounds heard were the result of attacks being intercepted, it said on social media.

Iran has continued to strike at countries around the region hosting American forces in response to ongoing US attacks.

Welcome to our live coverage of the continuing crisis in the Middle East.

The US military announced on Thursday evening it had began a 12th consecutive night of strikes against Iranian military targets at the direction of Donald Trump.

US Central Command said the strikes would “continue to further degrade Iran’s ability to threaten civilian mariners and commercial vessels transiting regional waters”.

It came as Yemen’s Houthis said they carried out missile and drone strikes on Wednesday on two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea after declaring a maritime embargo on the kingdom amid the US war with Iran.

The Houthis said in a statement posted on Telegram that they “carried out a qualitative military operation targeting two Saudi oil tankers that violated the blockade”, naming the vessels as the Encelia and the Layla.
