Travelers at Raleigh-Durham International Airport stand in silence, many with their hands over their hearts, as a dignified transfer for a fallen U.S. service member takes place on the tarmac outside. (The White House via Instagram)

"Out of respect for the service member, the family, and military notification procedures, we are unable to provide additional information regarding the individual or the transfer," the USO said.

The organization said its staff and volunteers are prepared to support military families during some of their most difficult moments.

"When a service member makes their final journey home, our staff and volunteers are prepared to provide compassionate support to families and work alongside our military partners to help ensure a dignified transfer and the respectful honoring of our nation’s fallen," the USO said.

The organization added that it works to ease logistical burdens and provide families with a welcoming space and essential services so they can focus on honoring their loved one.

The airport tribute came as the nation mourned four U.S. service members recently killed during the war with Iran.

Earlier Wednesday, the Department of War's Rapid Response account announced that four American service members who "made the ultimate sacrifice for our nation" had been welcomed home during dignified transfer ceremonies.

"Today, we welcomed home four brave Americans who made the ultimate sacrifice for our nation," the account wrote on X. "Their courage will never be forgotten."

President Donald Trump salutes as a U.S. Army carry team carries the flag-draped transfer case containing the remains of 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, during a dignified transfer solemn event for U.S. service members killed during operations in the Middle East, at Dover Air Force Base in Dover, Delaware, on July 22, 2026. Feehan was killed in action on July 18 during an enemy attack at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan.  (Saul Loeb / AFP via Getty Images)

FBI Director Kash Patel also paid tribute to the four fallen service members in a post on X.

"We owe the ultimate costs to our American patriots that paid the ultimate price for this country," Patel wrote. "We will never forget."

The Department of War said "Sgt. Angel Rampersad, 28, of Ozone Park, New York, is believed to have been killed in action on July 17, 2026, during an enemy attack at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base, Jordan." (10th Army Air and Missile Defense Command)

Patel named 1st Lt. Tyler Feehan, Sgt. Michael Swinton, Sgt. Angel Rampersad and Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, adding, "Or their families."

The first victims of restarted hostilities were identified as Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, and Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas, according to a Monday release. (U.S. Army)

It is not immediately clear whether the White House video from the airport depicts one of those transfers.

The four are among 18 U.S. service members killed during the war with Iran.

On Tuesday, the Pentagon identified Army Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton, 30, of Fayetteville, North Carolina, as one of the latest U.S. service members to die while deployed, announcing he was killed during an operation at Erbil Air Base in Iraq involving a downed Iranian attack drone.

Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton, 30, was killed in action during a controlled detonation of a downed One-Way Unmanned Aerial System on Friday, at Erbil Air Base, Iraq. (U.S. Army Space and Missile Defense Command via AP)

North Carolina Senate candidate Michael Whatley honored Swinton in a statement provided to Fox News Digital.

"May God Bless Sgt. Michael Swinton and his family," Whatley said. "North Carolina and the country owe an immeasurable debt to those who have given their lives to protect and preserve the liberties which make America the greatest nation on Earth."

Rep. Mark Harris, R-N.C., also mourned Swinton and Feehan while pointing to the airport tribute as a reflection of the nation’s gratitude.

"North Carolina and the Fort Bragg community mourn the loss of two remarkable individuals who made the ultimate sacrifice for our freedom," Harris said in a statement to Fox News Digital.

"Beth and I are praying for the families, friends, and loved ones of Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton and 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan."

"As today’s beautiful moment at Raleigh-Durham International Airport showed, we will never forget the debt our nation owes these two heroes."

Fox News Digital's Eric Mack contributed to this report.

Stepheny Price is a Writer at Fox News with a focus on West Coast and Midwest news, missing persons, national and international crime stories, homicide cases, and border security.

title: Experts reveal how to avoid deadly Legionnaires' disease as NYC outbreak kills 5
excerpt: NYC health officials warn residents of a Legionnaires' disease outbreak with 23 confirmed cases. Dr. Stephanie Widmer explains the bacterial pneumonia, its symptoms like cough and fever, and its curab...
body_text:
NYC health officials warn residents of a Legionnaires' disease outbreak with 23 confirmed cases. Dr. Stephanie Widmer explains the bacterial pneumonia, its symptoms like cough and fever, and its curability.

As New York City continues to investigate an outbreak of Legionnaires' disease that has claimed multiple lives and sickened dozens of people, doctors say there are ways people can reduce the risk of exposure.

As of July 21, there have been five deaths and 82 diagnoses linked to the Upper East Side outbreak, according to the New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene. Eight patients remain hospitalized.

Health officials said no new cases had been reported in the previous six days, suggesting the source of exposure has likely been eliminated, although the investigation remains ongoing.

NYC LEGIONNAIRES' DISEASE OUTBREAK KILLS FOURTH VICTIM, 76 CASES REPORTED

Legionnaires’ disease is a serious type of pneumonia that is typically spread by tiny water droplets containing Legionella bacteria, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

As New York City continues to investigate an outbreak of Legionnaires' disease that has claimed multiple lives and sickened dozens of people, doctors say there are ways people can reduce the risk of exposure. (iStock)

Common sources of infection include contaminated building water systems, such as cooling towers (large structures that remove heat from water used in building cooling systems), hot tubs or plumbing. Cases have also been reported on cruise ships and in spas. It is not generally spread from person to person, per the CDC.

"We get infected when it gets aerosolized in the water droplets, usually, and we breathe those droplets in," "Dr. Dana Mordue, associate professor of pathology, microbiology and immunology at New York Medical College, told Fox News Digital.

NYC HEALTH OFFICIALS WARN CENTRAL PARK VISITORS AFTER LEGIONNAIRES’ OUTBREAK GROWS ON UPPER EAST SIDE

"You can also get infected if you have water that's been contaminated and you aspirate it and swallow it into your lungs, but most often you get it from air droplets that are contaminated from various types of water sources."

Legionnaires’ disease is a serious type of pneumonia that is typically spread by tiny water droplets containing Legionella bacteria. (iStock)

Although it is possible to contract Legionnaires’ in the home, outbreaks are almost always linked to exposure to water droplets coming from a water system in a "commercial-type building," according to Mordue.

"The good news is you usually don't get it from the types of cooling systems that we have in our cars or our homes — but if you don't clean your humidifiers, technically any water source can get contaminated with Legionella."

The most widespread symptoms of Legionnaires' disease include cough, shortness of breath, fever, muscle aches and headaches, per the CDC. Some people may also experience diarrhea, nausea and confusion.

Symptoms typically begin 2 to 14 days after exposure to the bacteria, although they can appear later. The illness often requires treatment with antibiotics.

The most widespread symptoms of Legionnaires' disease include cough, shortness of breath, fever, muscle aches and headaches, per the CDC.  (iStock)

"The mortality rate is not particularly high for this disease if you are immune-competent and young and healthy," said Mordue.

Risk is higher for older adults, smokers and people with compromised immune systems — "a lot of the same risk factors you might see for COVID," the doctor noted.

The CDC shares the following measures to prevent the spread of Legionnaires’ disease.

For anyone who may have been exposed and develops symptoms, it is important to seek immediate medical care, doctors say. Early diagnosis and antibiotic treatment can reduce the risk of severe illness.

"If you have certain risk factors, particularly advanced age, get help as quickly as you can," Mordue advised.

Melissa Rudy is senior health editor and a member of the lifestyle team at Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to melissa.rudy@fox.com.

title: Cole Hauser admits Taylor Sheridan's absence from writers' room created 'challenges' on 'Dutton Ranch'
excerpt:
The "Dutton Ranch" star reflects on his family’s deep ties to Montana as the Paramount+ series premieres May 15 with two episodes, followed by weekly releases.

Cole Hauser is giving fans a glimpse be...
body_text:
The "Dutton Ranch" star reflects on his family’s deep ties to Montana as the Paramount+ series premieres May 15 with two episodes, followed by weekly releases.

Cole Hauser is giving fans a glimpse behind the scenes of "Dutton Ranch" — and it wasn't all smooth riding.

The actor, who reprises his fan-favorite role as Rip Wheeler in the "Yellowstone" spin-off, acknowledged the cast faced an uphill battle after franchise creator Taylor Sheridan stepped away from the show's writers' room during the spin-off’s first season.

"There were challenges, you know?" Hauser told Film Inside NYC. "Not having Taylor [Sheridan] there, for us to really kind of be able to work with these new writers, these new directors, these people... Everything had changed."

ED HARRIS SAYS HE WAS READY TO LEAVE DUTTON RANCH AFTER FEELING MISLED ABOUT HIS ROLE

Cole Hauser previously said his "Dutton Ranch" character Rip Wheeler reflects a "throwback to the old-school American man." (John Nacion/Variety via Getty Images; Lauren "Lo" Smith/Paramount+)

"The only… normality that we had whatsoever was Carter [played by Finn Little], Christina [director Alexandra Voros] … Kelly and myself," he added.

"Dutton Ranch," which wrapped its first season earlier this month, marked the first series in the "Yellowstone" universe not to be written by Sheridan.

While the franchise creator remained on board as an executive producer, day-to-day creative duties shifted to showrunner Chad Feehan and an entirely new writing staff.

For Hauser, the transition wasn't seamless.

"I enjoy a challenge, it certainly was a challenge," he said. "We worked our a--es off this year... And I don't think a lot of the outside noise or the thought of, like, 'What's the weight of the show?' was really apparent to me at the time."

LIKE WHAT YOU’RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Kelly Reilly as Beth Dutton and Cole Hauser as Rip Wheeler in "Dutton Ranch." (Emerson Miller/Paramount+.)

Rather than dwell on the expectations surrounding one of television's biggest franchises, Hauser said the cast focused on finding its footing with an unfamiliar creative team.

The actor pointed out that the experience ultimately strengthened the cast.

TAYLOR SHERIDAN’S 'YELLOWSTONE’ FORMULA TURNS COUNTRY STARS INTO SCENE-STEALERS AS MORGAN WADE MAKES HER DEBUT

Cole Hauser reveals the "Dutton Ranch" cast had to adapt after Taylor Sheridan stepped back. (Omar Vega/Getty Images)

"It was about putting my head down and working my a-- off with… what I had around me. The writers that we had, the new writers that we had, that we had to figure out on our feet how to really perform and tell a story. So, I'm proud of what we accomplished, and I look forward to doing it again."

Hauser's comments come after speculation surrounding the show's creative direction.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

Ed Harris previously said he told his team to get him out of "Dutton Ranch." (Emerson Miller/Paramount+.)

The franchise faced scrutiny following blunt comments from co-star Ed Harris, who said he was disappointed by how his character, Everett McKinney, was handled during Season 1.

Speaking to Variety, Harris said, "Midway through this first season, I was ready to say, 'Get me the f--- out of here,' to tell you the truth... I didn't feel I was being used... I felt a little bit misled."

Harris claimed he signed on believing Everett would be one of the show's central characters, but felt that promise never materialized.

"Prior to signing on for it, I talked quite a bit about what the season was and what my character was going to do. That I was one of the four main characters. And that wasn't really the case," Harris said, adding that he also was disappointed a scene in which his character sang was ultimately cut from the final edit.

The Oscar-nominated actor said producers have assured him his role will expand when production resumes.

"We don't start [Season 2] until February," Harris said. "And I'm being told that my character will have a little bit more to do. It just was a little frustrating, to tell you the truth."

Stephanie Giang-Paunon is an entertainment writer for Fox News Digital.

title: Trump gives emotional tribute to fallen Army Lt Tyler Feehan after flying grieving family on Air Force One
excerpt: President Donald Trump paid tribute to Army 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan during remarks at Wheeler High School in Marietta, Georgia, revealing he flew the fallen soldier's family aboard Air Force One an...
body_text:
President Donald Trump paid tribute to Army 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan during remarks at Wheeler High School in Marietta, Georgia, revealing he flew the fallen soldier's family aboard Air Force One and were in the crowd for Wednesday's speech after meeting them at Dover Air Force Base following a dignified transfer ceremony.

President Donald Trump paid an emotional tribute to fallen Army 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan during an event Wednesday at Wheeler High School in Marietta, Georgia, revealing he invited the slain soldier's grieving family aboard Air Force One and were in the crowd after meeting them earlier in the day at Dover Air Force Base in Delaware.

Trump traveled to Georgia after attending the dignified transfer ceremony of four U.S. service members killed in Jordan, including Feehan, where he met his parents, Shari and Stephen, and his uncle David.

Speaking to the crowd, Trump recounted learning the family was also headed to Georgia after the ceremony.

TRUMP TAKES INAUGURAL FLIGHT ABOARD NEW AIR FORCE ONE AHEAD OF LIBRARY DEBUT HONORING FAMED OUTDOORSMAN

Shari and Stephen Feehan react as President Donald Trump honors their son, Army 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, during remarks Wednesday at Wheeler High School in Marietta, Ga. (Julia Demaree Nikhinson/AP Photo)

"I said, 'If you want, I'll give you a ride on Air Force One,'" Trump told the audience. "Today they were on Air Force One, and it's quite nice, I must say."

Trump said the family was "heartbroken" but grateful for the gesture.

"They couldn't believe it," he said. "They live minutes away. We're here today."

ARMY IDS 2 VICTIMS KILLED IN JORDAN AIR BASE ATTACK; TRUMP: IRAN WILL PAY 'MANY TIMES OVER'

President Donald Trump salutes as the flag-draped transfer case containing the remains of Army 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, Wednesday, during a dignified transfer for U.S. service members killed during operations in the Middle East, at Dover Air Force Base in Dover, Del. (Saul Loeb/AFP via Getty Images)

Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, was among four U.S. service members killed in Jordan while supporting a U.S. mission. He was assigned to the 2nd Battalion, 55th Air Defense Artillery Regiment, 32nd Army Air and Missile Defense Command at Fort Bragg, North Carolina.

Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas, was also among those killed. U.S. Central Command announced the deaths Saturday.

Trump said he reviews biographies of fallen service members before each dignified transfer and described Feehan as an exceptional young officer with a promising future.

DEMOCRATIC MARYLAND GOV WES MOORE PRAISES TRUMP ACCOUNTS AS ‘SMART POLICY’

The family of Army 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan departs Air Force One with President Donald Trump at Dobbins Air Reserve Base in Marietta, Ga., on Wednesday. (Kevin Dietsch/Getty Images)

"Lieutenant Tyler Feehan ... he was a person that was going all the way," Trump said. "He was going to be a general. He would have been a general very quickly. He was heading in that path."

Trump then thanked Feehan's family, whom he said he got to know during the flight from Dover to Georgia aboard Air Force One.

"But I just want to thank Tyler's mother, Shari, his father, Stephen, and his uncle David. Great people. I got to know them a little bit on the plane and standing there and watching this site," he said.

The first victims of restarted hostilities were identified as Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, and Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas. (U.S. Army)

Calling dignified transfers the most difficult responsibility of the presidency, Trump said he would never forget the family.

"I think it's the toughest thing I do," Trump said. "But to Shari and Stephen and David, we will carry Tyler's memory in our hearts forever. I just want to thank you. It's an honor to have you here today."

The audience responded with sustained applause before Trump addressed Feehan's mother directly.

Family members of fallen Army 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan react as President Donald Trump talks about their son at Wheeler High School, Wednesday, in Marietta, Ga. (Fox News Channel)

"Shari, he's looking down right here," Trump said. "He's so proud right now."

Trump visited Georgia on Wednesday to tout his administration's Trump Accounts initiative, saying the program would help families build long-term savings for their children as part of what he described as America's "Golden Age."

Brittany Miller is a Fox News Digital breaking news writer and former Independent reporter who graduated from Syracuse University in 2023.

title: Manhunt underway after masked gunman allegedly shoots NYC cab driver in face during overnight robbery
excerpt: A 21-year-old NYC cab driver is recovering after a masked gunman allegedly demanded cash and keys before opening fire, striking the driver in the face. (New York City Police Department/Fox 5 New York)...
body_text:
A 21-year-old NYC cab driver is recovering after a masked gunman allegedly demanded cash and keys before opening fire, striking the driver in the face. (New York City Police Department/Fox 5 New York)

Police in New York City are searching for a masked gunman who allegedly shot a 21-year-old cab driver in the face during an overnight armed robbery in Brooklyn.

According to a New York Police Department (NYPD) statement obtained by Fox 5 New York, the incident happened around 4 a.m. Saturday when the suspect targeted a TLC-licensed livery cab, a vehicle typically used for pre-arranged rides rather than street hails.

Authorities said the suspect entered the vehicle and allegedly threatened the driver, demanding money and the keys to the cab.

NYPD DETECTIVE, CHILDREN SHOT IN SEPARATE BROOKLYN SHOOTINGS OVER HOLIDAY WEEKEND

A 21-year-old livery cab driver was shot in the face during an overnight robbery attempt in Brooklyn, police said. (STAN HONDA/AFP via Getty Images;NYPD Crime Stoppers/Fox 5 New York)

The suspect then opened fire as the driver attempted to exit the vehicle, striking him in the face.

"The victim proceeded to exit the vehicle when the unidentified individual discharged a firearm striking the 21-year-old male victim in the face," police said.

The suspect fled the scene on foot after stealing "an unknown amount of currency," police said.

MANHUNT UNDERWAY FOR KANSAS CITY SHOOTING SPREE SUSPECT NEAR WORLD CUP VENUE; FBI OFFERING $25K REWARD

Authorities are searching for an armed gunman who allegedly shot a New York City cab driver before fleeing on foot with cash. (NYPD Crime Stoppers/Fox 5 New York)

Authorities noted that the suspect did not take anything else from the vehicle.

Police received reports of the incident at approximately 4:15 a.m.

Emergency Medical Services responded and transported the victim to a local hospital in stable condition. The NYPD also described the shooting as non-fatal.

The NYPD released footage of a suspect wanted in connection with the armed robbery and shooting of a Brooklyn cab driver. (NYPD Crime Stoppers/Fox 5 New York)

Authorities are asking for the public’s help identifying the suspect, who was last seen wearing blue shorts, a black sweatshirt, a black surgical mask and black shoes while carrying a black backpack.

Anyone with information about the incident is asked to contact the NYPD Crime Stoppers Hotline at 1-800-577-TIPS (8477). Spanish speakers can call 1-888-57-PISTA (74782). Tips can also be submitted through the NYPD Crime Stoppers website or on X @NYPDTips.

Bonny Chu is a Breaking and Trending News Writer for Fox News Digital

For decades, neo-Nazis and far-right extremists flocked to the Austrian home where Adolf Hitler was born to glorify the Nazi leader. Now, the building has been transformed into a police station following a $22.8-million renovation.

title: Yemen’s Houthis claim attack on two Saudi oil tankers
excerpt:
Yemen’s Iran-aligned Houthi rebels have claimed an attack on two Saudi oil tankers transiting through the Red Sea.

“We targeted two Saudi oil tankers, named ENCELA and LAYLIA, for their violation of ...
body_text:
Yemen’s Iran-aligned Houthi rebels have claimed an attack on two Saudi oil tankers transiting through the Red Sea.

“We targeted two Saudi oil tankers, named ENCELA and LAYLIA, for their violation of the blockade decision issued by the armed forces,” Houthi military spokesperson Yahya Saree said on Wednesday.

Saree added that the attacks were carried out, “using a number of ballistic and cruise missiles, as well as drones”.

Saudi Arabia has not commented on the claims, but the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) said it had received reports that a tanker has been struck.

“The master of a tanker reports being struck by an unknown projectile which has caused a fire onboard that the crew are currently fighting,” UKMTO, which monitors shipping in the region, said.

Yemen’s Houthi movement, which has fought the Yemeni government and a Saudi-led coalition that backs it for more than a decade, had imposed a maritime blockade on Saudi Arabia on July 20. That followed some of the worst violence Yemen had seen for four years, after the Yemeni government bombed Houthi-controlled Sanaa Airport to stop an Iranian plane from landing.

Saudi Arabia has rejected claims by the Houthis that Riyadh was imposing a siege on the Yemeni people, and the group’s critics point out that they have rejected a proposal from Jordan to resume flights between Amman and Sanaa.

Amid the disruption in shipping through the Strait of Hormuz during the US-Israel war on Iran, Saudi Arabia has been attempting to facilitate oil flow through alternative routes through the Red Sea and the Bab al-Mandeb Strait. But that route is now under threat from the Houthis.

The Houthis disrupted global commerce when they began attacking ships around the Bab al-Mandeb Strait following the launch of Israel’s genocidal war on Gaza in October 2023. The attacks ended with the announcement of a “ceasefire” in Gaza in October 2025.

The Bab al-Mandeb chokepoint connecting the Red Sea to the Gulf of Aden is one of the world’s most important shipping routes, including for oil exports worldwide.

Between Yemen to the northeast and Djibouti and Eritrea in the Horn of Africa to the southwest, the strait is just 29km (18 miles) wide at its narrowest point, limiting traffic to two channels for inbound and outbound vessels using the Suez Canal.

In 2024, about 4.1 billion barrels of crude oil and refined petroleum products passed through the strait – about five percent of the global total.

With the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed since Israel and the United States launched their war on Iran in late February, shutting down Bab al-Mandeb as well could block 25 percent of the world’s oil and gas supply.

title: Journalists demand White House Correspondents' Association 'speak truth to power' in new dinner with Trump
excerpt:
Fox News' Mark Meredith and Jacqui Heinrich report on the shooting incident at the White House Correspondents' Dinner as details emerge about the suspect, Cole Allen, his weapons and more.

More than ...
body_text:
Fox News' Mark Meredith and Jacqui Heinrich report on the shooting incident at the White House Correspondents' Dinner as details emerge about the suspect, Cole Allen, his weapons and more.

More than 500 journalists called on the White House Correspondents' Association to "forcefully stand up" to President Donald Trump on Tuesday ahead of its rescheduled dinner.

"Our message to the White House Correspondents' Association is now more urgent: forcefully stand up for freedom of the press in front of the President, publicly defend the journalists he attacks, and vow to resist his administration’s concerted effort to undermine the First Amendment," a letter, organized by former ABC News correspondent Lisa Stark and former executive producer Ian Cameron, said.

The journalists stated that since the original White House Correspondents' Dinner, the Trump administration has only become more "egregious" in its attacks against the press and encouraged members at the event to publicly condemn the president, who will be in attendance.

WHITE HOUSE CORRESPONDENTS 'EXCITED' FOR TRUMP'S RETURN TO ANNUAL DC DINNER, INSIDERS SAY

The letter resembled a similar one put out by journalists ahead of the original White House Correspondents' Association Dinner in April. (Andrew Leyden/Getty Images)

"We believe it is hypocritical to celebrate the First Amendment in front of the man who relentlessly attacks it. We urge the WHCA to condemn the administration’s actions from the podium and pledge to fight all attempts by his administration to undermine this core pillar of a functioning democracy," the letter read.

In a statement to Fox News Digital, the WHCA appeared to stand by its plans to host Trump while still honoring the press.

"The White House Correspondents’ Dinner exists because the First Amendment matters most when it’s under pressure. As we continue to celebrate America’s 250th birthday, our choice to gather as journalists, newsmakers and the president in the same room is a reminder of what a free press means to this country — and why it must endure. Not for the media or the president, but for the people who depend on it," the statement read.

FLASHBACK: FIVE OF THE MOST POLITICALLY-CHARGED MOMENTS AT THE WHITE HOUSE CORRESPONDENTS' DINNER

The original White House Correspondents' Association Dinner ended abruptly in April after an armed suspect stormed the event. (Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images)

The letter resembled a similar one put out by more than 200 journalists, including former CBS News anchor Dan Rather, in April urging that the dinner "cannot be business as usual."

"We, the undersigned, call upon the White House Correspondents' Association to use the occasion of the White House Correspondents' Dinner, to forcefully demonstrate opposition to President Trump’s efforts to trample freedom of the press," the open letter read.

"The dinner has long served as a symbol of the vital and irreplaceable role of a free press in American democracy and a celebration of the First Amendment and the journalists who uphold it. President Trump's systematic, sustained, and unprecedented attacks on the free press ... render his presence at such an event a profound contradiction of its purpose."

WHITE HOUSE CORRESPONDENTS' ASSOCIATION PRESIDENT ANNOUNCES EVENT BEING RESCHEDULED AFTER SHOOTING

The original White House Correspondents' Dinner ended abruptly April 25 at the Washington Hilton when an armed suspect attempted to storm the event in what authorities say was an assassination attempt against Trump. The event was then rescheduled for July 24 at the Waldorf Astoria.

This year's dinner is the first time President Donald Trump has attended the White House Correspondents' Association event as president. (Mandel Ngan/AFP via Getty Images)

This year's White House Correspondents' Dinner is the first time Trump has attended the annual event as president after previously skipping it throughout his first term. He previously attended the dinner as a private citizen in 2011.

Lindsay Kornick is an associate editor for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to lindsay.kornick@fox.com and on Twitter: @lmkornick.

title: Sports media rushes to cast Ryan Clark as victim while portraying ESPN as the villain
excerpt:
OutKick reporter Dan Zaksheske joins Dan Dakich to highlight how Ryan Clark's end at ESPN went down.

Within hours of Ryan Clark’s dismissal, many of the biggest names in sports media had already reac...
body_text:
OutKick reporter Dan Zaksheske joins Dan Dakich to highlight how Ryan Clark's end at ESPN went down.

Within hours of Ryan Clark’s dismissal, many of the biggest names in sports media had already reached the same conclusion: Clark was the victim and ESPN was the villain.

Stephen A. Smith said he confronted ESPN executives over the decision and called Clark "arguably the best NFL analyst in America." Emmanuel Acho accused the network of handling the situation in a "cowardly" and "wildly disrespectful" manner.

Sarah Spain described Clark as "another incredibly smart, thoughtful, nuanced talent let go," while Pro Football Talk’s Mike Florio declared that Clark took "the high road" after "ESPN took the low road."

Ryan Clark's ESPN dismissal sparked outrage from Stephen A. Smith and Jemele Hill, but his history of apologies and friction tells a different story. (Patrick McDermott/Getty Images)

Hill suggested Clark had "a hater," perhaps multiple haters, inside ESPN. She attacked OutKick as a "disgusting" right-wing "rag" and theorized that someone within the network intentionally leaked Clark’s dismissal to OutKick in order to create a conservative media narrative against him. She offered no evidence for that claim.

But the fact that Clark’s dismissal leaked so widely and so quickly raises a question that his defenders have mostly avoided: How many enemies had he made inside ESPN?

Much of the criticism centered on the fact that Clark was informed during a live edition of NFL Live. The assumption was that ESPN knowingly allowed a longtime employee to begin the show before abruptly firing him during a break.

I think ESPN planned to inform Clark later and simply failed to anticipate that news of his dismissal would leak first. Once OutKick contacted the network with a confirmed story, ESPN realized it had lost control of the timeline and had to tell Clark before he learned about it publicly.

My colleague Bobby Burack and I developed separate lines of reporting that the network was preparing to part ways with Clark. Four sources confirmed the information. Despite concerns that alerting ESPN could jeopardize the story, I contacted the network’s communications department at 4 p.m. and asked ESPN to confirm or deny the report before our publication deadline.

ESPN asked us to wait until 5:45 p.m. while it was "trying to chase this down." We declined but told the network we would add anything it provided before that time. It's reasonable that ESPN wanted more time to inform Clark before the news became public.

ESPN simply lost control of the Ryan Clark story by failing to recognize how many enemies he made during his time at the company. (Nic Antaya/Getty Images)

At 4:49 p.m., ESPN unexpectedly told us we could "stick to" our original timeline. Fourteen minutes later, Andrew Marchand published a brief report in The Athletic. OutKick’s more detailed story followed one minute after that.

The most logical explanation is that ESPN believed it still had time to manage Clark’s exit quietly. Once OutKick informed the network that four sources had already confirmed the decision, executives were forced to accelerate a conversation they apparently had not planned to have during NFL Live.

ESPN’s mistake was not deciding to embarrass Clark on live television. It was failing to anticipate that the information would leak before the network was ready to tell him.

That raises a more uncomfortable question than the ones Clark’s defenders have been asking: Why were so many people inside or around ESPN willing to talk about his dismissal?

Hill may be correct that Clark had "haters" inside the company.

What she never seriously considered, or chose to ignore, is how he might have acquired them.

I worked at ESPN for years and spent time in rooms with Clark. He appeared on several shows I produced. The thing I remember most clearly is how uncomfortable he could make those rooms.

Clark often carried himself as though he were the smartest person present, and he made sure everyone knew it. That dynamic was especially difficult for producers and other employees beneath him in ESPN’s hierarchy. There was little room to challenge him or offer feedback that amounted to anything other than telling him he was great.

Ryan Clark's ESPN dismissal drew defense from Stephen A. Smith, Jemele Hill and Emmanuel Acho, but his history of controversies and internal friction tells a fuller story. (Ric Tapia/Getty Images)

I distinctly remember one NFL season when talent, producers and other ESPN employees gathered on Sunday afternoons to watch the games in a large conference room. There were usually 25 or 30 people there. The mood was light. Chris Berman often sat near the front and cracked jokes throughout the afternoon.

Clark joined the group one Sunday and sat in the back with his arms folded. Several times, someone offered an opinion about a game and Clark scoffed. Eventually, most people stopped talking. I left and watched the games from a control room because he had completely ruined the experience for me.

How dare anyone offer an opinion when the world’s greatest NFL analyst was available to explain what everyone else was apparently too stupid to understand?

That was one afternoon years ago. It does not prove why ESPN fired Clark, nor does it mean every person who worked with him had the same experience.

It does, however, make the speed of the leak less surprising to me.

People rarely risk their relationships with a company to leak sensitive personnel news about a universally beloved colleague.

Clark's defenders largely presented his dismissal as though it happened in a vacuum.

Clark was forced to apologize after making inaccurate claims on ESPN about the investigation into former LSU receiver Kyren Lacy’s role in a fatal crash. He presented claims from Lacy’s attorney as established fact and accused law enforcement of wrongdoing before Louisiana State Police released additional evidence that contradicted key parts of the story Clark told viewers.

He later acknowledged that he had failed to provide complete and accurate information.

Clark also admitted on ESPN that he had argued in favor of Bryce Young over C.J. Stroud despite privately believing Stroud was the superior quarterback. His explanation was that Young needed someone to support him publicly.

That’s a remarkable admission from an analyst whose job is supposed to be giving viewers his honest evaluation.

ESPN'S RYAN CLARK WANTS TO BE AN NFL GM, LIES ABOUT C.J. STROUD & BRYCE YOUNG TO PROVE HE CAN DO IT

Then there was Clark’s public feud with Robert Griffin III, during which he suggested Griffin was not qualified to discuss Black women because he is married to a White woman. The dispute became deeply personal and reportedly frustrated people inside ESPN. This led to another Clark apology.

Then there was Clark’s on-air confrontation with Peter Schrager that became another viral controversy. Clark later apologized (again), but the exchange likely contributed to concerns among network executives.

An on-air and subsequent off-air feud with ESPN colleague Peter Schrager was one of many examples of the problems Ryan Clark created for himself at the network. (Logan Bowles/Getty Images)

Plus, Clark’s difficult relationship with ESPN management was hardly limited to interpersonal friction.

His 2024 contract negotiations became a public spectacle. Clark allowed his deal to expire, posted videos about his status and openly applied pressure to the network while seeking a substantial raise. ESPN eventually brought him back on a deal reportedly worth around $2 million per year, but the process was contentious.

ESPN executives do not enjoy being publicly cornered during contract negotiations, particularly by employees using their own platforms to rally outside pressure against the company.

My belief is that Clark’s 2024 extension was always going to be his final contract with ESPN. The network may have decided that keeping him temporarily was preferable to another public fight at the time, while also concluding it had no desire to negotiate with him again.

That would help explain why ESPN was willing to move on despite his high salary and recent extension. The decision may not have been prompted by one argument, one apology or one uncomfortable television segment. It may have been the culmination of a relationship that had already run its course.

Essentially, Clark’s tenure had become more complicated than his defenders acknowledged.

Spain praised him as "thoughtful" and "nuanced" without mentioning the multiple inaccurate claims that forced him to apologize on national television. Hill assumed the existence of internal enemies proved something nefarious without examining Clark’s own role in creating friction. Smith emphasized Clark’s talent, which was never really in dispute.

Acho offered perhaps the most exaggerated defense, claiming Clark helped build NFL Live into the "juggernaut it is today."

Acho pointed to the show’s Sports Emmy victories as evidence. But awards demonstrate that people within the television industry and the Hollywood elites like a program. They do not necessarily demonstrate that the public at large enjoys a show.

The available audience data show that NFL Live drew larger audiences before Clark became one of the faces of the program than it has in recent years. ESPN reported that the show averaged 688,000 viewers during a comparable early-season period in 2013 and 624,000 in 2012. It averaged roughly 486,000 in 2015, 492,000 in 2016, 449,000 in 2021 and 440,000 in 2025.

That doesn't mean Clark hurt NFL Live. It does make the claim that he "built" a show that debuted in 1998 into a modern ratings juggernaut impossible to support.

The argument becomes even more amusing when compared with Acho’s commentary about Caitlin Clark. He has argued that the WNBA would be better off without her, even though she has driven enormous gains in television audiences, attendance and general interest.

By Acho’s standard, Caitlin Clark has a much stronger claim to having "built" the modern WNBA than Ryan Clark does to having built NFL Live.

Apparently, the meaning of the word depends on which Clark is receiving the credit.

By the time ESPN reached this week’s layoffs, Clark was an expensive employee who had publicly pressured the company during his previous contract negotiation, accumulated enemies inside the building and repeatedly created controversies outside it.

ESPN TO PART WAYS WITH CAM NEWTON AS PART OF TALENT LAYOFFS THAT ALSO INCLUDED RYAN CLARK

The sports media narrative was that ESPN betrayed Ryan Clark.

OutKick's reporting suggested something different.

ESPN didn’t lose control of the story because OutKick uncovered some closely guarded secret. It lost control because too many people already knew.

And too many people were willing to talk.

title: Indian police cracked down on 'cockroach' protesters. They went home and made memes about it
excerpt:
CJP protesters are responding to Monday's police violence with memes online

On Monday, 20-year-old Chhavi (name changed) paused outside a Delhi metro station on her way to what would become one of th...
body_text:
CJP protesters are responding to Monday's police violence with memes online

On Monday, 20-year-old Chhavi (name changed) paused outside a Delhi metro station on her way to what would become one of the largest anti-government demonstrations against Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government in the Indian capital in recent years.

She balanced her phone against a railing, stepped back and filmed a quick "fit check" - a video showing off her outfit before the protest.

"Going to get beaten by cops, guys," she smiled into the camera.

Hours later, police fired tear gas and charged demonstrators with batons as tens of thousands of supporters of the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) – a youth-led movement that grew from protests over exam irregularities into a broader campaign for government accountability – marched towards parliament.

They demanded the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over exam paper leaks and recruitment scandals. Pradhan has refused to resign, accusing the opposition of exploiting students for political gain.

Authorities suspended mobile internet around the protest site. By evening, protesters said scores had been beaten by police, while police said several officers had also been injured.

When Chhavi finally uploaded the videos that evening, one thing stood out. Before the march, she joked about being beaten by police. Hours later, she was still smiling - posing beside riot officers, flashing victory signs, filming through crowds and even interviewing an injured protester.

The clips were among thousands of reels, memes and first-person dispatches that flooded Instagram once protesters regained internet access. Some were raw with anger. Others were breathless, frightened or quietly defiant. Yet it was often the sardonic humour that drew the eye.

A lot of protesters posted "fit checks" - a viral Instagram trend - ahead of the march

One young woman calmly reapplied her lipstick in front of a line of riot police. Another joked she was "serving face at the protest because the government couldn't serve justice".

One reel began as a routine outfit reveal before abruptly cutting to protesters fleeing tear gas. Another gave a deadpan "review" of tear gas, rating its smell and sting like a new restaurant.

To many watching online, the humour seemed strangely at odds with the violence. For the young people making the videos, there was no contradiction.

None of the protesters interviewed for this story spoke about algorithms, engagement or going viral. They described filming as almost inseparable from protesting itself.

And when they turned to humour, it was almost always self-directed: joking about the kilometres logged fleeing baton charges or the absurdity of filming an outfit reveal moments before running into tear gas. The jokes did not erase the fear. They coexisted with it.

Experts say the trend hints at a broader shift in how a generation raised online experiences protest.

"Older generations separated political life from everyday life," said Joyojeet Pal, a professor at the University of Michigan who studies technology and society.

"Younger generations grow up with politics alongside their memes, their family pictures and their daily commutes."

For Chhavi, documenting the protest continued once the crackdown began. Some commenters later accused her of treating the violence as content.

From one-liners to satirical digs, the placards have become one of the defining features of the protests

"But I was adrenaline-soaked, scared, petrified," she said. "It was either laugh in the face of fear or cry."

That instinct to keep filming, even as the situation turned violent, is one that Payal Arora, a digital anthropologist at Utrecht University, says has become increasingly common around the world. Smartphones have turned ordinary people into chroniclers of political upheaval, recording moments that might otherwise disappear.

"The act of capturing a protest is often an end in itself," she said. "Their message is more like: 'This happened. We were there. We will remember.'"

The humour, too, followed a pattern. No young protester described the day as funny. They spoke instead of tear gas, baton charges and the confusion of watching a peaceful march descend into chaos.

India's 'cockroach' protest continues a day after police crackdown

India has a new political superstar - a cockroach

Why are thousands protesting for education reform in India?

The hunger-striking engineer demanding education reforms in India

People often mistake that humour for detachment, says Nishant Shah, director of Digital Narratives Center at  School of Journalism & Communication at the Chinese University of Hong Kong. In reality, he argues, it serves almost the opposite purpose.

"Politics is everyday, emotional and experiential," he said.

Humour has long been part of protest movements. It can create solidarity, ease tension and help people endure moments of fear.

"Even when the cause is brutal and the fight fierce," Shah said, "there is joy, laughter, songs and hope. Without those things, protests don't sustain themselves."

Ishaan (name changed), 21, came to Delhi for his first protest. When tear gas landed nearby, he reached for his phone instead of leaving.

Police fired tear gas to disperse protesters as they tried to reach parliament

The reel he posted later showed white smoke drifting across the street beneath a simple caption: "Delhi Police doesn't realise I'm from Jamuna Paar."

For most Delhiites, the joke needed little explanation. Jamuna Paar - literally "across the Yamuna" - is shorthand for the city's eastern neighbourhoods and the subject of endless teasing over their neglected civic infrastructure.

"The smell there is worse than the tear gas," Ishan said, laughing.

He told the BBC the joke was never about pretending nothing had happened.

"It was about refusing to let fear dictate the memory of the day," he said.

Protest movements have always developed their own emotional vocabulary to help people endure moments of fear together.

India's freedom movement had songs and speeches. Trade unions marched behind banners. Student movements embraced posters and street theatre. More recently, the anti-citizenship protests were marked by murals and public readings of the Constitution, while the farmers' movement became synonymous with tractor rallies and livestreams from Delhi's borders.

The CJP protests spoke in the idiom of a generation raised online. To dismiss that language as frivolous, Shah argues, is to misunderstand what it is doing.

"We often make the mistake of trying to understand new technologies through older ways of thinking," Shah said.

Manit even logged a running activity while evading a police baton charge

But a meme is not simply today's version of a political poster, he said. It gathers meaning as people remake it, parody it and pass it on. Sharing it becomes another way of participating in the protest.

That was true for Manit, an 18-year-old engineering student. It was his first protest, and he had almost decided not to film.

"I was scared," he said. Then he looked around. "Everyone was recording, talking to the camera, just being themselves. So I asked my friend to record me."

At first glance, the reel resembled the workout videos he usually posted. Only the final frame revealed the distance on his fitness app had been logged while fleeing police baton charges.

"I escaped the lathi charge," he laughed. "I ran and ran and ended up completing my workout too."

"Not all young people read the news," he said. "If humour can make important conversations accessible, why wouldn't we use it?"

*Some names have been changed to protect the identity of the person

Follow BBC News India on Instagram, external, YouTube,, external X, external and Facebook, external.

title: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia
excerpt:
Trump energy secretary hails agreement that could potentially allow kingdom to enrich uranium

The US has signed a landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could potentially allow the kingdom to e...
body_text:
Trump energy secretary hails agreement that could potentially allow kingdom to enrich uranium

The US has signed a landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could potentially allow the kingdom to enrich uranium.

In a statement on Wednesday, the US Department of Energy said its secretary, Chris Wright, and his Saudi counterpart, Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman, signed a “peaceful nuclear cooperation agreement”, along with a “bilateral safeguards agreement”.

According to the department, the partnership is expected to expand American nuclear technology exports, create “high-paying” US jobs, and “strengthen America’s energy and national security posture”.

“These agreements reflect our two nations’ shared commitment to strengthening US-Saudi commercial relations, delivering prosperity at home and security to our allies abroad,” Wright said in a statement.

“Rest assured, these agreements uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation, while relying on the world’s best nuclear technology and scientists, designed right here in the United States. Thanks to President Trump, the American nuclear renaissance is under way and will deliver long-term benefits to the American and Saudi people.”

The deal, which the energy department said would also help “meet Saudi energy needs”, will now be transmitted to Congress for review. It could face pushback from lawmakers concerned about how the deal will be monitored.

In a statement to CBS, Dan Sumner, chief executive of Westinghouse Electric Company – an American nuclear reactor company expected to support Saudi Arabia’s nuclear development – said: “The US government’s announcement to launch a civil nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia is a landmark step that strengthens energy security, expands opportunities for US industry and workers, and drives significant long-term economic growth and investment benefits for both countries.”

Although the full details of the deal have yet to be released, it comes at a controversial time, as the US remains engaged in its war with Iran – a conflict that the defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, says has cost $37.5bn so far.

Just a day earlier, Hegseth appeared before the US Senate to request additional funding from the Senate appropriations committee, as the US continues its campaign to prevent Iran from developing uranium enrichment capabilities and obtaining a nuclear weapon.

title: Rubio says US, China must ‘work through’ big differences at ASEAN meeting
excerpt: United States Secretary of State Marco Rubio has met his Chinese counterpart Wang Yi at a gathering of southeast Asian foreign ministers, to discuss how to manage bilateral tensions ahead of a planned...
body_text:
United States Secretary of State Marco Rubio has met his Chinese counterpart Wang Yi at a gathering of southeast Asian foreign ministers, to discuss how to manage bilateral tensions ahead of a planned summit between US President Donald Trump and China’s Xi Jinping.

The meeting on the sidelines of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) gathering of foreign ministers in Manila on Wednesday came amid tensions over China’s conduct in the disputed South China Sea and the US war against Iran. ASEAN countries have expressed concerns about both issues at the meeting.

Relations between Washington and Beijing have also been complicated by Trump’s accusations that China meddled in US elections, allegations Beijing has dismissed as “pure fabrication”.

Rubio said the US and China have “great differences” that are likely to persist “for the foreseeable future”, but that he and Wang discussed the need to manage those disagreements to ensure Xi has a “very positive visit” to the US. Xi is expected to visit in September.

Wang said the US must “respect China’s core interests … effectively manage differences and disagreements, and address China’s legitimate concerns”, according to an official Chinese summary of their meeting. It described the talks as “pragmatic, positive and constructive”.

The two sides agreed to “make sound preparations for the next stage of high-level exchanges,” China’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs said in a statement.

Wednesday’s meeting came two days after a confrontation between Chinese and Philippine vessels in the South China Sea left a Philippine sailor injured, leading both countries to summon each other’s ambassador.

The incident this week took place on Monday at the disputed Second Thomas Shoal. The Philippines ⁠said Chinese coastguard struck one of ⁠the Philippine navy staff on the head with a wooden baton, while China said the Philippine personnel were the first to attack and accused Philippine vessels ⁠of ramming a Chinese patrol boat.

Rubio said the altercation benefited nobody, adding that Washington had ⁠reiterated in all its meetings that it was bound by a defence treaty to stand up for Manila.

“We intend to live up to our treaty obligations, and I think we’ve done so, and will continue to do so,” he said.

Rubio’s remarks echo an opinion piece he published in Philippine media on Tuesday about Beijing’s conduct in the South China Sea. Rubio warned of “dire new threats” if those waters were to “fall under the control of a power willing to use commerce as a geopolitical weapon”.

Beijing claims sovereignty over almost the entire South China Sea via a line on its maps that cuts into the exclusive economic zones of Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines and Vietnam, and is at the centre of long-running disputes over a multitude of islands.

Disputes between the Philippines and China have increased in recent years as Manila takes a bolder stance while pursuing closer defence ties with the US, Australia and Japan.

The US has increased military financing for the Philippines to $100mn for the 2026 fiscal year and is providing an additional $9mn to expand a coastguard pier on the island of Palawan “to enhance Philippine maritime ⁠law enforcement presence and sustain operations in the South China Sea”, the State Department said in a fact sheet.

Discussing the US relationship with ASEAN member states, Rubio said the security and prosperity of the US and southeast Asia were closely interconnected.

“I believe very much that the future and the story of the 21st century will be written in this region and by how events unfold here,” he said.

Rubio also addressed the situation with Iran in a meeting with ASEAN foreign ministers, claiming that Tehran was responsible for the latest escalation.

“The problem we’re having right now is that they’re not serious about talks,” Rubio said. “If they’re serious, we’re serious. If they’re not, then we will do what’s necessary to protect our interests, and also the interests of our allies.”

The US has carried out strikes on Iran for eleven consecutive nights, including targeting roads, bridges and power plants in recent attacks, all but shattering an interim peace deal agreed by the two sides last month.

Rubio stressed that Iran could not be allowed to control the Strait of ‌Hormuz, as it could set a dangerous precedent for other parts of the world.

The top US diplomat also confirmed he would meet on Thursday with his Russian counterpart Sergei Lavrov to discuss the Ukraine war, saying that Washington remained open to playing a constructive role in ending the conflict.

title: William Shatner and daughter Melanie reveal they battled Stage 4 cancer at the same time
excerpt:
Shatner told Fox News Digital it's only natural to focus on different things at different stages in life.

William Shatner and his 61-year-old daughter revealed this week that the two of them battled ...
body_text:
Shatner told Fox News Digital it's only natural to focus on different things at different stages in life.

William Shatner and his 61-year-old daughter revealed this week that the two of them battled cancer at the same time several years ago.

Melanie Shatner Gretsch first discovered her cancer four years ago when she woke up in the middle of the night and felt a little lump on her breast she thought might be a bug bite.

She told People magazine she decided to have it checked out despite a mammogram not finding anything just five months earlier.

DAVE COULIER REVEALS 45-POUND WEIGHT LOSS, GRUELING SIDE EFFECTS FROM CANCER FIGHTS

William Shatner with his daughter, Melanie Shatner; her husband, Joel Gretsch; and their daughter, Willow, in 2006. (Barry King/WireImage)

Although the technician didn’t find anything during a scan the next day, "something pushed me back and said, ‘No, have her recheck it.’ She put the wand exactly where I put my finger and under her breath said, ‘Now, I would have missed that.’"

She was diagnosed with HER2-positive breast cancer that had spread to lymph nodes in her chest.

Shatner said his daughter was "very conscious of the importance of her life for herself and for her family. I was devastated."

Gretsch is a mother to two daughters she shares with husband Joel Gretsch.

Gretsch said her father told her not to worry about the medical costs and to send him the bills.

"Then you made me laugh so hard when I was still going to the doctor a year later," she added. "You said, ‘You’ve got to get well. We’re going to be in the poorhouse.’"

LIKE WHAT YOU’RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

William Shatner at MegaCon Orlando 2026 in March. (Getty Images)

For the next 18 months, her life was filled with multiple rounds of chemo, 30 radiation treatments and a double mastectomy.

"Chemo was the most physically draining, and the radiation I found so emotionally draining," she admitted.

Just as she was nearing the end of her treatments, a lump appeared on her dad’s cheek.

He said a family doctor told him it was just a blocked salivary gland that was nothing to worry about, but a doctor friend whose advice he asked while they were horseback riding advised him to get it removed.

CAMERON MATHISON SAYS NOTICING SUBTLE BODY CHANGES ‘SAVED MY LIFE’ AFTER CANCER DIAGNOSIS

"He placed his hands on my cheeks, and he said with love in his voice, ‘You better get this out,’" Shatner explained.

In June 2023, Shatner was diagnosed with Stage 4 malignant melanoma that had spread to his lungs and brain.

"I remember vividly thinking, ‘I don’t have the strength to take care of myself and lose my father at the same time,’" Gretsch admitted.

"They said, ‘You got cancer. If you came in later, you would have been dead.’ See? That’s my life. I think the universe is taking care of me."

The 95-year-old had the tumor removed and underwent two years of immunotherapy.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

"He wouldn’t stop his life," his daughter told People magazine. "He kept working and moving forward. Whenever he was home, Joel and I would bring dinner to him and watch movies, just always made sure that he’s not alone."

William Shatner on "Star Trek" (Bettmann Archive)

Shatner added, "We got the perfect doctors for her cancer and my cancer."

The "Star Trek" actor, who was the oldest man in space when he went up in a Blue Origin rocket, has stayed busy with two new books and two records, all expected to come out next year.

"I’m healthier now than I’ve been in years," Shatner said. "The brain is like a muscle. It feeds on activity."

Near the end of 2024, both were declared cancer free.

"It was the third time in my life I had heard him cry," Gretsch admitted of telling him her cancer was gone.

Shatner said the experience has given him a new perspective on life.

"I’m thinking of the miracle of a hummingbird, and of my dog taking a ball and my children," he said. "I’m aware of the magic of it all."

Brie Stimson is a Los Angeles-based entertainment and breaking news writer for Fox News Digital.

title: A year after deadly jet crash at Bangladesh school, families demand answers
excerpt:
Families of victims say they are still waiting for answers about the deadly jet crash

It was a normal day for sixth-grade student Nure Jannat Yusha when suddenly, a Bangladeshi air force jet crashed ...
body_text:
Families of victims say they are still waiting for answers about the deadly jet crash

It was a normal day for sixth-grade student Nure Jannat Yusha when suddenly, a Bangladeshi air force jet crashed into her school in the capital, Dhaka, killing 36 people - most of them children.

Within minutes, Milestone School and College, a private institution buzzing with student activity, was transformed into a scene of chaos and raging flames.

It was the deadliest aviation disaster the country had seen in decades.

Earlier this week, families and survivors gathered to mark one year since the incident - many of them students who say they are struggling to return to normal life, Yusha among them.

"Even now, if I go to school and see a plane, I feel afraid. I can't sleep at night. In my dreams, someone is throwing me into the fire," she told BBC Bengali.

She was one of more than 160 people who were injured in the crash at Milestone School - and among those at the memorial held at a hospital in Dhaka on Tuesday, where the families of some victims say they are still waiting for answers about the deadly crash.

Yusha's mother Yasin Akter says her child had once dreamt of becoming a dancer - an aspiration that has since faded.

"My daughter screams all day and is afraid," she says, adding that Yusha has become paranoid about planes falling from the sky.

"Earlier she was interested in studies. Now she does not want to study any more."

Nure Jannat Yusha, on left, was one of the many students who suffered burn injuries after the crash

On 21 July 2025, a training aircraft took off from a Bangladesh Air Force Base in Dhaka at around 13:00 local time (07:00 GMT) and crashed soon after in the Uttara neighbourhood.

Images from the scene hours after the crash showed scores of emergency service workers sorting through charred rubble in an attempt to find survivors.

Authorities announced that an investigation commission had been formed to investigate the crash, but in the days that followed, hundreds of student protesters took to the streets, clashing with police as they demanded accountability over the disaster and changes to training flights regulations.

The committee's findings, which it reportedly submitted to the government last November, have not yet been made public.

According to a copy of the full report obtained by BBC Bengali, it highlighted issues like Milestone School's lack of compliance with building codes. It also found more than 600 buildings - including schools and hospitals - located near Dhaka's international airport that had violated height restrictions.

The report also found that the pilot lacked sufficient flight training and proper ground supervision. And there was no technical fault with the aircraft, the report said - contradicting an early statement by the Bangladeshi Armed Forces that the jet had experienced a mechanical fault after taking off.

The Bangladesh Armed Forces did not respond to a request for comment.

"No one can give a guarantee that another plane will not fall onto this school in the future," Mansur Helal, the husband of one victim, told the crowd during the memorial, referencing a recent BBC documentary on the committee report.

"This is truly a horrific situation. Every day around 2,500-3,000 students go to that school."

Helal's wife, schoolteacher Mahreen Chowdhury, had died in hospital from extensive burns she had suffered while rescuing students.

"The building in which this accident happened had only one door. How can there be only one door for 800-900 students?" Helal added.

Helal and others are calling for the government to release the full report.

They are also urging better government support for survivors undergoing treatment, as well as compensation for them and victims' families - which was recommended in the investigative committee report but yet to be implemented.

Images from the scene hours after the crash showed scores of emergency service workers sorting through charred rubble

Health Minister Sardar Sakhawat Hossain, who was at the event on Tuesday, said the government would support survivors who require further medical treatment. And Zubaida Rahman, the wife of Prime Minister Tarique Rahman, hailed the courage shown by survivors as "a source of inspiration for the nation".

But many of the students who attended the memorial this week, sporting burn scars, are still grappling with their new reality.

Mehreen, who's in the fifth grade, says she is no longer able to go to school because of her injuries.

"If I get a little sunlight, my whole body burns. For that reason, I do not go to school any more."

It has also affected her sleep and learning ability: "Earlier, if I read something, I would memorise it in two minutes. Now I can't memorise a line in two hours.

"I can't sleep at night. When I do sleep, I often have strange dreams. I still can't forget that incident."

BBC reveals official report into deadly military jet crash at school

Screams and chaos: The moments after Bangladesh fighter jet hit a primary school

title: US and Saudi Arabia announce nuclear cooperation deal
excerpt:
The United States and Saudi Arabia have reached a civilian nuclear cooperation deal, the administration of President Donald Trump announced, as Washington and Riyadh push to deepen their ties.

The US...
body_text:
The United States and Saudi Arabia have reached a civilian nuclear cooperation deal, the administration of President Donald Trump announced, as Washington and Riyadh push to deepen their ties.

The US Department of Energy hailed the agreement as “historic” on Wednesday, saying that American companies will provide nuclear technology to the kingdom.

The deal consists of an accord under Section 123 of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954, known as a 123 agreement, “alongside an accompanying bilateral safeguards agreement”, the department said.

“These agreements reflect our two nations’ shared commitment to strengthening US-Saudi commercial relations, delivering prosperity at home and security to our allies abroad,” Energy Secretary Chris Wright said in a statement.

“Rest assured, these agreements uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation, while relying on the world’s best nuclear technology and scientists, designed right here in the United States.”

Some US media reports have suggested that the deal will allow Saudi Arabia to enrich uranium domestically rather than import nuclear fuel from the US.

The Department of Energy’s statement did not provide details on enrichment. If the reports are confirmed, the deal is likely to face opposition in Washington.

The statement called the agreement a “decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership” and said it will now be submitted to Congress for review.

Trump has gone to war with Iran to prevent the country from enriching uranium. The Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT) does not prohibit uranium enrichment – a process of isolating and garnering the most radioactive variant, or isotope, of the uranium atom.

Highly enriched uranium can be used to produce nuclear weapons.

Israel is the only country in the region that is widely believed to possess a nuclear arsenal.

The Trump administration said the cooperation deal with Saudi Arabia will create jobs in the US, “reinforce global nonproliferation standards”, and strengthen the strategic partnership with Riyadh.

The announcement comes as the war between the US and Iran continues to rage, with Trump insisting that the goal of the conflict is to prevent Iran from acquiring a nuclear weapon, which Tehran denies seeking.

The US and Israel bombed Iran’s nuclear facilities last year. During the ongoing hostilities, Iran has launched missiles and drones against bases hosting US troops in the region, as well as some energy facilities in the Gulf.

title: Australia news live: China hits back at Penny Wong’s comments; Victorian teachers due to begin strike
excerpt:
China’s foreign ministry says ‘relevant countries’ should ‘stop stirring up tension’. Follow live

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Australia is not ruling out stepping up ...
body_text:
China’s foreign ministry says ‘relevant countries’ should ‘stop stirring up tension’. Follow live

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Australia is not ruling out stepping up joint patrols of the South China Sea following “destabilising” missile testing in the region, Australian Associated Press reports.

The foreign affairs minister, Penny Wong, was asked last night about the potential of more regular regional sea patrols in tandem with the United States and Japan, following China’s launching of nuclear-capable missiles.

“The maritime zone is critical to stability in the region so, when we have a conversation about the South China Sea, that is something I’m sure we should all keep in mind,” she told reporters in Manila where she is attending the Asean foreign ministers’ meeting.

“We are concerned about some of the aggression we are seeing, we are concerned about some of the destabilising conduct we are seeing, and the countries of the region have a choice to make about how we all respond to that.”

Wong met her Chinese counterpart Wang Yi on the sidelines of the talks on Tuesday evening to highlight her concerns.

“You speak about the importance of mutual understanding,’’ she said.

“I agree, and I expect we will discuss our concerns ... including around peace and stability in our region, safe and professional military conduct, and the importance of transparency and reassurance of intent.”

China’s foreign ministry hit back at Wong’s comments, saying Australia was not party to the South China Sea issue and had no right to intervene.

“China urges relevant countries to stop stirring up tension and inciting confrontation,” spokesman ​Lin Jian told a regular briefing in Beijing.

It came as Wong prepared to warn China in a speech today that Australia would not be bullied by Beijing as it builds up its military strength.

Self-reported domestic violence incidents declining in New South Wales but deaths and hospitalisations fail to fall

New data shows self-reported domestic violence incidents in New South Wales have declined, but the number of women and children murdered or hospitalised in domestic and family violence incidents over the past eight years has not significantly budged.

The Bureau of Crime Statistics and Research (Boscar) released a report this morning that analysed five indicators over the eight years to June 2025 to determine if domestic and family violence had increased in the state.

It found three measures showed no significant change, including the number of women and children murdered or hospitalised in domestic and family violence incidents, and the number of police recorded incidents of grievous bodily harm.

Bocsar found two other domestic violence indicators, based on the annual ABS’ survey on crime victimisation, showed significant declines.

This included self-reported violent victimisation, which dropped from 4.1% in 2017 to 2018 to 3% in 2024 to 2025. Also, self-reported domestic and family violence victimisation, which declined from 0.91% in July 2016 to June 2019 to 0.65% in July 2022 to June 2025.

Boscar executive director Jackie Fitzgerald said:

double quotation markThe decline in self-reported violence is significant because it reflects underlying levels of violence in the community, not just what is reported to police. It suggests that, despite rising reports of domestic violence to police, domestic violence in the community is not increasing and may be trending down.

There’s a strong perception that violence against women is rising, which is understandable given increases in police-recorded incidents, heightened media attention and greater public awareness.

There are more people in NSW’ prisons on domestic violence charges than ever before, according to Bocsar’s latest custody data, and police recorded incidents have increased. Fitzgerald said the latter “appears to be driven by more proactive policing and increased reporting, rather than clear evidence of underlying violence”.

Last week the nation reached another crisis point for domestic and family violence after two women and two girls were allegedly murdered in four days. This included Lavanya Chappa and 13-year-old Layla Jeffery in Victoria, Jana Armstrong in Queensland, and a 17-year-old Yolngu girl in the Northern Territory. In May, a Sydney woman and her two children were allegedly murdered.

Labor’s national conference kicks off in Adelaide

Thousands of Labor true believers have descended on Adelaide for the party’s 50th national conference, laying out the party’s platform which will cast “practical” policies as the key to combating the anxiety and frustration gripping the electorate.

Day one on Thursday will start with an address from incoming Labor president Kate Ellis. The South Australian premier, Peter Malinauskas, will also give a speech, before the prime minister, Anthony Albanese, takes to the stage for his address.

Debate on Thursday will be led by the treasurer, Jim Chalmers, on economic issues, as well as energy minister Chris Bowen later in the day on the climate and environment.

Already announced is financial assistance for students to complete mandatory placements under a major expansion of the federal government’s paid work experience program. You can read about it here:

Jason Clare says domestic violence is a ‘crime’, not a ‘two-way street’ in rebuke of Pauline Hanson’s comments

Jason Clare was also asked about widely criticised comments from Pauline Hanson on domestic violence.

On Wednesday, she told the radio station 4BC domestic violence in Australia was a scourge but that society was under “so much pressure”, when asked to call out the issue, describing it as “wrong” but a “two-way street”.

double quotation markOne in four Australian women are victims of domestic violence … that’s one in four women who are violently attacked by people who tell them that they love them. You know, that’s not a two-way street. That’s a crime.

Asked if it would be a “big topic” at this week’s Labor conference, he said “I suspect it’ll be a topic”.

double quotation markI think when people hear these comments, if they’re thinking about voting for Pauline Hanson, they think, hang on. I didn’t sign up for that.

Federal education minister says teachers should be paid as well as ‘they possibly can be’

The education minister, Jason Clare, appeared on ABC News Breakfast this morning as Victorian teachers go on strike today for the second time this year.

Asked what he had to say to parents impacted by the strike, he said: “I hope this gets sorted out as quick as it possibly can.”

double quotation markThe teachers do the most important job in the world. I want to see them being paid as good as they possibly can be. We know that when you pay people more, more people want to do the job. In New South Wales, we’ve seen the evidence of that …

I know the Victorian government’s got a big offer on the table here of a 28% pay rise, which would be even bigger than New South Wales. So that’s a good thing and I really hope that the Victorian government and the teachers can get together and sort this out for parents and for our kids as quick as possible.

Asked if Victorian teachers should get a better offer, Clare said he wouldn’t get involved in individual negotiations but there was a teacher shortage and they should be paid “what they deserve”.

double quotation markI think that the government is determined to get a good outcome here for our teachers and I’m pretty confident that this will be worked out.

Thanks Martin Farrer for kicking things off, it’s Caitlin Cassidy here to take you through today’s news. I’m on caitlin.cassidy@theguardian.com if you’d like to get in touch.

Kennedy and Hodge to lead Australia at Commonwealth Games

Pole vault star Nina Kennedy and decorated para-swimmer Tim Hodge will lead the Australian team at the Commonwealth Games opening ceremony, Australian Associated Press reports.

Kennedy will carry the flag and Hodge will be the Games baton bearer when the team marches out early tomorrow morning AEST at Glasgow’s Hydro indoor arena.

While Kennedy will be the 24th Australian to carry the flag at a Commonwealth Games opening ceremony, the baton bearer is a new honour.

Rather than a single King’s baton, for the first time all 74 countries and territories were given their own to decorate and bring to the opening ceremony.

King Charles III started the main Scotland baton relay in March last year and it contains the full message he will read at the opening ceremony. The other batons each contain one word from his message.

Kennedy and Hodge won gold four years ago at the Birmingham Games and remain at the peak of their sports.

After winning pole vault gold at the Paris Olympics, Kennedy needed an extended break because of a hamstring injury.

But Kennedy showed only last week that she will be one of the top competitors in Glasgow, breaking the Australian record with her 4.95m vault to win the Monaco Diamond League.

Students in Victoria will be kept at home as a second teacher strike disrupts public schools, leaving thousands of families to make alternative arrangements, Australian Associated Press reports.

More than 35,000 teachers, principals, and education support staff are expected to gather in Melbourne’s CBD today after rejecting a pay deal proposed by the state government.

While all public schools were set to remain open, many classes will be disrupted.

Australian Education Union Victorian members on Tuesday knocked back the government’s updated offer of a 28.3% pay rise over the next four years, calling for a better offer to combat ongoing resourcing and staffing pressures.

“The government’s done precious little to do anything meaningful to address that and our members are carrying the weight of all the excessive workload,” Victorian branch president Justin Mullaly said.

Union educators have been locked in a pay dispute with the government for nearly a year, with graduate teachers – the worst paid in the country – earning $13,000 less than those in the Northern Territory, the best paid.

Former deputy principal Melinda Davis said the teacher strike was not simply a dispute over pay, it was about addressing conditions and class sizes.

“The government has cut funding to government schools over the past 10 years and that slow decrease in funding has meant we have schools needing repairs, but also we have lost so many teachers in the profession,” the Swinburne education expert said.

More on that response from China to Penny Wong’s comments at Asean:

They came in a press conference held by foreign ministry spokesperson Lin Jian.
