title: Nolan Wells’ autopsy inconclusive as family presses for answers in teen’s death
excerpt:
Independent exam of Black teen found cause and manner of death ‘undetermined’ as family questions timeline

Sign up for the Breaking News US newsletter email

Nolan Wells’ cause of death and manner of...
body_text:
Independent exam of Black teen found cause and manner of death ‘undetermined’ as family questions timeline

Sign up for the Breaking News US newsletter email

Nolan Wells’ cause of death and manner of death are both “undetermined, pending investigation”, according to an independent autopsy, announced on Wednesday by Wells’ family’s attorney, Ben Crump.

The results came nearly three weeks after Wells, an 18 year old college freshman, was found dead.

The teen and a group of friends took a boat out to Horn Island, a barrier island a few miles off the Mississippi Gulf coast, on the Fourth of July. Wells’ friends returned from the outing; Wells did not. His family launched a missing person’s search for the teen that continued until the teen’s body was found on the island on 6 July.

During the press conference on Wednesday, Christine Wonsley, Wells’ mother, said that a member of the Cajun navy said that Wells was found fully clothed and face down. In the last known photos of the teen, he was not wearing a shirt.

Wells was Black, while many of the people on the island, including ones with whom he was pictured, are white. There were 300 to 400 people on the island, Crump said, something that makes it difficult to believe no one knows what happened to one of the few Black people in attendance.

Due to a lack of information about what exactly happened to the teen, conversations about Wells’ case on social media are full of misinformation and online speculation. Black leaders, both locally and nationally, have called for transparency and clarity.

Wells was laid to rest in Ocean Springs, Mississippi, his hometown, on Monday. Shortly after Wells’ body was found, the Jackson county sheriffm John Ledbetter, said he did not suspect foul play, something Wonsley noted was premature during Wednesday’s press conference.

Speaking at the NAACP national convention in Chicago, Crump said that the preliminary autopsy notes Wells’ body, when received following the first autopsy, was missing its interior neck structures. He also said that there was significant “red discoloration” in the tissue in the back of the teen’s head for unknown reasons.

The independent investigators were not able to see the body when it was recovered, and, by the time it was received on 10 July, Wells’ body was so decomposed that it was difficult to determine whether or not he was bruised or decomposed.

The autopsy, which was paid for by former NFL quarterback Colin Kaepernick, was conducted by former Washington DC, chief medical examiner Roger Mitchell. Wells’ body was received for the autopsy by Dr Mitchell after the state of Mississippi did its own autopsy. The state of Mississippi has not yet released its autopsy results.

Wells’ friends told authorities that the teen decided to stay on the island, something that his parents find difficult to believe.

“We always taught him that if you go with a group, you stay with a group,” Elmore Wonsley, his father, said during a Good Morning America appearance. “If you go with five, you come back with five. Do not separate from the group. Because I always said: ‘Safety is in numbers.’ So he knew to stay with this group, so why would he split from the group? I don’t know.”

title: Lil Wayne praises Trump's White House ballroom project, calls out critics who 'don't know nothing'
excerpt: President Donald Trump gives an exclusive tour of the White House Ballroom project, highlighting its design, immense capacity and enhanced security features. He emphasizes it's a gift to America for f...
body_text:
President Donald Trump gives an exclusive tour of the White House Ballroom project, highlighting its design, immense capacity and enhanced security features. He emphasizes it's a gift to America for future generations.

Lil Wayne is throwing his support behind one of President Donald Trump's most talked-about White House projects.

The rap icon praised Trump's plan to build a massive White House ballroom during a Monday appearance on the "Friends Keep Secrets" podcast, arguing the new venue would spare performers from being pushed outside for major events.

"I'm so glad to hear Trump say — when they was mad at him about building the ballroom, and he was like, 'Because everything we do we have to do it on the damn lawn," Wayne, whose real name is Dwayne Michael Carter Jr., said.

KAI TRUMP REVEALS NEW CELEBRITY CRUSH AFTER DITCHING 'OUTER BANKS' STAR DREW STARKEY

The rapper praised Trump's White House ballroom plan, saying he was glad to hear the president push for the upgrade. (Greg Doherty / Getty Images for 2K; Kevin Dietsch / Getty Images)

"So, people who don't know nothing [are] hearing that thinking he's just being Trump — no, for real, I was glad to hear him saying that," he added.

Wayne's praise stemmed from a memorable performance he gave at then-Vice President Kamala Harris' 2023 celebration marking the 50th anniversary of hip-hop.

LIKE WHAT YOU’RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Lil Wayne said that a 2023 Kamala Harris hip-hop anniversary performance felt like a "backyard barbecue." (AP Photo/Jacquelyn Martin)

TRUMP CLAIMS DONOR FUNDED WHITE HOUSE BALLROOM INCLUDES HIDDEN BUILD BELOW WITH SECURITY FOCUS

The event unfolded outside at Number One Observatory Circle, where the rapper said it felt like he was attending a "backyard barbecue."

Trump has repeatedly pitched the ballroom as a long-overdue upgrade for the White House, arguing it would provide a permanent indoor venue for state dinners, diplomatic receptions, performances and other major events while enhancing security for high-profile gatherings.

Lil Wayne said he was confused when he arrived for the performance, admitting he thought he was heading inside the actual White House. (Erika Goldring/Getty Images)

In a Truth Social post in May, Trump said the ballroom is "going up rapidly" on the east side of the White House and dismissed reports of ballooning costs.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

President Donald Trump holds design for a proposed White House ballroom while speaking to reporters. (Aaron Schwartz/CNP/Bloomberg)

He argued the project grew in price only because it was redesigned to be "approximately twice the size, and a far higher quality, than the original proposal," which he said, "would not have been adequate to handle the necessary events, meetings, and even future Inaugurations."

Trump said the original proposal carried a $200 million price tag, while the expanded project "will be something less than 400 Million Dollars."

"It will be magnificent, safe, and secure!" Trump wrote, adding that the ballroom is "coming in ahead of schedule, and under budget!"

Stephanie Giang-Paunon is an entertainment writer for Fox News Digital.

title: French model recruiter with close ties to Jeffrey Epstein found dead
excerpt:
A French model scout who introduced women to the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein has died at his home near Paris, according to French prosecutors.

Daniel Siad, 69, was found dead in the Paris subur...
body_text:
A French model scout who introduced women to the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein has died at his home near Paris, according to French prosecutors.

Daniel Siad, 69, was found dead in the Paris suburb of Colombes on Monday, French deputy public prosecutor Marie-Celine Lawrysz said on Wednesday.

Siad was one of several French men accused of aiding Epstein to traffic and abuse women. Multiple women say he connected them to Epstein, who then abused them.

Siad maintained that Epstein had deceived him and that he had no reason to know the sex offender “was dangerous”.

Siad was himself under investigation in France over allegations of rape and human trafficking, which he denied.

Prosecutors in the Paris suburb of Nanterre said an investigation is underway to determine the cause of Siad’s death and an autopsy will be carried out.

Siad’s name appeared in nearly 2,000 documents released as part of the declassified Epstein files, which showed he regularly sent Epstein photographs of women he met while travelling.

In one email to Epstein in 2014, Siad likened his recruitment efforts to fishing.

“In This busyness I feel like fisherman some time I cache quick, some time no fish.”

Siad had not yet been questioned by investigators in France but denied the allegations against him, saying he wanted to be heard to give his version of events.

Siad’s lawyer said he died an innocent man.

“If he died of a heart attack, the unbearable waiting, the pressure and the anxiety he suffered every day will have had something to do with it,” Menya Arab-Tigrine told the AFP agency.

Siad is not the first person in the modelling industry with Epstein connections to have died suddenly in France.

In 2022, modelling agent Jean-Luc Brunel hanged himself in prison in France after being arrested over allegations he procured women for the US billionaire.

Epstein died in prison in the US in 2019 while awaiting trial for sex trafficking also by suicide, according to US authorities.

For victims, Siad represented a possible avenue to finally “shed light on what happened” in relation to Epstein, said Juliette G., a former French model who says Siad recruited her in 2004 to introduce to Epstein.

“Another link in the chain has disappeared that could have helped uncover the truth and identify those responsible,” said the woman, who asked to withhold her surname to protect her privacy.

Former Swedish model Ebba Karlsson, now in her fifties, filed the first complaint against Siad in February, accusing him of raping her when she was 20.

“It’s very frustrating,” Karlsson told AFP. “He was very close to be arrested. We worked so hard for this, trying to have justice”.

title: AusAlert test will blast emergency warning siren over most phones in Australia on Monday. Here’s what to know
excerpt:
National emergency test alert will be sent to all mobile devices on 27 July, with domestic violence advocates advising how to turn off safe phones

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news ...
body_text:
National emergency test alert will be sent to all mobile devices on 27 July, with domestic violence advocates advising how to turn off safe phones

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Virtually every phone in the country will blare an emergency siren at 2pm AEST on Monday 27 July, as part of the first Australia-wide test of a new national warning system called AusAlert.

Most phones, and some smartwatches and tablets, will receive a pop-up notification and an alert sound that cannot be silenced – even if your device is on “do not disturb” or silent mode.

The warning system will be deployed to prepare Australians for fires, floods, terrorist incidents or health emergencies – but it has raised questions about regional access and the risk it poses to women and children experiencing domestic and family violence.

AusAlert is Australia’s new national warning system, which will send emergency messages to mobile devices during local and widespread disasters.

Authorities currently use SMS texts to alert people about safety information. The new system will instead send a mass broadcast via mobile phone towers, with an alert popping up on devices, similar to a phone notification.

It will launch nationally in October, months after the national test.

“On Monday 27 July 2026, a test alert will be sent to all mobile devices in Australia. This includes most phones, and some smart watches and tablets,” the National Emergency Management Agency (Nema) advises.

Users will hear a siren and see a pop-up notification on their phone, which will look different depending on the device, but with text including “TEST MESSAGE” and “NO ACTION IS REQUIRED”.

“What people should expect to hear is a siren that will last for about 10 seconds,” Pat Hetherington, the interim coordinator general at Nema, told Guardian Australia.

He said it would be “loud enough to get people’s attention”, but quieter than a fire alarm.

“It will be noticeable, but it won’t won’t be too over the top.”

There have been local tests, but Monday will be the first nationwide test, occurring at 2pm AEST, which is:

2pm for New South Wales, Victoria, the ACT, Queensland and Tasmania.

1.30pm in the Northern Territory and South Australia.

Hetherington said AusAlert came from a recommendation of the royal commission into the 2019-20 black summer bushfires, which called for a nationally consistent warning system.

“The existing system is based on standard SMS capability, and throughout a series of emergencies and disasters, that system has had real latency in terms of people receiving the messages,” he said.

“You [could] get network congestion, text messages may not come through in a timely way … or come through at all, dependent upon who your provider is.”

Kristy McBain, the federal emergency management minister whose electorate of Eden-Monaro was hit by the black summer bushfires, said some people in her area didn’t receive emergency text alerts during that disaster because their phone provider was unavailable.

The new system allows nationwide alerts as well as localised “geofenced” alerts such as for floods or even warnings to boil water in emergency situations.

McBain said AusAlert was a “far superior” system and would reach “more people in a more timely manner” than current warning systems used by state governments. Existing systems will still be used in future, with McBain describing AusAlert as “another tool in the toolkit”.

Essentially, yes. Hetherington said phones made after 2019 should receive the alert, but some older phones may not.

The alert will come through on all compatible devices even if you are on a call, or if the phone is on silent. Nema has advice that about 80% of Apple iPhones will ring, and some 16m Android devices.

“The only way to not receive a critical alert is to turn your mobile device off or switch it to ‘airplane mode’,” Nema advised.

iPhone models 11 and up running iOS 26.4 or later.

Apple Watch models SE2, SE3, Series 6 and up running watchOS 26.4 or later.

Android devices running Android 12 or later.

Older Androids, or those not using iOS or Android, “might still receive AusAlerts”, Nema said, but Apple iPads will not.

Hetherington and Nema’s deputy coordinator general, Katarina Carroll, said the agency had engaged with “thousands” of groups including schools, businesses and even funeral associations to warn of the alert test.

They said it was important to run the test during an “ordinary” time of day, so people knew what to expect in future.

But noting some people may find it inconvenient to have a loud siren ring alert, such as during an important meeting or a funeral, Hetherington and Carroll said “if you have your phone off an hour before and an hour after [2pm AEST], we’re confident you won’t receive that message”.

Other countries around the world have had problems in testing or using such systems, with alerts delayed or not arriving at all. Nema said it had “learned a lot” from tests around the world and it was well prepared for Monday.

Domestic and family violence advocates have raised concerns about the risk of the alert exposing “safe phones”, or devices kept hidden for safety reasons by those experiencing violence.

Nema said the agency had engaged with family violence experts about the test, including assisting advocates to disseminate safety information, and posting a safety guide on its website.

Hetherington said phones switched off or on airplane mode would not receive the alert, and said: “The message for safe phones and hidden phones is very much [to switch off for] a 24 hour period.”

Carroll added: “The advice is if there’s anything that’s happening [during the test], have it off for an hour or so. But certainly for domestic and family violence, there’s that broader advice for that extension of time [24 hours].”

Groups such as the Women’s Safety Commissioner, DV Connect and the Council of Single Mothers and their Children have issued safety advisories to this effect.

Nothing at all. The test requires no action.

However, Hetherington encouraged Australians to answer a survey or give feedback after the test to help Nema assess the success of the trial and any changes that may be needed.

“People should read the message. But it is a test that doesn’t require people to do anything,” he said.

“We’re obviously interested in hearing if people haven’t received the message.”

Nema suggests the warnings could be used for legitimate hazards such as bushfires, floods, cyclones and earthquakes; serious public safety issues or terrorism; biosecurity incidents; and major public health incidents.

States and territories will be able to send alerts.

“I would expect some states to use it quite a lot, particularly where you want information to get out almost in real time,” Hetherington said.

title: Why are OpenAI and Anthropic cheering on regulation in Australia? The answer has global reach
excerpt:
The companies hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily n...
body_text:
The companies hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Top US AI developers Anthropic and OpenAI cheered when Australia announced it would set new AI rules.

Big tech celebrating limits on their Silicon Valley VC-funded free-for-all might seem counterintuitive but there’s a much broader play than just what happens in one relatively small market.

Anthropic and OpenAI aren’t even on sharemarkets yet but this week lost billions in predicted value after being shown up by a Chinese startup.

Moonshot AI on Friday launched a new model, Kimi K3, advertising it as the first open-source model of its size, allowing more user modification than Anthropic’s Claude and OpenAI’s ChatGPT.

Analysts are judging K3 is competitive with both the leading US models, which are also less freely customisable. Moonshot had to temporarily pause new subscriptions after demand for the new model strained capacity.

Implied values for Anthropic’s sharemarket debut has slumped US$232bn to $1.56tn from Friday to Tuesday on IG’s trading platform. OpenAI dropped US$160bn to $1.16tn.

The two companies are still privately owned and valued at less than US$1tn each, so IG’s numbers are market bets, not true values.

But they hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June.

Anthropic has already begun registering to list on US sharemarkets, which would allow it to raise billions from new investors. The better the story it can tell, the more money it can raise.

Chinese competition does not make for a good story. Nor do surprise lawsuits from unhappy creatives, such as the one Anthropic had to settle for US$1.5bn on Monday (Tuesday AEST).

The company will pay authors about US$3,000 for each of an estimated 500,000 books covered by the settlement.

One of the authors whose work Anthropic scraped was Andrew Charlton, Australia’s assistant technology minister. Charlton says the new regulation will mean Australian creatives can choose whether AI models are trained on their work and big tech companies will have to pay them.

Yet those new rules could be a boon to big tech, Charlton says.

“If we are upfront with what we expect in this Australian standard, we can give clarity to investors that in some ways enhances the attractiveness of Australia,” he told Sky News on Monday.

Malik Ahmed Khan, an equity analyst at Morningstar Equity, says technology companies would find it much easier to invest in Australia as they learn where regulation is heading.

The companies will also have an easier job convincing investors to buy in at high prices if they can show they’re focusing on building AI and managing the risk of running afoul of governments or local laws, Khan says.

While Australia is one of few countries to now be regulating AI directly, with little direct impact on companies based overseas, Anthony Albanese hopes to set the global tone. In his speech announcing the regulations, he cited Australia’s leadership on social media bans for under-16s as proof.

Khan says more countries following Australia’s lead on AI could boost to companies’ prospects and market values.

“A positive deal in Australia could enable them to pursue similar deals across the globe,” he said.

Regulation would also give companies the prospect of favourable terms.

Albanese signalled he would consult “closely” with industry and other countries to legislate regulations that would aim to enable AI’s arrival, not restrict it. OpenAI and Anthropic were quick to position themselves as partners ready to help shape the new approach.

Dario Amodei, Anthropic’s chief executive and a former OpenAI vice-president, has already given an indication of what big tech thinks it should look like.

In a June essay, he called for a coalition of democracies to dominate in AI and lock out China.

While there are real national security concerns in this area, there’s also a healthy dose of self-interest in blocking out competitors.

With the launch of Kimi’s K3, analysts believes it is increasingly likely that the US will block businesses from exclusively using Chinese-based models over national security fears.

Amodei also called for domestic regulation, asking governments to test, evaluate and even block AI models if they presented “unacceptable risks” – but with protections against local “political favoritism or arbitrary decisions”.

Anthropic’s feud with the Trump administration has left it on a US supply chain blacklist. Days after Amodei published his essay, the US government forced Anthropic to suspend access to its Mythos 5 models for foreign nationals.

Anthropic’s critics have accused it of self-interest as it nears its initial public offering (IPO) on sharemarkets. The Pentagon’s Kirsten Davies said in June: “Some things are simply more important than revenue cycles, clickbait and pre-IPO valuation.”

Yet Amodei’s warnings about security concerns have been echoed by Albanese and by Five Eyes intelligence agencies.

Aruna Sathanapally, the CEO of Australia’s Grattan Institute, met the Anthropic chief when he visited Canberra in April. She said Amodei’s concerns seemed sincere.

“He was seeing things within his own company that were giving him great concern about what Anthropic, as well as their competitors, may be capable of developing,” Sathanapally said.

“Just because they have vested interests in the particular shape of regulation, it doesn’t mean that the calls for regulation themselves are not well founded. It just means … you need to keep alive to competitive neutrality in how you design it.”

title: How will Australia’s emergency services cope if satellites go down? With a sextant and a street directory
excerpt:
Military and SES personnel being taught analog communication and navigation techniques as experts warn solar flare could knock out ‘critical space infrastructure’

Get our breaking news email, free ap...
body_text:
Military and SES personnel being taught analog communication and navigation techniques as experts warn solar flare could knock out ‘critical space infrastructure’

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Australia’s emergency services and military personnel are being trained to read paper maps, navigate without GPS support, and sail by the stars in case something knocks out the nation’s space capabilities.

A panel at the 19th Australian space forum in Adelaide on Wednesday was asked to consider what would happen “if we suddenly lost access to critical space infrastructure – no satellites, no GPS, no remote sensing, no secure communications?”

The experts warned that Australians are unaware just how dependent they are on space, and how vulnerable that connection is to enemy attacks and solar flares.

It’s “not science fiction”, Optus satellite and space systems head, Nick Leake, said.

He described ATM networks going down, emergency services losing their situational awareness, aircraft navigation degrading, defence losing secure communications and remote communities being entirely cut off.

Aviation, freight and energy grid communications all rely on satellites, and financial systems could face “immediate cascading failure”, he said. People may only realise their service is being delivered by a satellite when they lose it.

“Most Australians have no idea that space is part of their everyday life,” Leake said.

“This isn’t science fiction. This is how critical satellite infrastructure is. Australia has one of the highest per capita dependencies on satellite infrastructure in the world.

Flinders University adjunct professor Jackie Craig AM is an expert in cyber and virtual warfare and an incident management team leader with the South Australian state emergency service.

She said emergency services are “increasingly dependent on increasingly vulnerable systems”.

She said they now teach members to navigate with street directories and maps instead of phones – a once ubiquitous skill that has become lost in the age of Google Maps.

“What we discovered is the young people of the digital revolution can’t actually use a UBD [street directory]. They’re also very poor at reading and using maps,” she said.

She said if the satellite systems went down emergency services would be “back to pencil and paper”, radios, and hard copies of procedure manuals.

Michael Murphy, deputy chief executive of the Royal Flying Doctor Service SA/NT, said if satellite systems went dark tomorrow, they would continue as normal – for about 24 hours, thanks to inbuilt contingencies and redundancies. Then, they would prioritise life-saving missions, relying on manual procedures and alternative communication methods.

He said they could go “old school” with high frequency radio and use the bush telegraph to share information in an analogue way.

“[And] we’ve got heaps of really good landmarks, the river systems, deserts, ranges, borders, water. If you’re familiar with the land, you’ll get around pretty well,” he said.

The Australian Defence Force’s air commodore, Chris Robson, said planning for a loss of satellite support wasn’t “doomsday prepping” or a “scare tactic” but “something we need to be honest about”.

“I don’t think the general public understands,” the space forces group and joint forces space component commander said.

The defence force is already offering sextant training, so members can navigate by the stars, along with terrestrial navigation training. But Robson did not think it would be frequently used.

“I don’t think that you will see too many people sitting on the bridge looking at a sextant,” he said.

The biggest risk was not an enemy attack, he said, but “that big orange glowing thing that on any single day can have a hiccup and take out a significant portion of our space capabilities”.

The panel discussed ways to make the network more resilient, including having satellite redundancy in multiple orbits, more Earth stations, and sovereign satellite capabilities, instead of relying on US giants Amazon and Starlink.

title: Trump warns Houthis after shipping blockade threat: 'We take care of things'
excerpt: The Pentagon launched two new rounds of strikes aimed at degrading Iran's ability to threaten global shipping through the Strait of Hormuz after President Donald Trump promised to hit the region 'very...
body_text:
The Pentagon launched two new rounds of strikes aimed at degrading Iran's ability to threaten global shipping through the Strait of Hormuz after President Donald Trump promised to hit the region 'very hard' over the next week.

President Donald Trump said Tuesday the U.S. would "take care of things" if Yemen's Iran-backed Houthis follow through on threats to impose a naval blockade against Saudi Arabia, as his administration reportedly backed Riyadh's renewed military campaign against the group.

His comments came after the Houthis announced a naval blockade against Saudi Arabia Monday, only days after Saudi forces struck Sanaa International Airport and after Iran reportedly instructed the group to prepare to close the Bab el-Mandeb Strait if the U.S. attacks Iranian power infrastructure.

TRUMP THREATENS TO EXPAND STRIKES ON IRAN, SAYS POWER PLANTS ARE NEXT TO GO: 'HIT THEM HARD'

"So far, it hasn't happened, might happen, but we take care of things," Trump said in the Oval Office. "If something like that happens, we take care of it."

President Donald Trump announced "major combat operations" in Iran, marking a significant escalation in tensions between Washington and Tehran. (Al Drago/Getty Images)

Saudi forces targeted Sanaa International Airport on Monday after an Iranian Mahan Air flight carrying Houthi officials back from Iran was forced to divert, according to Axios. A U.S. official told the outlet the aircraft was believed to be carrying weapons, missile components and Iranian military personnel intended for the Houthis.

Trump's warning comes as the administration has expanded military operations against Iran while the Houthis threaten to disrupt shipping in the Red Sea. The developments have fueled concerns that Washington could become increasingly involved in efforts to secure one of the world's most important maritime trade corridors while simultaneously confronting Tehran in the Persian Gulf.

The prospect of simultaneous disruption at the Strait of Hormuz and the Bab el-Mandeb has become one of the biggest concerns for global shipping markets: together, the routes normally carry roughly one-third of global seaborne oil trade and serve as critical arteries for energy shipments between the Middle East, Europe and Asia, according to U.S. Energy Information Administration data.

The Houthis already have shown they can disrupt one of the world's busiest shipping corridors. Since late 2023, the group has carried out more than 190 attacks on commercial shipping, forcing major carriers to abandon the Red Sea route in favor of the far longer trip around Africa.

US President Donald Trump meets with Crown Prince and Prime Minister of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia Mohammed bin Salman in the Oval Office of the White House in Washington, DC on November 18, 2025. Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman arrived at the White House to fanfare and a jet flyover Tuesday, in his first visit to the United States since the 2018 murder of journalist Jamal Khashoggi. (Brendan Smialowski/AFP via Getty Images)

Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman sought Trump's approval before launching the latest strikes against the Houthis — and that approval was granted, according to the Axios report.

The White House could not be reached for comment. The Pentagon and U.S. Central Command declined to comment.

For years, the Houthis have evolved into one of Iran's most effective regional partners, allowing Iran to pressure global commerce while avoiding a direct military confrontation with the United States or its allies. The group has repeatedly launched missiles and drones at commercial vessels transiting the Red Sea, disrupting international shipping and forcing vessels to reroute around southern Africa.

IRAN-BACKED TERROR PROXY HOUTHIS THREATEN FRESH ATTACKS AFTER YEMEN AIRPORT STRIKE

Yemen's Iran-backed armed Houthi group has warned they will move to shutter the Bab Al-Mandeb Strait through missile-drone attacks if Gulf nations join the US-Israel war on Iran. (Mohammed Hamoud/Getty Images)

Although direct fighting between Saudi Arabia and the Houthis largely subsided following a U.N.-brokered truce in 2022, the group continued expanding its missile, drone and maritime capabilities with Iranian support. This week's exchange of Saudi airstrikes and Houthi missile attacks marked the most significant cross-border escalation between the two sides in roughly four years.

Bridget Toomey, a research analyst at the Foundation for Defense of Democracies, said the latest escalation was driven by Saudi concerns that Iran and the Houthis were attempting to establish a dangerous new status quo by opening a direct air link between Tehran and Houthi-controlled Sanaa.

She said such flights would create an easier route for Iran to move weapons, drones and Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps advisers into Yemen, posing a threat not only to Saudi Arabia but also to broader U.S. and international security interests.

TRUMP WON’T RULE OUT KHARG ISLAND TAKEOVER: WHAT A US ASSAULT COULD LOOK LIKE

"A direct air connection between Tehran and Sanaa would be a serious threat, not just to the international community, but also to Saudi Arabia," Toomey told Fox News Digital.

Rather than signaling a return to the yearslong Saudi-led intervention in Yemen, Toomey described Riyadh's response as "a measured escalation" intended to demonstrate it would not allow such an air bridge to become normalized.

That objective appeared to shape one of Saudi Arabia's most consequential strikes this week.

The Bab el-Mandeb Strait, which connects the Red Sea with the Gulf of Aden, is one of the world's busiest maritime choke points. Nearly 15% of global seaborne trade passes through the corridor each year, making it a critical route for commercial shipping and energy supplies moving between Asia and Europe.

A satellite imagery shows Bab el Mandeb Strait, a key shipping waterway and the gateway to the Red Sea, as Iran threatens using Yemen's Houthi allies to shut the Bab el-Mandeb gateway to the Red Sea, in this handout picture dated July 12, 2026. (Nasa Worldview/Handout via Reuters)

The latest threat comes as commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has also faced disruption amid escalating tensions between the United States and Iran. While the two waterways are separated by hundreds of miles, together they form the principal maritime corridor linking the Persian Gulf with Europe. Serious disruption to both would place additional pressure on global energy exports, increase shipping costs and deepen supply chain disruptions.

Toomey said the Houthis occupy a unique position within Iran's regional network because of where they are located.

"They're crucial to Iran's broader ability to expand the conflict," she said. "Iraqi militias or Hezbollah don't sit on a maritime choke point."

Still, she cautioned against viewing the Houthis simply as an extension of Tehran, noting that Iran's command and control over the group has historically been less direct than its relationships with some of its other proxy organizations.

Gregg Roman, executive director of the Middle East Forum, said Trump's backing of the Saudi operation and his latest warning to the Houthis point to an administration willing to support regional allies while confronting Iranian-backed groups that threaten maritime commerce.

"The Iranians are trying to project power. The president is supportive of any effort to limit that projection," Roman told Fox News Digital.

Roman said the airport strike reinforced Saudi Arabia's long-running effort to prevent Iran from establishing a direct supply corridor into Houthi-controlled Yemen.

"We're certainly not going to allow you to have any shipments to reinforce the Houthis," he said.

If the campaign continues with Washington's backing, it would place one of Iran's most strategically valuable regional partners under renewed military pressure while increasing the cost of Tehran's broader effort to project power across the Middle East.

title: Lawmakers target energy affordability through sweeping permitting overhaul in new House bill
excerpt: Oklahoma Sen. Alan Armstrong discusses the critical need for permitting reform to boost American energy independence. He explains that despite abundant U.S. oil and gas resources, outdated processes a...
body_text:
Oklahoma Sen. Alan Armstrong discusses the critical need for permitting reform to boost American energy independence. He explains that despite abundant U.S. oil and gas resources, outdated processes and litigation delay essential infrastructure projects. Armstrong, a former CEO, highlights how legal challenges can halt multi-billion dollar projects, emphasizing the need for robust political action to streamline energy development.

FIRST ON FOX: House Republicans are staking out their position as congressional permitting talks heat up with a sweeping legislative package aimed at lowering energy costs, strengthening grid reliability and speeding up the construction of new energy projects.

House Energy and Commerce Committee Chairman Brett Guthrie, R-Ky., will introduce the 96-page "Permitting Our Way to an Energy Resurgence (POWER) Act" on Wednesday, describing it as a "discussion draft" as the panel continues to hear feedback from lawmakers and industry stakeholders.

The measure combines nearly 20 Republican proposals that would streamline approvals for pipelines, transmission lines, power plants and manufacturing projects while addressing rapidly growing electricity demand tied to artificial intelligence data centers.

Beyond permitting, the mammoth legislation seeks to shield ratepayers from price hikes by requiring data centers to bear the costs of grid upgrades.

Rep. Brett Guthrie, R-Ky., speaks during a House Energy and Commerce Committee hearing on Wednesday, June 3, 2026. (CQ-Roll Call, Inc via Getty Images)

TRUMP’S REINDUSTRIALIZATION AGENDA FACES ITS BIGGEST HURDLE — AMERICANS

The legislation would also give dispatchable power plants priority over intermittent sources such as wind and solar in interconnection queues, expand federal regulators' authority to keep power plants online and overhaul portions of the Clean Air Act.

"To win the race to AI dominance, onshore jobs, and deliver reliable and affordable power for families, America needs a permitting system that works for their communities," Guthrie said in a statement obtained by Fox News Digital.

"The POWER Act cuts the red tape holding back American energy, protects hard-working families from higher electricity costs, and keeps the lights on for generations to come," he continued.

Guthrie said the energy package is the result of months of collaboration among GOP members across the committee.

"For too long, burdensome regulations that fail to meet the moment have held back American innovation and growth," he said. "I’m proud to introduce commonsense legislation that will make energy more affordable for every American."

The legislation’s introduction comes as bipartisan negotiations over permitting reform have long proven elusive, despite widespread acknowledgment of the need for reforms to the federal government’s complex permitting process.

It is unclear whether House GOP leaders will bring Guthrie's package to the floor before the midterm elections. Lawmakers have less than two dozen legislative days left on the calendar heading into November.

The cooling towers of Talen Energy's Susquehanna Steam Electric Station are seen beyond an Amazon Web Services data center construction project in Berwick, Pa., on July 3, 2026. (SOPA Images/LightRocket via Getty Images)

FOX NEWS POLL: MOVE OVER BIG BROTHER, VOTERS SEE BIG TECH AS GREATER THREAT TO US

Among the package's most consequential provisions is legislation from Rep. Troy Balderson, R-Ohio, that would check the federal government’s ability to issue far-reaching environmental regulations.

His measure requires federal agencies to submit major actions affecting electricity generation to the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission for review to ensure they do not negatively impact grid reliability.

Republicans say Balderson's Reliable Power Act is intended to ensure burdensome regulations from agencies such as the Environmental Protection Agency do not jeopardize the nation's power supply.

Another marquee proposal, authored by Rep. Gabe Evans, R-Colo., would require large commercial electricity users, including data centers, to front the full cost of infrastructure upgrades.

The provision effectively codifies Trump’s "ratepayer protection pledge," under which large companies committed not to pass data center development costs on to everyday consumers through utility rate hikes.

"America must build the energy infrastructure necessary to win the global AI race and outcompete adversaries like Communist China—but hardworking families should never be forced to subsidize that growth through higher electric bills," Evans said Wednesday after the measure advanced out of committee with bipartisan support as a standalone bill.

The package also includes several other long-standing GOP energy priorities, including Evans' FIRE Act, which would shield states from Clean Air Act penalties tied to wildfire prevention efforts such as prescribed burns and brush clearing.

Smoke billows over Sorrento Valley as firefighters battle the fast-moving Sorrento Fire near the Interstate 5 and Interstate 805 interchange in San Diego, Calif., on June 8, 2026. The brush fire rapidly expanded to more than 80 acres, prompting mandatory evacuation orders for nearby neighborhoods. More than 140 firefighters, supported by helicopters and air resources, responded to the blaze while authorities closed portions of Sorrento Valley Boulevard and established an evacuation center at Mandell Weiss Eastgate Park. (Anadolu via Getty Images)

That provision has taken on renewed urgency after Canadian wildfire smoke blanketed a swath of the United States over the weekend with some GOP lawmakers slamming the country’s forest management.

title: Pancreatic cancer vaccine shows promise in first human trial, preventing disease in high-risk patients
excerpt:
Fox News senior correspondent Jonathan Serrie reports on doctors seeing results from Daraxonrasib, a new drug targeting pancreatic cancer, on ‘Special Report.’

A new vaccine for the prevention of pan...
body_text:
Fox News senior correspondent Jonathan Serrie reports on doctors seeing results from Daraxonrasib, a new drug targeting pancreatic cancer, on ‘Special Report.’

A new vaccine for the prevention of pancreatic cancer has shown promise in an early study at Johns Hopkins School of Medicine in Baltimore, Maryland.

A phase 1 trial published in Cancer Discovery, a journal of the American Association for Cancer Research, tested an experimental vaccine called mKRAS-VAX in 20 people, according to a press release.

Participants had an inherited risk of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), the most common and aggressive form of pancreatic cancer, along with an abnormality detected in imaging, typically a pancreatic cyst. These individuals had not been diagnosed with pancreatic cancer.

NEW PANCREATIC CANCER PILL NEARLY DOUBLES SURVIVAL TIME IN BREAKTHROUGH TRIAL

Four injections were administered to participants during weeks one, three and five, along with a booster at week 13.

The vaccine targeted six common mutations of KRAS. Changes in this gene drive more than 90% of PDACs.

The vaccine targeted six common mutations of KRAS. Changes in this gene drive more than 90% of PDACs. (iStock)

The vaccine triggered a mutant KRAS-targeted immune response in 18 of the 20 participants (90%). On average, immune activity increased about 18-fold, although responses varied. Half of participants responded to all six KRAS mutations included in the vaccine.

Researchers also found the vaccine generated two types of T cells — one to attack abnormal cells and another to provide longer-term immune memory.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

The vaccine appeared to be safe, with participants experiencing only injection-site reactions and temporary flu-like symptoms.

While the phase 1 trial was designed primarily to evaluate safety rather than effectiveness, after about 16.5 months of follow-up, no participants had developed pancreatic cancer. At the same time, 37.5% of vaccinated participants experienced shrinkage or disappearance of the pancreatic cysts that had put them at increased risk for the disease.

Some pancreatic cancer cysts shrank or disappeared in vaccinated individuals, according to a press release. (iStock)

Study co-author Neeha Zaidi, MD, associate professor of oncology at the Sidney Kimmel Comprehensive Cancer Center at Johns Hopkins University, commented that the long-lasting response of this vaccine is "particularly noteworthy when assessing for possible interception of cancer, which requires long-lasting immunity."

"In addition, the vaccine was safe and well-tolerated, supporting its use in larger cancer interception studies," she said in the press release. "Overall, this study represents the first proof of concept for the use of vaccines for interception of pancreatic cancer in human patients."

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR HEALTH NEWSLETTER

Co-author Michael G. Goggins, MD, professor of pathology, medicine and oncology and the Sol Goldman Professor of Pancreatic Cancer Research at Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine, also commented on the findings.

"We observed evidence of stability or regression of the pancreatic cysts in association with the induction and durability of KRAS-specific T-cell responses," he said in the release. "However, larger studies are needed to demonstrate that this effect was in fact due to the vaccine."

The authors noted the study was limited by its small sample size and because it was not designed to determine clinical efficacy.

Prevention and interception "save lives and reduce the morbidity associated with cancer development and progression," a co-author says. (iStock)

Fellow senior author Elizabeth Jaffee, MD, deputy director of the Sidney Kimmel Comprehensive Cancer Center at Johns Hopkins, commented that prevention and interception "save lives and reduce the morbidity associated with cancer development and progression."

"This is especially important for cancers whose early-onset frequency is increasing and for which we do not have effective methods for early detection," she added in the release.

Jaffee noted that this research highlights the need for more funding to support developing strategies for preventing cancer in high-risk individuals.

"More studies are needed to find the best vaccine approaches, the best targets and the ideal timing for vaccination," she said.

Angelica Stabile is a lifestyle reporter for Fox News Digital.

title: Harry Potter publisher to receive millions in Anthropic copyright settlement
excerpt:
Bloomsbury has 14,087 titles listed within settlement between AI startup and authors over use of protected work

The publisher of Harry Potter has received a multimillion-pound payout as a beneficiary...
body_text:
Bloomsbury has 14,087 titles listed within settlement between AI startup and authors over use of protected work

The publisher of Harry Potter has received a multimillion-pound payout as a beneficiary of a $1.5bn copyright settlement between AI startup Anthropic and thousands of authors over the use of their protected work to power chatbots.

Bloomsbury, which is home to bestselling novelists Sarah J Maas and Susanna Clarke as well as JK Rowling, said it had 14,087 titles listed within the settlement, with a proposed compensation of about $3,000 each.

The London-based company expects to receive the cash from the settlement in instalments, potentially starting in the second half of this fiscal year, with the proceeds to be split with authors.

After a deduction of about 10% for attorney fees and other expenses, Bloomsbury and the group of affected authors can expect to each receive about $19m (£14m).

Copyright has become a major battleground in the AI boom because tools like Anthropic’s suite of Claude chatbots are trained on vast amounts of data taken from the open web, including copyright-protected work such as novels and news articles.

US-based AI companies have argued that using such work is allowed under the legal doctrine of fair use, which allows use of copyrighted work without the owner’s permission in certain circumstances. However, creative professionals and artists around the world have been demanding that AI companies seek permission first – or at least pay them for using their work.

US district judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín said on Monday that the settlement provided “meaningful relief” to affected authors and publishers. The case began when bestselling novelist Andrea Bartz and two other authors filed the lawsuit in 2024. About 91% of the 482,000 works covered in the suit have been claimed.

The authors’ lead lawyer, Justin Nelson, described it as “the largest known copyright recovery in history”.

It is the first major settlement out of dozens of AI copyright lawsuits being filed in the US on behalf of authors and news outlets, which are still going through the courts.

Anthropic’s deputy general counsel, Aparna Sridhar, said in a written statement after the ruling: “We are pleased that more than 91% of authors and publishers covered by the settlement have claimed their share of the payment, and we’re looking forward to bringing this matter to a close.”

Bloomsbury announced an AI licensing deal last year, which allows it to sell academic works to train up generative AI programmes.

The firm said recently that more subject areas were now being considered for AI training.

Authors have been given the opportunity to “opt in” to the scheme and will be paid royalties if they decide to let their work be used.

title: 'Looksmaxxing' influencer Clavicular rejects incel label, faults 'both' men and women
excerpt:
Clavicular discusses the philosophy of 'looksmaxxing' on 'Hangout with Hannity.'

Viral influencer Clavicular revealed his thoughts on "incel culture" — an online subculture of mostly young, heterosex...
body_text:
Clavicular discusses the philosophy of 'looksmaxxing' on 'Hangout with Hannity.'

Viral influencer Clavicular revealed his thoughts on "incel culture" — an online subculture of mostly young, heterosexual men who identify as "involuntary celibates," and are often stigmatized as misogynistic — as well as modern dating culture amid his controversial streaming antics.

The 20-year-old internet personality, whose real name is Braden Peters, sat down with Fox News host Sean Hannity for the July 21 episode of "Hang Out with Sean Hannity." When asked about his content strategy and his views on women, Clavicular defended his "looksmaxxing" movement and accusations that it promotes "incel culture."

"People will often try to associate looksmaxxing with incel culture and being super resentful of women, when it's actually the antithesis of that, right?" he said. "Looksmaxxing is trying to escape inceldom, if anything, so for that link to try to be made makes no sense to me whatsoever."

CONTROVERSIAL STREAMER CLAVICULAR EXPLAINS VIRAL 'LOOKSMAXXING' TREND AS GEN Z ADOPTS EXTREME REGIMEN

"Looksmaxxing" is a strict health regimen that is meant to perfect one's appearance, and includes practices such as taking stimulants and bone smashing, which Clavicular describes as striking your facial bones with a hammer in an effort to stimulate bone growth.

"Hang Out with Sean Hannity" dropped a new podcast episode featuring the viral influencer, Clavicular. (Fox News)

The streamer also mused about how incel culture may be affecting the dating scene and connections between men and women.

"Every time I talk about hypergamy and why the dating market is hard, I'll talk about how maybe women on dating apps will go for the top profiles and this and that," he said. "But then I'll also say right after, you know, can you really blame them with the generation of men that's out there for them? So I think it's a problem on both sides. It's not just women, it's not just men — it is holistic."

AI IS CHANGING MODERN DATING, BUT EXPERTS WARN IT'S MAKING PEOPLE 'RELATIONALLY STUPID'

Clavicular was previously pressed about whether he identifies as an incel during a tense sit-down interview with "60 Minutes Australia" correspondent Adam Hegarty in April. He lashed out at the reporter and criticized the question.

"I mean, how could you ask me that question as a follow-up after you asked me about my relationships to women?" he replied. "I mean, that’s quite literally the worst sequence of questions I think I’ve ever heard."

Braden Eric Peters, a "looksmaxxing" influencer was arrested for battery on Thursday, March 26, for battery in Broward County, Fla. (Getty Images and Broward County Sheriff's Department)

Hegarty repeatedly pressed Clavicular about his alleged ties to the incel community and his connection to fellow internet personality Andrew Tate, but he denied any links to the group.

DANA WHITE MOCKS CONCERNS ABOUT SO-CALLED 'TOXIC MASCULINITY,' WARNS SOCIETY HAS 'DISPLACED' YOUNG MEN

"I’m not linked to that group in any way," he countered. "Looksmaxxing is self-improvement, right? So it’s about potentially even ascending out of that category. So that would be kind of one of the goals — to dissociate from being an incel and overcome that. So that doesn’t make sense."

Clavicular ended the interview abruptly, storming off the set.Conversly, Hannity left Clavicular with some encouraging words after their wide-ranging conversation, wishing him "all the success in the world" and advising him to be "careful."

Fox News host Sean Hannity raises his fist at Fox Nation’s Patriot Awards at the Tilles Center in Greenvale, New York, on December 5, 2024. (Michael M. Santiago/Getty Images)

The controversial viral star was arrested in March on a battery charge after police alleged he instigated a fight between two women and posted the video to social media. The incident sparked additional concerns about his treatment of women, especially on his livestreams.

Fox News' Nora Moriarty contributed to this story.

Benji Ferraro is a recent University of Maryland grad and new digital production assistant, with a focus on channel coverage and culture stories. He can be reached at benji.ferraro@fox.com for any story tips.

title: IDF says Palestinian soccer players it targeted served Hamas and Jihad terror groups
excerpt:
Egypt head coach Hossam Hassan, a staunch Palestine supporter, spotted an Israeli flag in the stands on Tuesday and was not happy about it. (This video contains AI-assisted content.)

EXCLUSIVE: The I...
body_text:
Egypt head coach Hossam Hassan, a staunch Palestine supporter, spotted an Israeli flag in the stands on Tuesday and was not happy about it. (This video contains AI-assisted content.)

EXCLUSIVE: The Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) has told Fox News Digital that soccer players representing the Palestinian Authority it targeted with military strikes were allegedly also working as terrorists for Hamas and Jihadist groups.

"In recent weeks, false claims have been circulated alleging that the IDF deliberately targeted innocent Palestinian soccer players throughout the war," the IDF said in a statement to Fox News Digital.

"In light of these claims, the IDF is now revealing the identities of Palestinian soccer players, referees, and coaches who were presented as athletes and innocent civilians, but who in fact served as terrorists in the military wings of the Hamas and Islamic Jihad terrorist organizations in the Gaza Strip, and were struck and eliminated by the IDF during the war.

IDF CLAIMS TO HAVE TAKEN OUT HAMAS COMMANDER WHO PARTICIPATED IN OCT 7

Fan displays a Palestine flag amid the ongoing conflict between Israel and Hamas at Israel-France soccer match on Nov. 14, 2024. (REUTERS/Gonzalo Fuentes)

"Following an intelligence assessment, the IDF can now disclose these terrorists’ organizational affiliations and operational roles. In many cases, they were portrayed internationally only as players, with no mention of their affiliation with terrorist organizations."

The IDF identified the players as Mohammed Mohammed Hassan Barakat, Mahmoud Kamal Mohammad Ali Al-Rifi, Mohammed Mansour, Ahmed Mohammed Hassan Abu Al-Atta, Abdullah Riyadh Abdullah Khattab, Ali Mahmoud Ahmed Al-Kurd, Mohammad Emad Ata Hussouna, Tariq Ziad Hussein Al-Hour, Mohammed Ziad Hussein Al-Hour, Mahmoud Osama Al-Jazzar and Mohammad Nidal Mohammed Al-Hawajri.

According to the IDF, three of the players were allegedly involved in the Oct. 7, 2023 massacre, and others allegedly served in other terror operations and leadership roles.

"The false campaign portraying the IDF as deliberately targeting members of the soccer community, which was created as part of a broader effort promoted by various actors against the State of Israel, accompanies other false narratives advanced throughout the war," the IDF added.

"In those campaigns, Palestinian terrorists struck and eliminated by the IDF were presented as journalists, humanitarian aid workers, and other civilian professionals, while their involvement in terrorist activity was deliberately concealed in order to promote false claims that they had been targeted because of their civilian professions."

Fox News Digital has reached out to the Permanent Observer Mission of the State of Palestine to the United Nations for a response to the IDF's claims.

IRAN ALLEGEDLY USED SOCCER CLUBS TO SPY ON CITIZENS, OPPOSITION GROUP URGES FIFA BAN REGIME BEFORE WORLD CUP

FIFA President Gianni Infantino shakes hands with Basim Sheikh Suliman (left), vice president of the Israeli Football Association, as Jibril Rajoub (right), president of the Palestine Football Association, leaves the stage at the 76th FIFA Congress in Vancouver, Canada on April 30, 2026. (Reuters/Jennifer Gauthier)

The official Palestinian Football Association (PFA) reported earlier this month that about 1,009 members of its athletic community had been killed by IDF forces since the war began in October 2023, according to Al Jazeera.

The IDF's exclusive disclosures come against the backdrop of a fierce diplomatic and public relations battle on the global sports stage.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

For over two years, the Palestinian Football Association, headed by Jibril Rajoub, has aggressively lobbied the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and FIFA to suspend Israel from international competition. Rajoub and the PFA have repeatedly cited the mounting death toll of Palestinian athletes and the destruction of sports infrastructure in Gaza as grounds for an immediate, indefinite ban.

The campaign reached a boiling point at recent FIFA Congresses. However, following extensive legal reviews, FIFA's Disciplinary Committee ultimately rejected the PFA's demands to suspend Israel entirely.

Instead, FIFA recently opted to impose a fine on the Israel Football Association (IFA) for unrelated disciplinary breaches and ordered the Israeli team to display a "Football Unites the World – No to Discrimination" banner during upcoming matches.

However, FIFA did not let the IFA walk away completely untouched. In a compromise measure, FIFA's Disciplinary Committee opted to penalize the Israeli association for unrelated disciplinary breaches.

The IFA was fined 150,000 Swiss francs (approximately $190,700) for multiple breaches of anti-discrimination obligations. Additionally, FIFA ordered the IFA to implement a mandatory anti-discrimination prevention plan and required the Israeli team to prominently display "Football Unites the World – No to Discrimination" banners during upcoming matches.

Jackson Thompson is a sports reporter for Fox News Digital covering critical political and cultural issues in sports, with an investigative lens. Jackson's reporting has been cited in federal government actions related to the enforcement of Title IX, and in legacy media outlets including The New York Times, The Los Angeles Times, The Philadelphia Inquirer, The Associated Press and ESPN.com.
