title: English FA charges Southampton coach Tonda Eckert over ‘spygate’ scandal
excerpt:
Southampton manager Tonda Eckert has been charged by the English Football Association (FA) over the spying scandal that led to his side being kicked out of the Championship playoffs.

In a stunning co...
body_text:
Southampton manager Tonda Eckert has been charged by the English Football Association (FA) over the spying scandal that led to his side being kicked out of the Championship playoffs.

In a stunning controversy that rocked English football last season, Southampton admitted spying on a training session held by playoff semifinal opponents Middlesbrough in May.

They also spied on training sessions at fellow Championship clubs Ipswich and Oxford earlier in the season.

After beating Middlesbrough in the semifinals, Southampton were expelled from the Championship playoff final against Hull and deducted four points for the 2026-27 season.

Middlesbrough replaced Southampton in the final and were beaten by Hull.

The financial cost to Southampton was huge, with promotion to the Premier League worth an estimated 200 million pounds ($267m).

An independent disciplinary commission appointed by the Football League ruled that the espionage was authorised by Eckert and was a “contrived and determined plan from the top down”.

The FA subsequently confirmed they were investigating the scandal and on Wednesday English football’s governing body charged Eckert for his role in the spying.

“Tonda Eckert has been charged with three breaches of FA rule E3.1 in relation to misconduct between December 2025 and May 2026,” the FA said in a statement.

“It’s alleged that the head coach acted in an improper manner and/or brought the game into disrepute by directing and/or authorising the observation of training sessions of Oxford United FC, Ipswich Town FC and Middlesbrough FC ahead of fixtures between these clubs and Southampton FC in December 2025, April 2026 and May 2026 respectively.”

Southampton said they would “cooperate fully and openly” with the FA, adding: “The club remains fully supportive of Tonda and his staff as we work towards our ambition of returning to the Premier League.”

The panel found junior staff at Southampton felt “pressurised to do the observations that Eckert and the senior coaches wished them to do”.

Eckert, who has until next Tuesday to respond, accepted responsibility for the spying in a statement in June.

Southampton owner Dragan Solak said at the time he had no intention of sacking Eckert for his behaviour.

Southampton kick off their season with a League Cup first round tie at Colchester on August 8 before their opening Championship fixture at Watford on August 16.

title: Football influencer Kay Adams turns in worst Heisman pose ever, 7'9 first pitch & thick Chip Kelly
excerpt: Greg Gutfeld presents a mugshot to his panel on "Gutfeld!" and asks them to "guess the crime," eliciting funny and unexpected responses. The panel's lighthearted guesses sharply contrast with the grim...
body_text:
Greg Gutfeld presents a mugshot to his panel on "Gutfeld!" and asks them to "guess the crime," eliciting funny and unexpected responses. The panel's lighthearted guesses sharply contrast with the grim reality of the woman's alleged crimes.

The sun is out, the Canadian wildfire smoke suddenly doesn't exist anywhere east of the Rocky Mountains & it's time to pump out a fresh edition of Wednesday Screencaps for those of you who are just trying to pass the time until your next week off before football season.

Let's get rolling with Kay Adams, the football broadcasting influencer, self-admitting to turning in one of, if not the worst, Heisman poses in influencer history. She was at Fanatics Fest over the weekend and was hired to do some chat with famous athletes, including Joe Burrow.

I'm not a huge Kay Adams fan like OutKick's Zach Dean to the point where I'd take a bullet for her, but I get her appeal. Kay turned 40 in April and is one of the only women in the broadcasting game who seems fun, hasn't pivoted into a yoga instructor and doesn't tell guys that they're perverted for finding her attractive.

SOMEONE NEEDS TO STEP IN AND DEFEND CODY RHODES' AWFUL, TERRIBLE PLACEKICKING PERFORMANCE

Kay Adams attends the 95th Annual Academy Awards in Hollywood, Calif., on March 12, 2023. (Arturo Holmes/Getty Images)

For those of us in our 40s, Kay is a unicorn. I get it. We're used to being called pigs by the COVID-era, Sarah Spain types who ruined the sports/pop culture world for about six years. There was a brief period right there before 2020 where the Spain types ruled.

"Wanna see the worst Heisman pose of all time? 🙋🏻‍♀️what was I trying to do here? i need to take a lap," Kay cheekily wrote on her latest Instagram post.

KAY ADAMS HAD NO CHOICE BUT TO CALL FOR PUNISHMENT AFTER FEET END UP ONLINE, LIKELY LEAVING DMS IN SHAMBLES

📩 Email: joe.kinsey@outkick.com Send photos, stories, tips, rants—whatever you've got.

📰 Screencaps Page: 👉 Read the latest Screencaps

DOLPHINS CHEERLEADERS MAKE MAJOR MOVES TO COMPETE WITH COWBOYS CHEERLEADERS, TEBOWS GET OUT & RYAN CLARK!

▶️ YouTube: Screencaps with Joe Kinsey Subscribe for videos, rants, and behind-the-scenes.

🐦 Twitter/X: @JoeKinseyexp Tag me or drop a DM.

📸 Instagram: @OutKickScreencaps You guys need to start tagging me on content you're seeing.

👥 Facebook Group: Join the Screencaps Community

📬 Mail (Thursday Night Mowing League): 27072 Carronade Dr, Unit A 155 Perrysburg, OH 43551

– Rory remembers: Sad news as someone from a baseball family who spent childhood summer mornings watching Baseball Tonight on repeat.

I'm 99% positive I ran into him in an airport a few years ago, but he looked like he had spent the previous day in the sun drinking a bottle of scotch. Thought it best to leave him alone.

Kinsey: My hope is that Ravech loses the rug (allegedly), shaves his head, grows out a bad boy, Hogan NWO-style goatee and gets busy living life. Ravech is in his early 60s. I have to believe he's set financially. Now it's time to let loose.

– S.E. emails:  You did not do your research . The director of the Dolfins cheerlears is a former Dallas Cowboys Cheerleader. KaShara was the point for DCC.

Kinsey: That's true, I did not investigate the background of the Dolphins cheerleader management team. Guilty as charged, but this now explains why the Dolphins' program is suddenly being operated, at least on social media, like the Cowboys. This is great news for consumers. A rising tide lifts all cheerleader programs, S.E.

– Drew in Katy, TX says: Add Cracker Barrel to your "Doomed Restaurant Chain" list. The CEO crawled her way aback out of the hole that she created last year. Now she is starting down another one.

From WSJ: "Cracker Barrel Raises Guidance After Maple Street Biscuit Divestiture, Sale-Leaseback Deal" " The company said it had entered into the transaction with an institutional real estate investor for 26 of its company-owned Cracker Barrel locations.

"The deal generated net proceeds of about $77 million, which the company said it plans to put toward debt reduction."

This is like selling a house that you own debt free in order to pay off credit card debt so that you can start making house payments again. This has failed miserably for other companies (See Google AI results on the topic below the stock price chart.) The company shares moved up. "Shares in Cracker Barrel jumped 9% to $58.20 in after-hours trading on Monday." Now she will do more of this. It will be a downward spiral . . . just give it enough time . . .

https://www.wsj.com/business/cracker-barrel-raises-guidance-after-maple-street-biscuit-divestiture-sale-leaseback-deal-395f8281

Kinsey: I need Screencaps financial analysts to weigh in here. Even though the stock price has rebounded, is the writing on the wall that Cracker Barrel needs to be added to my "Restaurants That Won't Exist In 5 Years" list?

If the Facebook embed doesn't load, go here. It's not some conspiracy for the Facebook link to not load.

– Jim T. in San Diego opines: Wow - do the powers that be in the baseball-first SEC not know what a bad look their threats to break away from the NCAA is? The last time the South seceded it didn't turn out all that well.

Plus, if they break away, no more College World Series - which, realistically, is the SEC's only solid chance to claim a national championship these days ...

– Chris W. in California is living through this right now: I feel for Brad S. and the locust like scourge of having a bunch of teenage boys over at your house. Especially during the Summer we have an ever rotating group of my sons' friends hanging out at our house, all of them in the 10-14 year age range.

I'm pretty certain you could feed most small armies for less than what it costs me to keep food in my house. A $120 Taco Bell bill is one of the many reasons I make sure we have enough stuff so we don't have to order out very often (And when we do it's either pizza or blessedly, because we live in California, In-N-Out which is still the best bang for your buck in all of fast food), but I have both a Sam's Club and Costco membership because they are the only ways to keep the horde sated. When there's a sleepover I dread waking up the next morning to see the remnants of the blitzkrieg on my pantry and fridge, and I know no matter what I'm going to have to run out to get more eggs and bread (teen boys eat SOOOO much bread) to make breakfast happen.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Yes, it's a pain, yes it's expensive, but I look at it the same way I do how much time and gas money I spend on driving my kids around to all of their activities, it's just a sign I have healthy, active boys that aren't couch potatoes, kids that are going out and getting stuff done. If my grocery bill has to look like a second mortgage as a result, that's a small price to pay.

That's it for this random Wednesday in July. The first two NFL training camps open today. Carolina and Arizona will battle on August 6 in the Hall of Fame game. Guys, we're so close, but, as always, we do not waste summer days. Go out and make today count.

Joe Kinsey is the Senior Director of Content of OutKick.

title: Fake password-manager alerts could put your vault at risk
excerpt: Halimah Delaine Prado, Google General Counsel, reveals the rise of AI-powered phishing scams originating from China's 'outsider enterprise.' She explains how these criminals use artificial intelligenc...
body_text:
Halimah Delaine Prado, Google General Counsel, reveals the rise of AI-powered phishing scams originating from China's 'outsider enterprise.' She explains how these criminals use artificial intelligence to create highly convincing fake websites, impersonating trusted brands like T-Mobile to defraud hundreds of thousands of Americans, causing millions in losses. Prado highlights Google's strategy to combat these evolving threats.

You open your inbox and see a message about updated security policies. Nothing about it screams scam. The email looks polished, the wording sounds official and the button promises a quick way to review the changes. That is exactly what makes it dangerous.

LastPass is warning users about a newly identified phishing campaign that uses lookalike domains and a fake DocuSign page to lure people into downloading suspicious software.

The good news is that LastPass says its systems were not affected. The bad news is that scammers are counting on you to trust the logo, overlook the web address and click before taking a closer look. Here is what to watch for before one routine-looking email puts your entire password vault at risk.

Free live CyberGuy class: Sick of Spam? Join us July 22

Join us TODAY, Wednesday, July 22, at 1 PM ET for a free CyberGuy Live class that will help you cut down on robocalls, spam texts, junk email and other unwanted messages. Kurt "CyberGuy" Knutsson will walk you step by step through simple ways to filter spam, clean up your inbox and recognize the messages that could put your personal information at risk. No technical experience is needed. You’ll also receive our spam-stopping checklist, and every registrant will get a link to the class recording afterward.

Reserve your free spot today at CyberGuyLive.com.

Scammers are impersonating LastPass with fake policy emails that lead users to a fraudulent DocuSign page and suspicious software download. (Kurt "CyberGuy" Knutsson)

LastPass phishing scam starts with a routine policy email

The phishing email comes from hello@lastpassnewsletter.com. Its known subject line reads, "Action Required: Review Updated LastPass Security Policies." Inside, the message claims LastPass has made service policy changes. It mentions enhanced SaaS monitoring. It also claims administrators can reset master passwords and that the admin console has improved.

Those details make the email sound like a real company notice. However, the sending domain belongs to the attackers. LastPass says lastpassnewsletter[.]com has no affiliation with the company. The email includes a Review & Access Terms button. That button creates the next layer of the trap.

Clicking the button sends you to lastpasscompliance[.]com. The landing page copies the look of DocuSign and claims a document is ready for review. That choice makes sense from a scammer's perspective. Many people receive electronic signature requests at work or while handling personal paperwork. A familiar layout can lower your guard before you inspect the web address.

We have seen the same trust trick in other fake DocuSign email scams. The brand name gives the request a sense of legitimacy, even when the sender and domain do not match. LastPass says Microsoft Defender for Office 365 and Cloudflare classified the phishing site as malicious. The page also prompted visitors to download software that claimed to work on Windows and macOS.

LastPass was still investigating the download when it published its warning. Therefore, you should treat the file as dangerous and avoid opening it. The site also displayed a live support chat box, although it was unclear whether the chat worked. The malicious page had gone offline by the time the campaign was reported. However, attackers can quickly replace blocked domains with new ones.

LastPass users are not the only targets. Bitwarden customers have received similar messages from hello@bitwardennewsletter.com. Those emails directed recipients to bitwardencompliance[.]com. The matching format suggests attackers may be reusing the same campaign structure across password manager brands.

That matters because password manager customers present an attractive target. One stolen master password could put many saved accounts at risk. Multi-factor authentication may still block access, depending on your security settings.

A password manager remains valuable protection. In fact, autofill can help expose a fake website because the manager should recognize the legitimate domain. You can compare current options in our guide to the best password managers for 2026 at cyberguy.com

This campaign follows other LastPass-themed phishing attempts from earlier this year. In January, fake messages warned that users had only 24 hours to back up their vaults before maintenance. Then, a March campaign used fabricated email threads about unauthorized account access.

Both approaches relied on urgency to push people into acting before they verified the message. The new compliance notice uses a calmer approach. It looks like paperwork rather than a crisis. That may make it especially effective because policy updates feel normal and boring.

REDHOOK ANDROID MALWARE CAN QUIETLY HIJACK YOUR PHONE

The fraudulent page impersonates DocuSign and prompts visitors to download suspicious software for Windows or macOS. (LastPass)

A few careful steps can keep one convincing email from turning into a much larger problem.

Delete the message or report it as phishing. Do not reply. Avoid opening its links or downloading the file it offers.

Use the official LastPass app or type lastpass.com into your browser. Check for account notices after you sign in through the trusted route.

Lookalike domains often add a trusted brand name to words such as "newsletter" or "compliance." Check the website address before the first slash. A legitimate LastPass address should end in lastpass.com, such as support.lastpass.com, rather than merely containing the word "LastPass."

A password manager may refuse to fill your credentials on a fake domain. Treat that as a warning. Do not copy and paste the password to get around it. Instead, close the page and access your account through the official app or website.

Use a trusted device and go directly to LastPass. Change the master password immediately. Then review your vault for unexpected activity, as LastPass recommends. Next, change passwords for sensitive accounts stored in the vault if you see signs of access. Start with email, financial accounts, cloud storage and social media. Use a different password for every account.

AMAZON RECALL TEXT SCAM COMES WITH RED FLAGS

The scam relies on polished security notices, familiar branding and misleading web addresses to lower users’ defenses. (Kurt "CyberGuy" Knutsson)

Do not open software offered by a security notice you reached through email. If you already opened the file, disconnect the affected device from the internet. Then use strong antivirus software to inspect it. Our current best antivirus protection guide at cyberguy.com can help you compare tools that block malicious websites and dangerous downloads. It also covers protection against malware. You can also follow these steps after you clicked a suspicious email and entered information.

Enable multi-factor authentication for your password manager and other important accounts. An authenticator app or security key can add a barrier if someone steals your password. However, never approve a login request you did not initiate. A second security step only helps when you treat unexpected prompts as a warning.

Scammers often use information from data broker sites to make phishing emails feel more personal. That could include your phone number, home address, relatives or past employers. A data removal service can help find and remove some of that information from people-search websites. It will not secure a compromised password manager, but it may give scammers fewer details to use in future attacks. Check out my top picks for data removal services and get a free scan to find out if your personal information is already out on the web by visiting Cyberguy.com

Forward questionable LastPass-branded emails to abuse@lastpass.com. LastPass says no one from the company will ever ask for your master password.

What makes this scam dangerous is how normal the email looks. Most people expect password manager alerts to sound urgent. This one arrives dressed up as a boring policy update, which may make you less likely to question it. The biggest red flag is the web address. A company name inside a domain does not mean the company owns it. Before entering a master password or downloading anything, close the email and open the password manager directly. Your master password protects everything stored in your vault. Treat any request for it like someone asking for the keys to your house.

Have you ever received a security email that looked so real you almost clicked? What made you stop and take a closer look? Let us know by writing to us at Cyberguy.com

Copyright 2026 CyberGuy.com. All rights reserved.

Kurt "CyberGuy" Knutsson is an award-winning tech journalist who has a deep love of technology, gear and gadgets that make life better with his contributions for Fox News & FOX Business beginning mornings on "FOX & Friends." Got a tech question? Get Kurt’s free CyberGuy Newsletter, share your voice, a story idea or comment at CyberGuy.com.

title: 'Dutton Ranch' star Ed Harris blasts producers, says he wanted to void contract: 'Get me the f--- out'
excerpt:
The "Dutton Ranch" star reflects on his family’s deep ties to Montana as the Paramount+ series premieres May 15 with two episodes, followed by weekly releases.

Ed Harris says he came close to walking...
body_text:
The "Dutton Ranch" star reflects on his family’s deep ties to Montana as the Paramount+ series premieres May 15 with two episodes, followed by weekly releases.

Ed Harris says he came close to walking away from Taylor Sheridan's "Dutton Ranch."

The veteran actor revealed he felt "underused and inconsequential" while filming the show's first season, prompting him to tell his team to "get me the f--- out of here" before production wrapped.

"Prior to signing on for it, I was talked to quite a bit about what the season was, and what my character was going to do — and that I was one of the four main characters," Harris told Variety. "And that wasn't really the case."

COLE HAUSER SAYS HIS FAMILY HELPED SHAPE MONTANA AS FANS FOLLOW TAYLOR SHERIDAN FROM TREASURE STATE TO TEXAS

Ed Harris says he nearly quit Taylor Sheridan's "Dutton Ranch." (Emerson Miller/Paramount+.)

The Oscar-nominated actor, who plays veterinarian Everett McKinney in the "Yellowstone" spin-off, said he felt "misled" about the importance of his character after allegedly being promised a much larger role before production began.

By the middle of filming, Harris said he'd reached his breaking point.

Harris said he confronted the production about his concerns, though he did not identify Sheridan by name.

LIKE WHAT YOU’RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Harris is seen with Kelly Reilly as Beth Dutton in "Dutton Ranch." (Emerson Miller/Paramount+)

"I said, 'I'm feeling underused and inconsequential,' and they kind of went, 'Oh,'" Harris said. "I'm being told my character will have a little bit more to do [in Season 2]. I was just a little frustrated, to tell you the truth."

Looking back after watching the finished season, Harris admitted his perspective changed.

He called "Dutton Ranch" "pretty good" and said Everett ended up feeling "relatively important to the story." Still, he said that wasn't how it felt while cameras were rolling.

A cut musical scene left Harris fuming at "Dutton Ranch" producers, saying it was "enough reason" to void his contract with the show. (Emerson Miller/Paramount+)

Harris said he filmed a sequence in which Everett is invited to sing in a bar — only to discover it had been axed from production.

"So, there's this scene in there … I'm talking to [Beth Dutton] at the bar, and [Rip Wheeler] comes in and says, 'OK, Everett, come on, it's your turn,'" Harris said. "I walk up to the mic, and they cut it… They cut the song out. I'm going, 'F--- you, people.'"

According to Harris, producers told him the musical moment was removed because it was "too 'up' for the dark ending of that episode."

Harris said he felt that was "enough reason to say I'm voiding my contract."

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

Despite his blunt remarks, Harris remains signed on for two seasons of "Dutton Ranch," and it remains unclear whether he is seriously considering leaving the series.

Harris revealed he felt "underused" filming "Dutton Ranch" and nearly voided his contract on the Taylor Sheridan "Yellowstone" spinoff. (Omar Vega/Getty Images)

Sheridan's latest chapter in the "Yellowstone" universe debuted on Paramount+ in May.

The series picks up with fan favorites Beth Dutton (Kelly Reilly) and Rip Wheeler (Cole Hauser) as they trade Montana for South Texas, where they're determined to build a ranch — and a new life — from the ground up.

Joining Reilly and Hauser is an ensemble cast that includes Harris, Annette Bening, Finn Little, Juan Pablo Raba, Jai Courtney, J.R. Villarreal, Marc Menchaca and Natalie Alyn Lind.

Fox News Digital reached out to Paramount, Sheridan and Harris for comment.

Stephanie Giang-Paunon is an entertainment writer for Fox News Digital.

title: Angelina Jolie achieves 'complete cutting of ties' between kids and Brad Pitt as twins turn 18: expert
excerpt:
Jon Voight spoke to Fox News Digital while promoting his film "Reagan," and weighed in on the family drama between his daughter Angelina Jolie and Brad Pitt.

A decade after one of Hollywood's most bi...
body_text:
Jon Voight spoke to Fox News Digital while promoting his film "Reagan," and weighed in on the family drama between his daughter Angelina Jolie and Brad Pitt.

A decade after one of Hollywood's most bitter divorces began, Angelina Jolie has finally achieved a "complete cutting of ties" with ex-husband Brad Pitt.

With the former couple's youngest children now 18, the last legal thread connecting Jolie and Pitt has effectively been severed, a family law expert explained to Fox News Digital. The expert argued the Oscar winner has reached the outcome she had been seeking since filing for divorce in 2016.

"For those that followed the Pitt/Jolie divorce since 2016, it should be no surprise that Angelina Jolie is finally able to achieve what she attempted to get in the 2016 divorce — a complete cutting of ties between the children and Brad Pitt," Holly Davis, cofounder of Kirker Davis LLP, told Fox News Digital.

BRAD PITT FACES FRESH FAMILY BLOW AS DAUGHTER CLEARS MAJOR HURDLE TO DROP HIS LAST NAME

Angelina Jolie has achieved what one family law expert described as a "complete cutting of ties" between ex-husband Brad Pitt and their kids after the former couple's youngest children turned 18. (Toni Anne Barson Archive/WireImage)

Pitt and Jolie share six children: Maddox, Zahara, Shiloh, Pax, Knox and Vivienne.

Twins Knox and Vivienne reaching adulthood on July 12 means custody issues are no longer before the court, giving Jolie the freedom to make decisions that previously required court oversight.

"Because she was unable to achieve this result in the parties' divorce, she had to wait until the youngest children were 18 to be able to do the things that the court would not allow her to do," Davis explained.

What ultimately drove the children's estrangement from their father remains impossible to know from the outside, according to Davis.

"Whether or not she influenced them in hating their father based on the legitimate problems in her romantic relationship with Brad, or whether they all suffered similar difficult moments as a family which lead to their decision to no longer see him, will be anyone's guess," the family attorney said.

Jolie and Pitt's daughter Vivienne is seeking to remove "Pitt" legally from her last name — from Vivienne Marcheline Jolie-Pitt to Vivienne Marcheline Jolie — just over a week after turning 18, according to People magazine.

The petition was filed in Los Angeles County Superior Court, with the reason for the name change noted as "personal," per the outlet, with the hearing scheduled for November 2.

Two years prior, Vivienne worked on the Broadway play "The Outsiders" with mom Angelina, and was credited as "Vivienne Jolie" in the playbill. Vivienne worked as a producer assistant on the musical while Angelina served as a producer.

Family law and entertainment experts are divided over whether Brad Pitt's strained relationship with several of his children will have any lasting impact on the actor's public image. (Getty Images)

BRAD PITT FIGHTS FOR SURVIVAL IN 'HEART OF THE BEAST' TRAILER AS FANS SAY HE'S 'COOLER' THAN YOUNGER ACTORS

Jolie may have effectively severed the remaining legal family ties, but experts say the public fallout might not significantly damage Pitt's Hollywood standing.

"Brandwise, it’s not that big of a hit to Brad Pitt’s name or brand," entertainment attorney Tre Lovell told Fox News Digital. "He’s such a universally well-known star, and his divorce with Angelina Jolie and strained relationship with his kids have been well publicized."

"Most of the public would chalk it up to an unfortunate problem with the relationship with his kids, but as far as his brand, it’s still strong and would survive any type of criticism in this area," the lawyer for Los Angeles-based Lovell Firm explained.

Brad Pitt and Angelina Jolie's yearslong family divide has entered a new chapter as legal experts weigh in on what their twins reaching adulthood means for the former couple. (Jason LaVeris/FilmMagic via Getty)

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

Five of Pitt and Jolie's children have distanced themselves from the actor by dropping his surname, with Vivienne and three of her siblings choosing to make the change official legally.

"[Pitt's] talent remains unquestioned. But when multiple adult children publicly choose to stop using your surname, the conversation inevitably shifts from career to character," crisis communications expert Kaivan Shroff told Fox News Digital. "Whether fair or unfair, that’s the kind of narrative no amount of good publicity can easily overcome."

Four of Angelina Jolie and Brad Pitt's kids have made moves to legally drop the actor's surname. (Samir Hussein/WireImage)

Zahara filed to formally remove "Pitt" from her surname in June. If granted, her legal name will change from Zahara Marley Jolie-Pitt to Zahara Marley Jolie. Her name change follows Shiloh's name change in 2024. Maddox also reportedly filed a petition in May.

"This isn’t about a single family decision… it’s about accumulation," he added. "One child dropping a famous last name might be dismissed as an isolated event. When it happens repeatedly across multiple adult children, it stops looking like celebrity gossip and starts becoming part of a public figure’s legacy."

Angelina Jolie and Brad Pitt finalized their divorce in 2024, but experts say the former couple's highly public family conflict continues to generate headlines. (STR/AFP via Getty Images)

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Experts told Fox News Digital that Brad Pitt's Hollywood brand remains strong despite renewed attention on his relationship with his children and ex-wife Angelina Jolie. (Getty Images)

The public's reaction to the family drama, however, remains open to interpretation. While some experts believe the repeated rejection of Pitt's surname could become part of his public legacy, others contend the developments may instead generate sympathy for the Oscar-winning actor.

"One of the primary things that parents should focus on in a divorce is the well-being of the child, and that often means striving for the child to have a healthy relationship with both parents," Jordan Matthews told Fox News Digital.

The entertainment attorney for Los Angeles-based Holtz Matthews LLP argued that while the decision to drop Pitt's last name was "highly personal," it is likely to leave many members of the public "sympathetic to Pitt’s position."

title: Trump ally pledges to ‘work with people’ he disagrees with after Arizona primary win – US politics live
excerpt:
Andy Biggs – a hard-right congressman endorsed by Donald Trump – won the Republican nomination for Arizona governor

Hello and welcome to the US politics live blog.

Andy Biggs – a hard-right congress...
body_text:
Andy Biggs – a hard-right congressman endorsed by Donald Trump – won the Republican nomination for Arizona governor

Hello and welcome to the US politics live blog.

Andy Biggs – a hard-right congressman endorsed by Donald Trump – won the Republican nomination for Arizona governor, saying he will “work with people that don’t necessarily agree with me”.

He will now challenge the incumbent Democrat, governor Katie Hobbs, in a race that is expected to be among the most fiercely contested in the country this fall.

Biggs, a former chair of the House freedom caucus who was a central figure in the effort to overturn Trump’s election defeat, trounced fellow congressman David Schweikert, by appealing to the party’s deeply conservative base.

“Let’s make this the year that we turn Arizona red again,” Biggs said.

Schweikert argued during the campaign that Biggs would continue a decade of Republican backsliding. However, he struggled to raise money and did not even return to Arizona for Election Day, remaining in Washington as the House passed a government funding bill .

Biggs rejected Schweikert’s claim that he’ll be another Republican judged by voters to be too extreme.

“I am a conservative - and a principled conservative - but I work with people that don’t necessarily agree with me on every issue,” Biggs told a reporter after his victory speech.

Throughout the campaign, Biggs and Schweikert offered constrasting visions for reclaiming the governorship. Schweikert, who gave up his toss-up congressional seat to run for governor after eight terms, argued that Biggs’ hard-right politics and unswerving embrace of the president had allowed Democrats to make gains in once-ruby-red Arizona.

As he wages war on Iran to prevent that country from enriching uranium, Donald Trump has reportedly approved a deal with ⁠Saudi Arabia to have US firms build a civilian nuclear energy program in the kingdom, which could include a uranium enrichment facility, US officials told the Wall Street Journal. The deal, which the administration plans to submit ⁠to Congress ⁠in the ​coming days, does not include safeguards to stop ‌materials being used in nuclear weapons programs, Reuters reports.

The US-Israeli war on Iran has cost the Pentagon $37.5bn so far, defense secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee on Tuesday.

“Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal, the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” New York mayor Zohran Mamdani said in a video statement in which he called the International Criminal Court warrant for the Israeli prime minister’s arrest justified but admitted that he lacks the legal authority to enforce it.

Donald Trump announced a new package of tariffs on Tuesday, this time on generic drugs made overseas, to begin in 2028.

The Senate intelligence committee on Tuesday advanced the nomination of Jay Clayton to serve as the US intelligence director, despite criticism from Democrats.

The Trump administration paused more than $1bn in Medicaid funding to California and Minnesota, two states run by elected Democrats, citing “suspected fraud and noncompliance”.

A federal appeals panel has reversed a lower court decision ordering the release of pro-Palestinian activist Mohsen Mahdawi, opening the door to the re-arrest of a student protest organizer whom the Trump administration has spent more than a year trying to deport.

Tuesday’s ruling from the second US circuit court of appeals in New York City overturns a previous decision by a federal judge in Vermont, which likened the government’s efforts to expel him and other activists to McCarthyist repression.

In their unanimous decision, the panel found that the judge had overstepped his authority by intervening in Mahdawi’s case before it had moved fully through the immigration court system.

The ruling mirrors a separate appellate panel’s decision earlier this year against Mahmoud Khalil, a former classmate of Mahdawi’s at Columbia University.

As with the ruling against Khalil, the appeals court did not weigh in on the merits of Mahdawi’s claim that he was unconstitutionally targeted for criticizing Israel. He can seek a review from the federal courts over those claims once he has exhausted his options in the immigration courts, the panel wrote.

The Trump administration has repeatedly accused immigrants and protesters of “weaponizing” their vehicles to “assault” federal immigration agents, a claim used to justify arrests and deadly shootings by officers.

But a Guardian review of 26 cases that the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) has promoted as evidence of a surge in “vehicular attacks” reveals most of those claims have fallen apart under scrutiny, with the accusations consistently disproven by evidence or rejected in court. Only one of the 26 cases has resulted in an assault conviction, according to court records and information shared by prosecutors.

The findings come as DHS and Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) are facing intense scrutiny over the back-to-back fatal shootings of Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Texas and Joan Sebastián Durán Guerrero in Maine. In both cases, ICE officers killed the men during attempted traffic stops, with DHS arguing the men had endangered officers and the public with their vehicles – claims that witnesses have rejected.

DHS has repeatedly claimed its officers have faced a “3,300% increase in vehicle attacks” under Donald Trump’s second term, but has not provided evidence to support that figure and has not responded to requests for a list of cases.

To better understand how the government’s vehicle assault allegations have been adjudicated over time, the Guardian reviewed the most detailed list DHS has put out: a February press release asserting agents have experienced “more than 180 vehicle attacks” since Trump took office. The 2,100-word release provided summaries of 23 incidents from across the country from June 2025 to January 2026, saying it was a “non-exhaustive list”.

The Guardian looked at records for each case and contacted local and federal authorities and immigration and defense attorneys for more details. We identified 26 people accused in the cases of using their vehicles to threaten, assault or injure immigration officers. Five of them were shot by officers during the encounters, one fatally.

Nicolás Maduro returns to court with his lawyers Wednesday and is expected to continue objecting to the US military action that snatched the Venezuelan president from power and deposited him in New York to face drug trafficking charges.

Maduro, 63, and his wife Cilia Flores, 69, have been held at a Brooklyn jail ever since US forces seized them from their Caracas home in a stunning middle-of-the-night raid and brought them to New York in early January.

Both have pleaded not guilty. They face potential life prison sentences if a jury agrees that they were part of a conspiracy to send cocaine into the US, AP reports.

President Donald Trump’s administration has defended the raid as a “surgical law enforcement operation” in a criminal case first brought six years ago. Maduro has called himself a prisoner of war and his capture a kidnapping.

US prosecutors say Maduro carried out a conspiracy to move thousands of tons of cocaine into the US by colluding with Venezuelan law enforcement to help drug kingpins.

“I am not guilty. I am a decent man, the constitutional president of my country,” he said in Spanish at his arraignment in January.

The US House of Representatives on Tuesday approved a stopgap measure to head off a government shutdown until early December, with its Republican leaders seeking to defuse a volatile issue in the run-up to the November midterm elections.

The continuing resolution authorizing government spending until 4 December passed by a vote of 220-205, roughly along party lines. It next heads to the Senate, where it will need to win bipartisan support to clear the chamber.

The government’s current spending authority expires on 30 September, but top Republicans had argued the measure’s passage was necessary to ensure that Democrats do not block government funding measures in the Senate.

They did so last year for a record 43 days in protest against the expiration of tax credits that lowered some healthcare costs, and twice again this year, to push for reforms to immigration enforcement operations after the killings of two US citizens in Minneapolis.

“Our conference refuses to let the obstruction and inaction of Senate Democrats trigger another manufactured shutdown at the end of September. That is why we are acting before a funding lapse crisis, not in response to it,” said Tom Cole, the House appropriations committee chair who spearheaded the resolution.

Earlier on Tuesday, lawmakers agreed to move forward on the stopgap measure and a budget framework paving the way for a $95bn party-line package, in a 214-211 vote roughly along party lines.

Americans are 10 times more likely than people elsewhere in the world to regard politics as the greatest threat to their personal safety, according to a global survey that suggests the country’s deep partisan divisions are poisoning everyday life.

The latest Lloyd’s Register Foundation World Risk Poll found that one in 10 Americans identified politics as the biggest risk they face in their daily lives when asked an open-ended question. The figure is up from 6% in both 2021 and 2023 and well above the global average of 1% across 140 countries.

Although Americans remain far more likely to cite road accidents and crime as the worst dangers they face, the survey found the US to be the only country in which more people named politics than personal health conditions as the biggest risk to their safety.

“The US figure of 10% is unprecedented,” said Benedict Vigers, a senior global news writer at Gallup, which partnered on the research. “No other country ranked higher. It’s 10 times the global average.

“It speaks to how charged the political environment is and has been for many years in the United States that people are now saying that the political situation is not just an issue nationally but is a seeable daily risk to their safety as they go about their normal lives.”

The US has ranked among the countries most likely to cite politics as a personal safety risk in all three waves of the World Risk Poll but 2025 marks the first time it has reached the top of the global ranking.

Hello and welcome to the US politics live blog.

Andy Biggs – a hard-right congressman endorsed by Donald Trump – won the Republican nomination for Arizona governor, saying he will “work with people that don’t necessarily agree with me”.

He will now challenge the incumbent Democrat, governor Katie Hobbs, in a race that is expected to be among the most fiercely contested in the country this fall.

Biggs, a former chair of the House freedom caucus who was a central figure in the effort to overturn Trump’s election defeat, trounced fellow congressman David Schweikert, by appealing to the party’s deeply conservative base.

“Let’s make this the year that we turn Arizona red again,” Biggs said.

Schweikert argued during the campaign that Biggs would continue a decade of Republican backsliding. However, he struggled to raise money and did not even return to Arizona for Election Day, remaining in Washington as the House passed a government funding bill .

Biggs rejected Schweikert’s claim that he’ll be another Republican judged by voters to be too extreme.

“I am a conservative - and a principled conservative - but I work with people that don’t necessarily agree with me on every issue,” Biggs told a reporter after his victory speech.

Throughout the campaign, Biggs and Schweikert offered constrasting visions for reclaiming the governorship. Schweikert, who gave up his toss-up congressional seat to run for governor after eight terms, argued that Biggs’ hard-right politics and unswerving embrace of the president had allowed Democrats to make gains in once-ruby-red Arizona.

As he wages war on Iran to prevent that country from enriching uranium, Donald Trump has reportedly approved a deal with ⁠Saudi Arabia to have US firms build a civilian nuclear energy program in the kingdom, which could include a uranium enrichment facility, US officials told the Wall Street Journal. The deal, which the administration plans to submit ⁠to Congress ⁠in the ​coming days, does not include safeguards to stop ‌materials being used in nuclear weapons programs, Reuters reports.

The US-Israeli war on Iran has cost the Pentagon $37.5bn so far, defense secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee on Tuesday.

“Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal, the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” New York mayor Zohran Mamdani said in a video statement in which he called the International Criminal Court warrant for the Israeli prime minister’s arrest justified but admitted that he lacks the legal authority to enforce it.

Donald Trump announced a new package of tariffs on Tuesday, this time on generic drugs made overseas, to begin in 2028.

The Senate intelligence committee on Tuesday advanced the nomination of Jay Clayton to serve as the US intelligence director, despite criticism from Democrats.

The Trump administration paused more than $1bn in Medicaid funding to California and Minnesota, two states run by elected Democrats, citing “suspected fraud and noncompliance”.

title: DAVID MARCUS: In booming Detroit, voters skeptical of socialism snake oil
excerpt:
A Michigan plumber says far-left Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed needs to not be so "way left" in order to win Michigan voters.

DETROIT – It is rare to find a bartender who knows how to make a good V...
body_text:
A Michigan plumber says far-left Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed needs to not be so "way left" in order to win Michigan voters.

DETROIT – It is rare to find a bartender who knows how to make a good Vuex Carre, but Emily, at Le Supreme Brasserie, knocked it out of the park. She grew up in the suburbs, but loves living her 20s downtown, and the revitalization of the Motor City she enjoys may be a problem for far-left senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed.

A bit later, on Sunday night, I met Nick, 29, who grew up in Detroit and now works in advertising in Chicago. He marvels at the changes he has seen in his hometown.

"It’s so different now, a bunch of these places didn’t even exist a few years ago," he said.

DEMOCRATS' CIVIL WAR HEADS TO MICHIGAN WHERE PROGRESSIVES FACE BIGGEST TEST YET IN HIGH-STAKES SENATE SHOWDOWN

I heard it over and over again, and I could see it, as well. Along Washington and Woodward, the shops were buzzing, and even the heavy smoke from Canada couldn’t keep people from strolling the thoroughfares and valleys in the grand, sturdy Midwestern brick castle of downtown.

Not far away, earlier in the day, I had met Sean who was walking his attractive dog, and who moved to Detroit in 2019 from Philly. He lives in one of the fancier downtown buildings and loves his adopted hometown, and the lifestyle in the city.

"It’s easy going," he told me. "There is a lot to do. It’s great."

SWING-STATE POLL SPELLS TROUBLE FOR FAR LEFT AS SCHUMER-BACKED CANDIDATE DOMINATES WITH CRITICAL VOTING BLOCS

Sean is a committed Democrat who hates President Donald Trump.

"All that matters is that we elect a Democrat," he said. "R or D, that’s all it comes down to, which is a shame, but it's how things are."

For Sean, the top priority in the Aug. 4 Democratic Senate primary between Abdul El-Sayed and Rep. Haley Stevens is electability. That is not great news for El-Sayed, who has been pushing back against the idea that he is too radical to get elected in purple Michigan.

DAVID MARCUS: SOCIALISM AND EL-SAYED RUN INTO HARSH REALITY IN BLUE-COLLAR MICHIGAN

He has reason to be concerned, as I learned from James, a retired plumber in his 60s, who is Black and has been involved in civil rights movements.

Michigan Democratic candidate for Senate Abdul El-Sayed speaks at The People V. The Powerful rally at the Detroit Opera House on July 18, 2026 in Detroit, Michigan. (Sarah Rice/Getty Images)

"This just doesn’t feel like that movement I’ve been a part of, it feels different," James told me. "It feels foreign to me."

And he wasn’t alone, as it turns out. The day before, I had met Kelly, an American woman of Iranian descent who warned that today’s far-left candidates like El-Sayed "are just like in Iran." She said Muslim immigrants in nearby Dearborn, are not assimilating.

"When Joe [Biden] opened that border, they were planted there," she said.

Taken together, these recent encounters, from some very different people around Detroit, painted a picture that seems to be increasingly favoring Stevens, which is presumably why Sen. Bernie Sanders and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez did a three-stop tour for El-Sayed.

On Monday, El-Sayid got another boost of sorts as Sen. Elizabeth Warren endorsed him, although frankly, that is about as surprising as Tony the Tiger endorsing Frosted Flakes.

What is missing for El-Sayed – and all of his socialist out-of-town pals – in this city that is booming in comparison to many others in America, is any real, pressing need for socialism. Unlike New York City, where the rent is always "too damn high," in Detroit, affordability is not proving to be the same magical incantation that it was for Gotham Mayor Zohran Mamdani.

Many Democratic voters I talked to wanted a steadier hand on the system, and they want to beat Trump and the GOP. But they don’t want to blow up the system which has been thriving in many ways.

Michigan U.S. Sen. candidate, Rep. Haley Stevens, D-Mich., speaks with media after a debate at WoodTV studios on Tuesday, July 7, 2026, in Grand Rapids, Mich. (AP Photo/Kristen Norman)

There may be one more trump card to be played in this tight race in the form of Gov. Gretchen Whitmer, who has thus far declined to endorse either El-Sayed or Stevens. As confusing as it may be to Republicans around the country, Whitmer is very popular among Michigan Democrats, in part because of Detroit’s revitalization under her watch.

The stakes of this race for the national Democratic Party could not be higher. It is a true and fair test of whether the far-left wing of the party, led by college-educated Whites and immigrants, can replace the old party of Jefferson and Jackson led by the working class and native minorities.

Another thing that stood out in Motown was that, aside from the Bernie rally itself, not one voter I talked to brought up Israel. One way or the other, this matters because, while El-Sayed’s constant negative focus on Israel may put him on the right side of the issue with most Democrats, these days, it's not a driving issue.

Waiting in the background for the dust to settle is Republican Mike Rogers, who is set to take on the Democratic winner. From his point of his chances.

CLICK HERE TO READ MORE FROM DAVID MARCUS

David Marcus is a columnist living in West Virginia and the author of "Charade: The COVID Lies That Crushed A Nation."

title: Australia to warn China it won’t be bullied by ‘provocative‘ actions as it builds military and nuclear arsenal
excerpt:
Foreign minister Penny Wong will deliver speech criticising China’s recent launch of a nuclear-capable submarine-launched missile

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Penny Wo...
body_text:
Foreign minister Penny Wong will deliver speech criticising China’s recent launch of a nuclear-capable submarine-launched missile

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Penny Wong will warn China that Australia and other neighbouring nations will not be bullied by its “provocative” actions, after its involvement in another “destabilising and dangerous” incident this week.

The foreign affairs minister – who will deliver the address on Thursday in the Philippines, where the 59th Asean foreign minister’s meeting is being held – will also caution world leaders against placing a toll on the strait of Hormuz after both Iran and the US threatened to do amid the protracted conflict.

The speech comes as the crisis in the Middle East escalates once again, driving up fuel prices by 40 cents in three weeks amid dwindling global oil reserves.

Off the back of China’s launch of a nuclear-capable submarine-launched missile in the South China Sea earlier in July, Wong will say the test “came with too little information and notification”.

“It comes in the context of China’s rapid military buildup, including of its nuclear arsenal, without the transparency or reassurance the region expects,” the foreign minister will say.

“Which is why Australia has described the test as provocative and destabilising.”

Wong met with China’s foreign minister, Wang Yi, on the sidelines of Asean on Tuesday night AEST in their 12th meeting.

Wong had earlier criticised China’s conduct as “destabilising and dangerous” after the Philippine military accused the China Coast Guard of clubbing a Filipino sailor with a baton on Monday on a disputed shoal in the South China Sea.

“We will continue to register our concerns about China’s vessels engaging in destabilising and dangerous conduct in the South China Sea, including towards Philippine, Vietnamese, Malaysian, and Indonesian vessels,” Wong will say on Thursday.

China’s long-range missile test in the South Pacific happened just hours after Australia signed a defence agreement with Fiji, inciting widespread condemnation. The prime minister, Anthony Albanese, expressed concern at the missile’s long-range capabilities and the possibility to cause “considerable, considerable damage were it to be weaponised”.

In Manila, Wong will also warn the region will suffer if the strait of Hormuz is “weaponised” and the US and Iran fail to maintain a ceasefire.

Heavy strikes have resumed between the warring nations with the US launching consecutive rounds on Iran in an attempt to force the strait’s reopening.

Iran’s president on Tuesday said the country is in a “full-scale war” after the US expanded its aerial campaign and Tehran struck Washington’s Gulf allies. The Iran-allied Houthis in Yemen have also announced a blockade of Saudi ports.

The renewed outbreak of fighting has sent the international Brent crude benchmark surging by 23% over the past two weeks and back within reach of $US90 a barrel.

The surge in cost has pushed diesel above 220 cents per litre and petrol above 187 cents in some Australian cities. Luke Yeaman, the Commonwealth Bank chief economist, has predicted the federal government may have to restore the fuel excise discount back to 32 cents.

Wong will tell the audience much of the region was fuel- and energy-dependent on the strait of Hormuz, presenting a “grave challenge”.

“Across south-east Asia, energy prices have spiked, costs for families and businesses increased, and we continue to see ongoing pressure on food production,” Wong will say.

“The strait of Hormuz shows what we in this region have always understood. That people suffer when a strait is weaponised. And we all know that the costs of tolls on any chokepoint would not be solely economic.

“Our people depend on straits and sea lanes remaining open.”

Wong will tell Asean nations their assistance in plugging Australia’s supply and trade gaps during the conflict is for the region’s benefit.

“Because what goes to Australia as fuel and fertiliser comes back to the region as food,” she will say.

With additional reporting by Patrick Commins

title: Modelling scout linked to Epstein found dead in Paris suburbs
excerpt:
Modelling scout Daniel Siad has been found dead at his home

A modelling scout linked to the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein has been found dead at his home.

Daniel Siad, 69, was discover...
body_text:
Modelling scout Daniel Siad has been found dead at his home

A modelling scout linked to the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein has been found dead at his home.

Daniel Siad, 69, was discovered on Monday in Colombes, a north-west suburb of Paris, the Nanterre prosecutor's office confirmed to the BBC.

"An investigation to determine the cause of death was opened on Monday evening following the discovery," the office said, adding that an autopsy will be carried out.

Siad's name appeared thousands of times in the Epstein files released by the US government. He had previously denied any knowledge of the threat that Epstein posed.

Epstein victim Anya - not her real name - previously told the BBC Siad had introduced her to Epstein.

"It was a complete set-up," she said, describing Siad as "essentially a professional trafficker".

During one e-mail to Epstein released in the files, Siad wrote in badly-spelled English: "In This busyness I feel like fisherman some time I cache quick, some time no fish".

Ahead of a BBC investigation on the subject published on 18 July, Siad's lawyer had said he was not available for comment.

In a recent CNN interview, Siad said he did not know the women were being abused by Epstein: "I trusted him, I believed this guy is a professional person."

Siad himself was the subject of several complaints in France, including allegations of rape, with the first filed in February. He denied wrongdoing in multiple recent media interviews, telling BFMTV in May: "I have never raped anyone in my life."

A former model who had given a deposition to police in June as part of the investigation into Siad told the BBC his death meant information he may have had about Epstein was now lost.

"An important link in the chain has just vanished into thin air, without there even having been a chance to recover the information Daniel Siad might have possessed—given that he was never questioned," she said.

"Too much time has been lost... and it is yet another way for the leads to go cold. I hope we won't have to wait another seven years to see his home searched, even though I think it is already too late."

Anne-Claire Le Jeune, part of a group of lawyers from NGO Innocence en Danger which pushed for the start of an investigation into Epstein in Paris in 2019, said victims had been deprived of justice.

"My clients found the strength to break a particularly heavy silence, given Daniel Siad's ties to Jeffrey Epstein," she said. "They faced every stage of the proceedings with remarkable courage."

"It is deeply regrettable that the slow pace of the investigation resulted in his death before he could be questioned or taken into police custody, thereby depriving the victims of a crucial step in their pursuit of truth and justice."

Control, threats, disfiguring surgery: My life inside Jeffrey Epstein's 'cult'

title: Cocaine worth $370m seized in Liberia's biggest-ever drugs bust
excerpt:
Liberian Vice-President Jeremiah Koung praised the security forces for the operation

A huge quantity of cocaine has been seized during a raid on a suspected illegal storage facility near Liberia's ca...
body_text:
Liberian Vice-President Jeremiah Koung praised the security forces for the operation

A huge quantity of cocaine has been seized during a raid on a suspected illegal storage facility near Liberia's capital, Monrovia, in what authorities say is the biggest drugs bust in the country's history.

Videos and photos released by police showed dozens of sealed suitcases and boxes inside a lorry that investigators believed contained high-grade cocaine.

Police chief Gregory Coleman told the BBC that forensic tests had confirmed the substance was cocaine, with an estimated street value of more than $370m (£220m).

Several people were arrested during the raid in Duazon, a town about 20km (12 miles) east of Monrovia, including two foreigners and several Liberians.

No formal charges have yet been announced for those arrested.

The police chief said the seizure on Tuesday was the result of a long-running investigation into suspected drug-trafficking networks operating in Liberia.

"Between 2018 and 2023, apparently there was a resurface of major trafficking and transiting in Liberia. Over the last one-and-a-half years, we've been surveilling them and this is the result of that operation," said Coleman.

He said the drugs were "tested and confirmed" to be cocaine.

It was "largest drugs bust" in Liberia in recent years, he added.

It is still unclear from where the drugs originated or their intended destination.

Coleman told the BBC that the police had been investigating that when the two foreigners subsequently arrived in Liberia and were arrested.

He confirmed that one of them was carrying two passports - a Spanish one and a Colombian one - while the other held a Serbian passport.

It is still unclear from where the drugs originated or their intended destination

Liberian Vice-President Jeremiah Koung praised the security forces for the operation and said whosoever was found culpable would "feel the full weight of the law, regardless of who you are".

The Duazon operation follows the interception of six cargo boxes at Roberts International Airport last month after security officers detected irregularities during screening.

The shipment, falsely declared as containing food seasoning and textiles, was found to contain nearly 200 compressed blocks of cocaine destined for export, according to investigators.

The airport seizure sparked political controversy after critics accused the authorities of moving too slowly to identify suspects, raising concerns that some people linked to the case had left the country before arrests could be made.

President Joseph Boakai subsequently ordered a joint investigation involving police and the Liberia Drug Enforcement Agency (LDEA).

Several suspects have since been charged and international arrest warrants are being pursued for others still at large.

West Africa has long been regarded by international law enforcement agencies as a key transit route for cocaine trafficked from South America to Europe, taking advantage of porous borders and weak enforcement in parts of the region.

According to the Global Initiative Against Transnational Organised Crime, at least 30% of cocaine destined for Europe now passes through the region, while local cocaine consumption, including crack cocaine, has also been rising.

The organisation says cocaine seizures in West Africa have increased steadily since 2019, reaching about 30 tonnes in 2025.

The UN has also warned about the growing threat posed by drug-trafficking networks in the region, saying the "increasingly sophisticated operations launched by drug cartels is an issue of great concerns".

Liberia has experienced several high-profile drug seizures in recent years, including a 520kg cocaine haul intercepted at Monrovia's seaport in 2022.

Since taking office in 2024, President Boakai has reshuffled the leadership of Liberia's drug enforcement agency several times, as his government faces growing pressure to tackle the problem.

BBC undercover filming exposes Indian pharma firm fuelling opioid crisis

title: CNN's Jake Tapper hits Dems for rejecting illegal voting concerns amid New Jersey voter roll controversy
excerpt:
CNN anchor Jake Tapper called out Democrats while addressing the voter roll controversy that has rocked the blue state of New Jersey.

CNN anchor Jake Tapper took a swipe at Democrats who have long re...
body_text:
CNN anchor Jake Tapper called out Democrats while addressing the voter roll controversy that has rocked the blue state of New Jersey.

CNN anchor Jake Tapper took a swipe at Democrats who have long rejected concerns about illegal immigrants voting in elections following Tuesday's stunning revelation out of New Jersey.

Democratic New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill announced that thousands of non-citizens had been mistakenly added to voter rolls.

Sherrill said that "roughly 6,600 people" who were never eligible to vote were registered due to the "serious software error" by the state's DMV between June 2023 and June 2024 during the previous  administration.

NEW JERSEY REMOVES 6,600 PEOPLE FROM VOTER ROLLS WHO WERE ELIGIBLE TO CAST A BALLOT FOR 2024 ELECTION

Democratic New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill revealed that roughly 6,600 noncitizens were on the voter rolls due to a technical error in the state's DMV system that as many as 400 of them cast a ballot. (Heather Khalifa/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

She also revealed that "fewer than 400 individuals" among the noncitizens ended up voting in elections.

While covering the breaking news, Tapper acknowledged that the recent development does not benefit Democrats.

"This is not good for Democrats' argument that there's no such thing as undocumented immigrants voting," Tapper said during a panel discussion.

JOHNSON'S STRATEGY TO BYPASS DEMOCRATS' SAVE ACT BLOCKADE SURVIVES KEY VOTE, HANDING GOP BIG WIN

CNN's Jake Tapper called out Democrats for not having a "good argument" dismissing concerns about illegal voting following New Jersey's stunning voter roll controversy. (Bryan Bedder/Variety via Getty Images)

Conservative critics on social media took aim at liberals, suggesting that the New Jersey revelations validate their concerns about noncitizens being able to cast ballots in elections.

Members of the Trump administration have highlighted New Jersey's controversy to push the passing of the SAVE America Act meant to address election security.

Days before Sherrill's disclosure, President Donald Trump gave a primetime address from the White House renewing his call for Congress to pass the SAVE America Act while shedding light on election vulnerabilities in past elections cycles and China's effort to meddle in US elections.

CLICK HERE FOR THE LATEST MEDIA AND CULTURE NEWS

President Donald Trump speaks during the swearing in for Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin in the Oval Office of the White House, Tuesday, March 24, 2026, in Washington. (AP Photo/Alex Brandon)

Joseph A. Wulfsohn is a media reporter for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to joseph.wulfsohn@fox.com and on Twitter: @JosephWulfsohn.

title: Syrian people must not be failed again
excerpt: Syria has weighed on my mind for a long time: 15 years of trauma for its people and 15 years of failure in international engagement. I was directly involved in the latter – as a senior member of the U...
body_text:
Syria has weighed on my mind for a long time: 15 years of trauma for its people and 15 years of failure in international engagement. I was directly involved in the latter – as a senior member of the United Nations’ Special Envoy’s Office, and later as head of UN OCHA – and so I cannot claim innocence before the Syrian people.

In my time at the UN, I had to attend numerous meetings with senior officials of Bashar al-Assad’s regime, including the Syrian president himself. Some would accuse me and the UN of maintaining this engagement as part of a trade to get better humanitarian access.

The truth is different. Humanitarian agencies and their officials always meet anyone who can either help or impede humanitarian assistance. It’s not a trade. It’s a necessity – as is also true of mediators always meeting the parties to conflict in their efforts to end it.

All of my many meetings with al-Assad were to press for the decisions he needed to take for the welfare of his people. He was one of many people with deplorable power to create and sustain suffering whom I had to meet in my five decades working as a diplomat.

On one occasion, back near the beginning of al-Assad’s hideous war in 2012, I was living in Damascus and conducting daily futile negotiations with the government on behalf of the UN. I always raised the issue of detentions. One day, the man across the table stopped the clock. “Switch off the microphones,” he said, “and put down your pens.” Then turned to me and said very simply: “Stop wasting your time on this. We will never let them out. Nor will we ever admit to this truth.”

In 2022, in a private meeting with al-Assad, I suggested that he should empty the jails. “Of course,” he replied. “I have already emptied them. There are no detainees there now!”

A year later, in the aftermath of the devastating earthquakes, I met al-Assad three times. My job was to persuade him to do the necessary and obvious things for the welfare and the rights of his people.

I can now say that my efforts signally failed. The Syrian people were saved by military action. The takeover of Damascus set the table for peace.

As someone who has devoted a lifetime to dialogue and negotiation, I find it a bitter truth that action, not talk, was what was needed – and that it was required all those years earlier.

I now understand that the international community’s original sin was to set aside regime change from the outset as the route to end Syria’s suffering. Instead, we had “Friends of Syria” – a grouping of mainly Western powers and regional allies – supporting the opposition to al-Assad but making almost no difference to the daily toll of detentions and deaths affecting almost every Syrian family.

The lies endured throughout Syria’s suffering.

The war has now ended. What remains now is building peace. And as we have seen in the recent spate of bombings in Damascus, there is no shortage of those who want to see Syria fail.

The main priority is to rebuild the economy. Year after year, under the combined weight of war, sanctions and economic collapse, the people of Syria became steadily poorer. Today, two-thirds live in extreme poverty. And this is in a country of great talent and evident importance to its neighbours.

Aid is arriving, but as everywhere, it is sliced thinly. Gulf countries and EU member states are still giving. But I know well from my humanitarian work that a sustainable response to Syria’s crises has to come in the form of private investment in the economy.

Whether it be in banking, energy, construction, food processing, agribusiness and light manufacturing, Syria has tremendous potential for growth. Expatriate capital could help spearhead recovery, but significant global capital and technical support would still be needed.

Two problems remain: the removal of the last sanctions, long overdue and still a threat to building peace; and the management of risk for investors.

The United States’ recent announcement that it will delist Syria as a “state sponsor of terror” goes a long way in helping with the first point.

The second one remains a major challenge. A senior ambassador in Damascus told me recently that while there is international consensus to support Syria, the country still cannot offer predictable rules and legal protections that make investment possible.

The Syrian government needs to create a safer environment for investment, making Syria worth the risk, and to do so through well-thought-out legislation, not decrees or promises. Its capacity to follow through on declared commitments remains, sadly, limited. Too often bureaucratic inertia and official indecision are choking off opportunities. It needs to act publicly, inclusively and decisively.

The importance of this cannot be overstated. The country is pivotal to the stability of the region. This has always been true, and never more so than now amid escalating conflicts in the Middle East.

The views expressed in this article are the author’s own and do not necessarily reflect Al Jazeera’s editorial stance.

title: Trump ally Andy Biggs wins Republican nomination for Arizona governor
excerpt: A staunch ally of United States President Donald Trump, Representative Andy Biggs, has won the Republican nomination for Arizona governor in a race to secure one of the most competitive governorships ...
body_text:
A staunch ally of United States President Donald Trump, Representative Andy Biggs, has won the Republican nomination for Arizona governor in a race to secure one of the most competitive governorships in November.

Biggs overwhelmingly defeated a fellow member of the US House of Representatives, David Schweikert, in the primary election on Tuesday and will now face Democratic Governor Katie Hobbs in what could be one of the closest governor races of the midterm elections.

Arizona, which the Republican Party once dominated, has swung back and forth between it and the Democrats in recent elections.

Former Democratic President Joe Biden won the state in 2020 while Trump retook the state in the 2024 election.

As it now stands, Democrats hold both of Arizona’s US Senate seats and the top three state offices.

The Republican primaries also set up key congressional races in the battleground state as Democrats try to unseat the Republicans as the majority in the US Congress.

By choosing Biggs – who supported Trump’s failed attempt to block certification of his 2020 election loss and was the former head of the Conservative House Freedom Caucus – Republican voters now risk nominating another conservative who excites the party base but fails to win in November by turning off independents.

Still, Biggs celebrated his win on Tuesday night with a small crowd of supporters.

“Let’s make this the year that we turn Arizona red again,” he said, referring to the colour that denotes Republican Party wins on US election maps.

After his victory speech, Biggs rejected claims by Schweikert that he would be another Republican whom voters would judge as being too extreme.

“I am a conservative and a principled conservative, but I work with people that don’t necessarily agree with me on every issue,” Biggs told a reporter after his speech.

At the same time, Democrats were locked in a close primary contest in Arizona with former state Representative Amish Shah leading against party-backed Marlene Galan-Woods.
