Energy is the first requirement of endurance. By late February, China held an estimated 1.2 billion barrels of onshore crude, enough to cover roughly 109 days of seaborne imports. Those stocks gave Beijing more room to absorb the oil-market shock created by the Iran war.

China is also expanding its navy. Its latest warship is an amphibious ship capable of launching drones and aircraft, begins sea trials to test power and stability. (Zhang Liang/Xinhua via AP)

Beijing is extending its military reach as well. The Pentagon says China is developing a global logistics and basing network that could include permanent bases, shared facilities, military sites beside commercial infrastructure and temporary access to foreign ports. A joint logistics center opened at Cambodia’s Ream Naval Base in April 2025, while China continues to explore access along the sea lanes through the Malacca Strait, the Strait of Hormuz, Africa and the Middle East.

THREE KEY REASONS WHY AMERICA CANNOT AFFORD TO LOSE TAIWAN TO COMMUNIST CHINA

Beijing is building the ships that carry power and the access network that keeps them supplied.

China’s advantage is not confined to ships already afloat. It has built an industrial system able to manufacture and repair them. A U.S. Trade Representative investigation found that China’s share of global shipbuilding tonnage rose from less than 5% in 1999 to more than 50% in 2023. Commercial orders sustain the workers, steel mills, dry docks and suppliers that also strengthen the Chinese navy.

TIM SHEEHY EXPOSES A 'SCARY' SHIPBUILDING COLLAPSE THAT LEAVES THE US VULNERABLE TO CHINA

The same pattern is visible in the air. The Royal United Services Institute estimates that production of the upgraded J-20 stealth fighter reached about 120 aircraft a year by late 2025. An open-source assessment places the total delivered by mid-2026 at roughly 500. The exact number matters less than the lesson: China can mass-produce a sophisticated combat aircraft once dismissed as experimental.

On July 6, a Chinese nuclear-powered submarine launched a long-range ballistic missile into the Pacific. Whatever Beijing’s characterization, the test demonstrated greater sea-based nuclear reach and complicated any American calculation about intervening in a regional war.

China is not simply buying a military. It is building the means to replace one after losses begin.

Modern weapons depend on minerals, chips and data. The International Energy Agency reports that China is the leading refiner for 19 of 20 strategic minerals it tracks, with an average market share of about 70%. Those materials feed batteries, power grids, jet engines, electronics and defense systems. In a long confrontation, control over the ingredients needed to build the next missile may matter as much as the missile already in the launcher.

Beijing is shrinking its semiconductor vulnerability too. Chinese companies held 33% of global production capacity for foundational logic chips in 2023, up from 19% in 2015. These are not the most advanced chips, but they are workhorses inside vehicles, industrial equipment, communications systems and many weapons.

The ship does not prove that war is imminent. It does, however, fit a larger pattern. China is building the physical, industrial and technological capacity to absorb economic punishment, replace battlefield losses and project power long after a crisis begins.

Advanced computing power fits inside this strategy too. At the World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, Xi Jinping promoted Chinese open-source models, training for developing countries and a new coalition called the World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization. Beijing wants Chinese technology and standards embedded across the Global South.

Space ties these systems together. The U.S. Space Force says China ended 2025 with more than 1,353 satellites in orbit, including more than 510 capable of intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance. Those systems improve Beijing’s ability to locate American carriers, track deployed forces and support long-range targeting.

China’s Cross-Border Interbank Payment System now has 210 direct participants and 1,619 indirect participants. It does not replace the dollar; the renminbi accounted for only 1.99% of reported global currency reserves in the first quarter of 2026. Its purpose is more practical: keep essential trade moving if Western financial channels are restricted.

The campaign also reaches inside the United States. In June, the Justice Department and FBI disabled 13 websites allegedly backed by Chinese intelligence personnel. The sites posed as consulting businesses and targeted current and former U.S. officials and military personnel, especially security-clearance holders. Investigators said the operation used computer-generated profile photographs, professional networking sites and online payments to appear legitimate.

China is preparing to sustain power abroad while seeking leverage inside the societies most capable of resisting it.

Officers and soldiers on the Chinese People's Liberation Army PLA Navy's guided-missile destroyer Nanning wave as the ship enters the waters of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region in south China, July 2, 2026. (Ji Peng/Xinhua via Getty Images)

Beijing has serious weaknesses. China’s population fell by 3.39 million in 2025. Its property slump continues to depress confidence and domestic demand. The People’s Liberation Army has not fought a war since 1979 and lacks extensive modern combat experience, and the renminbi remains far from challenging the dollar.

Those weaknesses do not disprove the strategy. They explain why Beijing is building buffers. Xi understands where China is vulnerable and is methodically reducing those vulnerabilities before a major crisis.

Washington still treats shipbuilding as a Navy issue, semiconductors as a Commerce Department issue, minerals as a trade issue, espionage as an FBI issue and Taiwan as an Indo-Pacific Command issue. Beijing sees one contest.

America must do the same. We need larger stockpiles of munitions and strategic materials, sustained investment in shipbuilding and repair, secure allied supply chains, hardened space and communications networks, and deeper coordination with Japan, Australia, South Korea, the Philippines, Taiwan and Europe. We must also warn current and former officials about Chinese recruitment methods without treating Chinese Americans as suspects.

The giant vessel near Guangzhou may never dominate a television broadcast. It lacks the drama of a missile launch or a stealth fighter crossing the Taiwan Strait.

But it may tell us more about China’s plans than either.

Xi is building a country capable of fueling its forces, replacing losses, protecting industry and operating beyond the reach of a quick American knockout. China is preparing not only for a battle, but for the long contest that follows.

The urgent question is whether America is doing the same.

Nations that lose that race rarely get to run it twice. Beijing is spending every year of American hesitation to build its advantage. Washington still has time to close the gap, but not much of it remains.

CLICK HERE TO READ MORE FROM ROBERT MAGINNIS

Robert Maginnis is a retired U.S. Army infantry officer, senior fellow for National Security at the Family Research Council, and the author of 14 books. His latest, "The New AI Cold War: Liberty vs. Tyranny in the Age of Machine Empires," examines the global contest for AI supremacy and its implications for freedom.

title: STEVE FORBES, STEPHEN MOORE: America must free its airwaves before China pulls ahead
excerpt:
FOX Business reporter Madison Alworth discusses China's rapid progress in artificial intelligence, revealing a new AI model outperforming U.S. counterparts at a lower cost.

Washington rarely discover...
body_text:
FOX Business reporter Madison Alworth discusses China's rapid progress in artificial intelligence, revealing a new AI model outperforming U.S. counterparts at a lower cost.

Washington rarely discovers an economic policy that promotes investment, strengthens national security, improves services for consumers and raises billions of dollars for the Treasury without imposing a new tax. Federal Communications Commission Chairman Brendan Carr’s newly announced "super band" spectrum auction manages to do all four.

Spectrum may be invisible, but it is among the most valuable resources in the modern economy. It carries phone calls, financial transactions, emergency communications, industrial data, streaming video and the growing volume of information moving between connected devices.

The FCC has moved to auction 160 megahertz of the Upper C-band — the valuable mid-band airwaves that provide the combination of speed, capacity and geographic reach required by modern wireless networks.

AMAZON LEO SATELLITE INTERNET TAKES ON STARLINK

Congress directed the commission to make at least 100 megahertz available. The FCC went further, adding another 60 megahertz and combining it with spectrum cleared in 2020. The result will be a contiguous block of more than 400 megahertz, creating one of the most commercially useful bands of wireless spectrum in the world.

This will be the first major federal spectrum auction in five years. The commission also moved from a Notice of Proposed Rulemaking to a final order faster than it has in any comparable proceeding during the 5G era.

STEVE FORBES: REGULATORS RISK DESTROYING LOCAL TV BY BLOCKING KEY MEDIA MERGER

Keeping usable spectrum idle — or trapped in inefficient government allocations — has real costs. Networks aren’t built. Jobs aren’t created. New services don’t reach consumers. American companies lose ground to foreign competitors.

Auctions offer the quintessentially American solution: Free up and use markets.

A properly designed auction gets spectrum to companies that value it most and are prepared to put it to productive use. It establishes clear, enforceable rights. It encourages private investment. And it generates revenue for the federal government without raising anyone’s income, payroll or capital-gains taxes.

COULD THE NEXT CHINESE THREAT WALK INTO YOUR KITCHEN ON TWO BATTERY-POWERED LEGS?

The economic potential is enormous. The FCC estimates that putting this spectrum to work could add as much as $264 billion to gross domestic product, support 1.5 million jobs and create nearly $390 billion in benefits for consumers.

Those estimates may sound ambitious, but history shows the value of these airwaves. The first C-band auction in 2000 raised roughly $81 billion for the federal government. Few government actions can promote economic growth, encourage lower consumer prices and reduce federal borrowing at the same time.

This one can because it relies on competition rather than command-and-control regulation.

The auction is also a national security imperative.

The country that leads in mid-band spectrum will have an enormous advantage in 5G, the emerging 6G economy and the artificial-intelligence, robotics, transportation and defense applications that depend on advanced connectivity.

Wireless leadership isn’t merely about downloading movies more quickly. It determines which country’s companies develop the next generation of network equipment, whose technical standards are adopted internationally and where the investment and high-paying jobs associated with these technologies are created.

China understands this. Beijing has long treated spectrum as a strategic national asset. It has deployed extensive mid-band capacity, built networks at enormous scale and used subsidized telecommunications equipment to increase its influence around the world.

America’s regulatory apparatus, by contrast, has often moved at the speed of an old rotary telephone.

The Upper C-band order is crucially important, but it can’t be a one-time event. America badly needs a long-term spectrum strategy, not the haphazard, hit-and-miss approach we’ve had up to now.

Wireless companies and technology innovators make investment decisions years in advance. They need a predictable pipeline of licensed spectrum, not occasional auctions that are interrupted by political disputes and bureaucratic delays. Washington should establish a multiyear calendar that identifies which bands will be studied, cleared and auctioned.

Congress should also make the FCC’s auction authority permanent. Allowing that authority to expire, and then reviving it through temporary extensions, creates uncertainty and delays investment. No successful business would manage one of its most valuable assets that way. Neither should the U.S. government.

Washington must also take a harder look at the enormous amount of spectrum controlled by its own agencies, including the Defense Department.

National security operations must be protected. But "national security" shouldn’t become a mantra that prevents officials from examining whether every allocation is being used efficiently. There are effective ways to preserve government capabilities while freeing additional spectrum for commercial use.

The big problem is that federal agencies have little incentive to surrender unused and/or unneeded airwaves. Congress should change that by allowing agencies to share in the savings or auction proceeds generated when they consolidate operations, upgrade equipment or relinquish underused spectrum.

The broader principle is straightforward. Government shouldn’t attempt to build wireless networks, dictate business models or select favored technologies. Its responsibility is to free scarce spectrum, establish clear rights, enforce the rules and then get out of the way.

Private capital and American ingenuity will do the rest.

Chairman Carr’s Upper C-band auction follows that formula.

But technological leadership isn’t secured with a single auction. Washington must adopt a sensible, long-term strategy so that America has the spectrum resources required to dominate the next technological era.

Stephen Moore is a co-founder of Unleash Prosperity and a senior fellow at the America First Policy Institute.

Steve Forbes is chairman and editor-in-chief of Forbes Media.

title: Americans far more likely to view politics as greatest threat to safety, study finds
excerpt:
New survey reveals one in 10 Americans identify politics as biggest risk they face in life – 10 times the global average

Americans are 10 times more likely than people elsewhere in the world to regar...
body_text:
New survey reveals one in 10 Americans identify politics as biggest risk they face in life – 10 times the global average

Americans are 10 times more likely than people elsewhere in the world to regard politics as the greatest threat to their personal safety, according to a global survey that suggests the country’s deep partisan divisions are poisoning everyday life.

The latest Lloyd’s Register Foundation World Risk Poll found that one in 10 Americans identified politics as the biggest risk they face in their daily lives when asked an open-ended question. The figure is up from 6% in both 2021 and 2023 and well above the global average of 1% across 140 countries.

Although Americans remain far more likely to cite road accidents and crime as the worst dangers they face, the survey found the US to be the only country in which more people named politics than personal health conditions as the biggest risk to their safety.

“The US figure of 10% is unprecedented,” said Benedict Vigers, a senior global news writer at Gallup, which partnered on the research. “No other country ranked higher. It’s 10 times the global average.

“It speaks to how charged the political environment is and has been for many years in the United States that people are now saying that the political situation is not just an issue nationally but is a seeable daily risk to their safety as they go about their normal lives.”

The US has ranked among the countries most likely to cite politics as a personal safety risk in all three waves of the World Risk Poll but 2025 marks the first time it has reached the top of the global ranking.

It was effectively tied with Hungary, which recorded 9%; polling was conducted months before the country’s recent election. Serbia, Latvia and Lithuania followed at 6% while Slovakia, Russia, Myanmar, Spain and Israel each recorded 5%.

But the US in particular has witnessed a surge in political violence over the past decade, including three assassination attempts against Donald Trump, the 2025 killing of Charlie Kirk, the targeting of other national and state officials and a deadly insurrection at the US Capitol in January 6, 2021. There has been a rise in localised harassment, death threats against election administrators and the use of doxing and “swatting” to intimidate and silence political opposition.

The concerns cut across demographic and ideological lines. There were no statistically significant differences by age, gender, education or political affiliation, with Republicans, Democrats and independents all similarly likely to identify politics as their greatest concern.

Respondents often blamed the opposing political camp for creating an atmosphere of insecurity, while others pointed more broadly to the country’s increasingly fractured politics.

“I would have to say the state of polarisation of the American people,” said one 54-year-old Republican man.

A 42-year-old independent male respondent said: “I would say political extremism. I don’t have a lot of personal risk, but I do feel the biggest risk is people that are fanatical about their position and that they could be violent.”

A 69-year-old Democratic woman added: “The political situation has a lot to do with the fact whether if I am safe or not. I am concerned where we’re going or might go.”

Vigers said: “Almost all of the respondents who mention that politics is the top risk to safety mention or refer to polarisation whether directly or indirectly. It’s either Republicans saying the Democrats or it’s Democratic voters saying Trump and the Republicans are a big risk to my safety.

“But we have several other people who call it out directly and say, the political environment makes me fearful for my safety or the heightened sense of polarisation makes me fear for my safety. It is quite a uniquely American phenomenon.”

The findings echo other Gallup polling that has highlighted Americans’ growing disillusionment with politics. In 2025 a third of US adults identified politics and government as the country’s most important problem, well above the global median and exceeded only by respondents in the Philippines and Taiwan.

In 2024, 5% of Americans named the US as its own greatest enemy, the highest figure on record.

At the same time, confidence in government appears to have deteriorated. In 2025, 58% of Americans said the government does not care about them at all, the highest figure recorded and 11 percentage points higher than in 2021. Among OECD member states, only Colombia and Greece recorded higher levels of public alienation from government.

title: Al Jazeera reports from anti-US protest outside ASEAN summit
excerpt:
Al Jazeera reports from anti-US protest outside ASEAN summit

Al Jazeera’s Barnaby Lo reported from an anti-US protest taking place outside the ASEAN summit in the Philippines, which US Secretary of S...
body_text:
Al Jazeera reports from anti-US protest outside ASEAN summit

Al Jazeera’s Barnaby Lo reported from an anti-US protest taking place outside the ASEAN summit in the Philippines, which US Secretary of State Marco Rubio is attending.

Protesters condemned the US war on Iran and said that Rubio is a “war criminal”.

title: Iran, Gulf hit as Hormuz, Bab al-Mandeb close: What’s the latest?
excerpt:
US defence chief Hegseth says war on Iran has cost $37.5bn, seeks $87.6bn more

The conflict in the Middle East has widened as two oil tankers headed to Asia, carrying Saudi crude, turned around in th...
body_text:
US defence chief Hegseth says war on Iran has cost $37.5bn, seeks $87.6bn more

The conflict in the Middle East has widened as two oil tankers headed to Asia, carrying Saudi crude, turned around in the Red Sea on Tuesday after Yemen’s Houthis announced a naval blockade against Saudi Arabia.

The closure of the Bab al-Mandeb strait that connects the Red Sea to the Indian Ocean adds pressure on global trade routes, with ongoing disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz due to the ongoing US-Israel war on Iran.

Escalations continue, as the United States announced that it concluded its 11th round of attacks on Iran. Tehran retaliated, hitting US military targets in the Gulf.

Here is a recap of what happened on Tuesday night and Wednesday.

The US Central Command (CENTCOM) said that at 00:15 GMT on Wednesday, it concluded its latest round of strikes against Iran for the 11th consecutive night.

CENTCOM said it targeted Iranian military operations centres, maritime capabilities, aircraft hangars, drone storage facilities and military logistics infrastructure “to further degrade Iran’s ability to threaten commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz”.

The CENTCOM statement claimed that over the past three months, Iran had attacked more than 30 commercial vessels traversing the Strait.

The deputy governor of Iran’s southwestern Khuzestan province told the Fars news agency in Iran that US missile attacks targeted areas near the cities of Behbahan and Omidiyeh in the province on Wednesday.

The agency also reported that early on Wednesday, US forces attacked a military site on the outskirts of Tabriz in northwestern Iran. Al Jazeera’s Tohid Assidi reported from Iranian capital Tehran that explosions were heard in Tabriz.

Assidi additionally reported that air defences were activated in Tehran and that two blasts were heard in Bushehr.

Iran’s IRIB state broadcaster said that the deputy governor of Sistan and Baluchestan province had confirmed attacks on the southeastern cities of Chabahar and Konarak.

IRIB additionally reported, citing the deputy governor of Hamadan province, that US forces launched air attacks on Kabudarahang County in west-central Hamadan province in the latest round of attacks.

The broadcaster also reported that air attacks hit the Chavar and Abdanan regions in Iran’s western Ilam province and that US attacks hit the western Kurdistan province.

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said on Wednesday that “the operation to punish the aggressor continues”, IRIB reported.

Kuwait: Iran’s military says it launched drones at US military facilities at Camp Doha in western Kuwait on Wednesday, Iran’s semi-official Tasnim news agency reported.

Jordan: Iran’s military said in a statement on Wednesday that it targeted Jordan’s Muwaffaq Salti Airbase in Azraq with drones, according to the Mehr News Agency. The statement said: “The army targeted the ‘housing and welfare buildings’ and ‘equipment warehouses’ of the terrorist US army at the Azraq base in Jordan with drones in the morning.”

Bahrain: The statement, cited by Mehr news agency, added that Iran targeted Bahrain’s Isa Airbase with drones.

“[Drones] also targeted the ‘large equipment warehouses and sheds’ and the ‘heavy aircraft maintenance and repair sheds’ of the child-killing and criminal US army at the Sheikh Isa Airbase in Bahrain,” the statement said.

The US Department of Defense said in a statement on Wednesday that a third US soldier is believed to have been killed in action during an Iranian strike on Muwaffaq Salti Airbase in Jordan on July 17.

The department said the 28-year-old had previously been reported missing, but that the soldier’s status has now been updated to “Duty Status -Whereabouts Unknown, believed deceased”.

Two other soldiers were killed in the attack on the Jordan base. Another soldier was also killed in Iraq last week while attempting to detonate explosives on an Iranian drone. The latest death brings the total number of US soldiers killed to 18 since the war on Iran began on February 28.

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said that Washington is open-minded on the war on Iran and willing to engage, negotiate and settle differences.

“If they’re serious, we’re serious. If they’re not, then we will do what’s necessary to protect our interests and also the interests of our allies,” Rubio said in a meeting with Southeast Asian counterparts in Manila.

Meanwhile in Washington, US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told senators during a hearing that the war had cost the country’s military $37.5bn so far, urging them to approve an additional $67bn to replenish depleted missile supplies.

title: Middle East crisis live: US renews strikes on Iran as Trump threatens to attack nuclear site ‘very heavily’
excerpt:
US military completes 11th night of strikes as attacks overshadow diplomatic efforts in Pakistan to salvage interim ceasefire deal

Houthis threaten attacks on shipping tankers if they use Saudi ports...
body_text:
US military completes 11th night of strikes as attacks overshadow diplomatic efforts in Pakistan to salvage interim ceasefire deal

Houthis threaten attacks on shipping tankers if they use Saudi ports on Red Sea

What is Pickaxe Mountain, Iran’s underground nuclear facility?

Welcome to our live coverage of the latest developments in the US-Iran war, and its regional and global ramifications.

The US military completed its 11th night of strikes on Iran, including on aircraft hangars and drone storage sites, as the renewed conflict continues to undermine diplomatic efforts to salvage an interim ceasefire deal.

In what could open up a potential new front in the war, Yemen’s Iran-aligned Houthis, who control the coast at the mouth of the Red Sea, announced a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia. With the strait of Hormuz blocked, Saudi Arabia has been relying on a pipeline to the Red Sea to get millions of barrels of oil out to market.

The latest US barrage came as Donald Trump warned strikes would intensify.

The US leader also declared the US would “soon” target “very heavily” the area near Pickaxe Mountain – a fortified underground site near one of Iran’s primary nuclear enrichment facilities, which Iran’s military leaders said would be considered a major escalation.

Before the latest strikes, Iranian interior minister Eskandar Momeni visited Pakistan, a key mediator in the conflict. But it was unclear what new arrangement might be reached to end the war, which is now centered on a battle for control over the strait of Hormuz.

The US war in Iran has cost $37.5bn so far, US defence secretary Pete Hegseth said, an increase of nearly $8bn since the last public estimate.

The number of wounded US personnel in the Iran war has grown to well more than 500 troops, a US official told the Associated Press.

Brent crude was trading up 1.2% at $92.09 a barrel at around 0130 GMT, its highest intraday price since 11 June. West Texas Intermediate (WTI), the main US oil benchmark, climbed 1.1% to $85.23.

The US secretary of state Marco Rubio told South-east Asian foreign ministers on Wednesday that Iranian control of the strait of Hormuz would set a dangerous precedent with repercussions beyond the Middle East.

Trump said he would authorise the resumption of direct flights by US airlines to Lebanon, which have been suspended since the 1985 hijacking by Lebanese Islamist militants of a TWA flight with many Americans aboard.

Donald Trump has approved an agreement with Saudi Arabia that could provide the kingdom with uranium enrichment capability for its civilian nuclear programme, according to news reports citing people familiar with the matter.

The deal is expected to last 30 years and is also expected to involve US firms in developing the programme. It could allow for the building of a uranium enrichment facility in Saudi Arabia after a joint US-Saudi study.

The agreement is not expected to include the IAEA’s “additional protocol”, which would allow for more monitoring, inspections and verification, according to one of the people who revealed it.

Donald Trump on Tuesday made new threats to attack and destroy Iran’s nuclear site known as Pickaxe Mountain.

Here’s what we know about Pickaxe Mountain, why it has captured Trump’s attention and how the US military could strike at the deeply buried complex.

Welcome to our live coverage of the latest developments in the US-Iran war, and its regional and global ramifications.

The US military completed its 11th night of strikes on Iran, including on aircraft hangars and drone storage sites, as the renewed conflict continues to undermine diplomatic efforts to salvage an interim ceasefire deal.

In what could open up a potential new front in the war, Yemen’s Iran-aligned Houthis, who control the coast at the mouth of the Red Sea, announced a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia. With the strait of Hormuz blocked, Saudi Arabia has been relying on a pipeline to the Red Sea to get millions of barrels of oil out to market.

The latest US barrage came as Donald Trump warned strikes would intensify.

The US leader also declared the US would “soon” target “very heavily” the area near Pickaxe Mountain – a fortified underground site near one of Iran’s primary nuclear enrichment facilities, which Iran’s military leaders said would be considered a major escalation.

Before the latest strikes, Iranian interior minister Eskandar Momeni visited Pakistan, a key mediator in the conflict. But it was unclear what new arrangement might be reached to end the war, which is now centered on a battle for control over the strait of Hormuz.

The US war in Iran has cost $37.5bn so far, US defence secretary Pete Hegseth said, an increase of nearly $8bn since the last public estimate.

The number of wounded US personnel in the Iran war has grown to well more than 500 troops, a US official told the Associated Press.

Brent crude was trading up 1.2% at $92.09 a barrel at around 0130 GMT, its highest intraday price since 11 June. West Texas Intermediate (WTI), the main US oil benchmark, climbed 1.1% to $85.23.

The US secretary of state Marco Rubio told South-east Asian foreign ministers on Wednesday that Iranian control of the strait of Hormuz would set a dangerous precedent with repercussions beyond the Middle East.

Trump said he would authorise the resumption of direct flights by US airlines to Lebanon, which have been suspended since the 1985 hijacking by Lebanese Islamist militants of a TWA flight with many Americans aboard.

title: Mamdani urges US government to arrest Netanyahu if Israeli PM visits US
excerpt:
New York mayor admits he does not have authority to arrest Netanyahu, adding Israeli PM is ‘architect of a horrific genocide’

Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, acknowledged on Tuesday he does ...
body_text:
New York mayor admits he does not have authority to arrest Netanyahu, adding Israeli PM is ‘architect of a horrific genocide’

Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, acknowledged on Tuesday he does not have the authority to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu, but urged US federal authorities to do so if the Israeli prime minister visits the US later this year.

“Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal, the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” Mamdani said in a video posted to social media.

Mamdani said he believed the international criminal court warrant for Netanyahu’s arrest over alleged war crimes was justified, but admitted he lacked the legal authority to enforce it.

“My administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City could execute the international criminal court’s arrest warrant if Benjamin Netanyahu came here,” Mamdani said. “It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant.

“The federal government, however, does – and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant,” the mayor added. “And I want to be equally clear: Benjamin Netanyahu is not welcome in New York City, nor is any other war criminal at large.”

Netanyahu is expected to travel to New York in September for the UN general assembly.

Mamdani said last week he was reviewing whether he would be able to arrest Netanyahu during that visit, after pledging to do so during his mayoral campaign last year.

“I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani said in reference to the Dutch city where the international criminal court, which issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu in 2024, is located.

The US does not recognize the ICC’s authority and is not a party to it.

His remarks drew rebuke from Donald Trump, who insisted Netanyahu “will not be arrested” on US soil.

Trump said in a social media post Netanyahu is “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”.

Netanyahu, meanwhile, said he was “not concerned” about Mamdani’s comments.

title: DHS issues $84B in fines to illegal immigrants under Trump administration
excerpt:
Retired NYPD Inspector Paul Mauro discusses the complexities of I.C.E. operations, attributing recent enforcement issues to the large number of unvetted illegal immigrants on 'America Reports.'

FIRST...
body_text:
Retired NYPD Inspector Paul Mauro discusses the complexities of I.C.E. operations, attributing recent enforcement issues to the large number of unvetted illegal immigrants on 'America Reports.'

FIRST ON FOX: The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) has issued more than 100,000 civil fines totaling roughly $84 billion to illegal immigrants under the Trump administration while collecting more than $1 billion in payments, Fox News Digital has learned.

Since President Donald Trump returned to the White House about 18 months ago, DHS has issued 103,000 civil fines totaling approximately $84 billion, a DHS official told Fox News Digital.

As of July, the agency had collected about $1.2 billion in fine payments.

TRUMP PUTS ASYLUM LAWYERS ‘ON NOTICE’ WITH NEW FRONT IN WAR ON FRAUD

The Department of Homeland Security said it has collected more than $1 billion in civil fine payments from illegal immigrants since President Donald Trump returned to office.

Shortly after Trump's return to office, the administration announced it would begin fining illegal immigrants subject to final removal orders $998 per day if they failed to voluntarily leave the United States or otherwise comply with their removal orders. Illegal immigrants are required to pay the fines before they are deported, and DHS has said it may seize assets from those who fail to pay.

The Trump administration has unveiled a series of measures designed to encourage illegal immigrants to leave the country, including DHS' announcement that those who refuse to self-deport could face "significant financial penalty."

DHS has argued the program costs significantly less than traditional deportation proceedings and is designed to encourage illegal immigrants to voluntarily leave the United States.

TRUMP ADMINISTRATION ORDERS GREEN CARD APPLICANTS TO LEAVE THE US, APPLY FROM THEIR HOME COUNTRIES

President Donald Trump shook hands with newly sworn-in Department of Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin during a ceremony in the Oval Office at the White House on March 24, in Washington, D.C. (Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images)

U.S. Customs and Border Protection released a flyer outlining the "benefits" and "consequences" for illegal immigrants who self-deport using the CBP Home app.

"Self-deportation is safe," the flyer stated. "Leave on your own terms by picking your departure flight."

According to DHS, non-criminal illegal immigrants who voluntarily leave the United States may keep money they earned while in the country and preserve eligibility to pursue legal immigration in the future. Some may also qualify for subsidized flights if they cannot afford travel home.

TOM HOMAN WARNS ICE SURGE COMING TO NEW YORK CITY AS MAMDANI BOASTS ABOUT SANCTUARY STATUS

The Department of Homeland Security said it has issued more than 100,000 civil fines to illegal immigrants since President Donald Trump returned to office while collecting more than $1 billion in payments. (Yuri Gripas/Abaca/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Meanwhile, the flyer warns that those who fail to self-deport will be "apprehended by DHS with no opportunity to get your affairs in order beforehand."

In May, Fox News Digital reported that more than 3 million illegal immigrants had left the United States during Trump's first year back in office, according to figures provided by DHS. The department said an estimated 2.2 million of those migrants self-deported.

DHS also said the administration had deported nearly 900,000 illegal immigrants and arrested more than 900,000 others as of May 17.

The department said the figures demonstrate that Trump's immigration crackdown has reshaped migration patterns following a record number of illegal crossings during former President Joe Biden's administration.

Fox News Digital's Brittany Miller and Landon Mion contributed to this report.

Michael Sinkewicz is a writer for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to michael.sinkewicz@fox.com

title: Burnham announces bus fares across England to be capped at £2
excerpt:
Measure backed by £454m of funding, with £400m coming from switching investment in international climate finance to loans

Single bus fares across England will be capped at £2 from January, the prime ...
body_text:
Measure backed by £454m of funding, with £400m coming from switching investment in international climate finance to loans

Single bus fares across England will be capped at £2 from January, the prime minister, Andy Burnham, has announced.

The nationwide cap is currently set at £3 until the end of March 2027 although some areas – including London and Greater Manchester, where Burnham set a £2 limit as mayor – have lower top rates for single tickets.

The measure, which will apply across England except London, is backed by £454m of funding, including money to allow devolved governments to take similar action.

About £400m of that funding will come from switching investment in international climate finance to loans, with the rest from savings in the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero budget.

The total cost of the scheme is expected to be more than £500m, with the rest coming from Department for Transport funding already allocated to buses.

The £2 cap will be in place throughout 2027 and reverses an increase put in place by Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves in 2024.

A £2 cap had been in place between January 2023 and December 2024 but the incoming Labour administration argued it was not affordable, although some metro mayors, including Burnham, decided to fund an extension of the lower limit in their areas.

Burnham said: “Good, affordable transport links are an essential. No one should be priced out of those and left behind.

“But for too long people have said that cheaper transport isn’t an option. I don’t accept that. I’ve done it before and I will do it again now: a £2 cap on bus fares for millions across the country.

“Lower fares will help people get to where they need to – giving them breathing space to help with the cost of living.”

The measure is backed by £454m of funding, including money to allow devolved governments to take similar action.

The chancellor, John Healey, said millions of people would benefit from fares being slashed by a third and insisted the measure could be paid for. “This action to ease the cost of living is funded by savings made elsewhere, so there’s no burden to British taxpayers, just pounds going back into their pockets,” he said.

The transport secretary, Heidi Alexander, said the move would make “everyday journeys easier and cheaper”.

title: Can Pakistan-administered Kashmir’s election overcome a violent summer?
excerpt:
Muzaffarabad, Pakistan-administered Kashmir: The traffic crawled beneath densely strung election bunting.

Overhead hung the Pakistan Muslim League-Nawaz’s (PMLN) green tigers. Further down, Pakistan ...
body_text:
Muzaffarabad, Pakistan-administered Kashmir: The traffic crawled beneath densely strung election bunting.

Overhead hung the Pakistan Muslim League-Nawaz’s (PMLN) green tigers. Further down, Pakistan Peoples Party’s (PPP) red and black flags bearing the party’s arrow encircled the clock tower at Lal Chowk, a square near Muzaffarabad’s main market, built to honour the original Lal Chowk across the Line of Control in Indian-administered Kashmir’s Srinagar.

Muzaffarabad, the capital of Pakistan-administered Kashmir, does not look like a city in the grip of a crisis. It looks like a city preparing for an election.

Since early June, the territory has witnessed weeks of protests and sit-ins that have killed dozens. The protests are tied to elections for the region’s provincial legislature that were scheduled for July 27, but that on Monday were divided into voting now to be held on three separate days. Voting in the region’s Mirpur division will continue as planned on July 27. Elections in Poonch division, which has been the hotbed of protests, will now be held on August 10.

Voting in Muzaffarabad and for 12 other seats reserved for refugees from Indian-administered Kashmir who now live elsewhere in Pakistan will be held on August 2, the region’s Chief Election Commissioner Justice (retired) Ghulam Mustafa Mughal told Al Jazeera, citing security concerns arising from the unrest as the reason for the changed schedule.

It is those reserved seats for refugees that are at the heart of the agitation, with protesters demanding that they be scrapped, arguing that only those living in Pakistan-administered Kashmir should now be eligible to contest in elections to the regional legislature.

And it is in the backdrop of those tensions that the region is now readying to vote.

“I have never joined them, I never participated in their protest,” a 60-year-old driver, bespectacled and bearded, said of the Jammu Kashmir Joint Awami Action Committee (JKJAAC), commonly known as JAAC, the movement whose protests have dominated the politics of Pakistan-administered Kashmir, particularly Poonch division, in recent months.

“But my only reason to support them is that all the people in this movement are poor people, my sympathies are with them. They are the ones fighting and demanding for my rights,” the driver, who requested anonymity, told Al Jazeera, as he finished his lunch at a roadside hotel in Muzaffarabad on July 20.

When it came to the election, however, he was unequivocal: he would not vote.

“We should be part of the democratic process, but the person you want to vote for isn’t allowed on the ballot,” he said, referring to the JAAC, which was banned in June. “A vote is a responsibility, and it should only be given to those who deserve it. Right now the candidates on offer are all corrupt, and I will not be giving my vote.”

The Kashmir region, a scenic but disputed Himalayan territory, has been at the centre of multiple wars between Pakistan and India since both gained independence from British rule in 1947.

While both countries claim the region in full, they administer separate parts of it.

Home to more than four million people, Pakistan-administered Kashmir, locally known as Azad Jammu and Kashmir (AJK), has a semi-autonomous government with its own president, prime minister and Supreme Court.

The election was always going to be shaped by the latest crisis, JAAC’s fifth major mobilisation since the movement was founded in September 2023.

But Mughal, AJK’s chief election commissioner, said Poonch was the only division where, he said, “we have a bit of security-related concern”.

He is not the only official insisting the electoral process remains on track.

In Muzaffarabad, Deputy Commissioner Munir Qureshi pointed to bustling markets, heavy traffic and an active campaign trail as evidence that daily life continues largely uninterrupted.

“It is not our job to tell people to go and vote,” he said. “If they decide, for whatever reason, not to show up, it is their democratic right.”

The city, he added, has five constituencies and 890 polling stations, with additional security personnel requested ahead of polling day.

What neither official addressed directly, however, is the absent party hanging over the election.

The banned JAAC is absent from the ballot in any form. Some of its members who attempted to contest as independents had their nomination papers rejected.

The movement’s sit-ins have entered their second month across Rawalakot, Sudhnoti and other parts of Poonch division, closer to the Indian border, while its leadership has openly urged the public to boycott the vote.

How the region votes – or not – will tell whether the election carries legitimacy in the eyes of the people of AJK.

The protests that have shaped this election began more than two years ago over prices.

JAAC was formed in 2023 around 10 demands, including subsidised wheat, cheaper electricity, and a government commitment to curb the perks and pensions enjoyed by AJK officials and elected representatives.

The Pakistani government intervened in May 2024, agreeing to provide subsidies, while a charter of demands signed by the AJK government in October that year was intended to resolve most of the movement’s concerns.

By this June, however, JAAC’s focus had shifted to the 12 assembly seats reserved for Kashmiri refugees settled in Pakistan, which it wants abolished.

The group argues the seats give outsiders influence over AJK’s governance and budget, while residents themselves have no say over who occupies them.

On June 5, the AJK government declared JAAC a proscribed organisation under anti-terrorism legislation ahead of a planned June 9 protest, just weeks before the election.

Within days, police had arrested dozens of JAAC members and supporters, including the movement’s leader and public face, Shaukat Nawaz Mir.

What followed was the deadliest period of unrest AJK has witnessed in years.

The death toll remains disputed by JAAC and the authorities, but even conservative estimates place it at nearly 30 people since early June, including protesters and law enforcement personnel.

Access to Pakistan-administered Kashmir was also restricted, with entry into Poonch division almost entirely blocked for weeks, disrupting supplies of flour and other essential goods to a region dependent on routes from Muzaffarabad and Mirpur.

Banks remained closed for two weeks, leaving residents without access to cash. Mobile and fibre internet services have also remained suspended across almost the entire territory, with exceptions only for banks and a handful of government offices, including district administrations and the Election Commission.

And the Election Commission itself has become an institution whose role faces questions from the JAAC.

Sardar Amanullah, a JAAC member from Sudhnoti district in Poonch division, more than 155km (96 miles) south of Muzaffarabad, said he had submitted nomination papers as an independent candidate, only for them to be rejected.

“I don’t think these are free and fair elections,” he said. “When you cannot let people’s representation out there, the government keeps finding ways to reject our papers,” he told Al Jazeera.

Chief Election Commissioner Mughal offered a different account.

“There were only two or three people who submitted their nomination papers, who claim to be affiliated with JAAC, and their papers were rejected by the returning officer since their names were mentioned in FIRs and the Fourth Schedule,” he said, referring to police reports and Pakistan’s anti-terrorism watchlist.

Abid Shaheen, a JAAC core committee member from Bagh district, was more blunt. With the group now proscribed, he described the vote as “a selection, not an election”.

Of the AJK Legislative Assembly’s 45 general seats, 12 are reserved for Kashmiri refugees who settled in Pakistan after 1947: six linked to Jammu and six to the Kashmir Valley.

The arrangement dates back to legislation introduced in the 1960s and was later entrenched in AJK’s 1974 Interim Constitution.

In June, the AJK Supreme Court, ruling on a presidential reference, held that the seats could only be abolished through a formal constitutional amendment passed by the assembly, not through public protest.

AJK’s electorate has grown from just over 3.2 million in 2021 to more than 3.8 million this year, according to Election Commission figures.

Within that, the 12 refugee constituencies alone have expanded from about 405,000 voters to nearly 439,000. JAAC argues that the increase has further amplified the influence of seats that were never intended to carry such political weight.

Since AJK adopted its current parliamentary system in 1975, all 10 general elections have produced governments aligned with the party in power in Islamabad.

Zulfiqar Ali, a Muzaffarabad-based political analyst, argued that the seats themselves have rarely determined who governs AJK.

“Across all the governments AJK has had, these 12 seats have played no significant role in actually making or breaking a government,” he said. “That role has always belonged to native AJK politicians, or whichever party holds federal power in Islamabad.”

Islamabad, meanwhile, has shown little interest in rethinking the refugee seats.

Defence Minister Khawaja Asif told the National Assembly last month that abolishing the seats under pressure from street protests would amount to “sabotaging the electoral process”.

For the parties actually on the ballot, the crisis all but wiped out campaigning in June.

“Frankly speaking, the campaigning was dead due to the protests all June,” said Iftikhar Gillani, a PMLN candidate contesting from Muzaffarabad.

“The campaign only picked up in the last few days,” Gillani told Al Jazeera after meeting supporters at a polling station on July 20, where government officials were casting advance ballots.

Dressed in a white shalwar kameez and repeatedly wiping his face with a tissue on a severely humid Monday afternoon, Gillani argued that many Kashmiris distinguish between supporting JAAC and participating in the election, even if some committed supporters may stay away from the polls.

“People here are cognisant of the importance of the election and understand that they don’t have to mix their support for JAAC with their responsibility to participate in the polls,” he said. “But there is obviously no denying that there is a chunk of the population which fully believes in JAAC, and they might not cast their votes.”

A 28-year-old PMLN activist, who requested anonymity to speak candidly, said the internet shutdown had forced campaign workers to abandon social media in favour of door-to-door canvassing and loudspeakers mounted on vehicles to publicise the party’s manifesto.

But, he added, the election had also exposed social divisions unlike any he had witnessed before, fuelled by the JAAC-led protests.

“Families are divided over this,” he said. “Those who don’t support JAAC are being called supporters of our oppressors.”

The PPP, which currently governs Pakistan-administered Kashmir, has maintained the more visible street presence.

Its flags dominate Lal Chowk, and a Saturday night corner meeting near the square drew a crowd of supporters waving the party’s arrow banner close to midnight.

But Sardar Mukhtar, a PPP candidate in Muzaffarabad, acknowledged the changed mood.

“The campaign environment is not like previously. I personally feel that turnout might not be as strong as in the past,” he told Al Jazeera from his camp office, where party workers crowded in to catch a glimpse of the candidate.

The party’s clearest position has come from its chairman.

In a letter to JAAC’s negotiating committee last week, Bilawal Bhutto Zardari proposed establishing a Truth and Reconciliation Commission to “examine the present situation, establish the relevant facts, consider the grievances and positions of all sides”.

He also urged JAAC to suspend its protest once such a commission was agreed, while calling on the state to halt further action pending its findings.

Bilawal is scheduled to address a public rally in Muzaffarabad on July 24.

AJK’s Prime Minister Faisal Mumtaz Rathore, the PPP’s most senior leader in the territory, has been more measured on the campaign trail, telling reporters after the revised election schedule was announced only that “the current environment is not suitable for the state” and that “problems can only be solved through dialogue”.

In the region’s legislature, he has previously said that the JAAC bears “the primary responsibility for this situation,” while conceding that “we have not absolved ourselves of responsibility as a government”.

For Ali, the Muzaffarabad-based political analyst, the real issue lies not in the vote itself but in everything that has unfolded over the past few years.

He pointed to commitments the government made to JAAC in October 2024 that remain unfulfilled, including promises to devolve development budgets to local councillors and eliminate the perks and privileges enjoyed by politicians.

“However, people will vote,” he told Al Jazeera. “Because the entire system is designed in a certain way, tying government jobs and small favours to elected representatives. So turnout will be fine, but it will not resolve people’s fury and anger.”

Ershad Mahmud, a Canada-based researcher and author of Poonch: Identity, Politics and Resistance, sees the problem differently, arguing that the election is diminished by who is missing from the ballot.

“PTI did win last time, and they have significant following, as well as JAAC,” he said, referring to Imran Khan-founded Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf, which ruled Pakistan from 2018 to 2022 before being ousted through a vote of no confidence.

PTI also formed the AJK government after the 2021 election, but since 2023 the territory has had four prime ministers.

Mahmud argued that with two major democratic forces absent from the contest, many voters will stay away as well.

“I don’t think this election will be seen as legitimate. The distance from Muzaffarabad to Islamabad is more than 120km [75 miles]. But the mental distance is now 1,000km [621 miles],” he told Al Jazeera.

However, not everyone has reached the same conclusion.

Muhammad Ramzan, a property dealer from Mirpur division in the south of the territory, supported JAAC when its demands centred on subsidised wheat and lower electricity prices.

His support cooled once the movement shifted its focus to abolishing the 12 refugee seats, a campaign he considers disconnected from the concerns of residents and his own livelihood.

“Even I am curious,” he said. “Earlier they asked for roads or electricity or school, but now that they have changed their focus to 12 seats, I am flabbergasted why would they do that.”

He intends to vote, although he expects turnout to fall, with much of the boycott driven by younger voters aged between 18 and 35 who remain committed to JAAC.

“There are a lot of people like me,” he said, describing his gradual drift away from the movement.

But he remains doubtful that the election will resolve the crisis.

“I fear that things will go bad,” he said quietly.

title: State of play: Dems battle for shot at crucial Arizona House seat amid GOP's razor-thin majority
excerpt: RNC senior advisor Danielle Alvarez highlights the growing influence of Democratic Socialists in primaries, citing the Arizona House 4 race where incumbent Greg Stanton faces Kai Newkirk. Alvarez crit...
body_text:
RNC senior advisor Danielle Alvarez highlights the growing influence of Democratic Socialists in primaries, citing the Arizona House 4 race where incumbent Greg Stanton faces Kai Newkirk. Alvarez criticizes Newkirk's platform, including abolishing ICE, and points to the DSA's surging membership as a "disaster" for mainstream Democrats and the nation.

Two Democratic candidates remained locked in a battle as of early Wednesday morning to claim their party’s nomination in Arizona’s 1st Congressional District for a chance to face former NFL kicker Jay Feely.

Former television news anchor Marlene Galán-Woods and former state Rep. Amish Shah, the two frontrunners, fell shy of capturing enough of the vote to win the nomination outright by early Wednesday morning.

The winner of the primary will go on to have a rare opportunity to flip a seat in the Canyon State and, more broadly, potentially decide the balance of power in Washington, D.C. Feely, who received President Donald Trump’s "complete and total endorsement" in the final stretch of the race, won his GOP primary late Tuesday night.

TRUMP-BACKED FIREBRAND WINS ARIZONA GOP GOVERNOR PRIMARY, SETTING UP HIGH-STAKES SHOWDOWN

Republican Rep. David Schweikert has repeatedly fended off well-funded Democratic challengers in Arizona's competitive 1st Congressional District, winning reelection in 2024 (Stefani Reynolds/Bloomberg/Getty Images)

The seat could very well decide whether Republicans hold on to a slim majority in the House of Representatives or whether Democrats break a Republican governing trifecta come the November midterms.

Although the district has favored Republicans since 2020, incumbent Rep. David Schweikert, R-Ariz., has only narrowly hung on to the seat in the past two cycles, beating out Shah in a 51.9% to 48.1% victory in 2024.

Schweikert announced that he would not pursue reelection last year, announcing that he would instead pursue a gubernatorial bid. Trump-backed Rep. Andy Biggs, R-Ariz., won his primary and was ahead of Schweikert by over 50 points early Wednesday.

VULNERABLE REPUBLICAN WINS GOP PRIMARY IN ARIZONA TOSS-UP DISTRICT DEMS AIM TO FLIP BLUE

The 2026 midterms will determine control of the House and Senate, shaping President Donald Trump's legislative agenda during the final two years of his term. (Al Drago/Getty Images)

"I’ve grown to believe Washington is unsavable. I do believe Arizona is savable," Schweikert told Axios.

This isn’t the first time Amish Shah and Marlene Galán-Woods have competed against each other for their party’s nomination, having clashed in the Democratic 2024 primary for the district.

At that time, Shah narrowly defeated Galán-Woods before advancing to the 2024 general election, where he lost to Republican Rep. David Schweikert, who has represented the Phoenix-area seat since 2013 despite Democrats investing heavily to flip the district blue.

HOUSE DEMOCRATS' CAMPAIGN ARM DRAWS PROGRESSIVE FURY FOR TRYING TO 'TIP THE SCALES' IN KEY HOUSE PRIMARY

He is seeking a second consecutive nomination after arguing he remains the strongest candidate to challenge Feely in the general election. Galán-Woods, meanwhile, is expected to emphasize her background as a longtime Arizona television journalist and her ability to appeal to independent and suburban voters.

Former Arizona state Rep. Amish Shah, an emergency room physician who was the Democratic nominee in the district in 2024, is seeking another chance to flip the seat after narrowly losing to Republican David Schweikert in the general election. (Patrick T. Fallon/AFP/Getty Images)

Galán-Woods is making another bid for the nomination with support from several national Democratic organizations, which view Arizona's 1st District as one of the party's best pickup opportunities in 2026.

The winner will go on to the general election on Nov. 3.

Leo Briceno is a politics reporter for the congressional team at Fox News Digital. He was previously a reporter with World Magazine.

title: UK inflation falls by more than expected to 2.6% in lift for Andy Burnham
excerpt:
Annual June figure comes after new PM pledges to reduce cost of living and improve economy

UK inflation dropped by more than expected in June to 2.6%, in a boost for Andy Burnham’s plans to reduce th...
body_text:
Annual June figure comes after new PM pledges to reduce cost of living and improve economy

UK inflation dropped by more than expected in June to 2.6%, in a boost for Andy Burnham’s plans to reduce the cost of living.

The new prime minister has pledged to bring down the cost of living to give households more disposable income and improve the outlook for the economy, announcing a winter VAT holiday on electricity bills and lowering the national cap on bus fares.

Last month’s consumer prices index reading outdid economists’ forecasts of a slide from 2.8% in May to 2.7% , amid a fall in the prices of fuel, particularly diesel, helped by the unstable truce in the Middle East conflict.

The Office for National Statistics said clothing also dropped in price month on month along with the cost of transport and food, offsetting modest price increases in most other goods and services.

Grant Fitzner, the ONS chief economist, said: “Food prices fell this month, driven by products including chocolate, margarine and beef. Clothing prices also fell with the start of summer sales, with bigger discounts than last year.

“The cost of raw materials dipped for the first time since January, mainly due to the lower price of crude oil, while the increase in the costs of goods leaving factories slowed again.”

The new chancellor, John Healey, said the drop in prices growth was “news families want to hear” but warned “there is much more to do to give people the breathing space they need”.

He added: “That is why yesterday we cut VAT on electricity bills and today we’re announcing a £2 cap on bus fares from January. We have chosen to focus on the cost of living in our first week, signalling that concern for working people will be at the heart of everything we do.

“Both these changes are a win-win. They help keep inflation down, while helping people afford the essentials.”

Joe Nellis, the economic adviser at the accountancy firm MHA, said the fall in inflation to 2.6% marked “a welcome piece of good news for the incoming prime minister and his chancellor as they look to set out their policy agenda”.

Nellis, an emeritus professor at Cranfield University, added: “The escalation of tensions in the Middle East in February led to fears of inflation spiralling out of control, as supply chains were disrupted and oil prices surged.

“However, while inflation has remained consistently above the Bank of England’s 2% target, it remains far below expected levels – in its world economic outlook published in April, the IMF predicted inflation to head towards 4% by the end of the year.

“For Andy Burnham, the easing of inflation creates a more stable platform for his fledgling administration. As he looks to support those struggling with the cost of living crisis and encourage job creation, lower inflation rates will help to improve household spending power and boost business confidence, as long as earnings growth continues to outpace price rises.”

Concerns that the Bank of England could increase interest rates later this month are likely to ease. Several members of the central bank’s monetary policy committee have said they are worried about inflation running persistently above their 2% target unless they push up interest rates from 3.75%.

However, the recent escalation of hostilities in the Middle East will pose a problem for Burnham and the central bank after it pushed the price of Brent crude back up above $90 a barrel.

title: Meat multinational faces legal challenge over green credentials of $6bn global expansion plan
excerpt:
Greenpeace petitions Dutch courts to order JBS to disclose how its aims are compatible with responsibilities to climate and human rights

The world’s biggest meat company, JBS, faces a landmark legal ...
body_text:
Greenpeace petitions Dutch courts to order JBS to disclose how its aims are compatible with responsibilities to climate and human rights

The world’s biggest meat company, JBS, faces a landmark legal challenge to disclose how its $6bn (£4.5bn) global expansion plan is compatible with responsibilities to the climate, nature and human rights.

Courts in the Netherlands, where JBS is headquartered, will assess the petition filed by Greenpeace for information on whether the company is compliant with Dutch laws on “duty of care” as well as its own publicly stated commitments.

Lawyers said the case opened a new frontier in the fight against climate change, potentially creating a precedent for legal challenges against the agriculture sector, which is the biggest cause of deforestation and a major source of methane emissions.

“The primary objective is to challenge JBS’s business model and expansion model,” said Richard Brown, legal counsel at Greenpeace International. “We have a suspicion that they’re flying blind and not pulling together the information they need to understand the impacts.”

He said the request for information is a preliminary stage towards filing formal litigation against the multinational meat company.

Until now similar lawsuits have only been filed against fossil fuel companies. Last month, a Paris court ​ruled that the French oil company TotalEnergies must disclose the climate risks linked to emissions from its oil and gas products, and set out plans to ⁠address them in a case brought by NGOs and the city of Paris, but did not order specific measures such as limiting overseas exploration and production.

In the Netherlands, Shell is being challenged in The Hague by environmental groups Milieudefensie (Friends of the Earth Netherlands) and Friends of the Earth to reduce the company’s carbon dioxide output by 45% by 2030 in line with the Paris climate agreement. A final ruling is expected in early 2027.

At the centre of the petition against JBS is a perceived lack of transparency over its massive expansion plans in Nigeria. In the coming years, the meat company plans to invest $2.5bn in six giant meat-processing plants that are seen as a bridgehead to the fast-growing African market.

Civil society groups say there is scant information about whether JBS has assessed the social and environmental consequences of this plan, which would threaten smallholder farmers, domestic food security, land-use priorities, as well as locking in huge quantities of methane emissions. Methane is one of the most climate-destabilising greenhouse gases.

“This is the biggest agriculture investment that I have heard of in Nigeria’s history but there have been few details and no consultation for civil society,” said Elujulo Opeyemi, the spokesperson for Youth in Agroecology and Restoration Network.

“The information is not there. We want the Nigerian government to take a pause until we can make a concrete cost-benefit analysis. This investment will only be worthwhile if it is good for the people and planet. Otherwise it will just create more problems.”

To illustrate the risks, he said the Niger state government has already promised 1.2m hectares to JBS.

JBS, which was founded in Brazil in 1953, moved its headquarters to the Netherlands last year as part of its strategy to list on the New York Stock Exchange. The shift allowed the company to tap international financial markets for expansion funds, and to enable the founding family’s Batista brothers – Joesley and Wesley Batista – to significantly raise their ownership stake.

But the move to Europe also exposed the company to more rigorous transparency rules. “We say that if you come to the Netherlands, you have to play by Dutch rules and be held to account to Dutch standards,” said Brown. “Ultimately this is about a global conglomerate and how its activities should be limited within safe planetary boundaries.”

Behind the lawsuit is dismay at JBS’s environmental track record, particularly in its home country, Brazil, where it has repeatedly made sustainability commitments that it later abandoned or postponed.

Beef production is the primary driver of deforestation, as trees are cleared to raise cattle, and scientists warn this has pushed the Amazon close to a tipping point that would accelerate its shift from a carbon sink into a carbon emitter. JBS, which dominates the Brazilian cattle market, promised to address this with a commitment to clean up its beef supply chain in the region by the end of 2025. But the Guardian and its investigative partners revealed last year that JBS would break its promise. JBS then failed to meet its year-end deadline and has since scrapped its “net zero by 2040” pledge. Its latest sustainability report also excluded the calculations of indirect emissions, primarily methane, from cows – which are a primary component of JBS’s climate impact.

Marieke Vellekoop, the executive director at Greenpeace Netherlands, said that the NGO had been left with “no choice but to seek our right to information through the Dutch courts.”

Opeyemi said Nigeria needed transparency from JBS to ensure the full consequences of its planned investment can be calculated for the people, the environment and the wider region. “Once JBS has a foothold in Nigeria, they can encroach across Africa. That’s why the Nigerian government has to get this right,” he said. “This court case is helpful so we can get clarity.”

In an email to the Guardian, JBS said it “does not comment on potential legal proceedings of which it has not been notified”.

title: How could Britain look under proportional representation? See the results for your area
excerpt:
These visualisations from exclusive polling modelling show how the UK parliament could change under different voting systems

New analysis produced exclusively for the Guardian reveals how different v...
body_text:
These visualisations from exclusive polling modelling show how the UK parliament could change under different voting systems

New analysis produced exclusively for the Guardian reveals how different voting systems would return wildly different seat totals in the UK parliament based on current polling.

While the current first past the post (FPTP) system could leave Reform UK just 30 seats short of a majority based on recent polls, the modelling shows how proportional systems would be likely to give the UK a more finely balanced parliament that better represents the votes people cast.

New research conducted by More in Common suggests that, under proportional systems such as single transferable vote or regional party list, the UK could end up being governed by a progressive alliance of parties.

Incoming prime minister Andy Burnham has recently signalled support for proportional representation, adding his voice to other Labour figures as well as parties such as the Liberal Democrats, the Greens, the SNP and Plaid Cymru. Electoral reform has also enjoyed increased public support in recent years.

Proportional representation (PR) aims to produce a parliament that more closely reflects how people voted, allowing voters to back their preferred party without worrying as much about tactical voting. It often makes it harder for a single party to win a majority and can result in more coalition governments.

Alongside the current first past the post system, More in Common modelled three different PR systems for the Guardian: regional party list, single transferable vote (STV) and additional member system (AMS).

The More in Common research shows that no single party would achieve a majority according to their projected polling, regardless of which system is used.

The current FPTP would give Reform 46% of the seats off just 28% of the vote, in touching distance of a strong coalition if the Conservatives lent their support.

Meanwhile, the proportional systems would not give Reform such a boost: regional party list is estimated to give them 28% of seats off 28% of the vote; AMS would give them 33% of the seats off 28% of the vote; and STV would give them 27% of the seats off 26% of the vote.

Under each of these systems, either a progressive coalition of parties – consisting of a combination of Labour, the Lib Dems, the Greens, the SNP or Plaid Cymru – or a Reform-Conservative coalition would be the only paths to forming a government.

In the north-east of England, Reform is estimated to win 85% of seats under FPTP. However, this number would drop dramatically under proportional systems – to 51% under AMS, 33% under party list and 30% under STV. Each of Labour, the Greens and the Conservatives would be the main beneficiaries – with the Greens going from no seats under FPTP to at least four under the different proportional systems.

The same disparity can be seen in London, with Labour dominating the city under FPTP, with 64% of the seats. They wouldn’t get more than 40% of the seats under any of the other three proportional systems, with Reform and the Greens able to increase their representation in the capital.

Anouschka Rajah, Research Manager at More in Common, said: “Proportional representation would redraw the electoral map – Reform faring far worse in the North-East and East Midlands, the Greens establishing themselves in London, and Labour avoiding a wipeout in Wales.”

Use the search tool below to see how your local representation would change under each system. Due to some of the systems using regional-level vote counts to create a more proportional system, you may have regional rather than constituency-level representatives.

The most recent polling in Britain, as well as recent elections, show an unprecedented fragmentation of the political system. Reform currently leads on an average of 26%, with the Conservatives and Labour tying on 20%. The Greens and the Lib Dems are close behind on 14% and 12% respectively.

This is based on an average of the last 10 polls, and it is unclear how the polls may shift in the coming weeks with the Labour party choosing Andy Burnham as their new leader and prime minister.

With five parties clustered closely together in the polls, the FPTP system means that small shifts in vote share could produce highly volatile and sometimes counterintuitive seat projections, as marginal changes in local contests are amplified into large swings in parliamentary representation.

In the last general election, Keir Starmer’s Labour won 411 of 650 seats with just 34% of the vote.

A report from the Electoral Reform Society modelled the 2024 vote under different voting systems. Only first past the post gave Labour a majority, with all proportional systems giving smaller parties more representation and leading to a hung parliament.

Several recent MRP projections have pointed to Reform winning a majority in parliament with just under a third of the votes. While More in Common’s most recent MRP have Reform falling slightly short of a majority, Nigel Farage’s party would still benefit from the boost the FPTP often gives a leading party.

There have been renewed calls for proportional representation. Incoming prime minister Andy Burnham told the Observer in May 2026: “I am committed to proportional representation. I think it would change the political culture. I don’t see how first past the post and the point-scoring inherent within it lifts Britain out of the doom loop it is in.”

Farage has previously called for electoral reform, calling the current system “outdated” and “not fit for purpose”, but has gone quiet on the subject since Reform’s rise to lead the polls.

Experts say any attempt to introduce proportional representation for Westminster elections would likely face strong pressure for a referendum, given its significance as a constitutional change and the precedent set by the 2011 referendum on the alternative vote system (AV).

That referendum saw voters decisively reject AV, showing the difficulty of winning public support for electoral reform. Experts warn that any future move to proportional representation could face similar challenges, as voters are familiar with first past the post and may be reluctant to replace a system they already understand.

More in Common’s Anouschka Rajah added: “Under proportional voting systems, which might replace [first past the post], the same votes could leave the country with dramatically different parliaments.

“Multi-party politics can lead to disproportional results – in our latest MRP model, a Green seat costs 280,000 votes, while a Reform seat costs 26,000. But proportionality isn’t all that voters want from their electoral system, and addressing this could have major implications for our politics.

“Under a party list system – the most proportional – smaller parties would become serious players, with knock-on effects on the kinds of coalitions which could form a government, and the likelihood of a government having a stable majority. Strikingly, on current polling a party list system puts the left and right blocs neck and neck, whereas first past the post hands the right a 157-seat lead.

“It is hard to predict how voters might adjust their own behaviour under a new system, but what is clear is that whether Burnham decides to change the voting system, and in what way, will shift the arithmetic of power for years to come.”

Darren Hughes, the Chief Executive of the Electoral Reform Society, told the Guardian: “The last general election produced the most unrepresentative Parliament on record in terms of the votes cast matching the seats parties won.

“That is in part because Westminster’s two-party first past the post system cannot cope with the multiparty politics we now have and is throwing up more volatile and distorted election results.

“Simply put, proportional representation is about ensuring Parliament more accurately reflects what voters express at the ballot box by ensuring seats match votes. That is the strongest foundation for our democracy and a vital step to rebuilding trust in our politics.”

Latest polling and seat distribution figures were provided by More In Common (MIC). Alternative electoral system modelling is based on MIC and Guardian assumptions about constituency boundaries.

Party list uses eleven regions – the nine official English regions plus Scotland and Wales – with variable seat totals. AMS uses a combination of the eleven regions and 631 existing first-past-the-post constituencies. STV uses 52 subregional constituencies – composed of the current FPTP constituencies – of variable size depending on aggregate population.

Northern Ireland and the Commons Speaker’s seat of Chorley are excluded from the modelling. This results in 631 total modelled seats for FPTP, STV and party list systems, and in 1,052 seats for AMS.

title: People with secret ‘safe phones’ advised to turn off devices during national test of AusAlert emergency warning
excerpt:
Ten-second ‘siren’ to blare on most phones on Monday as part of national test despite reservations from some states

Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

Get our breaking news email...
body_text:
Ten-second ‘siren’ to blare on most phones on Monday as part of national test despite reservations from some states

Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

An unsilenceable siren will blare out of most phones in Australia on Monday afternoon for 10 seconds, with the federal government confirming a national test of the new AusAlert emergency alert system will go ahead, despite reservations from some state governments.

Domestic violence campaigners and the National Emergency Management Agency have also repeated advice that people who may have secret “safe phones”, kept hidden for personal safety reasons, may want to turn off their devices for up to 24 hours after the test scheduled for 2pm (AEST) on Monday.
