Health authorities also found that the restaurant used ants that "contained up to 55 times more heavy metals than typical edible insects", according to a report on The Chosun Daily.

South Korean authorities consider several criteria in deciding whether insects can be approved as food. These include food safety risks, toxicity, nutritional value, and whether they can be bred and processed hygienically.

In its defence the restaurant owner argued that just 60% of diners had opted to try the ants, while the rest were served alternatives like fermented vinegar and edible flowers.

He added that restaurants in other countries, such as Australia, Denmark and the UK, also use ants as ingredients.

Court documents did not name the restaurant nor its owner. Ten restaurants in South Korea, all located in capital city Seoul, have been awarded two Michelin stars.

The Michelin Guide awards up to three stars to eateries based on ingredient quality, flavour mastery, cooking technique, chef's personality and consistency.

Two stars denote restaurants that do "excellent cooking" and are "worth a detour", according to Michelin's criteria.

The court is set to deliver its verdict on 2 September.

Ants are eaten, sometimes even valued as a delicacy, in several Asian, African and Latin American cultures. Weaver ants and their eggs are used in Thai and Cambodian cooking, while in Colombia, queen ants are as prized as caviar.

South Korea banned dog meat. So what happens to the dogs?

Would you eat insects if they were tastier?

title: Progressive wave crashes in Sun Belt battleground as moderate holds his seat
excerpt: Fox News political analyst Lisa Boothe analyzes the recent Colorado primary election where a Democratic socialist defeated a 15-term incumbent. Boothe emphasizes the rising anti-establishment fervor w...
body_text:
Fox News political analyst Lisa Boothe analyzes the recent Colorado primary election where a Democratic socialist defeated a 15-term incumbent. Boothe emphasizes the rising anti-establishment fervor within the Democratic Party, which is pushing the party further left. She highlights concerns this trend could propel more extreme candidates into the House in the midterm elections this November.

After socialists won a series of Democratic primaries in New York, Colorado and Washington, D.C., the party’s moderate wing has secured what appears to be an increasingly rare victory in Arizona’s 4th Congressional District.

Incumbent Rep. Greg Stanton fended off progressive challenger Kai Newkirk on Tuesday night after months of bitter primary sparring. On the campaign trail, Newkirk accused Stanton of being too pro-business and too pro-Israel — accusations similar to those leveraged by socialists in New York who went on to dethrone establishment Democrats.

While those angles worked in New York, they fell flat in Arizona, allowing Stanton to advance to November’s general election, where he is a strong favorite to win a fifth term in the House of Representatives.

Stanton’s long-running support for Israel was a major flashpoint in the campaign.

BERNIE SANDERS, DSA REVEAL DEMANDS FOR DEM PARTY AFTER SOCIALISTS SWEEP NEW YORK ELECTIONS

PHOENIX, AZ - NOVEMBER 06: Democratic supporters listen to Greg Stanton speak after he won Arizona's 9th congressional district during an election night event at the Renaissance Phoenix Downtown on November 6, 2018 in Phoenix, Arizona. (Photo by Christian Petersen/Getty Images)

"He has refused to do anything to try to hold the extremist Israeli government accountable for its genocide in Gaza, for the war crimes around the region," Newkirk said of Stanton.

The progressive specifically criticized Stanton for voting to send aid to Israel, for opposing the Biden administration’s efforts to restrict arms sales to the country and for voting to sanction the International Criminal Court for pursuing cases against Israeli leaders.

NEW YORK TIMES RAISES EYEBROWS BY REFERRING TO AIPAC AS 'HARD-RIGHT' PRO-ISRAEL GROUP

WASHINGTON, DC - DECEMBER 13: Rep. Greg Stanton, D-Ariz., votes to approve the second article of impeachment as the House Judiciary Committee holds a public hearing to vote on the two articles of impeachment against U.S. President Donald Trump in the Longworth House Office Building on Capitol Hill December 13, 2019 in Washington, DC.  (Patrick Semansky-Pool/Getty Images)

Stanton, who receives support from AIPAC, has called for ceasefires and backed a two-state solution to end the war.

The incumbent congressman’s relationship with business interests was another sore spot for Newkirk.

"It's always been clear to me that he's a corporate Democrat who's never going to fight for the kinds of changes that we need to really shift from oligarchy in our country to a real democracy that puts working-class people first," Newkirk told Axios early in the campaign.

DEM CIVIL WAR HITS PRIMARY DEBATE STAGE IN FIERY BATTLEGROUND SHOWDOWN: 'WHAT ARE YOU HIDING?'

UNITED STATES - JANUARY 8: Former Rep. Gabby Giffords, D-Ariz., center, her husband Sen. Mark Kelly, D-Ariz., second from left, Reps. Greg Stanton, D-Ariz., left, Adelita Grijalva, D-Ariz. and House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries, D-N.Y., pose for a photo in the U.S. Capitol. ((Tom Williams/CQ-Roll Call, Inc via Getty Images))

Stanton is a member of the New Democrat Coalition, a center-left caucus within the House of Representatives that takes relatively pro-business stances when crafting policy. Newkirk, meanwhile, is a member of the Democratic Socialists of America.

Newkirk became a DSA member just days after requesting their endorsement, a local DSA member told the Arizona Republic earlier in July.

Newkirk also went after Stanton for his stances on immigration, specifically his support for the Laken Riley Act.

According to Newkirk, Stanton’s support for the Laken Riley Act "gave Trump a green light for his authoritarian mass deportation campaign and began this erosion of due process."

The law, which was passed in 2025 after 46 House Democrats crossed party lines to support it, requires that federal immigration enforcement detain illegal immigrants who have been charged with serious crimes.

While Stanton voted for the Laken Riley Act and criticized the Biden administration for its handling of the border, he has also called for greater oversight of Immigration and Customs Enforcement and criticized the Trump administration’s immigration agenda.

The final stretch of the election was marred by controversy for Newkirk as a number of local activists and party organizations retracted their endorsements in July after former Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner was accused of sexual assault, ultimately dooming his campaign.

A blog post from 2019, which Newkirk has called defamatory, accused him of "grooming" young female political activists. One of the post’s authors later denounced it, saying that it fell short of journalistic standards.

Despite this, the Young Democrats of Arizona pulled their endorsement of Newkirk on July 9, claiming that they were "recently made aware of some concerning patterns of behavior" from the candidate. A leader within the Democratic Socialists of America's Phoenix chapter also rescinded his endorsement of Newkirk around that time, though declined to comment on the specific reasoning behind the decision.

title: Ex-NFL star backed by Trump wins battleground state primary: 'True America First Conservative'
excerpt: President Donald Trump demonstrates an iron grip over the Republican Party's primary races, securing victories for all his endorsed candidates in the 2026 primary season. Joe Concha notes the GOP is n...
body_text:
President Donald Trump demonstrates an iron grip over the Republican Party's primary races, securing victories for all his endorsed candidates in the 2026 primary season. Joe Concha notes the GOP is now the 'MAGA party.' Former Senator John Cornyn lost his primary to Trump-endorsed Ken Paxton in Texas, further underscoring Trump's enduring power.

Former NFL kicker Jay Feely won the Republican primary for Arizona’s 1st Congressional District on Tuesday, giving the GOP a Trump-backed nominee in a competitive open-seat race that will help determine which party controls the House.

Feely, who received President Donald Trump’s "complete and total endorsement" in the final stretch of the race, defeated former state lawmaker Joseph Chaplik and businessman John Trobough in the GOP contest to replace Rep. David Schweikert, R-Ariz., who is running for governor but lost in his primary Tuesday night.

Arizona’s 1st Congressional District is a Phoenix-area battleground that both parties see as competitive heading into November. The district includes northeastern Maricopa County and large parts of Scottsdale, Paradise Valley and Fountain Hills, according to Arizona PBS’ candidate forum.

ARIZONA GOVERNOR VETOES CHARLIE KIRK MEMORIAL LICENSE PLATE, SPARKING GOP OUTRAGE: 'THIS BILL FALLS SHORT'

Jay Feely, former NFL kicker and Republican Congressional candidate for Arizona, greets voters outside a polling location at Fountain Hills Community Center during a primary election in Fountain Hills, Arizona, US, on Tuesday, July 21, 2026. (Cassidy Araiza/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Trump endorsed Feely in a video message days before the primary, calling him a "true America First conservative" and urging voters to support him in the July 21 election. The National Republican Congressional Committee (NRCC) also placed Feely in its "MAGA Majority" program, which the committee says is aimed at supporting America First candidates in battleground districts.

The GOP primary became increasingly personal in its final weeks when Chaplik accused Feely on X of having "imported Haitians," a remark Feely described as racist and directed at Haitian men he considers family, according to Axios Phoenix.

"This tweet had NOTHING to do with your two family members and everything to do with your history of bringing large numbers of Haitian migrants to NGOs in an attempt to get amnesty here," Chaplik shot back on X. "You have a history of opposing Trump on TPS for Haiti."

MASS SHOOTING ROCKS CROWDED DOWNTOWN TUCSON DISTRICT, LEAVING 10 WOUNDED

President Donald Trump demanded "NO CHANGES" to the funding deal he brokered with Senate Democrats as a cohort of House Republicans push for voter ID laws to be added into the mix. (Bonnie Cash/UPI/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Feely, who has spent years doing nonprofit relief work in Haiti after a devastating 2010 earthquake there, has defended his work as humanitarian, not political, telling Axios Phoenix that he helped two young Haitian men — a translator he worked with and the translator’s brother — come to the U.S. Feely said he considers them members of his family and has also tried to assist several of their relatives in relocating.

Feely will advance to a general election in one of the most closely watched House races in the country, as Republicans try to hold Schweikert’s seat and Democrats look to capitalize on an open race in a district that has remained competitive in recent cycles.

Jay Feely, former NFL kicker and Republican Congressional candidate for Arizona, speaks to members of the media outside a polling location at Fountain Hills Community Center during a primary election in Fountain Hills, Arizona, US, on Tuesday, July 21, 2026. Arizona's primaries will provide an early test of how much President Trump's focus on debunked claims of 2020 election fraud motivates Republicans. (Cassidy Araiza/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

The Democrat candidates for Arizona's 1st Congressional District include former state lawmaker Amish Shah and former television news anchor Marlene Galán-Woods, the two leading Democrats in the race, along with Rick McCartney and Jonathan Treble.

Alec Schemmel is a Politics Reporter for Fox News Digital who is originally from Charlotte, North Carolina but now resides in the Washington D.C.-Baltimore Metro Area.

title: Los Angeles Angels fired advance scout nicknamed 'Baseball Spy' after he was caught filming Rockies
excerpt:
Dan Dakich gives his reaction to Ryan Clark being laid off by ESPN on Monday while he was live on air.

If you're going to torch your baseball career, at least do it spying on a team worth spying on.
...
body_text:
Dan Dakich gives his reaction to Ryan Clark being laid off by ESPN on Monday while he was live on air.

If you're going to torch your baseball career, at least do it spying on a team worth spying on.

As first reported by The Athletic, the Los Angeles Angels fired advance scout Justin Prinstein after he was caught filming the Colorado Rockies' coaching staff from the stands during their June series at Coors Field.

This story somehow gets even funnier once you learn the details.

Prinstein unironically referred to himself as "The Baseball Spy" on social media, and, to reiterate, he threw away his entire professional career spying on the Colorado Rockies.

MLB EFFECTIVELY OUTLAWS USE OF AI ON DUGOUT IPADS DURING GAMES

Los Angeles Angels general manager Perry Minasian stands during batting practice before a game between the Los Angeles Dodgers and the Los Angeles Angels at Dodger Stadium in Los Angeles on Friday, May 16, 2025. (MediaNews Group via Getty Images)

Think about that for a second. This wasn't the Dodgers. It wasn't the Yankees. It's a squad more than 20 games behind the division lead.

It was the Rockies, a franchise so bad that whatever he filmed probably hurt the Angels more than it helped. Imagine burning down your life's work just to gain an insider edge against a team that barely knows how to play fundamental baseball.

According to The Athletic, Prinstein sat in the designated scout section in Denver, pulled out his smartphone, and started recording Rockies coaches in broad daylight.

He wasn't using covert equipment à la Conor Stalions. The "spy" was just holding up a cellphone in the middle of a packed ballpark, making him perhaps the least subtle sleuth in baseball history. Someone in the stadium spotted him, notified MLB, and the league office quickly alerted Anaheim.

Front entrance of Angel Stadium of Anaheim before the game between the Anaheim Angels and the Boston Red Sox on July 17, 2004, in Anaheim, Calif. The Angels won 8-3. (Getty Images)

Since the Houston Astros sign-stealing scandal, MLB strictly bans using personal electronics to record coaches or decode signals.

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

Former GM Perry Minasian, who was fired in late June, acted fast and fired Prinstein on the spot.

Minasian then had to make one of the most embarrassing phone calls of his life, reaching out to Rockies management to apologize for trying to steal secrets from a team that has no secrets worth stealing.

MLB concluded Prinstein acted alone, sparing the Angels any fines or lost draft picks.

An iPad sits in the dugout during an MLB game between the Athletics and the Los Angeles Angels at Angel Stadium in Anaheim, Calif., on May 21, 2026. (Brian Rothmuller/Icon Sportswire via Getty Images)

To top it all off, the stolen intel did not even work.

The awful Rockies still went out and won two out of three games in the series anyway.

Risking your entire career to break MLB rules while spying on Colorado, getting caught immediately, and still losing the series ... the only secret he uncovered was how fast you can get fired.

Send us your thoughts: alejandro.avila@outkick.com / Follow along on X: @alejandroaveela

Alejandro Avila is a longtime writer at OutKick, living in Southern California.

title: Trump ally wins Republican primary for Arizona governor, setting up race against Katie Hobbs
excerpt:
The victory for Andy Biggs, who was a central figure in the effort to overturn Trump’s  2020 election defeat, sets up a high-stakes contest in the battleground state

Arizona voters on Tuesday chose t...
body_text:
The victory for Andy Biggs, who was a central figure in the effort to overturn Trump’s  2020 election defeat, sets up a high-stakes contest in the battleground state

Arizona voters on Tuesday chose their nominees for governor, Congress and other top offices, setting up a fall campaign that will test whether Republicans can reverse some of the Trump-era losses they suffered in a premier battleground state.

Andy Biggs, the hard-right congressman endorsed by Donald Trump, easily won the Republican nomination to challenge the incumbent Democrat, governor Katie Hobbs.

The race is expected to be among the most fiercely contested in the country, with control of the governor’s office carrying outsized national significance in a state that became “ground zero” for election denialism after Trump’s narrow loss there in 2020.Biggs, a former chair of the House freedom caucus who was a central figure in the effort to overturn Trump’s election defeat, trounced fellow congressman David Schweikert, by appealing to the party’s deeply conservative base.

Throughout the campaign, Biggs and Schweikert offered constrasting visions for reclaiming the governorship. Schweikert, who gave up his toss-up congressional seat to run for governor after eight terms, argued that Biggs’ hard-right politics and unswerving embrace of the president had allowed Democrats to make gains in once-ruby-red Arizona.

But Republican voters in Arizona, as they have across the country, followed the president’s lead, even though Trump’s candidates have repeatedly fallen short in the state’s most competitive general election contests.

Businessmen Ken Miceli and Scott Neely were also vying for the nomination. Hobbs, who flipped the seat from red to blue in 2022, ran unopposed.

Arizona has transformed in recent years from a Republican stronghold into a competitive swing state, fueled by rapid population growth, shifting suburban voting patterns around Phoenix and an influx of college-educated voters.

In the decade since Trump’s election in 2016, Democrats have gained control of the top three statewide offices – governor, attorney general and secretary of state – as well as both US Senate seats. Trump narrowly carried Arizona in 2024 after losing the state four years earlier – a defeat he unsuccessfully sought to overturn.

This year, Republicans are contending with unfavorable national political conditions. Historically, the president’s party loses ground in the midterm elections, and Democrats are hopeful they can seize control of the House – and possibly the Senate, riding a wave of discontent over high gas prices and an unpopular Iran war that have dragged down Trump’s approval ratings. Two toss-up House districts in Arizona are key to that effort.

Arizona’s first congressional district, anchored in affluent Scottsdale, has long been a Democratic target as the suburbs shifted away from the Republican party over the past decade. Two years ago, Schweikert won re-election with just under 52% of the vote.

Republicans chose Jay Feely, a former Arizona Cardinals kicker, to defend the seat in November, over Joseph Chaplik, a former state lawmaker, and John Trobough, a technology and telecommunications executive.

The Democratic primary for the seat included former state lawmaker Amish Shah, who received 48% of the vote against Schweikert as the party’s nominee in 2024. He faced former local news anchor Marlene Galán-Woods, who earned the controversial backing of the Democratic congressional campaign committee. She is the widow of former Republican attorney general Grant Woods, who publicly broke with his party over Trump.

In the sixth congressional district, which extends from the suburbs of Tucson to the US-Mexico border, encompassing much of south-eastern Arizona, Democrats hope national headwinds facing the president’s party will dislodge Republican congressman Juan Ciscomani, who is seeking a third term. Democrat JoAnna Mendoza, a Marine Corps veteran, was running unopposed.

Meanwhile, the contest to succeed Biggs in the safely Republican fifth congressional district has drawn unusual attention. The Trump-backed Republican nominee, former Pinal County Sheriff Mark Lamb, spent the closing weeks of the primary campaign confronting allegations of sexual misconduct and abuse of power reported in a multi-part investigation by the Arizona Republic. Prosecutors found no basis for criminal charges, and Trump has re-affirmed his support for Lamb, who has denied wrongdoing.

He will face Democrat Elizabeth Lee, a nurse, in the general election.

Elsewhere, Republicans chose their nominees for two top state positions that Democrats see as critical to pushing back against the Trump administration.

Alex Kolodin, a Republican state representative and attorney who helped lead failed legal efforts to overturn Arizona’s 2020 and 2022 election results, will face Democratic secretary of state Adrian Fontes, in November for the office that will oversee elections in a swing state during the 2028 presidential race. Fontes has cast himself as a bulwark against Trump’s election denialism while overseeing elections in one of the nation’s most closely watched battleground states.

Kolodin defeated Gina Swoboda, a former state party chair who abandoned an earlier bid for Schweikert’s House seat to instead run for secretary of state.

Tuesday’s primary also settled tight contests in legislative races across Arizona, where Republicans hold slim majorities in both chambers of the state capitol.

title: Ex-sheriff backed by Trump storms to victory in race to replace longtime GOP firebrand
excerpt: Sean Hannity exposes the Democratic Party's embrace of radical figures, focusing on Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner, who faces serious allegations of sexual misconduct and proudly displayed a Na...
body_text:
Sean Hannity exposes the Democratic Party's embrace of radical figures, focusing on Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner, who faces serious allegations of sexual misconduct and proudly displayed a Nazi tattoo for nearly two decades.

Mark Lamb, a sheriff with the backing of President Donald Trump, won a GOP primary on Tuesday evening to fill the seat of outgoing Rep. Andy Biggs, R-Ariz., in the Grand Canyon State’s fifth congressional district.

The victory puts Lamb on a trajectory to win the seat in November which, in the past, has heavily leaned Republican.

Lamb has framed his race as part of a larger effort to protect the cultural fabric of the country, championing "God, family freedom."

TRUMP-BACKED NOMINEES DOMINATE PRIMARY CONTESTS AS PRESIDENT TIGHTENS PARTY VICE GRIP

Sheriff of Pinal County Mark Lamb speaks during Republican Senate candidate Jim Lamons town hall event at the Combs Performing Art Center in San Tan Valley, Ariz., on Thursday, Jul. 7, 2022. (Bill Clark/CQ-Roll Call, Inc via Getty Images)

"That’s why I’m running for Congress. We gotta make sure our grandchildren and our great-grandchildren have the same freedoms that we have," Lamb said in a campaign video.

The seat came open when Biggs, R-Ariz., announced he would run for governor last year.

"Arizona has a bright future, but we will need strong leadership to reach its full potential," Biggs said in a press release. "I have been honored to serve Arizona at the state and federal levels and will bring my experience home to my native state to help fulfill its tremendous capacity."

The seat is one of the state’s most reliably Republican — and became more so after the state’s redistricting in 2022. Biggs last won reelection in 2024 in a 60.4% to 39.6% victory over Democratic challenger Katrina Schaffner.

During his time in office, Biggs was one of Trump’s most prominent backers, pushing for aggressive conservative stances on areas like federal spending and border enforcement.

TRUMP-BACKED AIR FORCE VETERAN WINS GOP RUNOFF IN NEWLY-REDISTRICTED HOUSE DISTRICT

President Donald Trump addresses the nation from the East Room of the White House in Washington, D.C., on July 16, 2026. (Saul Loeb/AFP via Getty Images)

For his own part, Lamb has echoed many of Biggs’ priorities.

"The Joe Biden administration came in, and immediately, overnight, we started seeing hundreds of thousands of people — the majority of these men were military-age men from all over the world. Many of them didn’t love America," Lamb said in a campaign video.

"We stood by as our government not only allowed them to come in but rolled out the red carpet for people that broke our law."

Lamb’s campaign came under scrutiny earlier this year when an Arizona investigation found that Lamb allegedly had a track record of sending sexual images to women outside of his marriage.

Janel Lamb, Mark Lamb’s wife, has called the accusations a political stunt.

TRUMP DOESN’T CARE ABOUT THE MIDTERMS? WHY THAT MISSES THE MARK

Rep. Andy Biggs (R-AZ) speaks during a Turning Point USA event at Dream City Church in Phoenix, Arizona, on April 17, 2026 (Evelyn Hockstein/REUTERS)

"Politics can be dark and ugly, but if you know us, you know that’s not how we roll. The only way to dispel darkness is not with more darkness, only light. After all we’ve been through, a massive smear campaign is certainly not going to stop us from doing what we know is right," Janel Lamb said in a post to Facebook.

Having cleared the primary, Lamb will head to the general election on Nov. 3.

Leo Briceno is a politics reporter for the congressional team at Fox News Digital. He was previously a reporter with World Magazine.

title: Trump-backed firebrand wins Arizona GOP governor primary, setting up high-stakes showdown
excerpt: President Donald Trump demonstrates an iron grip over the Republican Party's primary races, securing victories for all his endorsed candidates in the 2026 primary season. Joe Concha notes the GOP is n...
body_text:
President Donald Trump demonstrates an iron grip over the Republican Party's primary races, securing victories for all his endorsed candidates in the 2026 primary season. Joe Concha notes the GOP is now the 'MAGA party.' Former Senator John Cornyn lost his primary to Trump-endorsed Ken Paxton in Texas, further underscoring Trump's enduring power.

President Donald Trump's kingmaker status in Republican primaries was on the line Tuesday in Arizona's GOP gubernatorial nomination showdown – and the president easily passed the test.

Rep. Andy Biggs, a former House Freedom Caucus chair who landed the president's endorsement, captured the GOP nomination for Arizona governor with more than 70% of the vote, the Associated Press announced Tuesday night with just under 50% of the votes counted. Biggs was beating his nearest competitor by more than 50% at that point.

DEMOCRACY ’26: STAY UP TO DATE WITH THE FOX NEWS ELECTION HUB

Rep. Andy Biggs, R-Ariz., speaks during a Turning Point USA event at Dream City Church in Phoenix, Arizona, on April 17, 2026. (Evelyn Hockstein/REUTERS)

Biggs' nearest competitor was Republican Rep. David Schweikert, who had 17% of the vote when AP called the race with just under 50% of the total counted.

Contractor Scott Neely, who was making his second straight run for governor, had just 7% when the race was called, while business owner Ken Miceli had only 5.4%.

Biggs will now face off in the general election with Democratic Gov. Katie Hobbs, a former Arizona Secretary of State who narrowly defeated then-GOP nominee Kari Lake four years ago. Hobbs was unopposed as she ran for re-nomination in the southwestern battleground.

Arizona Gov. Katie Hobbs, a Democrat, is running for re-election in the southwestern battleground in 2026. (Rebecca Noble/Getty Images)

The Republican primary turned combative and expensive.

Schweikert earlier this year went up with campaign mailers accusing Biggs of associating with white supremacist and antisemitic groups. Responding, the Biggs campaign labeled the move as "a desperate tactic by our opponent."

Republican Rep. David Schweikert ran for Arizona governor in 2026 rather than seek re-election. (Stefani Reynolds/Bloomberg/Getty Images)

Biggs, who was also backed by the political wing of the conservative powerhouse Turning Point USA, mostly ignored his rivals and only joined them on the stage at one of the multiple debates.

DEMOCRACY ’26: STAY UP TO DATE WITH THE FOX NEWS ELECTION HUB

Arizona Gov. Katie Hobbs, a Democrat, is running for re-election this year. (Rebecca Noble/Getty Images)

Trump's endorsements in a handful of key races, including in a top swing congressional district, could impact the midterm battles for the House majority and control of governors' offices.

Paul Steinhauser covers the national campaign trail from coast to coast for Fox News

title: Alleged 'mime' stalker stripped to underwear, covered himself in cooking oil: officials
excerpt:
Hernan Villagran Lux, 29, was arrested by Massachusetts State Police on Sunday evening and appeared to faint on the stand in court this week. (Boston 25)

Not all mimes stay silent. One is now accused...
body_text:
Hernan Villagran Lux, 29, was arrested by Massachusetts State Police on Sunday evening and appeared to faint on the stand in court this week. (Boston 25)

Not all mimes stay silent. One is now accused of terrorizing women.

A Massachusetts man known for roaming in a black-and-white mime costume is accused of frightening women for months at a popular state park, where prosecutors say he allegedly stripped to his underwear, slathered himself in cooking oil and worked out near female employees before later collapsing in court as the allegations were read aloud.

Hernan Villagran Lux, 29, of Framingham, is facing criminal harassment charges after three female state Department of Conservation and Recreation (DCR) employees had up to over 50 encounters by one of the women over the past two months at a Natick state park near Boston.

Prosecutors said all three women knew Lux by name and appearance, with one reporting more than 50 encounters that authorities said escalated over time, eventually leaving employees afraid to work around him.

FLORIDA MAN WHO ALLEGEDLY LURED WOMEN WITH DATING APP COULD HAVE MORE VICTIMS: AUTHORITIES

Appearing in court earlier this week, stalker 'mime' Hernan Villagran Lux, 29, fainted on the stand as his charges were read. (Boston 25 News)

The allegations have also sparked warnings on social media, where people across several Massachusetts communities said they recognized the suspect and claimed he had approached or followed women on the nearby rail trail.

Authorities arrested Lux Sunday night on the Cochituate Rail Trail, after multiple women and park workers alleged he repeatedly approached them, stared at them and refused to leave despite repeated requests, according to Boston 25 News.

"The women report an extensive number of contacts with the defendant involving him staring at them, smiling in what they describe as 'a really unnerving and frankly terrifying manner,'" Framingham District Court Supervisor Andrew Mange said during Monday's court hearing.

MAN ACCUSED OF REPEATEDLY TRESPASSING AT CIA HEADQUARTERS IN VIRGINIA

A sign marks the entrance to Cochituate State Park in Natick, Mass., where a man is accused of repeatedly harassing female park employees over several months. (Boston 25 News)

Mange said the encounters became increasingly bold. In one incident, prosecutors allege Lux "stripped down to his underwear, slathered cooking oil all over his body and began working out right by their staff area."

The women also reported that Lux repeatedly refused to leave the park after being asked to do so, according to the station.

Prosecutors also alleged Lux complimented one employee's appearance, commented on her lips and refused to leave as she and coworkers abandoned their work area because they felt unsafe.

Boston 25 reported that numerous people posted on social media saying they had spotted a man matching Lux's distinctive mime-like appearance throughout Framingham, Natick and Wayland.

Several women also claimed a man fitting the same description approached and followed them on the Cochituate Rail Trail.

Hernan Villagran Lux appears in Framingham District Court in Framingham, Mass., on criminal harassment charges stemming from allegations involving female employees at a state park outside Boston. (Boston 25 News)

"I spotted him, he looked kind of odd, but I thought he was just dressed in a costume," neighbor Nancy Dlott told Boston 25. "It's kind of upsetting, especially if you're walking alone," she added.

"I just don't understand what goes through someone's head to do that," Christopher Labella told the station. "I hope this guy gets the help that he needs."

As prosecutors continued reading the allegations in court Monday, Lux fainted and was treated on scene before proceedings resumed, according to Boston 25.

When court resumed later in the afternoon, the judge ordered Lux to undergo a mental health evaluation at Bridgewater State Hospital.

Jasmine Baehr is a breaking news writer for Fox News Digital, where she covers politics, the military, legal debates surrounding life and family policy, as well as faith and culture.

title: Jelly Roll's daughter fiercely denies cheating rumors, days after divorce from Bunnie Xo finalized
excerpt: Country music singer Jelly Roll isn't afraid to tackle the "uncomfortable stuff" in his marriage to TikTok star Bunnie XO up front in order to keep their marriage strong, the "Son of a Sinner" musicia...
body_text:
Country music singer Jelly Roll isn't afraid to tackle the "uncomfortable stuff" in his marriage to TikTok star Bunnie XO up front in order to keep their marriage strong, the "Son of a Sinner" musician shared with Fox News Digital.

Jelly Roll's daughter fiercely defended her family days after his divorce from Bunnie Xo was finalized.

Bailee Ann, 18, staunchly denied infidelity rumors between the former couple during a TikTok livestream on Sunday.

The "Son of a Sinner" singer petitioned Tennessee courts for a divorce on May 18, according to documents obtained by Fox News Digital. A judge finalized the end of their marriage on Friday.

JELLY ROLL, BUNNIE XO HINTED AT DIVORCE WEEKS BEFORE THE NEWS BROKE WITH CRYPTIC LYRICS AND MISSING RINGS

Jelly Roll has dodged rumors of infidelity as the cause of his divorce with Bunnie Xo. (Terry Wyatt/WireImage)

He listed the date of separation as May 9 and cited irreconcilable differences.

"Nobody cheated," Bailee said on the livestream, according to People. "Period. [Get] the f--- out of my comments, my DMs, my live with that s---, I don’t care, I don’t want to talk about it."

She told her followers that they had "no f---ing idea what happened, and wait ‘til y’all find out," before threatening that "the internet in totality is about to f---ing crumble the day that I open my mouth."

BUNNIE XO SAYS SHE FUNDED JELLY ROLL'S EARLY CAREER BY 'TURNING TRICKS' WHILE REFLECTING ON DIVORCE

"It’s not a difference in f---ing opinions," she said. "Nobody else lived in it. So don’t f---ing even. I’m for real so done. Good? Everybody good? We’re on the same page? Cool."

Jelly Roll has two children from previous relationships. (Getty Images)

Bunnie Xo and Jelly Roll (born Jason DeFord) have both fiercely defended their private relationship after news of their split broke the Internet with accusations of cheating.

Terms of their settlement remained confidential. However, People reported the former couple will split their assets, including homes, cars, an aircraft and any intellectual properties.

Additionally, the "Save Me" singer will make a "one-time, confidential lump sum payment to Bunnie, so there will be no alimony claims to be made in court," the outlet reported.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

Jelly Roll filed for divorce from Bunnie Xo in May and cited irreconcilable differences in his petition to end the marriage. (Taylor Hill/Getty Images)

Bunnie – who's full name is Alisa DeFord – recently gave her social media followers a tour of her new Nashville-area home, which included hidden stairways, multiple massive closets, opulent floral rooms, an outdoor shower and infinity pool.

Of the many topics discussed in her since-scrubbed "divorce" podcast episode, Bunnie admitted that her past life as a high-end escort funded their lifestyle when they first dated.

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

"When Jay and I first got together, we were two kids who literally had nothing," Bunnie said of her ex. "Everybody always likes to say that I had all the money when we first got together, and I did this for him and I did that for him. And I mean, I had sugar daddies at the time that were paying for all of this."

Bunnie Xo has since moved on and recently shared her new Nashville digs on social media. (Kevin Mazur / Getty Images for The Recording Academy)

"Like, we have — and I'm not trying to take the, the light off of what I did for him, but like, we were still in the streets. We were making money as it came."

She added, "It wasn't like I was over here just sitting on f---ing a million dollars and was taking care of him. Like, I was turning tricks to pay our bills and to get lawyers and to travel and to, you know, go on tour and stuff like that."

The former couple tied the knot in August 2016 during a whirlwind Las Vegas ceremony after meeting the year prior. She became a stepmom to the country singer’s daughter, Bailee, and son, Noah.

Tracy Wright is an entertainment reporter for Fox News Digital. Send story tips to Tracy.Wright@fox.com.

title: Angels fans' 'tarps off' cheer helps team break 106-game streak against Cardinals
excerpt:
St. Louis Cardinals star Jordan Walker beat hometown hero Kyle Schwarber to win the Home Run Derby and secure a big check. (This video contains AI-assisted content)

One of the most fun celebrations i...
body_text:
St. Louis Cardinals star Jordan Walker beat hometown hero Kyle Schwarber to win the Home Run Derby and secure a big check. (This video contains AI-assisted content)

One of the most fun celebrations in baseball may have just helped the Los Angeles Angels break a 100-plus-game streak Monday night.

For the first time in 106 games, the Angels won a game when trailing in the ninth inning, according to ESPN. And their fans had a cheer to help propel their team to a walk-off victory against the St. Louis Cardinals.

It’s called the "tarps off" cheer, and it’s become a movement this season. Simply put, fans take off their shirts and wave them around in a specific section of the field, and that happened to be right field at Angel Stadium Monday night.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Shirtless fans cheer during the seventh inning of a game between the Texas Rangers and the Los Angeles Angels at Globe Life Field July 9, 2026, in Arlington, Texas. (Getty Images)

Hundreds of fans waved their shirts and cheered for their squad, which was down 2-1 in the top of the eighth inning.

The rabid cheering section continued through the bottom of the ninth when Jo Adell came to the dish with the bases loaded and a tie ballgame with the chance to walk it off.

Perhaps it was the "tarps off" movement that got to the Cardinals’ pitcher, who plunked Adell, allowing Zach Neto to trot to home plate with the winning run.

It’s also something the Cardinals’ fan base made famous this season. Busch Stadium has become a hot bed for some shirts-off celebrating.

Fans in the stands chant "Sell the Team" during an MLB game between the Colorado Rockies and the Los Angeles Angels June 3, 2026, at Angel Stadium in Anaheim, Calif. (Icon Sportswire)

On May 15, the Cardinals were facing the Kansas City Royals when in the stands did the cheer. It was even something Cardinals manager Oliver Marmol said urged him to buy tickets for those fans that wished to participate in it.

However, Angels fans gave those Cardinals faithful a taste of their own medicine, and it’s something they’ve been running with since May.

The movement has also become a form of protest. "Sell the team" has been chanted from those right-field seats on numerous occasions this season. Angels owner Arte Moreno has come under fire since the team has not made the playoffs since 2014.

A group of shirtless fans cheers for the Los Angeles Angels during a game against the Colorado Rockies at Angel Stadium of Anaheim June 1, 2026, in Anaheim, Calif. (Getty Images)

The Angels are once again at the bottom of the barrel in the AL West with a 40-61 record despite the 3-2 win Monday night. They sit 11 games out of the division lead.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

Scott Thompson is a sports writer for Fox News Digital.

title: Sophie Cunningham speaks out on protecting girls from competing against 'biological men'
excerpt: After the Supreme Court dealt a win for the "Save Women's Sports" movement, Riley Gaines and MyKayla Skinner sent a message to Simone Biles after Biles attacked Gaines over the issue on social media l...
body_text:
After the Supreme Court dealt a win for the "Save Women's Sports" movement, Riley Gaines and MyKayla Skinner sent a message to Simone Biles after Biles attacked Gaines over the issue on social media last year.

WNBA star Sophie Cunningham has made her position on protecting women's sports clear.

In an ESPN interview, the fan-favorite Caitlin Clark teammate addressed the issue for the first time.

SUPREME COURT MAKES RULING ON TRANS ATHLETES IN WOMEN'S SPORTS

Indiana Fever superstars Sophie Cunningham and Caitlin Clark are arguably the two most popular players in the WNBA. (NBAE via Getty Images)

"I got a lot of negative feedback about me hating trans. And I'm like, 'I never once said that,'" Cunningham said.

"I think that I am here to extend love. But I also think with that love is truth, being honest. And I want to protect young girls in a locker room, or young girls in sport who shouldn't have to go against biological men.

"I'm very much in the middle. I agree with things on both sides, disagree with things on both sides. And that's all I've ever said about my political beliefs. But people love to assume."

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

Sophie Cunningham announces a round during a lightweight fight at T-Mobile Arena in Las Vegas, Nev., on July 11, 2026. (Chris Unger/Zuffa LLC)

Cunningham previously garnered backlash from the left in March 2022 after re-sharing a tweet from CBS Sports host Josh Pate about the controversy surrounding former Penn transgender swimmer Lia Thomas competing on the women's team.

"Female collegiate athletes deserve so much better than this," Pate’s tweet said.

Cunningham faced so much backlash over the re-share that she released a statement.

"To my teammates, friends, family, supporters and fans, you know that I stand with all people, especially my fellow athletes. I do not judge, but support all races, religions, sexual preferences and gender equity. We all have a place in this world to compete and I hope we are able to grant everyone that opportunity fairly." she wrote.

Cunningham has emerged as one of the biggest stars in the WNBA over the last year, especially among conservatives.

Sophie Cunningham of the Indiana Fever stands for the national anthem before their game against the Golden State Valkyries at Chase Center. (Ezra Shaw/Getty Images)

Riley Gaines, who infamously tied with Thomas at the 2022 NCAA women's swimming championships, told Fox News Digital on the last day of June, after the U.S. Supreme Court ruled to uphold state laws that keep biological males out of women's sports, that she hoped the ruling would be a "clarion call" for athletes like Cunningham to speak out in support of protecting women's sports.

"Let this be a clarion call, not just to Simone, but to every, I think especially elite female athlete, professional female athlete, the likes of Caitlin Clark and Sophie Cunningham, Serena Williams, to link arms," Gaines said.

Jackson Thompson is a sports reporter for Fox News Digital covering critical political and cultural issues in sports, with an investigative lens. Jackson's reporting has been cited in federal government actions related to the enforcement of Title IX, and in legacy media outlets including The New York Times, The Los Angeles Times, The Philadelphia Inquirer, The Associated Press and ESPN.com.

title: IRS in the hot seat over potential cash grab from Spain's World Cup winnings: 'It's a rip-off'
excerpt: House lawmakers react to Spain's $50 million FIFA World Cup prize facing federal taxes in the U.S., with bipartisan questions on whether the IRS should take a cut of the championship payout. (Credit: ...
body_text:
House lawmakers react to Spain's $50 million FIFA World Cup prize facing federal taxes in the U.S., with bipartisan questions on whether the IRS should take a cut of the championship payout. (Credit: Nicholas Ballasy for Fox News Digital)

As reigning 2026 FIFA World Cup champions, Spain won $50 million in prize money, but a large portion of that could be subject to federal taxes in the United States — potentially up to 30%.

"I think it's a rip-off," Rep. Tim Burchett, R-Tenn., told Fox News Digital.

"I'm not a fan of it, but Americans have to do it. American professional athletes do it, so they knew that when they came over here."

SPAIN BEATS ARGENTINA TO WIN 2026 FIFA WORLD CUP BEHIND FERRAN TORRES' EXTRA-TIME GOAL

Congressman Tim Burchett speaks to reporters after a House Republican conference meeting at the U.S. Capitol July 23, 2024, in Washington. (Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images)

Income earned from activities performed in America is generally, in almost all instances, taxable by the IRS. Under tax law in the U.S., certain payments to nonresident foreign athletes are generally subject to a 30% federal withholding unless reduced by a tax treaty or a different exception.

"It's wrong, and that kind of highlights something bigger," Rep. Jonathan Jackson, D-Ill., said about the potential high tax rate on Spain’s prize.

Burchett argued the potential tax burden sends the wrong message as the U.S. prepares to host more major international sporting events, saying the country should be encouraging foreign athletes and visitors to spend money domestically rather than subjecting them to steep tax obligations.

US LIFTS COSTLY VISA BOND REQUIREMENT FOR SOME WORLD CUP TRAVELERS, TRUMP ADMINISTRATION SAYS

French fans celebrate after their team won a 2026 World Cup match against Sweden in the Brooklyn borough of New York City June 30, 2026. (Leonardo Munoz/AFP via Getty Images)

"I'm not a big fan of the IRS," Burchett said. "They made that money over here, I guess, but I don't like all that. We want to encourage these people to come over here and spend their money, and then we take a big chunk of it.

The World Cup’s prize pool totaled $871 million, with $655 million of that total relying on performance in the tournament. All teams that participated in games in the U.S., even if the only payment received was for participation, will have the earnings taxed at some amount.

ON FILING DEADLINE, GOP BLASTS DEMOCRATS FOR OPPOSING TRUMP TAX CUTS, ‘MAKING LIFE MORE EXPENSIVE’

Jackson also criticized the broader U.S. tax code, arguing that corporations should be paying more in taxes rather than workers. He referred to it as a "classic example of what’s wrong with our taxation system."

"They should be paying the taxes as opposed to having tax loopholes," Jackson said. "The people, the laborers that are working, they should not have to pay 30% of their income on taxes."

Rep. Burgess Owens, R-Utah, agreed with his colleagues that the potential 30% tax on the prize is "too much," but he used the opportunity to touch on the importance of hosting the World Cup in America. As a retired NFL player, he shared how the experience drew him to soccer, a sport he never had any interest in prior to the World Cup.

"I have such an appreciation for soccer now," Owens told Fox News Digital. "I think it's going to be a game changer for so many of our kids. And, so, I want to congratulate the president, everyone who made this happen.

"It is what it is here, unfortunately, in our country of taxes."

Hannah is a Production Assistant for the Politics team at Fox News Digital.

title: EXCLUSIVE: Nick Shirley fires back at Minnesota critics: 'Numbers don't lie, and convictions don't lie'
excerpt:
Independent journalist responds to critics of his Minnesota investigation and explains why he stands by his reporting.

Nick Shirley never planned to become one of the nation's best known independent ...
body_text:
Independent journalist responds to critics of his Minnesota investigation and explains why he stands by his reporting.

Nick Shirley never planned to become one of the nation's best known independent journalists, making videos that would dominate the national conversation and be retweeted by vice presidents and CEOs.

Yet, in the wake of his Minnesota fraud investigation, Shirley has exploded onto the national scene as a one-man journalism machine.

Recently, he sat down with Fox News Digital at Freedom Fest in Las Vegas to discuss his unconventional rise, his reporting philosophy and the stories the mainstream media is still ignoring.

NICK SHIRLEY EXPLAINS FRAUD 'ENTREPRENEURSHIP' HE SAYS IS RUNNING RAMPANT IN CA

Independent journalist Nick Shirley speaks during a roundtable discussion in the State Dining Room of the White House in Washington, D.C. (Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images, File)

"Well, when I was younger, I'd make vlog pranks, and then I started to get a little bit older and wanted to see what was happening in the world. And it turned into journalism," Shirley said about his start in reporting. "I didn't know it was journalism at the time. I thought I was just making YouTube videos on more interesting topics.

"And now I'm one of the biggest and most well-known journalists in America — in the world, actually."

Shirley conceded his particular style is hard to define.

"I think that's a good mix, like an … independent YouTube journalist, like it's journalism with a bit of documentary style as well. So, it's kind of different than what anyone has ever done before as far as this new realm of YouTube journalism," he said.

Shirley said he realized he had an explosive story as the Minnesota fraud video "Feeding Our Future" scandal cracked nine-digit views on X.

YOUTUBER TO TESTIFY BEFORE CONGRESS ON MINNESOTA'S MASSIVE $9B FRAUD NETWORK INVESTIGATION

NIck Shirley's brand of independent reporting has caught the eye of Vice President JD Vance, among other luminaries. (Urs Flueeler, Pool Photo via AP, File)

"When I saw it hit 100 million views, I had never seen a video get to 100 million views on X before. And then it became shared by some of the biggest voices in the world as well," said Shirley, who boasts 1.7 million followers on the platform and nearly 2 million subscribers on YouTube.

Among them were Vice President JD Vance, Elon Musk and FBI Director Kash Patel.

Shirley said he believes in zeroing his investigations on primary sources when he is devoting time and resources to new stories.

"I take all the information that's public from the government, and I go straight to the source," he said.

Despite the massive success Shirley has seen, he still comes across stories that do not get the exposure he feels is warranted.

"On a national level, I think the fentanyl crisis in the United States is really bad," Shirley said. "I've done a few videos on that, but people seem to not want to stop the fentanyl crisis from happening."

OWNER OF DAYCARE IN VIRAL NICK SHIRLEY VIDEO CHARGED IN $4.6M FRAUD SCHEME, PROSECUTORS SAY

Unsurprisingly, from time to time, Shirley said his journalistic work takes him to the limits of his comfort zone.

"In the favelas of Brazil, I thought I was going to die. That was a little scary," he said. "In Kenya and Philadelphia as well, that was pretty dangerous."

Shirley cited his Spanish language skills as a motivating factor in his coverage of the Biden administration border crisis, which he witnessed firsthand.

"I had heard about what was happening. I was living in Chile at the time. And I started to see what was happening with Venezuelan migrants coming into Chile," Shirley said. "When I came back to the United States, I started looking into what was going on at the southern border.

"And since I could speak Spanish, I started to reveal what was actually happening. And people didn't know that children were being smuggled. Seventeen-year-old girls were coming over all by themselves. Twelve-year-old girls were all coming over all by themselves. And I kind of started to showcase what was happening from the border all the way to the shelters in New York City."

Nick Shirley upended the news cycle last month with a 42-minute video investigating Minnesota daycare centers that appeared inactive despite receiving millions of dollars in government funding.  (Nick Shirley)

Shirley did a two-year mission with the LDS Church, and credits his faith as a motivation for his work.

"Well, I think our faith invites us to look for the truth and expose evil," he said. "Like, the scriptures, the Bible, everything, it's a bunch of strong men exposing what's happening as well, and I think that's kind of lost."

Shirley said the recent guilty plea of Fahima Egeh Mahamud, the woman tied to Minnesota's massive "Feeding Our Future" scandal and daycare fraud scheme, as evidence of the veracity of his work, as the mainstream media and Democratic establishment have worked hard to discredit his investigations.

"Well, numbers don't lie, and convictions don't lie either," he said. "The largest daycare in Minnesota, government-funded, just pleaded guilty today."

David Unsworth reports on Latin America. You can follow David Unsworth on Twitter @LatinAmerUpdate

title: Perth teenager charged with murdering his mother and attempting to kill sister
excerpt:
Police found woman dead in Bentley home and seriously injured girl on nearby highway

Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcas...
body_text:
Police found woman dead in Bentley home and seriously injured girl on nearby highway

Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

A teenage boy has been charged with murder after he allegedly killed his mother with a knife and stabbed his little sister, who managed to flee to get help.

The 16-year-old boy was charged on Wednesday with one count of murder and one count of attempting to unlawfully kill after police attended a home at Bentley, in Perth’s south-east, on Tuesday morning.

The 35-year-old mother was found dead by officers when they arrived at the family residence.

The 12-year-old girl was also stabbed but managed to flee the house, making it to a nearby highway for help.

Bystanders provided the girl with life-saving first aid and she was taken by ambulance to Perth children’s hospital, where she remains in a critical but stable condition.

Western Australia police acting inspector Jessica Securo said the incident had shocked the community.

“This incident will have a significant impact on the family, friends and loved ones of all those involved,” she said.

“Our thoughts are with them all, particularly the 12-year-old girl.”

The 16-year-old was due to appear before the Perth children’s court on Wednesday.

title: US strikes Iran for ’11th consecutive night’
excerpt:
The United States military has launched an eleventh consecutive night of strikes against Iran, with explosions reported in northwestern Iran’s Tabriz region.

US Central Command (CENTCOM) said that th...
body_text:
The United States military has launched an eleventh consecutive night of strikes against Iran, with explosions reported in northwestern Iran’s Tabriz region.

US Central Command (CENTCOM) said that the strikes began at 7 pm Eastern Time (2300 GMT) on Tuesday.

“The strikes are designed to continue degrading Iran’s ability to threaten commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz,” CENTCOM said.

An attack on Tabriz, if confirmed, would mark a departure from the general trend of the strikes in the latest round of attacks, which have largely focused on southern Iran and the area around the Strait of Hormuz.

Residents of northeastern Tehran have also reported hearing air defence activity, the Fars news agency reported, adding that several explisions had also been heard around Sirik in southern Iran.

Meanwhile, Iran’s Tasnim news agency reported explosions being heard in parts of Bushehr, also in southern Iran.

The US and Iran are competing for control of the vital waterway in the Gulf, with attacks resuming following the breakdown of a June 17 memorandum of understanding agreed between the two sides.

Earlier on Tuesday, the Kuwaiti army said that its air defences were intercepting an Iranian drone attack.

Iran has repeatedly attacked Gulf countries, including Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar, since the resumption of US attacks this month.

title: WWE star adds to her legacy with 'built in the front & stacked in the back' explanation for locker room nudity
excerpt:
WWE stars Tiffany Stratton and Chelsea Green gave a hat tip to Sophie Cunningham on "Friday Night SmackDown." (This video contains AI-assisted content.)

WWE Superstar Natalya Neidhart, aka Nattie, ha...
body_text:
WWE stars Tiffany Stratton and Chelsea Green gave a hat tip to Sophie Cunningham on "Friday Night SmackDown." (This video contains AI-assisted content.)

WWE Superstar Natalya Neidhart, aka Nattie, has, or at least had, a habit of hanging out in the locker room completely naked. According to retired wrestler Brandi Rhodes, she would just stand there with her phone in hand.

She would even give an occasional lesson to a fellow wrestler in the buff. Her lack of clothing and her explanation for not putting any on are now part of her legacy after it was brought up on Rhodes' show "A Shot of Brandi."

LIV MORGAN BROUGHT HER WWE TITLE BELT TO THE ESPYS, AND SOMEHOW ALL ANYONE IS TALKING ABOUT IS HER OUTFIT

WWE wrestler Natalya Neidhart discusses "Total Divas" at Build Studio on Oct. 25, 2017, in New York City. (Getty Images)

"When I was in the locker room with you back, this is so long ago... Back in the day. You were always naked. Are you always naked?" Rhodes said.

"When she was being naked, this is what she would do. I’m telling you, she would do this. She would have her phone, be totally naked... But it was like F-ing with everybody. Just to be like 'my physique is so great. Just look at it.'"

Why did the third-generation member of the Hart wrestling family, the daughter of Jim "The Anvil" Neidhart and the niece of Bret "The Hitman" Hart, hang out in the locker room naked?

Natalya makes her way to the ring during Monday Night RAW at TD Garden in Boston, Mass., on Dec. 23, 2024. (WWE via Getty Images)

Because why not? Nattie explained, "Listen, I think it was because, why I probably was naked in the locker room, is because if you’re built in the front and stacked in the back, you may as well just let it all hang out."

WWE STAR LIV MORGAN OPENS UP ABOUT HER LEGACY, WHAT SHE WANTS TO BE REMEMBERED FOR

That's as good of a reason as you're going to find. It's a tip of the cap to a time in wrestling when a lack of clothing was sometimes used to tell a story. To connect to the audience. To give that sense of authenticity.

Nattie let it all hang out so often that she forgot that she was naked while going over a match with another wrestler.

Natalya is ringside during Monday Night Raw at The SSE Arena Belfast on Jan. 19, 2026, in Belfast, Northern Ireland. (WWE via Getty Images)

"Poor Eve Torres. I was going over a match with her once, and I was trying to correct her on some forearms," she said.

OUTKICK IS NOW ON THE FOX APP: CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD

"I was like, 'You've got to throw your hips into it. Every kind of sport, it’s all coming from the [hips]. 'And Eve’s like, 'Nattie, put something on.'"

That's solid advice and an even better overall story. Nattie could have danced around the naked locker room story and downplayed it. She chose to lean into it, and that's what the legends do.

title: Mamdani calls out Democratic Party as 'well-paid consultants and big money interests'
excerpt:
New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani told Rolling Stone the Democratic Party is now effectively run by ‘well-paid consultants and big money interests.’

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani claimed Tuesda...
body_text:
New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani told Rolling Stone the Democratic Party is now effectively run by ‘well-paid consultants and big money interests.’

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani claimed Tuesday to Rolling Stone magazine that the Democratic Party has become "run by well-paid consultants and big money interests."

During an interview, Mamdani was asked about a comment from former Democratic National Committee (DNC) Chair Jaime Harrison after candidates backed by the Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) defeated incumbent politicians in a primary election last month despite previously attacking the party.

"I say this with no ill will or animosity: if you hate the Democratic Party, then please don’t run for our nomination. Don’t use our resources. Don’t rely on our volunteers. Don’t use our infrastructure. Don’t ask Democrats to invest their time, money, and energy in your campaign. Focus on building the party you actually support," Harrison wrote on X.

"Political parties aren’t perfect, but they’re built by millions of people who knock doors, make calls, organize meetings, and fight for the values they believe in. If you don’t believe in the party, then don’t ask its members to carry you across the finish line."

FLASHBACK: MAMDANI'S HISTORY OF DODGING QUESTIONS ABOUT WHERE HE STANDS ON KEY ISSUES

Former DNC chair Jaime Harrison criticized the focus on socialist candidates who "hate the Democratic Party." (Spencer Platt/Getty Images)

In response, Mamdani said, "I think usually if someone says this is not of any ill will or animosity, what they're about to say is characterized by ill will and animosity."

He emphasized that the Democratic Party needs to be beholden to the voters who have voiced their support for socialist candidates.

"You say that that is not representative of the party. It may not be representative of what has effectively become our party, which is something that's run by well-paid consultants and big money interests, but it is representative of what Democrats themselves want to see," Mamdani said.

ZOHRAN MAMDANI ADMITS HE WANTS TO END HIS ‘ONE-SIDED BEEF’ ABOUT TAXES WITH RAPPER 50 CENT

"And it's representative of the fact that, in this country, there is just one majority and that's of the working class. And that's not just true in this city. It's not just true in another city. It's true across the entirety of our nation. And people want to see a party that understands that and fights for that."

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani pushed back against critics of democratic socialist candidates. (Yuki Iwamura/AP Photo)

Fox News Digital reached out to the DNC for comment.

Mamdani previously criticized the Democratic Party for having "lost its focus on working people" during an interview last month and pushed for more efforts to be done to combat high prices.

FORMER CLINTON OFFICIAL WARNS 'RADICAL' SOCIALIST TAKEOVER WILL 'DESTROY' THE DEMOCRATIC PARTY IF UNCHECKED

"You know, you look at the four freedoms. You look at the real core of the New Deal. There was a moment when this party was unabashed about its focus on working people. And I'm excited to bring it back there," Mamdani said.

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani has criticized the Democratic Party for failing to appeal to the "working class." (Getty Images)

However, over the weekend, Mamdani struggled to offer a clear definition of who qualifies as the "working class." He described the working class as "those who are working to try and afford the basic dignities of life and aren’t able to do so" and drew a line at "about a million dollars a year."

Lindsay Kornick is an associate editor for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to lindsay.kornick@fox.com and on Twitter: @lmkornick.

title: California toddler bitten by rattlesnake hiding beneath Amazon package amid active snake season
excerpt: Jesse Watters reports on a significant increase in snake bites across the United States this summer, noting that California has already exceeded its yearly quota for fatal bites. Watters details the s...
body_text:
Jesse Watters reports on a significant increase in snake bites across the United States this summer, noting that California has already exceeded its yearly quota for fatal bites. Watters details the severe injuries sustained by Chris, who required 36 vials of antivenom after a Western rattlesnake bite, and a 2-year-old girl in Colorado who was airlifted to the hospital following a rattlesnake attack.

A Southern California family is urging people to stay alert after their 3-year-old daughter was bitten by a rattlesnake hiding beneath an Amazon package outside their home, the latest in a string of snakebite incidents during an unusually active season across the state.

The frightening encounter comes weeks after a hiker was airlifted from a remote Ventura County trail following a rattlesnake bite.

Earlier this year, Ventura County firefighters told Fox News Digital they had responded to seven rattlesnake bites in roughly a month, and officials attributed the increase to warmer weather that has driven snakes out earlier than usual.

Manuel Barajas said his daughter, Addy, ran outside after her mother told her an Amazon package had been delivered and tossed over the family's front gate. As the 3-year-old reached for the padded envelope, a Southern Pacific rattlesnake concealed beneath it bit her on the left hand.

TOURIST DIES AT LUXURY RESORT AFTER COBRA FROM SNAKE SHOW CLIMBS UP PANTS, BITES HIM: POLICE

A Southern Pacific rattlesnake is held by a wrangler in Landers, Calif. A 3-year-old California girl was recently bitten by the species after it hid beneath an Amazon package outside her home. (Gina Ferazzi/Los Angeles Times via Getty Images, File)

Her mother rushed Addy to a nearby grocery store parking lot, where paramedics met the family before transporting her to a hospital. She was later transferred to a pediatric intensive care unit, where she spent two days receiving additional doses of antivenom.

Wildlife experts say an unusually warm spring has fueled increased rattlesnake activity across California this year, contributing to a rise in snakebite incidents and three reported snakebite deaths statewide.

The latest incident follows several other serious encounters this year. In May, a woman hiking in Ventura County was airlifted to a hospital after being bitten by a rattlesnake on a remote trail. A Thousand Oaks woman also died in March after being bitten while hiking at Wildwood Regional Park.

TICK BITE ER VISITS HIT HIGHEST SEASONAL LEVEL IN YEARS AS DOCTORS WARN OF DISEASE SURGE

The snake was underneath a padded Amazon package when it lunged at the 3-year-old child. (Lindsey Nicholson/UCG/Universal Images Group via Getty Images, File)

Health officials say anyone bitten by a rattlesnake should seek emergency medical care immediately, remain as still as possible and avoid attempting home remedies that can worsen the injury.

Barajas told Nexstar's KTLA he hopes sharing his family's experience will encourage others to stay vigilant around their homes and know which hospitals are equipped to treat pediatric snakebite emergencies.

Experts say California has seen increased rattlesnake activity this year. (Smith Collection/Gado/Getty Images, File)

"She’s doing great now. She’s, I want to say, about 70% recovered," Barajas said. "She’s still bruised on her left hand where she was bitten. Her spirits are high, her demeanor is great, but she keeps asking why she was bitten by a snake."

A GoFundMe campaign has been launched to help the family cover Addy's medical expenses and lost income while she continues to recover.

Brittany Miller is a Fox News Digital breaking news writer and former Independent reporter who graduated from Syracuse University in 2023.

title: Mistrial declared in case over Sacramento’s deadliest shooting
excerpt:
Mtula Payton and Dandrae Martin were tried together over shooting on K Street in April 2022 that left six dead

The trial of two men accused of carrying out the deadliest shooting in the city of Sacra...
body_text:
Mtula Payton and Dandrae Martin were tried together over shooting on K Street in April 2022 that left six dead

The trial of two men accused of carrying out the deadliest shooting in the city of Sacramento, California, ended in a mistrial on Tuesday.

The suspects, Mtula Payton and Dandrae Martin, were tried together and faced up to 15 years to life in prison for their role in the shooting, which killed six people and injured at least a dozen. While they were not convicted for the deaths, they were found guilty of being in possession of firearms despite having prior felonies.

The jury could not come to a consensus on the murder charges. Now it will be up to Sacramento county’s prosecutors to decide whether they will pursue a retrial for the murder charges.

The trial, which lasted more than two months, included dozens of hours of footage of the shooting and testimony from responding officers, witnesses of the shooting, and the two men prosecutors allege planned the gun battle against rival gang members.

Taking place just blocks from the state’s capitol, the 2022 shooting rocked Sacramento. And it was one of the few events of its kind that was the subject of a major trial. Gang-related shootings rarely command sustained attention, said Leia Schenk, a victims’ advocate in Sacramento, who is supporting three of families who lost loved ones. But the shooting’s lethality, and its high-profile location, meant it attracted media from all over the world, she said.

In the early morning hours of 3 April 2022, the sound of rapid gunshots from multiple directions broke through the rumblings of hundreds of people pouring out of bars and clubs that line K Street in Sacramento. Milling toward parked cars and ride-shares quickly turned into sprinting and ducking as people ran for their lives and tried to shield themselves from bullets.

By the end of the chaotic scene, six people were dead. Three were women who were bystanders: Johntaya Alexander, 21; Melinda Davis, 57; Yamile Martinez-Andrade, 21. The others were three men who were later alleged to have been a part of the conflict that led to the melee: Sergio Harris, 38; Joshua Hoye-Lucchesi, 32; and DeVazia Turner, 29.

Martin and his brother Smiley Martin were arrested days after the shooting, and Payton was found by police in Las Vegas and arrested in late May 2022. All three were charged with murder. In 2024, Smiley Martin was found dead in his cell at the Sacramento county main jail from a methadone overdose, a coroner later determined.

As the investigation continued, investigators argued that the shooting was the result of a conflict between rival groups that climaxed with the shooting. Prosecutors argued that Martin and Payton went to K Street to kill their rivals, while the defense argued that the pair fired shots in self-defense and out of fear from the unexpected bursts of gunfire. And while the pair were both armed that night, their defense argued that neither planned to shoot anyone nor were looking to settle any gang conflict.

Initially, the pair were charged with first-degree murder. But prosecutors later downgraded the charges to second-degree murder after the jurors found themselves deadlocked, the Sacramento Bee reported.

During the trial, Payton testified about his life as a member of a local gang, which he joined when he was 11, and claimed that he was afraid for his life at the time of the shooting. “Everybody was running in the same direction as me. People were getting shot around me. I was scared,” Payton told his attorney during his testimony.

Martin claimed that he was never affiliated with a gang, but that his deceased brother was.

Since opening arguments in the trial began in late April, there have been scores of witnesses testifying to what they saw the night of the shooting and tense moments in and outside of the courtroom as the families of the victims showed surveillance footage of people firing guns while bystanders ran for cover.

The jury also took a two-week break in early July, adding to the protracted nature of this trial , which made the process of grieving that much harder for the families of the deceased, Schenk said.

“Ultimately, justice would be to have their loved ones back. But there’s no such thing as justice in a case like this,” Schenk said in a post-verdict press conference outside the courthouse. “Now we’re starting all over again. So the families can’t begin to heal because every time they come here it’s ripping the Band-Aid off.”

If a new trial were to begin, Schenk added that she hoped to see the district attorney’s office offer compensation for the time the victims’ families had to take from work and money spent on gas as they commuted to and from court.

title: Bay Area city manager charged for allegedly breaking into 66ft yacht while drinking
excerpt:
Sausalito city manager Elaine Forbes faces misdemeanor charges for allegedly trying to steal the three-story vessel

A Bay Area city manager will face misdemeanor charges for allegedly breaking into a...
body_text:
Sausalito city manager Elaine Forbes faces misdemeanor charges for allegedly trying to steal the three-story vessel

A Bay Area city manager will face misdemeanor charges for allegedly breaking into a 66ft yacht while drinking and trying to steal the vessel.

Elaine Forbes, Sausalito’s city manager, was arrested on the morning of 18 July after police received a call for a noise disturbance. Police say Forbes had “apparently broken into” a three-story yacht, where she was consuming alcohol.

Forbes, who previously served as director of the Port of San Francisco, appeared to make a ruckus on the yacht. The boat’s owner, Andy Cunningham, told ABC7 that she found a bottle of maraschino cherries dumped into a shoebox and four empty bottles of wine.

“She had the horn going, the windshield wipers going, the spotlight going,” Capt Bill Aubrey, who called 911 on Forbes, told ABC7. Forbes had been living on a houseboat near the marina where she was arrested, ABC7 reported.

Police initially charged Forbes with felony burglary, and her bail was set at $50,000. But prosecutors have since lessened those to misdemeanor charges for loitering, squatting and petty theft, according to Sgt Adam Schermerhorn, a spokesperson for the Marin county sheriff’s department.

Yacht theft is not common in Marin county, he said.

The Sausalito city council had placed Forbes on leave last week during a closed meeting, according to NBC News. The city council is evaluating her job performance at a meeting today. She was appointed to her position three weeks ago.

“As the facts become clearer and Elaine obtains legal and healthcare counsel in the coming hours and days, we hope people will respond with compassion, patience and respect for her and her family during an extraordinarily difficult time,” spokesperson Sam Singer said in a widely circulated statement issued on behalf of the Forbes family.

title: Elite universities face federal cash squeeze over millions tied to China-linked entities
excerpt:
Gatestone Institute senior fellow Gordon Chang emphasizes the Chinese influence threat posed by universities across the United States on ‘The Ingraham Angle.’

Elite American universities have accepte...
body_text:
Gatestone Institute senior fellow Gordon Chang emphasizes the Chinese influence threat posed by universities across the United States on ‘The Ingraham Angle.’

Elite American universities have accepted millions of dollars from foreign sources on federal watchlists, including some with ties to the Chinese military, according to government data.

State and Education Department officials said dozens of universities reported receiving $27.6 million from foreign entities on at least one federal watchlist during the second half of 2025, according to CBS News. The universities include Northwestern University; the University of California, Irvine; the University of Connecticut; Bryant University and the University of California, Berkeley.

"American universities have received nearly $405 million from counterparties of concern — entities that pose a very real risk to our national security and that of our allies," an Education Department spokeswoman told Fox News Digital.

"While previous administrations often looked the other way or kept this data hidden, that policy changes now. Through our updated Section 117 Dashboard, the Trump Administration is bringing this cash out of the shadows so every student, family and taxpayer knows exactly who is signing the checks, while equipping the State Department to use that data to protect our nation."

TRUMP JOLTS IMMIGRATION HAWKS WITH SURPRISING DEFENSE OF CHINESE STUDENTS IN USA

According to syllabuses obtained by Campus Reform, UC Berkeley has been offering courses on immigration law that promote the abolish ICE movement. (Smith Collection/Gado/Getty)

If these institutions, among them some of America’s top research universities, continue their relationships with these entities, they could see their access to federal grant funding restricted, according to a letter Under Secretary of State for Public Diplomacy Sarah B. Rogers sent them last week.

"In light of the above, I urge you, as board members, to ensure heightened diligence on this matter which has important national security implications for our country," the letter, obtained by CBS News, reads.

CBS News was the first to report on the payments to universities from watchlisted foreign entities.

The Aero Engine Corporation of China, which is on the Treasury Department’s Foreign Assets Control List due to its relationship with the Chinese military, paid $7.8 million spread across Northwestern University, UC Irvine and the University of Connecticut over the past several years.

INSIDE CHINA’S AI ‘WOLF PACK’ DRONES BUILT WITH TAIWAN CONFLICT IN MIND

A member of the People's Liberation Army assists a child looking through the scope of a rifle during an open day at the Ngong Suen Chau Barracks in Hong Kong, China, July 1, 2015. (Justin Chin/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Documents filed with the Department of Education do not disclose how the funding was used.

Previously, the records did not even show the specific origin of the funds, simply their country of origin. President Donald Trump issued an executive order in April 2025 directing the Department of Education to collect more information from universities about the sources of their foreign funding.

In addition to appearing on the Treasury Department’s Foreign Assets Control List, the Aero Engine Corporation of China is also considered a "military end-user entity" by the Department of Commerce. The company develops engines for fighter jets and attack helicopters used by China’s People’s Liberation Army.

UC BERKELEY SUED FOR ALLEGEDLY STONEWALLING PROBE INTO VIOLENT AMBUSH AT TPUSA ROB SCHNEIDER EVENT

Quadrangle bordered by fraternities on the campus of Northwestern University in Evanston, Ill. (Chicago Tribune via Getty Images)
