Wolff said: “No one should have to have courage to simply place a symbol of their faith on the doorpost of their very own home.”

title: Australia news live: Pauline Hanson says Trump had ‘a great way’ of dealing with migrants; Nine newspapers announces redundancies
excerpt:
One Nation leader says Trump’s methods dealt with people ‘using all these ploys to stay in the country’. Follow today’s news live

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Pauline ...
body_text:
One Nation leader says Trump’s methods dealt with people ‘using all these ploys to stay in the country’. Follow today’s news live

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Pauline Hanson says she thinks Donald Trump’s approach to migration was “a great way” of dealing with what she sees as people “using all these ploys to stay in the country”.

Hanson was speaking at a Perth event this morning. Over the course of an hour or so, she reiterated many of her claims about migration and other matters, such as her relationship with Gina Rinehart, which Guardian readers will be familiar with from our previous reporting.

On migration, Hanson said if she came to power she’d “work with the departments” who might be able to identify people overstaying their visas.

double quotation markI tell you what I do like. Trump did this – Trump said OK, all those in the country illegally, I’ll actually give you a one-way ticket out of the country. And he said, then you can apply to come back in some time later. But if you don’t go, he said, and we find you, you’ll never get back into the country again … I think that’s a great way of doing it.

Interfaith relations following Bondi attack have broken down, royal commission hears

The chief rabbi of Sydney’s Central Synagogue, Levi Wolff, has told the royal commission on antisemitism and social cohesion that interfaith relations renewed following the Bondi attack have broken down.

He said that previously good relations had “chilled” following October 2023. He said while Jewish community leaders had had a constructive meeting with leaders from the Islamic community following the December attack, relations had returned to their previous state following the visit by the president, Isaac Herzog, in February:

double quotation markRelationships have broken down since. We’ve always tried to reengage that, and we hope that we can in the future.

The royal commissioner, Virginia Bell, has asked Wolff, who was born in the US but came to Sydney in 2001, if conversations with his family in New York show “that even if there’s been a spike in antisemitism there, there hasn’t been the same degree of distress for members of the community?”

double quotation markYes, there seems to be something which stands out about what’s happening here almost more than anywhere else. It could also be to the fact that it is of such a shock. You know, Australia was just, it’s always looked at as this paradise so far away.

Wolff noted that Australia had become home to the highest number per capita of Holocaust survivors outside Israel.

He ended his submission by calling for “three pillars” to respond to antisemitism: “security, legislation and education”. He said education about antisemitism should go beyond the Holocaust, saying it “should encompass how Jews live, not only how Jews died”.

Queensland government to spend similar on CFMEU inquiry as CCC

The Queensland government will spend more on the investigation of a single union as the annual budget for the state’s Crime and Corruption Commission.

The attorney general, Deb Frecklington, and the treasurer, David Janetzki, announced that the state would budget $95m for the state’s CFMEU inquiry, an additional $75m than first budgeted.

The Crime and Corruption Commission is responsible for investigating complaints against all state government bodies including the police; administers the state’s witness protection program; recovers the proceeds of crime; and combats major crime. It has an $82.1m budget this year.

The commission investigates fewer than 1% of complaints referred to it, according to its reporting to parliament. It charged a single person with a corruption offence last year.

Asked whether the lack of investigation was related to budgetary constraints, Frecklington said:

double quotation markI don’t know whether that’s true. I think I’d like to see your evidence for that one.

Frecklington compared the CFMEU inquiry – Queensland’s version of a royal commission – to the Fitzgerald inquiry, which caused the early resignation and unsuccessful prosecution of the state’s longest-serving premier, four ministers and a police commissioner to be jailed for corruption, led to reforms to the state’s voting system, the creation of the an independent anti-corruption commission and is the subject of several books and films.

The inquiry was initially scheduled to run until October but was extended by another 18 months in May. It will now run about six months longer than Fitzgerald’s inquiry.

Pauline Hanson says she thinks Donald Trump’s approach to migration was “a great way” of dealing with what she sees as people “using all these ploys to stay in the country”.

Hanson was speaking at a Perth event this morning. Over the course of an hour or so, she reiterated many of her claims about migration and other matters, such as her relationship with Gina Rinehart, which Guardian readers will be familiar with from our previous reporting.

On migration, Hanson said if she came to power she’d “work with the departments” who might be able to identify people overstaying their visas.

double quotation markI tell you what I do like. Trump did this – Trump said OK, all those in the country illegally, I’ll actually give you a one-way ticket out of the country. And he said, then you can apply to come back in some time later. But if you don’t go, he said, and we find you, you’ll never get back into the country again … I think that’s a great way of doing it.

KPMG has made a lifelong staff member and top executive its new Australian chief executive, rejecting external candidates, as it grapples with its audit leaks scandal.

John Sams will take over after Andrew Yates resigned as CEO and took accountability for the firm’s failure to address staff breaches of client confidentiality.

Sams started his career as a graduate at KPMG UK in 2003, moving to Australia in 2006. He has been a partner at the firm for over a decade, was elevated to chief financial officer in October 2025 and replaced KPMG’s chief operating officer, Eileen Hoggett, after she resigned in June for her role in the leaks.

KPMG Australia said candidates from KPMG Australia, the firm’s international branches and outside the firm were considered for the position, announcing Sams’ appointment on Tuesday.

The firm earlier this month replaced its other headline resignation, its chair, with an internal candidate: independent director Michael Ebeid. Ebeid sat on the firm’s CEO selection panel and cheered Sams’ appointment on Tuesday. He said:

double quotation markJohn has a clear mandate from the Board: to strengthen leadership and culture, improve confidence with our people, clients, regulators, government and the Parliament, and focus KPMG on the areas where it can make the greatest contribution.

Sams said he was ready to take “tough decisions” to put KPMG “on the right path”. He said:

double quotation markThe firm fell short of the standards rightly expected of us, and the accountability for these failures will continue to be implemented … Despite everything we have faced so far, I am optimistic about the future. Otherwise I would never have taken on this role. I will lead the changes that will make us better, whatever that takes.

The chief rabbi of the Central Synagogue in Sydney, Levi Wolff, has told the royal commission he receives “almost daily” threats and abuse after his public profile was raised after the Bondi terror attack.

Wolff, whose synagogue is about a kilometre from Bondi beach, has told the inquiry that Australian Jews “feel that they’re being asked to justify their place in a country to which they have contributed loyally and peacefully and constructively for generations.”

He has described an incident after an attack in which a member of the community had anonymously posted him a mezuzah, a box with a scroll with Hebrew verses, normally affixed to the entrance of Jewish homes, “with a note with it that said: ‘I am afraid to have this’”:

double quotation mark‘I’m afraid to have this on my door. Please pass it on to somebody who is brave enough and who needs it.’ That note has stayed with me … No one should have to have courage to simply place a symbol of their faith on the doorpost of their very own home.

Wolff said that after the Bondi attack, “my public profile has increased significantly and, with that, the hatred that has been directed towards me has also increased significantly”:

double quotation markAlmost daily, I receive messages of abuse and threats of violence, and initially those messages actually shocked me. But what shocks me now is something else. I have become accustomed to it. I have become accustomed to threats of violence. That should disturb every Australian.

Staff numbers at the Sydney Morning Herald and the Age will be cut by 30 in another round of redundancies announced by Nine’s publishing arm today.

The managing director of publishing, Tory Maguire, called staff to a town hall meeting at 10.30am on Tuesday to unveil what she said was structural change in a digital-first era.

Maguire said in an email seen by Guardian Australia:

double quotation markThe best way to protect our quality journalism is to ensure our organisation adapts to a changing media landscape, that we’re growing commercially and investing in new areas which connect with new audiences.

In particular we need to keep shifting towards the digital-first future.

Earlier this year Nine made a number of roles in its broadcasting division redundant. This new round of redundancies will not affect the Australian Financial Review.

Marco Rubio expected to join Penny Wong at Asean conference

The US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, was scheduled to land in Manila on Tuesday morning for the Asean conference, joining the Australian foreign affairs minister, Penny Wong, and their international counterparts, as the rekindled war with Iran is placing traditional American allies under economic strain.

To give some international context to the talks, the import-dependent Philippines declared a national energy emergency in March and has been forced to cast an ever-wider net for fuel, including purchases of oil from Russia, whose foreign affairs minister, Sergei Lavrov, will arrive on Tuesday as well.

Before departing from Washington on Monday, Rubio said he was open to the possibility of sideline meetings with both Lavrov and China’s top diplomat, Wang Yi, who Beijing only officially confirmed would attend on Monday.

A meeting with Wang would come days after the US president, Donald Trump, accused Beijing of meddling in US elections.

The week is also expected to see renewed efforts towards regional energy security, including the ongoing push for ratification of the bloc’s Framework Agreement on Petroleum Security fuel-sharing scheme.

In May, members signed off on a push for an Asean power grid, aimed at connecting national electricity networks to enable cross-border power trading.

Rubio will meet the Philippines president, Ferdinand Marcos Jr, as part of a strengthened US partnership with the Philippines, with the disputed South China Sea certain to be on the agenda. Chinese and Philippine ships regularly clash in the crucial waterway, which Beijing claims nearly in its entirety, despite an international ruling that its assertion has no legal basis.

The Philippines has previously said it hoped to conclude a code of conduct between Asean and China before ending its year as the bloc’s rotating chair but analysts have suggested any deal Beijing would be willing to sign would be toothless.

A Melbourne synagogue was unsuccessful in applications for government grants for security before a December 2024 arson attack that has seen its doors closed ever since.

The royal commission’s fifth block of hearings, which are being held in Melbourne this week, is examining security arrangements for the Jewish community.

Yesterday, counsel assisting, Richard Lancaster SC, said evidence would show “a substantial and ongoing threat” to the security of the Jewish community, leading to a “shrinking of Jewish communal life”.

Today’s first witness is Benjamin Klein, a board member of the Adass Israel synagogue, the subject of an arson attack in December 2024. Asked about applications for grant funding for security in 2023 and 2024 before the attack, he said:

double quotation markThere were letters sent out to both government, local members, to different parts of the Jewish of the community seeking grants. It was local council, there was state government, there were federal grants, which we saw were available.

We’d sent out letters and were unsuccessful. We also sent out a letter to Mark Dreyfus at the time, who was the attorney general, and we weren’t successful. There were technicalities in terms of incorporated entities, unincorporated entities. There was a lot of red tape to sort of get through and we weren’t successful.

The commission has heard that the synagogue’s board has since had to employ someone to oversee funding applications for security grants. After the attack, the federal government has pledged up to $30m to rebuild the synagogue.

As we mentioned earlier, the foreign affairs minister, Penny Wong, is in the Philippines today for the Asean foreign affairs ministers’ meeting, the East Asia summit foreign ministers’ meeting and the Asean regional forum.

The 11 Asean nations include Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Brunei Darussalam, Vietnam, Laos, Myanmar, Cambodia and Timor-Leste.

Crucially, it means Wong could have an opportunity to put pressure on Lao officials after they said they do not have enough evidence to lay charges over the methanol poisoning deaths of Australian citizens Holly Bowles and Bianca Jones – because autopsies were not carried out.

In a statement on Friday, Wong said the government was “deeply frustrated and bitterly disappointed that authorities in Laos are not pursuing the most serious charges” in relation to the deaths.

The federal government has announced a $25m package of support for aid in Gaza and “reforms for governance” in Palestine, “to strengthen the institutional foundations of the State of Palestine”.

The foreign affairs minister, Penny Wong, and the minister for international development, Anne Aly, announced the funding this morning, saying it forms part of “coordinated international efforts to advance a two-state solution.”

The funding includes $15m to the UN Horizon Fund, whose strategic goal is to “contribute to inclusive early recovery and reconstruction for Palestinians in the Gaza Strip” after the 10 October ceasefire last year.

More than $80m has been pledged to the Horizon Fund internationally, with contributions from France, Germany, Italy, the Netherlands, Norway, Sweden, Switzerland and the United Kingdom, the government’s media release notes.

Australia has also pledged $10m to the World Bank’s Palestinian Fund for Reconstruction and Development (PFRD) “to strengthen the Palestinian Authority’s public financial management”. Japan, the UK, Canada, France, Sweden, Switzerland, and Iceland have also contributed to the PFRD.

double quotation markThis package demonstrates Australia’s practical commitment to Palestinian recovery, reform and institution-building. Hamas can play no role in the State of Palestine.

A lasting peace requires legitimate governance, economic opportunity and security for both Palestinians and Israelis.

Australia will continue to work closely with international partners to help create those conditions.

double quotation markThe humanitarian needs of Palestinians remain immense, and communities will require sustained support to recover and rebuild. This funding package will complement our existing humanitarian assistance.

This investment will help strengthen institutions, advance reform efforts and support communities as they work towards recovery and resilience.

Australia’s development assistance delivers practical outcomes for vulnerable people while supporting the governance and institutional reforms needed for long-term stability and prosperity.

Qantas has apologised to about 250 passengers who were stuck on the tarmac in Sydney for four-and-a-half hours on Monday afternoon while the airline dealt with multiple separate aircraft issues.

Flight QF43 to Denpasar had been scheduled to leave at 4.20pm yesterday.

A passenger on the flight called ABC Radio Sydney last night, after four hours of waiting on the tarmac, saying:

double quotation markThey’ve come back with multiple messages and they’ve constantly been changing throughout, the time frame, to the point where a lot of people are not really sure now what’s going on and what is the truth and are we actually going to get off the ground? But there’s been some engineers in and out for hours trying to work out what’s going on with the plane, and now we’re just being told they’re about to shut the computers down and reboot them.

A Qantas spokesperson told Guardian Australia that the flight was delayed initially due to a refuelling issue but then engineers identified a separate issue that required assessment and resulted in further delays.

double quotation markWe understand this was a frustrating experience for our customers and sincerely apologise for this inconvenience. We’re reviewing this incident and acknowledge that our communication could have been better in this instance.

The flight departed for Denpasar at 8.50pm.

Penny Wong congratulates Ed Miliband on appointment as UK foreign secretary

As the UK prime minister, Andy Burnham, announces his new cabinet, the congratulations from Australian ministers have been rolling through.

The foreign affairs minister, Penny Wong, congratulated Ed Miliband on his appointment as UK foreign secretary, saying on X:

double quotation markAustralia and the UK have an enduring partnership, underpinned by shared history, values and interests. I look forward to working with you again to further strengthen and modernise our relationship.

She also congratulated the outgoing UK foreign secretary, Yvette Cooper, on her new appointment as secretary of state for health and social care, saying on X:

double quotation markThank you to @YvetteCooperMP for your friendship and for your contribution to our close partnership.

Your leadership and determined diplomacy have helped deepen cooperation between Australia and the UK, and I wish you every success as Health Secretary.

Wong is currently in the Philippines to attend the Asean-Australia foreign ministers’ meeting, the East Asia summit foreign ministers’ meeting and the Asean regional forum.

She described the meetings as an opportunity “to advance Australia’s interests and continue work on shared challenges, including energy security, supply chain resilience and long-term economic prosperity.”

The meetings will also mark the 50th anniversary of the Treaty of Amity and Cooperation in Southeast Asia, and 80 years of diplomatic ties between Australia and the Philippines.

title: US launches further strikes on Iran; Houthis threaten Saudi shipping blockade – Middle East crisis live
excerpt:
US says latest attack aimed at further degrading Iranian military capabilities; Yemen militant group threatens traffic moving through Bab el-Mandeb strait

US launches 10th round of strikes as Iran sa...
body_text:
US says latest attack aimed at further degrading Iranian military capabilities; Yemen militant group threatens traffic moving through Bab el-Mandeb strait

US launches 10th round of strikes as Iran says it is in ‘full-scale war’

Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthi rebels have announced a maritime embargo of Saudi Arabia.

The rebels “declare a maritime embargo against the criminal Saudi enemy, based on the equation of ‘an eye for an eye’ effective immediately upon the issuance of this statement,” military spokesperson Yahya Saree said.

The territory controlled by the Houthis in Yemen sits alongside the critical Bab al-Mandab strait, which connects the Red Sea to the Gulf of Aden and the Arabian Sea, and is vulnerable to Houthi attacks by drones missiles and small boats. The Red Sea route is responsible for about 15% of global naval shipping trade.

The decision by the Houthis could put new pressure on Saudi Arabia as it is using its East-West Pipeline to try to get oil out of the kingdom as the strait of Hormuz remains in the effective control of Iran.

Tensions between the Houthis and Saudi Arabia were inflamed earlier this month after Yemeni government planes, supported by Saudi Arabia, bombed the Houthi-controlled Sana’a airport in protest at Iranian efforts to send a plane to the city containing a Houthi delegation returning from the funeral of the Iranian supreme leader Ali Khamenei. The Houthis responded by targeting Saudi Arabia’s Abha international airport.

The Houthi militant group controls Sana’a, while Yemen’s internationally-recognised government, backed by Saudi Arabia, is based in the southern Yemeni city of Aden. For over a decade, aircraft entering Yemeni airspace have needed permission from the Saudi-led coalition first.

Two oil tankers ⁠caught fire after explosions while attempting ⁠to transit ⁠the ​southern route of the strait of ⁠Hormuz, Iran’s Revolutionary Guard has said.

It ​added in the statement on ⁠Tuesday that the ‌strait ‌would remain closed as ‌long as the US military actions in the region continued.

However, the US military said after its 10th consecutive night of strikes on Iran that commercial vessel transits continue through the vital energy route and that US forces would “hold Iran accountable for unwarranted aggression toward civilian mariners”.

The Guard’s statement, carried by state news agency Irna, said:

double quotation markTwo non-compliant oil tankers attempting to pass through the unsafe southern route of the Strait of Hormuz were stopped after explosions caused extensive fires aboard them.

The US military says it has completed its latest round of strikes against Iran and asserts that transits through the strait of Hormuz “continue”.

Central Command (Centcom) said in a statement posted on X:

double quotation markU.S. forces struck Iranian military command centers, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defense systems to degrade Iran’s ability to continue attacking commercial vessels flowing through the Strait of Hormuz.

Commercial vessel transits through the vital international maritime corridor continue. Since early May, CENTCOM forces have helped facilitate the transit of approximately 900 commercial vessels and 450 million barrels of crude oil.

double quotation markAmerican forces remain postured and prepared to hold Iran accountable for unwarranted aggression toward civilian mariners seeking to freely and openly transit the strait.

The latest strikes were completed at 9pm ET (1am GMT Tuesday), it said.

The US military has just said it finished its latest round of strikes against Iran.

Oil prices have softened as markets weigh reports of mediation efforts between the US and Iran against ⁠the fresh strikes between ​the two and threats of a naval blockade of Saudi Arabia by Yemen’s Houthis.

Brent crude futures eased 35 cents, or 0.4%, to $88.87 ⁠a barrel by 0052 GMT on Tuesday, while US West Texas Intermediate crude for September delivery was steady at $82.47 a barrel, Reuters is reporting.

Both contracts were trading below ⁠their highest levels in more than a month hit in the previous session.

The chief market analyst at KCM Trade, Tim Waterer, said:

double quotation markThe threats of a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia by the Houthis are significant because they raise the risk of disruption to another major oil exporter.

The latest US-Iran hostilities are likely ⁠to cast ​a shadow over a diplomatic gathering this week in south-east Asia – a region that relies heavily on Middle East oil and gas and was severely hit by the global supply and price shocks the war triggered.

The Middle East crisis is expected to feature prominently in the talks of foreign ministers from the 11-member Association of Southeast Asian Nations ⁠in Manila, said a spokesperson for host the Philippines.

“We’re looking at several occasions where they can raise this very, very critical topic,” Dominic Xavier Imperial said, quoted by Reuters.

The import-dependent Philippines declared a national energy emergency in March and has been forced to cast an ever-wider net for fuel, including purchases of oil from Russia, whose foreign minister Sergei Lavrov is to arrive on Tuesday, AFP reports.

US secretary of state Marco Rubio landed in Manila on Tuesday morning as the rekindled war with Iran places traditional American allies under economic strain.

More now on Iran firing missiles at a US base in Kuwait: the army was targeting US Himars missile systems, state news agency Irna said.

It quoted Iranian army officials as saying that Himars – or high mobility artillery rocket system – has “the ability to move quickly against ground targets, which, when targeted, causes damage to the offensive and defensive layers and reduces the enemy’s missile power”.

Camp Arifjan – south of Kuwait City – houses US military elements including the air force, navy, marines and coast guard.

Here’s more on the US military’s 10th consecutive round of strikes on Iran to force the reopening of the strait of Hormuz.

William Christou reports that earlier on Monday, Iran’s president said the country was in a “full-scale war” after the US expanded its aerial campaign and Tehran struck Washington’s Gulf allies, even as diplomats said talks to de-escalate were continuing.

Iranian media reported strikes around Tabriz, believed to hold underground missile bases, as well as Bushehr, home to the country’s only operational civilian nuclear power plant.

The US military said it had struck “military command centres, air defence and coastal surveillance sites, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites and communications networks”.

Iran launched attacks against Bahrain – which hosts the US navy’s fifth fleet – and Kuwait, in addition to striking tankers in the strait of Hormuz. Bahrain said its air traffic systems were damaged by the strikes but traffic was not affected.

The Iran-allied Houthis in Yemen also announced a blockade of Saudi ports.

“The reality is that today the Islamic Republic of Iran is engaged in a full-scale war,” the Iranian president, Masoud Pezeshkian, said on Monday, warning it might have to endure more strikes.

US president Donald Trump signalled an escalation in tactics, saying Iran ⁠would “pay” ​for killing US soldiers “many times over” after multiple service members ‌were killed ​in action over the ​weekend.

Iran’s army is saying it has targeted US missile systems at Kuwait’s Arifjan base with surface-to-air missiles, according to Iranian state television.

The Kuwait army said about an hour ago that its air defences were responding to Iranian missile and drone attacks as Tehran continues striking at US-allied countries around the Gulf region.

Democrats have seized on the deaths of three US troops killed in Iranian strikes to urge Donald Trump to urgently reverse his resumption of the war with Iran amid widespread anxiety over climbing casualties.

Even as US forces launched a 10th consecutive wave of attacks against Iranian targets, concern over the deaths of American service personnel threatened to complicate moves to escalate the conflict, which has reignited after he last week walked away from a memorandum of understanding agreed with Iran in June.

The Pentagon released the names of two soldiers killed in a strike on Muwaffaq Salti air base in Jordan on Friday. They were First Lieutenant Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, and Private Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas.

A third member of the armed forces was killed in northern Iraq on Saturday in what US Central Command called a “controlled detonation” of unexploded ordnance. The remains of a possible fourth dead service member were found over the weekend in Jordan.

Chris Murphy, a Democratic senator for Connecticut, accused Trump of having “frighteningly lost control of this war”.

A British maritime agency says it has received multiple reports that a tanker is saying it has been struck by an unknown projectile in the strait of Hormuz.

It occurred eight nautical miles north-east of Limah, Oman, and authorities are investigating, the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency says in a report posted on X.

Kuwait’s air defences are responding to hostile missile and drone attacks launched by Iran, the Kuwaiti army said on Monday.

It comes after the US military earlier announced a tenth consecutive night of strikes on Iran. Tehran has been responding with attacks on its Gulf neighbours that host US forces, including Kuwait.

Iranian media reported explosions in parts of the country including the port city of Bandar Abbas, after US Central Command announced a new wave of strikes on Iran for the tenth night in a row. The Mehr news agency also reported air defence systems being activated near the Bushehr nuclear power plant. “The strikes are designed to further degrade Iranian military capabilities used to attack commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz,” Centcom said.

It came hours after Donald Trump said Iran would pay for killing US soldiers “many times over” after multiple service members ‌were killed ​in ⁠action ​over the ​weekend. The US military on Monday identified two soldiers killed in Jordan while defending against Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks. An examination of remains is underway to determine whether they belong to a third service member who was reporting missing in that attack. And in Iraq, another US service member died after “a controlled detonation” of an Iranian drone. It brings the total number of US military deaths in the war with Iran, which the US and Israel started in late February, to 17.

Earlier, Iran’s president said the country is in a “full-scale war” after the United States expanded its aerial campaign and Tehran struck Washington’s Gulf allies, even as diplomats said talks are ongoing to de-escalate. “The reality is that today the Islamic Republic of Iran is engaged in a full-scale war,” Masoud Pezeshkian, said in a statement, warning Iran might have to endure more strikes. There were glimmers of hope, however, on the diplomatic front, as Iran’s interior minister traveled to Pakistan, a key mediator in the conflict, for talks.

Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthi rebels announced a maritime blockade Saudi Arabia after the two sides traded fire last week for the first time in years. Condemning the blockade, Saudi Arabia’s foreign ministry said the kingdom “will take all necessary measures to protect its vessels in accordance with international law”.

The Lebanese army began taking charge of security in three southern villages, the United States said, as a deal to secure an Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon and the disarmament of Hezbollah faced its first test on the ground. The US state department said operations had started in “pilot zones” in Froun, Srifa and Zawtar al-Gharbiya in accordance with the trilateral framework. However, the mayor of Froun, Hassan Bazzi, told Al Jazeera on Monday that the Lebanese army had not yet begun deploying in Zawtar al-Gharbiya, because Israeli forces are yet to withdraw from the outskirts of the town. “There have been no new developments in the town of Froun. The situation remains unchanged,” said.

⁠Trump also said on Monday that Israeli prime minister Benjamin Netanyahu will not be arrested ⁠when he visits ⁠the ​United States, after New York City mayor Zohran Mamdani said he was still ⁠considering the move. “Benjamin Netanyahu will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form, while ⁠in the United States of America,” Trump wrote ​on social media, without referring to Mamdani’s comments.

Hamas named Khalil al-Hayya as its new overall leader, according to a statement from the Palestinian militant ⁠group, succeeding Yahya Sinwar who was killed by the Israeli military in Gaza in October 2024. Hayya, ⁠the group’s exiled Gaza chief and top negotiator, is generally seen as more hardline than ⁠Khaled Meshaal, the head of the militant group’s office in exile and the other frontrunner for the leadership.

Jordan said three missiles launched from Iran were intercepted. No injuries or material damage has been reported. Sirens sounded in the capital Amman on Monday evening, AFP reported.

Saudi Arabia on Monday condemned an announcement by Yemen’s Houthis of a maritime blockade of the kingdom, as the Iran-backed rebels threatened the top oil exporter’s ability to bypass the strait of Hormuz.

In a statement the Saudi foreign ministry said it “expresses the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia’s strongest condemnation of the statements made by the so-called military spokesman of the terrorist Houthi militia ... imposing a maritime blockade on the Kingdom.”

The kingdom, it said, “will take all necessary measures to protect its vessels in accordance with international law.”

It added that Riyadh “continues to support the brotherly Yemeni people and their legitimate government”.

Iranian state TV is reporting explosions in the port city of Bandar Abbas. And the Mehr news agency is reporting air defence systems being activated near the Bushehr nuclear power plant.

The US military began a fresh round of strikes against Iran about 20 minutes ago, according to Centcom.

“The strikes are designed to further degrade Iranian military capabilities used to attack commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz,” it said in a statement.

title: Weather warnings as another tropical storm develops along US Gulf coast
excerpt:
Tropical Storm Bertha could bring heavy rain on US Gulf coast, with its center located 110 miles south of Panama City, Florida

Tropical Storm Bertha developed along the US Gulf coast on Monday evenin...
body_text:
Tropical Storm Bertha could bring heavy rain on US Gulf coast, with its center located 110 miles south of Panama City, Florida

Tropical Storm Bertha developed along the US Gulf coast on Monday evening, bringing the threat of heavy rain and storm surge flooding as Tropical Storm Fausto strengthened off Mexico’s south-west Pacific coast.

The center of Bertha was located about 110 miles (180 km) south of Panama City, Florida. Maximum sustained winds were around 40 mph, according to an evening advisory from the Miami-based National Hurricane Center.

The slow-moving storm has been drifting erratically, but is expected to stay near or along the northern Gulf coast early this week, bringing heavy rainfall to areas of northern Florida, Alabama, Mississippi and Louisiana as it pushes westward toward Texas. Forecasters said some additional strengthening is expected in the coming hours.

Bertha is the second tropical storm of the Atlantic hurricane season and is expected to make landfall in south-east Louisiana on Wednesday. Tropical Storm Arthur last month brought heavy rainfall to the south-eastern United States.

A storm surge watch was in effect from the Alabama-Florida border westward to the mouth of the Mississippi River in Louisiana. A tropical storm watch, meanwhile, extended from the Mississippi-Alabama border to the line separating Louisiana’s Jefferson and Plaquemines parishes, the NHC added.

The NHC said the storm surge watch indicated a risk of life-threatening coastal flooding from rising water moving inland from the shoreline within the next 48 hours. Combined with high tide, storm surge could flood normally dry coastal areas along parts of the northern Gulf coast.

Water levels could rise by 2-4ft (60-120cm) from the mouth of the Mississippi River to the Alabama-Florida border, with 1-3ft of surge possible across portions of coastal Louisiana and Florida, including Lake Pontchartrain.

The depression was also expected to produce widespread rainfall totals of 2-4in, with isolated amounts of up to 8in through Friday from western Florida to the middle Texas coast. The heavy rain could trigger flash flooding in some areas, while an isolated tornado or two was possible across Florida’s Big Bend region.

Meanwhile, Tropical Storm Fausto continued to strengthen over the eastern Pacific. As of Monday morning, the storm was moving west-northwest at about 13mph with maximum sustained winds of 65mph. It is forecast to become a hurricane later Monday, with additional strengthening expected over the next few days.

Although no coastal watches or warnings were in effect for Fausto, the NHC said swells generated by the storm were expected to reach the west coast of the Baja California Peninsula on Wednesday and persist for several days. The swells were likely to create life-threatening surf and rip current conditions.

Fausto is forecast to continue tracking west-northwest through Wednesday.

title: Bodycam catches Sunny Hostin invoking her legal chops as cops cite Harvard-grad son for trespassing
excerpt:
Body camera video shows "The View" co-host Sunny Hostin urging MTA police to show leniency after her son was stopped for trespassing on Metro-North railroad property. Credit: MTA

"The View" co-host S...
body_text:
Body camera video shows "The View" co-host Sunny Hostin urging MTA police to show leniency after her son was stopped for trespassing on Metro-North railroad property. Credit: MTA

"The View" co-host Sunny Hostin repeatedly reminded police she was a "former federal prosecutor" after officers stopped her Harvard-educated son for trespassing on Metro-North railroad property, as newly released body camera video captured the TV host urging officers to cut him a break.

"My name is Sunny Hostin and I'm one of the co-hosts of 'The View' and I'm a former federal prosecutor," Hostin tells an officer over the phone in bodycam video obtained by Fox News Digital.

After arriving at the scene, Hostin continued making her case.

Body camera video shows "The View" co-host Sunny Hostin speaking with MTA police as her son, Gabriel Hostin, stands nearby after being stopped for trespassing on Metro-North railroad property. (Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA))

"That's my son. He's a Harvard graduate. He doesn't have a criminal record," she says. "He's not a kid who's ever in trouble. It's an innocent mistake."

According to records obtained by Fox News Digital, Gabriel Hostin was stopped by Metropolitan Transportation Authority police shortly before 8 p.m. June 16 after officers observed him on the Metro-North railroad right-of-way at the New Rochelle station.

Police alleged he was trespassing in an area marked with "NO TRESPASSING" signs.

"The View" co-host Sunny Hostin speaks with Metropolitan Transportation Authority police officers after her son, Gabriel Hostin, was stopped for trespassing on Metro-North railroad property in New Rochelle, New York. (Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA))

An MTA Police spokesperson told Fox News Digital the 23-year-old was observed running near an electrified third rail before officers stopped him near Commerce Drive, conducted a criminal background check and escorted him from the area.

Because Hostin had no criminal history and cooperated with officers, police said he was issued a summons for trespass and released.

The bodycam video shows Gabriel Hostin asking officers whether issuing the summons was necessary.

Co-host Sunny Hostin speaks during ABC's "The View" on July 6, 2026. (ABC/TheView)

"Because it is an arrestable offense. I want to make that clear," one officer tells him.

"We're dimming it down to a trespass violation because you were in the right-of-way."

The officer goes on to explain that police were taking his lack of criminal history into account.

"We know you're not a criminal or anything like that, but our hands are tied with cameras everywhere," the officer says.

Hostin again urged officers to exercise discretion.

"He's a Harvard grad who teaches fourth-grade geometry to South Bronx kids," she says. "He's not a kid who's ever in trouble. It's an innocent mistake."

A Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) subway sign is pictured on June 29, 2017 in New York City. (Spencer Platt/Getty Images)

According to charging documents obtained by Fox News Digital, an officer alleged he observed Gabriel Hostin on the active railroad right-of-way at the New Rochelle station "in violation of the posted NO TRESPASSING signs."

The citation narrative similarly states officers observed him on the railroad right-of-way before conducting a warrant check that came back negative and issuing him an appearance ticket for trespass.

The records also include photographs of warning signs reading, "NO TRESPASSING – PRIVATE PROPERTY – VIOLATORS WILL BE PROSECUTED," posted near the entrance to the property, along with photos of the gated access point.

According to court records, Gabriel Hostin is scheduled to appear in New Rochelle City Court on July 31.

In a letter obtained by TMZ, Hostin argued her son mistakenly entered the property while jogging after spotting what appeared to be a gravel incline suitable for hill training.

She said the entrance gate was open and that the only "No Trespassing" sign was attached to the left gate and was not visible when he entered.

The bodycam video also sparked reaction online, with several conservative accounts criticizing Hostin for invoking her legal background during the encounter.

The View's Sunny Hostin claimed earthquake, cicadas and the eclipse could be seen as signs of climate change. (screenshot)

Libs of TikTok contrasted Hostin's past remarks that "no one is above the law" with her comments to officers that she was "a former federal prosecutor" and that her son was a Harvard graduate with no criminal record.

End Wokeness posted a similar critique, arguing the footage conflicted with Hostin's previous public comments about equal treatment under the law.

Fox News Digital reached out to Hostin's representatives for comment.

Stepheny Price is a Writer at Fox News with a focus on West Coast and Midwest news, missing persons, national and international crime stories, homicide cases, and border security.

title: Trump exploring additional tariffs on Canada for wildfire smoke impacts in US, White House official says –as it happened
excerpt:
Donald Trump to impose 50% tariff on most Canadian goods, White House says

As the global economy reels from the war he started with Iran in February, Donald Trump stepped up his trade war with Canada...
body_text:
Donald Trump to impose 50% tariff on most Canadian goods, White House says

As the global economy reels from the war he started with Iran in February, Donald Trump stepped up his trade war with Canada on Monday, signing three proclamations to impose 50% tariffs on most imported Canadian goods, citing a provision of a 1930 trade law.

Trump claimed that the tariffs on one of the most important US trade partners, which risk higher inflation in the US, were justified by what he claimed was Canada’s unfair discrimination against American cars, alcohol and dairy products.

A Trump administration official told the Associated Press that Canada was one of the only nations other than China that retaliated against Trump’s previous tariffs and must be held accountable.

Several Democratic lawmakers last year proposed repealing the section of the 1930 Tariff Act used by Trump, fearing that he could use it to destabilize the economy after many of his tariffs were ruled illegal by the US supreme court.

The new 50% tariffs exclude energy products, fish and critical minerals, but include goods that had previously been protected from import taxes by the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, or USMCA. That 2020 trade pact recently expired and triggered a new set of negotiations that could run until 2036.

There could be more tariffs to come. The official who briefed reporters on the proclamations said that Trump had also asked his aides look into additional tariffs on Canada because its wildfires hurt air quality in the US.

At the World Cup final on Sunday, Trump congratulated players on stage with Canada’s prime minister Mark Carney, a tournament co-host, who has pushed back on Trump’s tariffs and threats to annex Canada.

Trump claims in the proclamations that Canada discriminates against American autos, alcohol and cheese relative to other nations, but his argument rests in large part on retaliatory actions taken by Canada after he imposed a series of tariffs on Canada last year.

Trump’s proclamation on alcohol notes that Canadian provinces and territories halted the purchase and retailing of American alcoholic beverages last year, without noting that action was in response to Trump’s tariffs and taunts about making Canada the 51st US state.

This concludes our live coverage of the second Trump administration on a day when the president imposed new tariffs under an obscure provision of a 1930 law that worsened the Great Depression. Or, as the international trade law expert Scott Lincicome observed: “Tariff Crazytown is officially BACK”.

As the global economy reels from the war he started with Iran in February, Donald Trump stepped up his trade war with Canada on Monday, signing three proclamations to impose 50% tariffs on most imported Canadian goods, citing a provision of a 1930 trade law.

Canadians across the political spectrum united in anger at Trump. “If these tariffs proceed, Canada should respond tariff for tariff, dollar for dollar,” Ontario premier Doug Ford said. Canada’s prime minister, Mark Carney, called the tariffs a “direct violation” of the US-Mexico-Canada free trade agreement Trump negotiated. Canada’s Conservatives called the tariffs “another unacceptable and unjustified attack on Canadian workers and our businesses,” adding: “Canadians are not a punching bag.” The leader of Canada’s New Democrats called them “the latest economic grenade thrown over the border by an out of control President who has lost touch with reality.”

Ilya Somin, a professor of law at George Mason University who helped file the successful challenge to Donald Trump’s “liberation day” tariffs, points out, the president’s new 50% tariffs on Canadian imports “are based on Section 338 of the notorious 1930 Smoot-Hawley tariff legislation, which severely exacerbated the Great Depression.”

Nancy Mace, a Republican congresswoman from South Carolina, announced on Monday that she is not entering the race to replace the late senator Lindsey Graham in the snap Republican primary next month.

In a blog post for Reason magazine, Ilya Somin, a professor of law at George Mason University who helped file the successful challenge to Donald Trump’s “liberation day” tariffs, points out, the president’s new 50% tariffs on Canadian imports “are based on Section 338 of the notorious 1930 Smoot-Hawley tariff legislation, which severely exacerbated the Great Depression.”

This novel use of the infamous 1930 law, which, as all viewers of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off know, did not work, is, Somin argues “just the latest of a series of efforts by Trump to usurp congressional tariff authority and impose harmful and dangerous trade restrictions that damage the US economy and poison relations with our allies and trading partners, of which Canada is one of the most important.”

As the economic historian Phil Magness predicted last year, Trump cited Section 338 of the 1930 law to justify his new tariffs on Monday.

As Magness explained in October the authority comes from the Smoot–Hawley Tariff Act, “which turned an economic downturn into a decade-long catastrophe.” Magness went on:

double quotation markFearing foreign retaliation against its own protective tariff schedule under Smoot–Hawley, Congress authorized the president to impose tariffs of up to 50 percent on any country that deploys discriminatory trade practices against the United States. Section 338 did not work as intended, as Smoot–Hawley triggered a catastrophic global trade war that brought international commerce to a halt, destroyed the U.S. export market, and thrust us deeper into the Great Depression. Its language remains on the books as a vestigial relic of this notorious law, although its functions have been superseded by later tariff law revisions that imposed more restrictions on the president’s authority.

Canadians across the political spectrum united in anger on Monday to denounce Donald Trump’s new 50% tariffs on imports from Canada.

While the prime minister, Mark Carney, chose his words carefully, opposition parties from his right and left were more forceful in their reaction.

Leaders of Canada’s Conservative party, including the shadow minister for Canada-US relations, Shuv Majumdar, called Trump’s latest tariffs “another unacceptable and unjustified attack on Canadian workers and our businesses,” adding: “Canadians are not a punching bag. Their anxieties about their future have only been deepened. These tariffs must be withdrawn immediately.”

“Fourteen months ago, Prime Minister Mark Carney described the breakdown in our relationship with the United States as the worst economic crisis since the Second World War. He was right,” the generally pro-Trump party leaders continued.

“President Trump is wrong to target Canadian workers and must reverse these tariffs immediately,” they added.

From the opposite end of the spectrum, the leader of Canada’s progressive New Democrats party, Avi Lewis, was even more blunt.

“The announcement of 50% tariffs on a range of Canadian goods is the latest economic grenade thrown over the border by an out of control President who has lost touch with reality,” Lewis posted on social media. “It is a direct attack on Canadian workers and industries, putting thousands of jobs at risk. And it will drive up costs for American consumers, who like Canadian consumers, are already feeling pain at the pumps and supermarket checkouts because of Trump’s illegal war on Iran.”

“Donald Trump is trying a new, obscure legal basis for this latest attack,” Lewis went on, referring to the legal authority Trump cited, Section 338 of the 1930 Smoot–Hawley Tariff Act that’s never been used before to impose tariffs.

“If these tariffs go ahead, we must be prepared to fight them with counter-tariffs and put every dollar behind supporting workers in affected sectors,” Lewis added. “The Carney government must refrain from more concessions. We are tired of being treated as Trump’s tariff guinea pigs. New Democrats stand with workers, communities and all Canadians in defiance of this newest threat to our economy and sovereignty.

Canada’s prime minister, Mark Carney, has released a statement in response to threats from Donald Trump to impose new 50% tariffs on Canadian goods in 30 days unless Canada removes barriers on US auto, alcohol and dairy products.

Carney noted that all of the measures imposed by Canada on imports from the US that Trump complained of on Monday were taken in retaliation to previous US tariffs imposed by Trump.

double quotation mark“Today, the United States administration announced its intention to impose a new 50% tariff on a significant number of Canadian goods.

This is the latest in a series of unilateral U.S. trade actions that began with the U.S. imposing a series of tariffs in direct violation of the Canada-United States-Mexico Agreement (CUSMA), the free trade agreement between Canada, the United States, and Mexico.

These include tariffs on the Canadian auto sector, in violation of CUSMA. Canada, as is its right, has merely matched those measures.

More broadly, in response to these measures and threats to Canadian sovereignty, provinces, territories, and Canadians from coast to coast to coast have stood together, taking the necessary actions to support our economy and defend our workers, farmers, businesses, and families.

Recognising that the U.S. has been transforming all of its trade relationships, including those covered under CUSMA, over the past 18 months, Canada has made a series of detailed and comprehensive proposals to resolve this dispute and to modernise CUSMA. We stand ready to intensify those discussions in the coming weeks.

Canada believes in the benefits of free and fair trade, as evidenced by our new government signing more than 20 new economic and security partnerships. This trade dispute has raised costs for families, particularly in the U.S. Canada stands ready to engage intensively to address outstanding issues with the U.S. to the mutual benefit of our citizens.

In all circumstances, Canada will work relentlessly and take any measures necessary to build our strength at home and to support Canadian workers, farmers, businesses, and families.”

As part of the Trump administration’s continuing attack on left-leaning groups, the US state department has listed several activists and organizations as “front groups and fellow travelers” for Cuba, including Hasan Piker and Amazon Labor Union founder Chris Smalls.

In a 100-page report released on Monday, the state department denounces Cuba as the “capital of 21st-century communism”, accusing the Cuban government of carrying out a “campaign of subversion” that has “infiltrated the highest reaches of the US government, recruited and cultivated generations of American activists [and] backed an unprecedented wave of leftwing terrorism on American soil”.

The state department report alleges that the so-called campaign is “designed to turn America against itself” and singles out several left-leaning activists and organizations, including the Democratic Socialists of America, Isra Hirsi – the daughter of Minnesota Democratic representative Ilhan Omar – New York City mayor Zohran Mamdani, and journalist Amy Goodman, citing their criticism of US policy toward Cuba or participation in Cuba solidarity movements.

Describing the DSA, of which Mamdani is a member, the report says the organization “serves as a particularly potent illustration of the ideological victory of Cuba’s effort to position itself as the spiritual capital of Third Worldist radicalism”. It accuses the DSA of maintaining a “fierce, almost religious commitment to the cause of the Cuban regime – not because the group is controlled by Cuban agents, per se, but because that commitment is now simply the de facto policy orthodoxy on the American far left”.

Trump has doubled down on similar messaging in recent days, using the term communism at political rallies and presidential engagements alike.

For more than 60 years, the US has maintained a sweeping economic embargo on Cuba, in addition to crippling sanctions imposed by numerous administrations. Despite US claims that its policies are designed to pressure the Cuban government, Havana and many international critics including human rights organizations say they have severely hurt the island’s economy.

American distillers are worried that Donald Trump’s new 50% tariffs on spirits imported from Canada, announced on Monday and due to go into effect 19 August, could intensify the trade war the president began last year, denting their profits.

“For nearly a year and a half, American spirits have been pulled from store shelves across much of Canada as collateral damage in a broader trade dispute unrelated to our sector, and we appreciate the Administration’s recognition of the significant damage these restrictions have caused U.S. distillers,” Chris Swonger, chief executive of the Distilled Spirits Council of the United States, said in a statement on Monday.

“We had hoped, however, that this issue could be resolved without further escalation,” Swonger continued. The new tariff “deepens trade tensions and raises the risk of further retaliation at a time when many U.S. hospitality businesses continue to face financial hardships,” he added, before calling for “policymakers on both sides of the border to pursue a negotiated solution”.

The US trade group makes clear what the Trump administration has not: that Canadians only stopped selling US alcohol last year in response to Trump’s actions. “Canadian provinces removed U.S. spirits from retail shelves in March 2025 in retaliation to U.S. tariffs on Canadian goods,” the council says. As a result, US exports to Canada “fell more than 70% year-over-year from the start of the retaliatory ban in March 2025 through December 2025,” the industry group adds.

The bans have only been lifted in two Canadian provinces: Alberta and Saskatchewan.

The president of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, Candace Laing, called for talks between the US and Canada before Donald Trump’s newly announced 50% tariffs on Canadian imports take effect in 30 days.

“We knew this would get bumpier before landing. We shared our concerns with the Canadian government about the rising threat Section 338 tariffs posed, given something like this could be coming, “Laing said in a statement, referring to the section of a 1930 US trade law cited by Trump.
 ”While this is a regrettable escalation by the U.S. Administration, we now have 30 days before this comes into effect,” she added. “Both sides need to use this window to make meaningful progress in advancing formal talks. There are important discussions to get to that will strengthen not threaten our shared prosperity.”

Ontario’s premier, Doug Ford, who leads the Canadian province that has suffered the most from US auto tariffs, called on Canada to retaliate for the new 50% tariffs announced on Monday by Donald Trump, should they go into effect in 30 days.

“I’ll never stop fighting to protect Ontario,” Ford wrote on social media. “If these tariffs proceed, Canada should respond tariff for tariff, dollar for dollar.”

Last year, Ford’s province paid for an anti-tariff television ad that infuriated Trump, because it used parts of a 1987 speech against tariffs given by Ronald Reagan and ran during Major League Baseball World Series games between the Toronto Blue Jays and the Los Angeles Dodgers.

Earlier on Monday, Ford also called Trump’s threat to impose additional tariffs on Canada for failing to prevent wildfire smoke from drifting over the border into the US a “bunch of rubbish.”

Ford pointed out that smoke from wildfires in the US also travels north to Canada.

“With all the assets the US has, and he’s sitting there complaining, why don’t you send up some water bombers?” Ford also asked of Trump. “Why don’t you start helping, like we would help you, in a heartbeat?”

“You should support your neighbors, that’s my message to the president,” Ford added.

Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, said in a statement that the new 50% tariffs on imported Canadian goods imposed on Monday by Donald Trump, under Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930, was entirely justified.

“While the Administration continues to secure fair and reciprocal trade deals with our trading partners, Canada, unlike other partners and allies, continues to retaliate against the United States for its efforts to rebalance trade and protect U.S. industry in national-security sensitive sectors,” Greer said. “Specifically, Canada has taken U.S. alcohol products off Canadian shelves, given better market access to dairy products from the European Union, and has put a cap on U.S. vehicle exports to Canada from companies reshoring to the United States.”

What Greer did not say is that Canada’s actions, including local bans on selling US alcohol products in some Canadian provinces, were taken in response to tariffs imposed by Trump in the first place, and his threats to annex the country.

Democrats have seized on the deaths of three US troops killed in Iranian strikes to urge Donald Trump to urgently reverse his resumption of the war with Iran amid widespread anxiety over climbing casualties.

Even as US forces launched a 10th consecutive wave of attacks against Iranian targets, concern over the deaths of American service personnel threatened to complicate moves to escalate the conflict, which has reignited after he last week walked away from a memorandum of understanding agreed with Iran in June.

The Pentagon released the names of two soldiers killed in a strike on Muwaffaq Salti air base in Jordan on Friday. They were First Lieutenant Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, and Private Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas.

A third member of the armed forces was killed in northern Iraq on Saturday in what US Central Command called a “controlled detonation” of unexploded ordnance. The remains of a possible fourth dead service member were found over the weekend in Jordan.

Chris Murphy, a Democratic senator for Connecticut, accused Trump of having “frighteningly lost control of this war”.

“For those of us who pay attention to the hour-to-hour pace of the Iran War, things are getting very dark very quickly,” he wrote on social media. “The scale of attacks – and threats of escalation – are increasing rapidly.”

“The reckless and costly war of choice in Iran has been a disaster for the American people,” wrote Hakeem Jeffries, the Democratic leader in the House of Representatives. “May God continue to watch over our troops who have needlessly been put into harm’s way.”

Mark Warner, the vice chair of the Senate intelligence committee and a Democrat from Virginia, said there were “hundreds of seriously wounded” US personnel, and added it was “remarkable” that casualty figures had not been higher.

As the global economy reels from the war he started with Iran in February, Donald Trump stepped up his trade war with Canada on Monday, signing three proclamations to impose 50% tariffs on most imported Canadian goods, citing a provision of a 1930 trade law.

Trump claimed that the tariffs on one of the most important US trade partners, which risk higher inflation in the US, were justified by what he claimed was Canada’s unfair discrimination against American cars, alcohol and dairy products.

A Trump administration official told the Associated Press that Canada was one of the only nations other than China that retaliated against Trump’s previous tariffs and must be held accountable.

Several Democratic lawmakers last year proposed repealing the section of the 1930 Tariff Act used by Trump, fearing that he could use it to destabilize the economy after many of his tariffs were ruled illegal by the US supreme court.

The new 50% tariffs exclude energy products, fish and critical minerals, but include goods that had previously been protected from import taxes by the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, or USMCA. That 2020 trade pact recently expired and triggered a new set of negotiations that could run until 2036.

There could be more tariffs to come. The official who briefed reporters on the proclamations said that Trump had also asked his aides look into additional tariffs on Canada because its wildfires hurt air quality in the US.

At the World Cup final on Sunday, Trump congratulated players on stage with Canada’s prime minister Mark Carney, a tournament co-host, who has pushed back on Trump’s tariffs and threats to annex Canada.

Trump claims in the proclamations that Canada discriminates against American autos, alcohol and cheese relative to other nations, but his argument rests in large part on retaliatory actions taken by Canada after he imposed a series of tariffs on Canada last year.

Trump’s proclamation on alcohol notes that Canadian provinces and territories halted the purchase and retailing of American alcoholic beverages last year, without noting that action was in response to Trump’s tariffs and taunts about making Canada the 51st US state.

Donald Trump has said Benjamin Netanyahu, the Israeli prime minister, “will not be arrested” if he visits the US.

His comments came days after Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, said he was exploring whether his administration could arrest Netanyahu if he visits the city during the UN general assembly in September.

In a Truth Social post on Monday, Trump said Netanyahu, who has been the subject of an international criminal court arrest warrant since November 2024 over alleged war crimes in Gaza, “will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form while in the United States of America”.

Trump added that Netanyahu is “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and said “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”.

Speaking to the New York Times on Saturday, Mamdani had said: “I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” where the international criminal court is based, adding: “He’s a war criminal who has been charged by the international criminal court, and what you will find is that is an opinion that is held by many, purely because of what his actions have wrought over these past many years.”

Mamdani, who has described Israel’s war on Gaza as a genocide, also said he is in “an active conversation” with New York City’s law department about his legal authority to direct the New York police to detain Netanyahu. “Whatever the law allows me to do in New York City, that’s what we will do,” he said, adding: “But we won’t be writing our own laws to that end.”

title: US launches fresh strikes on Iran, as Trump warns of retaliation for deaths of soldiers
excerpt:
The US military says it launched another round of strikes against Iran to "further degrade" its capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

The strikes - the 10th consecutive night of attac...
body_text:
The US military says it launched another round of strikes against Iran to "further degrade" its capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

The strikes - the 10th consecutive night of attacks - come after US President Donald Trump vowed to punish Iran for the deaths of US soldiers, two of whom have been identified by the Pentagon.

Trump earlier said Iran had been hit "very hard" the previous night "in honour" of three US soldiers killed recently - two in Jordan and one in Iraq. Iran's military and communications sites were struck.

Iran said it had targeted sites in Bahrain, Jordan and Syria, with President Masoud Pezeshkian saying Iran was now fighting a "full-scale war" with the US.

At the same time, Tehran said diplomatic messages were still being exchanged by both sides through intermediaries, despite a collapse of a ceasefire.

US Central Command (Centcom) said on X that the latest strikes had hit Iranian "military command centres, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defense systems".

Commercial vessels have continued to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, Centcom said.

On Tuesday night the Department of Defense confirmed the identities of two of the US soldiers – Pte Isabella Gonzales, 19, from Texas, and Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25, from Hawaii - who were killed in an Iranian strike on the Muwaffaq Salti base in Jordan over the weekend.

Centcom said earlier a third service member also went missing in action after the attack, and unidentified remains were later found in the area.

Elsewhere in the region, Jordan's military said late on Monday it had shot down three Iranian missiles as sirens sounded across the country warning of incoming fire.

Earlier in the day, Kuwait's armed forces said they had intercepted Iranian drones.

Bahrain said drones launched by Iran had targeted its air navigation systems - but traffic remained unaffected.

On the US strikes the previous night, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported that several cities had been hit including Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini.

Iranian state media reported that two oil tankers had exploded as the vessels tried to cross the strait.

Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said the tankers were going through what it called an "unsafe and hazardous southern route" of the strait and "exploded and were brought to a halt". Centcom disputed the claims.

Yemen's Houthis announce 'maritime embargo' on Saudi Arabia

title: Guyana recovers 27 bodies after ferry disaster as crew comes under scrutiny
excerpt:
Authorities in Guyana say they have recovered 27 bodies from a ferry that capsized off the country’s coast with 179 people on board.

The MV Barima issued a distress call on Saturday night, having ove...
body_text:
Authorities in Guyana say they have recovered 27 bodies from a ferry that capsized off the country’s coast with 179 people on board.

The MV Barima issued a distress call on Saturday night, having overturned while ferrying passengers from the capital, Georgetown, to Port Kaituma in Guyana’s northwest.

Officials said on Monday that 69 people had been rescued, while 83 remained missing. Minister of Public Works Juan Edghill described the sinking as the country’s worst marine disaster in decades.

Prime Minister Mark Phillips launched an investigation into potential negligence by the ferry’s crew in the lead-up to the accident.

Officials say that while the MV Barima was initially thought to have been carrying 116 passengers and 17 crew members, 32 people rescued from the disaster were not recorded in the ferry’s manifest, putting the number of potential victims much higher.

Police have also announced that the ferry’s captain and at least one other crew member were taken into custody after testing positive for marijuana.

“We have a zero tolerance for members of the crew drinking or smoking while at work. And for us to have positive tests from the captain, this is serious,” Edghill said.

Passengers had complained for years that officials at state-run ferry terminals were selling seats for cash without recording the true number of passengers in their manifest, which Edghill called “a criminal action”.

President Irfaan Ali visited survivors and their families in hospital.

“It was a deeply emotional experience. I listened to their stories, shared in their pain and anxiety, and assured them that every practical measure would be taken to provide the support and assistance they need,” he said.

title: New York far-left group claims reparations 'owed' as Blacks' median wealth 'nearly 15 times less' than Whites
excerpt:
‘The Big Weekend Show’ co-host Joey Jones discusses Democratic lawmakers’ efforts to advance reparations plans in Democrat-led states.

A prominent civil rights organization is pushing for statewide r...
body_text:
‘The Big Weekend Show’ co-host Joey Jones discusses Democratic lawmakers’ efforts to advance reparations plans in Democrat-led states.

A prominent civil rights organization is pushing for statewide reparations, arguing that a historical debt is owed to Black New Yorkers due to deep wealth disparities rooted in the state's ties to slavery and decades of systemic discrimination.

"Reparations are a debt that is owed. Enslaved Black people built the foundation of New York’s economy, but Black families in our state still face racism that has been codified into our laws and budgets," said Lanessa Owens-Chaplin, Director of the Racial Justice Center at the New York Civil Liberties Union (NYCLU). "New York State was not a bystander to slavery — it created, enacted, and protected the institution. Reparations must be extended to all Black New Yorkers descended from those enslaved by New York State, and to all Black New Yorkers still living under the systems slavery built."

In a joint report released last week titled "The Other New York: The Legacy of Slavery and the Case for Reparations Now," the NYCLU and the Legal Defense Fund (LDF) detailed the lasting economic impacts of slavery and Jim Crow–era policies on Black communities across the state.

CHICAGO SUBURB LOCALS HOPE REPARATIONS ADDRESSES 'AFFORDABILITY PRESSURES' AS BLACK POPULATION DWINDLES

The New York Civil Liberties Union claimed a "debt is owed" in their push for reparations, citing median wealth in Black households being less than White households. (Getty Images)

According to the report, the median wealth of Black New Yorkers stands at $18,870 — nearly 15 times less than the median wealth of White New Yorkers, which sits at $276,900. The groups noted that New York state exhibits a wider racial wealth gap than the national average.

The report points to government-backed practices like redlining as major catalysts for today's wealth inequality, noting that the exclusion and displacement of Black communities denied them "the stability and generational wealth that homeownership provides."

"Nearly 90 years after redlining and urban renewal, formerly redlined communities remain some of the highest-poverty neighborhoods in New York," the report states. "Zoning laws reinforced the racial hierarchy established under slavery, forcing Black people to live in industrial areas with increased pollution — leading to disproportionately high asthma, cardiovascular disease, premature delivery, and premature death. Schools serving predominantly Black students have been severely underfunded and disproportionately affected by environmental hazards, crumbling infrastructure, and lead poisoning."

The groups argue that reparations must go beyond symbolic gestures, advocating for both direct compensation for financial losses and broad institutional policy changes.

The groups express reparations as a broader structural change in the "racial wealth gap," policing and incarceration, "environmental racism," and "underfunded schools."  (iStock)

"Supporting reparations is about more than compensation; it is about acknowledging harm, restoring dignity, and creating pathways for communities to thrive," the authors wrote. The report outlines proposed remedies to "compensate for the harm by addressing tangible losses, such as loss of wealth, wages, and other damages caused by wrongdoing," while also targeting broader structural changes in criminal justice, environmental policy, and public school funding.

"In order to build a thriving economy and a resilient multi-racial democracy, New York must repair past harm and break down barriers to opportunity for Black communities. Reparations benefit all New Yorkers," the report urged.

The NYCLU did not respond to Fox News Digital's request for comment.

KEY TRUMP AGENCY PUTS TOP UNIVERSITIES IN CROSSHAIRS OVER ALLEGED RACE-BASED DORM PROGRAMS

These localized efforts come as state and municipal racial redress initiatives face growing legal and political scrutiny nationwide. In Evanston, Illinois — the first U.S. municipality to distribute cash and housing grants as reparations — the program is facing a high-profile federal lawsuit. The U.S. Department of Justice intervened in the suit, arguing that race-based benefit distributions violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.

New York Gov. Kathy Hochul holds up signed legislation creating a commission for the study of reparations in New York on Dec. 19, 2023, in New York City. (Michael M. Santiago/Getty Images)

Meanwhile, in New York, progress on state-level recommendations has stalled.

The New York State Community Commission on Reparations Remedies was created under legislation signed by Democratic Gov. Kathy Hochul in late 2023. The panel was tasked with examining the state's historical ties to slavery, subsequent Jim Crow practices, and modern economic disparities to formulate recommendations for policy changes and financial remedies.

However, the NYCLU slammed state officials after a provision quietly embedded in the state budget pushed the commission’s final report deadline back two years — from its original 2025 date to 2029.

Joshua Q. Nelson is a reporter for Fox News Digital.

Joshua focuses on cultural trends, education, and public policy. He extensively covered reparations developments across the U.S., the Department of Education, and immigration issues.

Joining Fox News Digital in 2019, he previously graduated from Syracuse University's Maxwell School of Citizenship and Public Affairs and received the University of Pennsylvania's Wharton Public Policy Certificate.

Story tips can be sent to joshua.nelson@fox.com and Joshua can be followed on Twitter and LinkedIn.
